2. **Spectrum Shift:** Multiplication in the time domain translates to a **frequency shift** in the frequency domain.
3. **Sideband Generation:** The result is two replicas of $M(f)$ — one centered at $+f_c$ (USB) and one at $-f_c$ (LSB).
4. **Carrier Suppression:** Because the modulator is **balanced**, the carrier component at $\pm f_c$ is cancelled out.

## 2.2 Frequency Domain Representation

Applying the **modulation theorem** of the Fourier Transform:

$$
m(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t) \;\xleftrightarrow{\;\mathcal{F}\;}\; \frac{1}{2} \left[ M(f - f_c) + M(f + f_c) \right]
$$

Therefore, the spectrum of DSB-SC is:

$$
S_{DSB-SC}(f) = \frac{A_c}{2} \left[ M(f - f_c) + M(f + f_c) \right]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the absence of discrete impulses at $\pm f_c$ — this is the visual signature of a **suppressed carrier** in the frequency domain.

## 2.3 Bandwidth Analysis

The bandwidth of the DSB-SC signal is identical to that of DSB-FC (Conventional AM):

$$
\boxed{BW_{DSB-SC} = 2W \;\text{Hz}}
$$

where $W$ is the **highest frequency component** in the message signal $m(t)$.

## 2.4 Power Analysis

The total average transmitted power of a DSB-SC signal is:

$$
\boxed{P_{DSB-SC} = \frac{A_c^{\,2}}{2} \cdot P_m}
$$

where $P_m$ is the average power of the message signal $m(t)$, defined as:

$$
P_m = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{T} \int_{-T/2}^{+T/2} m^2(t) \, dt
$$

### Power Distribution — DSB-FC vs DSB-SC

| Parameter | DSB-FC (Conventional AM) | DSB-SC |
|---|---|---|
| Carrier Power $P_c$ | $\dfrac{A_c^{\,2}}{2}$ | **0** |
| Sideband Power $P_{SB}$ | $\dfrac{A_c^{\,2}}{2} P_m$ | $\dfrac{A_c^{\,2}}{2} P_m$ |
| Total Power $P_t$ | $P_c + P_{SB}$ | $P_{SB}$ only |
| Power Efficiency $\eta$ | $\dfrac{P_{SB}}{P_t} = \dfrac{\mu^2}{2 + \mu^2}$ | **100 %** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> DSB-SC achieves **100 % power efficiency** because the entire transmitted power resides in the information-bearing sidebands. This is the prime engineering motivation for using DSB-SC over DSB-FC.

## 2.5 Generation Methods (High-Yield)

### (a) Balanced Modulator
Uses two identical AM modulators whose outputs are combined such that the carrier components **cancel out**, leaving only the sidebands.

### (b) Ring Modulator (Double-Balanced Modulator)
A four-diode lattice that switches the polarity of the message signal at the carrier rate — gives excellent carrier suppression (> **40 dB** typical).

### (c) Lattice Modulator
Modern implementation using diode rings; highly linear and broadband.

## 2.6 Demodulation — Coherent Detection

DSB-SC **cannot be demodulated** with a simple envelope detector (no carrier to ride on). It requires **synchronous (coherent) detection**:

$$
v_{out}(t) = s(t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t + \phi) = A_c \, m(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t) \cos(2\pi f_c t + \phi)
$$

Using the trigonometric identity:

$$
v_{out}(t) = \frac{A_c}{2} m(t) \left[ \cos(\phi) + \cos(4\pi f_c t + \phi) \right]
$$

After low-pass filtering:

$$
v_{LPF}(t) = \frac{A_c}{2} m(t) \cos(\phi)
$$

> [!WARNING]
> If $\phi \neq 0$ (phase error), the recovered message is **attenuated** by $\cos(\phi)$. A **quadrature error of 90°** results in **complete signal loss**. This is the famous "phase problem" of DSB-SC.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit / Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | DSB-SC Time Signal | $s(t) = A_c \, m(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | Volts |
| 2 | DSB-SC Spectrum | $S(f) = \dfrac{A_c}{2}\big[M(f - f_c) + M(f + f_c)\big]$ | V/Hz |
| 3 | Transmission Bandwidth | $BW = 2W$ | Hz |
| 4 | Total Transmitted Power | $P_t = \dfrac{A_c^{\,2}}{2} P_m$ | Watts |
| 5 | Power Efficiency | $\eta = 100\,\%$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Carrier Power | $P_c = 0$ | Watts |
| 7 | Demodulation | Coherent (synchronous) detection | — |
| 8 | Modulation Index Limit | $\mu$ can be $\geq 1$ (no overmodulation) | Dimensionless |
| 9 | Phase Sensitivity | Output $\propto \cos(\phi)$ | Radians |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Television Video Transmission** (Vestigial Sideband is a derivative)
- **Point-to-Point Telephony Microwave Links**
- **Stereo FM Multiplex Baseband Generation**
- **Single-Sideband (SSB) Generation** (DSB-SC is the intermediate stage)
- **Software Defined Radio (SDR)** test waveforms
- **Color TV Chrominance Subcarrier Encoding** (Quadrature DSB-SC)

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: DSB-SC Waveform from First Principles

### Given
- Message signal: $m(t)$
- Carrier signal: $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$

### Goal
Derive the mathematical expression of the DSB-SC signal and its spectrum.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Multiplication in the time domain**

The DSB-SC modulator performs a product of the message and the carrier:

$$
s(t) = m(t) \cdot c(t)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the carrier expression**

$$
s(t) = m(t) \cdot A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)
$$

**Step 3 — Factor out the constant $A_c$**

$$
\boxed{s(t) = A_c \, m(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)}
$$

This is the canonical DSB-SC time-domain equation. Notice the **absence of any DC offset** (no $1 + m(t)$ envelope).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Frequency Spectrum Using the Modulation Theorem

**Step 1 — Recall the modulation property of the Fourier Transform**

If $m(t) \;\xleftrightarrow{\;\mathcal{F}\;}\; M(f)$, then:

$$
m(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t) \;\xleftrightarrow{\;\mathcal{F}\;}\; \frac{1}{2}\big[M(f - f_c) + M(f + f_c)\big]
$$

**Step 2 — Apply to our signal**

$$
s(t) = A_c \, m(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)
$$

Taking the Fourier Transform:

$$
S(f) = A_c \cdot \frac{1}{2} \big[M(f - f_c) + M(f + f_c)\big]
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify**

$$
\boxed{S(f) = \frac{A_c}{2}\big[M(f - f_c) + M(f + f_c)\big]}
$$

**Interpretation:**

- $M(f - f_c)$ is the **Upper Sideband (USB)** centered at $+f_c$.
- $M(f + f_c)$ is the **Lower Sideband (LSB)** centered at $-f_c$.
- No impulses exist at $\pm f_c$ → **carrier is suppressed**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Power of a DSB-SC Signal

**Step 1 — Definition of average power**

For a signal $s(t)$ over a load of $1\,\Omega$:

$$
P_t = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{T} \int_{-T/2}^{+T/2} s^2(t) \, dt
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $s(t)$**

$$
P_t = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{T} \int_{-T/2}^{+T/2} A_c^{\,2} \, m^2(t) \cos^2(2\pi f_c t) \, dt
$$

**Step 3 — Expand $\cos^2(2\pi f_c t)$ using the half-angle identity**

$$
\cos^2(2\pi f_c t) = \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{2} \cos(4\pi f_c t)
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back**

$$
P_t = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{T} \int_{-T/2}^{+T/2} A_c^{\,2} \, m^2(t) \left[ \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{2} \cos(4\pi f_c t) \right] dt
$$

**Step 5 — Distribute the integral**

$$
P_t = \frac{A_c^{\,2}}{2} \cdot \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{T} \int_{-T/2}^{+T/2} m^2(t) \, dt \;+\; \frac{A_c^{\,2}}{2} \cdot \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{T} \int_{-T/2}^{+T/2} m^2(t) \cos(4\pi f_c t) \, dt
$$

**Step 6 — Recognise the integrals**

The first integral is, by definition, the average message power $P_m$:

$$
P_m = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{T} \int_{-T/2}^{+T/2} m^2(t) \, dt
$$

The second integral — for a **band-limited message** $m(t)$ whose highest frequency $W \ll 2f_c$ — evaluates to **zero** (the high-frequency cosine averages to zero over many cycles):

$$
\lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{T} \int_{-T/2}^{+T/2} m^2(t) \cos(4\pi f_c t) \, dt = 0
$$

**Step 7 — Final power expression**

$$
\boxed{P_t = \frac{A_c^{\,2}}{2} \cdot P_m}
$$

This shows that **all transmitted power is information power** — no carrier power is wasted.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Coherent Demodulation

**Step 1 — Multiply received DSB-SC with a locally generated carrier**

Let the local oscillator produce $c_{LO}(t) = \cos(2\pi f_c t + \phi)$, where $\phi$ is the phase error.

$$
v(t) = s(t) \cdot c_{LO}(t) = A_c \, m(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t) \cos(2\pi f_c t + \phi)
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the product-to-sum identity**

$$
\cos(A) \cos(B) = \frac{1}{2}\big[\cos(A - B) + \cos(A + B)\big]
$$

With $A = 2\pi f_c t$ and $B = 2\pi f_c t + \phi$:

$$
v(t) = \frac{A_c}{2} m(t) \big[\cos(\phi) + \cos(4\pi f_c t + \phi)\big]
$$

**Step 3 — Apply a low-pass filter (LPF) with cutoff $W$**

The LPF removes the high-frequency term $\cos(4\pi f_c t + \phi)$ because its frequency is $2f_c \gg W$:

$$
v_{LPF}(t) = \frac{A_c}{2} m(t) \cos(\phi)
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the result**

$$
\boxed{v_{LPF}(t) = \frac{A_c}{2} \cos(\phi) \cdot m(t)}
$$

- If $\phi = 0$ (perfectly synchronized) → $v_{LPF}(t) = \dfrac{A_c}{2} m(t)$ ✓
- If $\phi = 90°$ → $v_{LPF}(t) = 0$ (signal lost) ✗
- If $\phi$ is a constant $\neq 0$ → **attenuation** by $\cos(\phi)$ only.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Carrier Suppression in a Balanced Modulator

A balanced modulator uses two AM modulators driven by **carrier signals that are $180°$ out of phase**.

**Step 1 — Output of Modulator 1 (in-phase branch):**

$$
s_1(t) = A_c \left[ 1 + k_a m(t) \right] \cos(2\pi f_c t)
$$

**Step 2 — Output of Modulator 2 (anti-phase branch):**

$$
s_2(t) = A_c \left[ 1 + k_a m(t) \right] \cos(2\pi f_c t + \pi) = -A_c \left[ 1 + k_a m(t) \right] \cos(2\pi f_c t)
$$

**Step 3 — Sum the two outputs**

$$
s(t) = s_1(t) + s_2(t) = A_c \left[ 1 + k_a m(t) \right] \cos(2\pi f_c t) - A_c \left[ 1 + k_a m(t) \right] \cos(2\pi f_c t)
$$

**Step 4 — Cancel the carrier terms**

After careful subtraction, the **DC component (carrier)** cancels, but the message-dependent sidebands remain (because they have opposite signs in the two branches and add when subtracted properly using a differential output):

$$
\boxed{s(t) = 2 A_c k_a \, m(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, the two AM modulators share the **same message but inverted carrier**, and the output is taken **differentially**, yielding perfect carrier cancellation.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A DSB-SC signal is generated with $A_c = 20$ V and message $m(t) = 5 \cos(2\pi \cdot 1000 t)$ V. Find:
1. The DSB-SC time-domain expression.
2. The frequencies present in the spectrum.
3. The total transmitted power (assuming 1 Ω load).

### Solution

**Part (1):**

$$
s(t) = A_c \, m(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t) = 20 \cdot 5 \cos(2\pi \cdot 1000 t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t)
$$

$$
\boxed{s(t) = 100 \cos(2\pi \cdot 1000 t) \cos(2\pi f_c t) \;\text{V}}
$$

**Part (2):** Using product-to-sum:

$$
s(t) = 50 \big[ \cos(2\pi(f_c - 1000)t) + \cos(2\pi(f_c + 1000)t) \big]
$$

Frequencies present: $(f_c - 1\text{ kHz})$ and $(f_c + 1\text{ kHz})$. No component at $f_c$ alone.

**Part (3):** Message power:

$$
P_m = \frac{5^2}{2} = 12.5 \;\text{W}
$$

DSB-SC total power:

$$
P_t = \frac{A_c^{\,2}}{2} P_m = \frac{20^2}{2} \times 12.5 = 200 \times 12.5
$$

$$
\boxed{P_t = 2500 \;\text{W}}
$$

All **2500 W** resides in the sidebands; **zero carrier power**.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a DSB-SC Transmitter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Message Signal m of t] --> B1[DC Blocking Capacitor]
    B1 --> C1[Balanced Modulator Core]
    D1[Local Oscillator at fc Hz] --> C1
    C1 --> E1[Bandpass Filter fc to fc plus W]
    E1 --> F1[RF Power Amplifier]
    F1 --> G1[Transmit Antenna]
    style A1 fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#d4a017
    style D1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003e7e
    style C1 fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#a30000
    style F1 fill:#d5f5d5,stroke:#2e7d32
```

**Read the diagram left to right:**
- The message enters the balanced modulator.
- A local oscillator provides the carrier.
- The balanced modulator **multiplies** them and **cancels** the carrier component.
- A bandpass filter cleans up the sidebands.
- An RF amplifier boosts the signal for transmission.

## 4.2 Coherent DSB-SC Receiver Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Receive Antenna] --> R2[RF Front End and LNA]
    R2 --> R3[Bandpass Filter fc]
    R3 --> R4[Multiplier Stage]
    R5[Local Oscillator Phase Locked to fc] --> R4
    R4 --> R6[Low Pass Filter Cutoff W]
    R6 --> R7[Baseband Amplifier]
    R7 --> R8[Output Recovered m of t]
    style R1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003e7e
    style R4 fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#a30000
    style R5 fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#d4a017
    style R8 fill:#d5f5d5,stroke:#2e7d32
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Phase-Locked Loop (PLL)** in the local oscillator is critical — any drift in $\phi$ will cause **attenuation or distortion** of the recovered message.

## 4.3 Internal Architecture of a Balanced Modulator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Message m of t] --> MUX1
    M2[Local Osc fc] --> INV[Inverter 180 degrees]
    M2 --> MUX2
    INV --> MUX2
    MUX1[AM Modulator Branch 1] --> SUM[Differential Subtractor]
    MUX2[AM Modulator Branch 2] --> SUM
    SUM --> OUT[DSB SC Output Carrier Cancelled]
    style M1 fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#d4a017
    style M2 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003e7e
    style INV fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#a30000
    style OUT fill:#d5f5d5,stroke:#2e7d32
```

**Operation:**
- Two AM modulators receive the **same message**.
- The carrier is fed directly to Branch 1 and **inverted** for Branch 2.
- The differential subtractor cancels the carrier while the sidebands add constructively.

## 4.4 Spectral Comparison — DSB-FC vs DSB-SC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SPEC_FC[DSB-FC Spectrum]
        A1[Impulse at minus fc Carrier] --> B1[LSB around minus fc]
        B1 --> C1[USB around plus fc]
        C1 --> D1[Impulse at plus fc Carrier]
    end
    subgraph SPEC_SC[DSB-SC Spectrum]
        E1[LSB around minus fc NO IMPULSE] --> F1[USB around plus fc NO IMPULSE]
    end
    style SPEC_FC fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#d4a017
    style SPEC_SC fill:#d5f5d5,stroke:#2e7d32
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **absence of impulses at $\pm f_c$** in DSB-SC visually confirms the carrier suppression.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define DSB-SC modulation. Why is it preferred over conventional AM for power-critical applications?**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

**Definition (2 marks):**
DSB-SC modulation is a technique in which the message signal $m(t)$ is multiplied with the carrier $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$, producing an output $s(t) = A_c \, m(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ that contains **only the two sidebands** (USB and LSB) with the **carrier completely suppressed**.

**Why preferred (1 mark):**
The power efficiency of DSB-SC is **100 %**, whereas conventional AM wastes up to **66 %** of the transmitted power in the carrier. Hence, DSB-SC is preferred in satellite and mobile communication links where power budget is critical.

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Explain why an envelope detector cannot be used for DSB-SC demodulation.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

In DSB-SC, the message $m(t)$ is bipolar (i.e., crosses zero), and the modulated signal envelope is $\vert A_c \, m(t) \vert$ with **frequent zero-crossings**. An envelope detector tracks only the **magnitude of the envelope**, not the original sign of $m(t)$. Since DSB-SC has **no carrier component** to provide a DC offset, the envelope around the zero-crossings is ambiguous, leading to **phase reversal and distortion**. Hence, a **synchronous (coherent) detector** is mandatory for DSB-SC demodulation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the mathematical expression for a DSB-SC signal in both the **time** and **frequency** domains, clearly showing the sideband structure. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a DSB-SC signal with $A_c = 10$ V and message $m(t) = 4 \cos(2\pi \cdot 2000 t)$ V transmitted over a $50\,\Omega$ antenna, calculate:
- (i) The total transmitted power.
- (ii) The frequencies present in the spectrum.
- (iii) The transmission bandwidth. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** The DSB-SC signal is obtained by multiplying $m(t)$ and the carrier:

$$
s(t) = m(t) \cdot A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)
$$

$$
\boxed{s(t) = A_c \, m(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Take the Fourier transform using the modulation theorem:

$$
m(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t) \;\xleftrightarrow{\;\mathcal{F}\;}\; \frac{1}{2} \big[M(f - f_c) + M(f + f_c)\big]
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Multiply by the constant $A_c$:

$$
\boxed{S(f) = \frac{A_c}{2}\big[M(f - f_c) + M(f + f_c)\big]}
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Sideband structure explanation:

- $M(f - f_c)$ — Upper Sideband (USB) centered at $+f_c$
- $M(f + f_c)$ — Lower Sideband (LSB) centered at $-f_c$
- **No impulse** at $\pm f_c$ → carrier is suppressed.

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Bandwidth:

$$
\boxed{BW = 2W \;\text{Hz}}
$$

where $W$ is the highest frequency component in $m(t)$.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)**

**Given:** $A_c = 10$ V, $m(t) = 4 \cos(2\pi \cdot 2000 t)$ V, $R = 50\,\Omega$.

**(i) Total Transmitted Power (3 marks):**

Message power across $R = 50\,\Omega$:

$$
P_m = \frac{4^2}{2 \cdot 50} = \frac{16}{100} = 0.16 \;\text{W}
$$

DSB-SC total power:

$$
P_t = \frac{A_c^{\,2}}{2} \cdot P_m = \frac{10^2}{2} \cdot 0.16 = 50 \cdot 0.16
$$

$$
\boxed{P_t = 8 \;\text{W}}
$$

**(ii) Frequencies Present (2 marks):**

Using product-to-sum:

$$
s(t) = 10 \cdot 4 \cos(2\pi \cdot 2000 t) \cos(2\pi f_c t) = 20 \big[\cos(2\pi(f_c - 2000)t) + \cos(2\pi(f_c + 2000)t)\big]
$$

Frequencies: $(f_c - 2\text{ kHz})$ and $(f_c + 2\text{ kHz})$.
**No carrier at $f_c$.**

**(iii) Bandwidth (2 marks):**

Message frequency $W = 2000$ Hz.

$$
\boxed{BW = 2W = 2 \times 2000 = 4000 \;\text{Hz} = 4 \;\text{kHz}}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a **block diagram**, explain the operation of a **balanced modulator** for generating DSB-SC. Derive the carrier suppression expression. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **coherent detection** method for DSB-SC demodulation. Show mathematically the effect of **phase error $\phi$** on the recovered message. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Balanced Modulator (7 Marks)**

**Block Diagram Description (2 marks):**

The balanced modulator uses **two AM modulators** in parallel:

```
         ┌── AM Modulator 1 ──┐
m(t) ───┤                    ├── Subtractor ──→ DSB-SC output
         └── AM Modulator 2 ──┘
                  ▲
                  │
        LO ──── Splitter ──── Direct
                          └── 180° Inverter
```

- **AM Modulator 1** receives carrier $A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$.
- **AM Modulator 2** receives carrier $-A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ (anti-phase).
- Both modulators receive the **same message** $m(t)$.

**Mathematical Derivation (5 marks):**

**Step 1:** Output of Modulator 1:

$$
s_1(t) = A_c \left[1 + k_a m(t)\right] \cos(2\pi f_c t)
$$

**Step 2:** Output of Modulator 2:

$$
s_2(t) = A_c \left[1 + k_a m(t)\right] \cos(2\pi f_c t + \pi) = -A_c \left[1 + k_a m(t)\right] \cos(2\pi f_c t)
$$

**Step 3:** Differential subtraction:

$$
s(t) = s_1(t) - s_2(t)
$$

$$
s(t) = A_c \left[1 + k_a m(t)\right] \cos(2\pi f_c t) - \left(-A_c \left[1 + k_a m(t)\right] \cos(2\pi f_c t)\right)
$$

**Step 4:** Simplify:

$$
s(t) = 2 A_c \left[1 + k_a m(t)\right] \cos(2\pi f_c t)
$$

**Step 5:** Extract sideband terms using $2\cos A \cos B = \cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)$:

When properly differenced (accounting for the sign convention), the **DC carrier cancels** and only the sidebands remain:

$$
\boxed{s(t) = 2 A_c k_a \, m(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)}
$$

This is the desired DSB-SC signal — **carrier-free, sideband-only**.

---

**Part (b) — Coherent Detection (7 Marks)**

**Principle (2 marks):**
Coherent (synchronous) detection multiplies the received DSB-SC signal with a **locally generated carrier** that is **frequency- and phase-synchronized** with the transmitter's carrier, followed by a low-pass filter to extract the baseband.

**Mathematical Derivation with Phase Error (5 marks):**

**Step 1:** Local oscillator output:

$$
c_{LO}(t) = \cos(2\pi f_c t + \phi)
$$

where $\phi$ is the **phase error** between local and transmitted carrier.

**Step 2:** Multiplication:

$$
v(t) = A_c \, m(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t + \phi)
$$

**Step 3:** Apply product-to-sum:

$$
v(t) = \frac{A_c}{2} m(t) \big[\cos(\phi) + \cos(4\pi f_c t + \phi)\big]
$$

**Step 4:** Low-pass filter removes the $2f_c$ term:

$$
v_{LPF}(t) = \frac{A_c}{2} m(t) \cos(\phi)
$$

**Step 5 — Effect of phase error:**

- $\phi = 0$ → $v_{LPF}(t) = \dfrac{A_c}{2} m(t)$ → **perfect recovery**.
- $\phi = 90°$ → $v_{LPF}(t) = 0$ → **complete signal loss**.
- $\phi$ constant $\neq 0$ → **attenuation by $\cos(\phi)$** (no distortion).
- $\phi$ **time-varying** → **distortion** of $m(t)$ (severe in practice).

$$
\boxed{v_{out}(t) = \frac{A_c}{2} \cos(\phi) \cdot m(t)}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students forget the **phase error $\cos(\phi)$** term in coherent detection derivations. Even if $\phi$ is a **constant** small value, marks are deducted if you write $v_{out}(t) = \dfrac{A_c}{2} m(t)$ without the $\cos(\phi)$ factor. **Always include phase error in the KTU answer.**

---

## 5.3 Common KTU Mistakes and Valuation Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $\cos(\phi)$ phase term** in coherent detection → loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Writing $P_t = A_c^{\,2} P_m / 4$** instead of $A_c^{\,2} P_m / 2$ (missing the $\cos^2$ expansion).
> 3. **Claiming DSB-SC bandwidth is $W$** — it is $2W$, same as AM.
> 4. **Confusing DSB-SC with SSB** — SSB has **only one** sideband; DSB-SC has **two**.
> 5. **Writing impulse functions at $\pm f_c$** in the DSB-SC spectrum — they **do not exist**.
> 6. **Stating that envelope detection works** for DSB-SC — it does **not**; only coherent detection works.
> 7. **Drawing the ring modulator with 3 diodes** — it has **4 diodes** in a bridge configuration.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — DSB-SC Modulation**
>
> ☐ DSB-SC time signal: $s(t) = A_c \, m(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)$
>
> ☐ Carrier is **completely suppressed**; spectrum has **no impulses** at $\pm f_c$.
>
> ☐ Spectrum contains only **two sidebands** (USB + LSB) mirrored about $\pm f_c$.
>
> ☐ Transmission bandwidth: $BW = 2W$.
>
> ☐ Total power: $P_t = \dfrac{A_c^{\,2}}{2} \cdot P_m$ (no separate carrier power).
>
> ☐ Power efficiency: $\eta = 100\,\%$.
>
> ☐ Modulation index $\mu$ can be $\geq 1$ (no envelope distortion).
>
> ☐ Generated by **balanced modulator** or **ring modulator** (4-diode bridge).
>
> ☐ Demodulated by **coherent (synchronous) detection** — envelope detector fails.
>
> ☐ Phase error in local oscillator causes **attenuation by $\cos(\phi)$**; 90° error → total loss.
>
> ☐ Demodulated output: $v_{out}(t) = \dfrac{A_c}{2} m(t) \cos(\phi)$.
>
> ☐ Used as an **intermediate stage** in generating SSB and VSB.
>
> ☐ Compared to DSB-FC: DSB-SC has **same bandwidth**, **same sideband power**, but **zero carrier power**.
>
> ☐ Real-world applications: TV video, microwave links, SDR, chroma encoding, telemetry.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Single Sideband Modulation (SSB)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET864 - ELECTRONIC COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Single Sideband Modulation (SSB)**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Single Sideband Modulation (SSB)** is an amplitude modulation technique in which the modulated wave is transmitted with **only one of the two sidebands** (either the Upper Sideband $\text{USB}$ or the Lower Sideband $\text{LSB}$), while **completely suppressing the carrier** and the **other sideband**.

In SSB, the carrier component $C$ and one of the sidebands are removed from a conventional AM signal. The result is a bandpass signal $s(t)$ whose spectral support lies entirely in a single continuous band of width $W = f_{m(\text{max})}$ centered just above (USB) or just below (LSB) the suppressed carrier frequency $f_{c}$.

$$s_{\text{SSB}}(t) \;=\; \frac{A_{c}}{2}\,m(t)\cos(2\pi f_{c} t) \;\pm\; \frac{A_{c}}{2}\,\hat{m}(t)\sin(2\pi f_{c} t)$$

where $\hat{m}(t)$ is the **Hilbert Transform** of the message $m(t)$.

| Parameter | Conventional AM | DSB-SC | **SSB** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Carrier Transmitted | Yes | No | No |
| No. of Sidebands | 2 | 2 | **1** |
| Transmission Bandwidth | $2 f_{m(\text{max})}$ | $2 f_{m(\text{max})}$ | $f_{m(\text{max})}$ |
| Power Efficiency | $\leq 33.3\%$ | $50\%$ | $\mathbf{100\%}$ |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Half-Full Suitcase"

> [!NOTE]  
> **Intuition:** Think of an AM signal as a suitcase containing three things: a heavy brick (the carrier), a woolen sweater (LSB), and a cotton shirt (USB). The brick does no useful work, and one piece of clothing is **redundant** because they carry the *same information* (they are mirror images of each other).  
> **SSB = the smart traveler** who throws out the brick, removes the redundant piece, and ships **only one useful sideband**. The information is perfectly preserved because the discarded sideband is a mathematical mirror image of the one kept.

## 1.3 Why SSB Exists — Engineering Motivation

1. **Spectrum Crowding** — Saves **50% of bandwidth**, allowing **double the number of channels** in a given RF allocation (used in HF amateur radio, maritime, and aviation bands).
2. **Power Economy** — All transmitted power carries information; no energy is wasted in the carrier.
3. **Noise Immunity** — A narrower occupied bandwidth admits less **thermal noise** (noise power $\propto$ bandwidth).
4. **Reduced Selective Fading** — In ionospheric HF propagation, the two sidebands fade independently; transmitting only one eliminates this distortion.

> [!TIP]  
> **KTU Quick-Remember:** *SSB = 1 sideband + 0 carrier = Half the bandwidth, full information, full power efficiency.*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Mathematics — The Hilbert Transform

The cornerstone of SSB theory is the **Hilbert Transform**, which provides a 90° phase shift for all frequency components of a real signal without altering their magnitude.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Hilbert Transform Definition:**  
> $$\hat{m}(t) \;=\; m(t) * \frac{1}{\pi t} \;=\; \frac{1}{\pi}\,\text{P.V.}\!\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}\frac{m(\tau)}{t-\tau}\,d\tau$$

In the **frequency domain**, this becomes a multiplication by a sign-dependent imaginary unit:

$$\hat{M}(f) \;=\; -j\,\text{sgn}(f)\cdot M(f) \;=\; \begin{cases} -j\,M(f), & f>0 \\ +j\,M(f), & f<0 \end{cases}$$

- For positive frequencies, $\hat{m}(t)$ is $m(t)$ shifted by $-90°$.
- For negative frequencies, it is shifted by $+90°$.

## 2.2 Time-Domain SSB Expression (Phasor View)

The modulated SSB signal can be interpreted as a **rotating phasor** in the I-Q plane:

$$s_{\text{SSB}}(t) \;=\; \underbrace{\frac{A_{c}}{2}\,m(t)}_{\text{In-Phase (I) component}}\cos(2\pi f_{c} t) \;\underbrace{\mp}_{\text{USB / LSB}}\;\underbrace{\frac{A_{c}}{2}\,\hat{m}(t)}_{\text{Quadrature (Q) component}}\sin(2\pi f_{c} t)$$

The "$\mp$" sign is the **sideband selector**:
- Use the **minus ($-$) sign** $\Rightarrow$ **Upper Sideband (USB)** — keeps frequencies above $f_c$.
- Use the **plus ($+$) sign** $\Rightarrow$ **Lower Sideband (LSB)** — keeps frequencies below $f_c$.

## 2.3 Single-Tone Analysis (Most Common KTU Problem)

For a sinusoidal message $m(t) = A_{m}\cos(2\pi f_{m} t)$, the Hilbert transform is $\hat{m}(t) = A_{m}\sin(2\pi f_{m} t)$. Substituting:

$$s_{\text{USB}}(t) \;=\; \frac{A_{c}A_{m}}{2}\cos\!\big[2\pi(f_{c}+f_{m})t\big]$$

$$s_{\text{LSB}}(t) \;=\; \frac{A_{c}A_{m}}{2}\cos\!\big[2\pi(f_{c}-f_{m})t\big]$$

> [!NOTE]  
> **Observation:** The SSB spectrum is a *single* spectral line shifted to $f_{c}+f_m$ (USB) or $f_{c}-f_m$ (LSB). All other components (carrier, opposite sideband) are mathematically eliminated.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **All formulas below are direct KTU 2024 Scheme exam-ready.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | SSB Time-Domain Signal | $s(t)=\frac{A_c}{2}\big[m(t)\cos\omega_c t \pm \hat{m}(t)\sin\omega_c t\big]$ | Volts (V); $+$ = LSB, $-$ = USB |
| 2 | Single-Tone SSB-USB | $s(t)=\frac{A_c A_m}{2}\cos\big[2\pi(f_c+f_m)t\big]$ | V |
| 3 | Single-Tone SSB-LSB | $s(t)=\frac{A_c A_m}{2}\cos\big[2\pi(f_c-f_m)t\big]$ | V |
| 4 | Transmission Bandwidth | $B_T = f_{m(\text{max})}$ | Hz (Half of DSB!) |
| 5 | Total Transmitted Power (general $m(t)$) | $P_T = \frac{A_c^{\,2}\,\langle m^{2}(t)\rangle}{4}$ | Watts (W) |
| 6 | Total Transmitted Power (single tone) | $P_T = \frac{A_c^{2}\,A_m^{2}}{8}$ | W |
| 7 | Carrier Power (suppressed) | $P_c = 0$ | W (by design) |
| 8 | Power Efficiency | $\eta = 100\%$ | All power in info |
| 9 | Demodulated Output (Coherent) | $v_o(t) = \frac{A_c}{4}\,m(t)$ | V (after LPF) |
| 10 | Carrier Recovery Required | Yes — strict frequency & phase sync | Demodulation need |
| 11 | SNR Improvement over AM | $\left(\frac{S_o}{N_o}\right)_{\text{SSB}} = \left(\frac{S_o}{N_o}\right)_{\text{DSB-SC}}$ | Same as DSB-SC; > AM |
| 12 | Channel Capacity Ratio | SSB packs **2× more channels** in same BW | Spectrum economy |

## 2.5 Three Methods of SSB Generation (Theory Snapshot)

> [!NOTE]  
> **KTU 2024 Scheme specifically tests all three methods. The full diagrams appear in Section 4.**

1. **Filter Method (Frequency-Discrimination Method)** — Generate DSB-SC first, then a sharp **Band-Pass Filter (BPF)** removes the unwanted sideband. *Industry standard* (e.g., telephony).
2. **Phase-Shift Method (Hartley Modulator)** — Uses **two balanced modulators** and a **wideband 90° phase-shift network** on the message. Cancels one sideband by phase opposition.
3. **Weaver's Method (Third Method)** — Replaces the impractical wideband Hilbert transformer with **two cascaded stages** of balanced modulation + low-pass filtering. Operates at baseband.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| HF / Amateur Radio (3–30 MHz) | Voice communication over ionospheric sky-wave |
| Maritime & Aeronautical Mobile | Long-range voice with narrow bandwidth |
| Analog TV (Vestigial SSB) | The video signal of NTSC/PAL TV |
| Point-to-Point Telephony (old) | Carrier-suppressed multiplexed voice trunks |
| Military / Tactical Comms | Power-efficient covert transmissions |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Time-Domain SSB Expression from First Principles

### Goal
Start with the message $m(t)$ and carrier $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$, and show that combining in-phase and quadrature components yields a **single sideband**.

### Step 1 — Define the quadrature pair
The message $m(t)$ is real. Its Hilbert transform $\hat{m}(t)$ is its 90° phase-shifted version. Together, $\{m(t), \hat{m}(t)\}$ form an **analytic pair**:

$$m_a(t) \;=\; m(t) \;+\; j\hat{m}(t)$$

### Step 2 — Modulate the analytic signal
Multiply the analytic message by the complex carrier $e^{j\omega_c t}$:

$$x(t) \;=\; m_a(t)\cdot e^{j\omega_c t} \;=\; \big[m(t)+j\hat{m}(t)\big]\big[\cos\omega_c t + j\sin\omega_c t\big]$$

### Step 3 — Expand the product
Distribute term by term:

$$x(t) \;=\; m(t)\cos\omega_c t \;-\; \hat{m}(t)\sin\omega_c t \;+\; j\big[\hat{m}(t)\cos\omega_c t \;+\; m(t)\sin\omega_c t\big]$$

### Step 4 — Take the real part
The real part $s(t) = \text{Re}\{x(t)\}$ gives a **real, physical** signal:

$$s_{\text{USB}}(t) \;=\; m(t)\cos\omega_c t \;-\; \hat{m}(t)\sin\omega_c t$$

> **Interpretation:** The minus sign in front of $\hat{m}\sin\omega_c t$ mathematically **cancels the lower sideband** and **preserves the upper sideband**. This is the **USB** form.

### Step 5 — Switch the sign for LSB
Flipping the sign of the quadrature term yields the **LSB** form:

$$s_{\text{LSB}}(t) \;=\; m(t)\cos\omega_c t \;+\; \hat{m}(t)\sin\omega_c t$$

### Step 6 — Combine into a single compact formula
$$\boxed{\,s_{\text{SSB}}(t) \;=\; \frac{A_{c}}{2}\Big[\,m(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) \;\pm\; \hat{m}(t)\sin(2\pi f_c t)\,\Big]\,}$$

The factor $\frac{A_c}{2}$ appears after we include the standard carrier amplitude $A_c$ explicitly.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Coherent (Synchronous) Demodulation of SSB

### Goal
Recover $m(t)$ from $s_{\text{SSB}}(t)$ using a locally generated carrier that is **frequency- and phase-synchronized** to the transmitter's suppressed carrier.

### Step 1 — Multiply the received SSB by the local carrier
Let the local oscillator produce $c_{\text{LO}}(t) = \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ (assume ideal lock — zero phase error, zero frequency error).

$$r(t) \;=\; s_{\text{SSB}}(t)\cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

Substitute the USB form (the LSB case is identical by symmetry):

$$r(t) \;=\; \frac{A_{c}}{2}\Big[m(t)\cos\omega_c t - \hat{m}(t)\sin\omega_c t\Big]\cos\omega_c t$$

### Step 2 — Apply the product-to-sum identities
Use the trigonometric identities:

$$\cos^{2}\omega_c t \;=\; \frac{1 + \cos(2\omega_c t)}{2}$$

$$\sin\omega_c t\cdot\cos\omega_c t \;=\; \frac{\sin(2\omega_c t)}{2}$$

Substitute into $r(t)$:

$$r(t) \;=\; \frac{A_{c}}{4}\Big[m(t)\big(1+\cos 2\omega_c t\big) \;-\; \hat{m}(t)\sin 2\omega_c t\Big]$$

### Step 3 — Separate the baseband and high-frequency terms

$$r(t) \;=\; \underbrace{\frac{A_{c}}{4}\,m(t)}_{\text{baseband (message)}} \;+\; \underbrace{\frac{A_{c}}{4}\Big[m(t)\cos 2\omega_c t - \hat{m}(t)\sin 2\omega_c t\Big]}_{\text{centered at }2f_c}$$

### Step 4 — Pass through a Low-Pass Filter (LPF)
The LPF (cutoff $f_m < f_{\text{LPF}} < 2f_c - f_m$) removes the high-frequency SSB-like component centered at $2f_c$:

$$v_o(t) \;=\; \frac{A_{c}}{4}\,m(t)$$

$$\boxed{\,v_o(t) \;=\; \frac{A_{c}}{4}\,m(t)\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Result:** The original message is recovered **exactly** (within a constant scale factor $A_c/4$). This is the fundamental reason SSB **requires coherent detection** — there is no carrier envelope to rectify against, unlike standard AM.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Transmitted Power of a Single-Tone SSB

### Setup
$m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$, the SSB-USB signal is:

$$s(t) \;=\; \frac{A_c A_m}{2}\,\cos\!\big[2\pi(f_c+f_m)t\big]$$

### Power over 1 Ω
For a sinusoid of amplitude $B$, average power is $B^{2}/2$:

$$P_T \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{A_c A_m}{2}\right)^{2} \;=\; \frac{A_c^{2}\,A_m^{2}}{8}$$

$$\boxed{\,P_{T,\text{SSB}} \;=\; \frac{A_c^{2}\,A_m^{2}}{8}\,}$$

### Comparison
- For standard AM (100% modulated, $m=1$, $A_m = A_c$): $P_{T,\text{AM}} = \frac{3 A_c^{2}}{8}$.
- For DSB-SC: $P_{T,\text{DSB-SC}} = \frac{A_c^{2} A_m^{2}}{4}$.
- **For SSB:** $P_{T,\text{SSB}} = \frac{A_c^{2} A_m^{2}}{8} = \frac{1}{2} P_{T,\text{DSB-SC}}$.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sideband Selection Summary

| Configuration | Sign in $\pm$ | Output | Frequency Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| USB | Use **$-$** | $m\cos\omega_c t - \hat{m}\sin\omega_c t$ | $f_c \;\text{to}\; f_c + f_{m(\text{max})}$ |
| LSB | Use **$+$** | $m\cos\omega_c t + \hat{m}\sin\omega_c t$ | $f_c - f_{m(\text{max})} \;\text{to}\; f_c$ |
| DSB-SC | (Both sidebands) | $A_c m(t)\cos\omega_c t$ | $f_c \pm f_m$ |
| Conventional AM | (Both + Carrier) | $A_c[1+m_a m(t)]\cos\omega_c t$ | $f_c \pm f_m$ + carrier |

## 3.5 Why "Coherent Detection Only"? — Frequency Drift Sensitivity

Suppose the local oscillator drifts by $\Delta f \ll f_c$. The received signal multiplied by the local carrier produces a baseband term at $\Delta f$:

$$v_o(t) \;\approx\; \frac{A_c}{4}\,m(t)\cos(2\pi \Delta f\,t) \;-\; \frac{A_c}{4}\,\hat{m}(t)\sin(2\pi \Delta f\,t)$$

For speech, $\Delta f > 20$ Hz is **intelligibility-destroying** (Donald Duck effect). This is why SSB transmitters require:
- **Pilot carrier** (low-level tone for AGC), OR
- **AFC (Automatic Frequency Control)** loops, OR
- **DSB-SC transmitter + pilot reference** (used in SSB exciters).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Method 1 — Filter Method (Frequency-Discrimination Method)

This is the **most widely used** SSB generation technique. The unwanted sideband is removed by a **sharp bandpass filter** immediately after a DSB-SC balanced modulator.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Message m of t] --> nodeB[Balanced Modulator]
    nodeC[Carrier Ac cos wct] --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeD[DSB-SC output]
    nodeD --> nodeE[BPF Pass USB]
    nodeD --> nodeF[BPF Pass LSB]
    nodeE --> nodeG[SSB-USB output]
    nodeF --> nodeH[SSB-LSB output]
```

> [!NOTE]  
> **Working Principle:** The balanced modulator suppresses the carrier, producing a DSB-SC spectrum. A highly selective BPF (crystal or mechanical filter) then passes only the desired sideband. In practice, the filter is placed at a **low intermediate frequency** first, then up-converted — this makes the filter design feasible (sharp skirts at low $f$ are easier to build than at high $f$).

## 4.2 Method 2 — Phase-Shift (Hartley) Modulator

A brilliant method that **avoids the need for sharp filters** by using phase cancellation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Message m of t] --> nodeB[Direct to BM1]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Hilbert Transformer 90 deg shift]
    nodeC --> nodeD[hat m of t to BM2]
    nodeE[Carrier Ac cos wct] --> nodeF[Direct to BM1]
    nodeE --> nodeG[90 deg phase shifter]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Ac sin wct to BM2]
    nodeB --> nodeI[Summing Junction]
    nodeD --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ[SSB output]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Why it works:** Balanced Modulator 1 (BM1) produces $m(t)\cos\omega_c t$ (DSB-SC). Balanced Modulator 2 (BM2) produces $\hat{m}(t)\sin\omega_c t$ (DSB-SC with the message pre-shifted 90°). When summed with the **minus** sign, the LSB components are **in phase** and the USB components are **out of phase** — they cancel. The remaining output is SSB.

## 4.3 Method 3 — Weaver's Method (Third Method)

Avoids the need for a **wideband 90° phase-shift network** by using two-stage modulation with **low-pass filters**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Message m of t] --> nodeB[BM1]
    nodeA1[hat m of t] --> nodeB1[BM2]
    nodeC[Sub carrier cos w1 t] --> nodeB
    nodeC --> nodeD[90 deg shifter]
    nodeD --> nodeC2[sin w1 t] --> nodeB1
    nodeB --> nodeE[LPF 1]
    nodeB1 --> nodeE1[LPF 2]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Summing Subtraction]
    nodeE1 --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG[BM3 for upconversion]
    nodeH[Final carrier Ac cos wct] --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeI[SSB output]
```

> [!NOTE]  
> **Working Principle:** The first stage shifts the spectrum to a low sub-carrier frequency $\omega_1$. LPFs replace the wideband Hilbert transformer. The second stage translates the baseband to the final RF carrier. This is **mathematically identical** to the Hartley method but **practically realizable** over wide message bandwidths.

## 4.4 SSB Coherent Demodulator (Receiver)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Received SSB r of t] --> nodeB[Multiplier]
    nodeC[Local Oscillator cos wct] --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeD[LPF cutoff fm]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Recovered message m of t]
    nodeF[AGC from pilot] --> nodeC
```

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Crucial Detail:** The local oscillator's frequency and phase **must match** the suppressed carrier within a few Hz. In real systems, a low-level **pilot carrier** is transmitted alongside SSB to drive a **phase-locked loop (PLL)** that synchronizes the local oscillator.

## 4.5 Frequency-Domain Picture (USB)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Baseband m of t] --> nodeB[DSB-SC spectrum]
    nodeB --> nodeC[After BPF selects USB]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Final SSB USB]
```

**Textual description of the spectrum (because Mermaid cannot draw axes):**

- **$m(t)$ Baseband:** $M(f)$ occupies $-f_m \leq f \leq +f_m$ (two-sided).
- **DSB-SC after balanced modulator:** Two replicas of $M(f)$ at $\pm f_c$, no carrier spike.
- **After USB-BPF:** Only the replica centered at $+f_c$ survives, occupying $f_c$ to $f_c + f_m$.
- **The LSB** (centered at $-f_c$ and $f_c - f_m$ side) is rejected by the filter.

## 4.6 Comparison Flow — AM → DSB-SC → SSB Progression

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Conventional AM] -->|Remove carrier| nodeB[DSB-SC]
    nodeB -->|Remove one sideband| nodeC[SSB]
    nodeC -->|Vestige of other sideband| nodeD[VSB used in TV]
```

> [!TIP]  
> **Engineering Insight:** This evolutionary chain shows how **each refinement improves spectrum/power efficiency**, with **SSB being the theoretical optimum** for analog voice. The next stage beyond SSB for video is VSB (Vestigial Sideband) — used in analog TV broadcasting where the video signal has significant low-frequency content (DC) that cannot be easily filtered.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
*Define Single Sideband Modulation (SSB). List its two main advantages over conventional AM.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]  
> **Definition (2 marks):** Single Sideband Modulation (SSB) is a type of amplitude modulation in which the modulated signal is transmitted with **only one of the two sidebands** (Upper or Lower), while the **carrier** and the **other sideband** are suppressed.

**Advantages (1 mark):**
1. **Bandwidth Saving:** Occupied bandwidth is reduced by **50%** ($B_T = f_{m(\max)}$ instead of $2f_{m(\max)}$), allowing **twice as many channels** in the same frequency spectrum.
2. **Power Efficiency:** The carrier is suppressed, so **all transmitted power carries information**. Power efficiency is theoretically **100%**, compared to a maximum of **33.3%** in conventional AM.

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
*Why is coherent detection essential for SSB demodulation? What happens if the local carrier drifts by $\Delta f$?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Reason for coherent detection (2 marks):** SSB suppresses the carrier completely, so a simple **envelope detector** (used in AM) cannot recover the message because the envelope of an SSB signal is distorted (it is a function of both $m(t)$ and $\hat{m}(t)$, not just $m(t)$). Hence, a **synchronized local oscillator** is required to re-introduce a carrier reference at the receiver for coherent (synchronous) demodulation.

> **Effect of frequency drift (1 mark):** If the local oscillator drifts by $\Delta f$, the demodulated output becomes $v_o(t) \approx \frac{A_c}{4}\big[m(t)\cos 2\pi\Delta f t - \hat{m}(t)\sin 2\pi\Delta f t\big]$, producing a **chirp-like distortion**. For voice, even a $\Delta f$ of a few tens of Hz renders the output **unintelligible** (the famous "Donald Duck" effect in amateur radio).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**  
**(a)** Derive the time-domain expression of an SSB signal in terms of the message $m(t)$ and its Hilbert transform $\hat{m}(t)$. Clearly distinguish between USB and LSB. **(7 marks)**  
**(b)** For a message $m(t) = 5\cos(2\pi \cdot 1000\,t)$ and a carrier $c(t) = 20\cos(2\pi \cdot 100\,\text{k}\,t)$, generate the **SSB-USB** signal and compute its **average transmitted power** over a $50\;\Omega$ load. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define the analytic signal $m_a(t) = m(t) + j\hat{m}(t)$, where $\hat{m}(t)$ is the Hilbert transform of $m(t)$ (a 90° phase shift for all positive frequencies).

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Multiply the analytic message by the complex exponential carrier $e^{j\omega_c t}$:

$$m_a(t)\cdot e^{j\omega_c t} = \big[m(t)+j\hat{m}(t)\big]\big[\cos\omega_c t + j\sin\omega_c t\big]$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Expand and take the real part:

$$s(t) = \text{Re}\{m_a(t)e^{j\omega_c t}\} = m(t)\cos\omega_c t - \hat{m}(t)\sin\omega_c t$$

This is the **USB** signal. The minus sign causes the lower sideband components to cancel in quadrature.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Flipping the sign of the quadrature term gives the **LSB**:

$$s_{\text{LSB}}(t) = m(t)\cos\omega_c t + \hat{m}(t)\sin\omega_c t$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Including the carrier amplitude $A_c$ explicitly:

$$s_{\text{SSB}}(t) = \frac{A_c}{2}\big[m(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) \pm \hat{m}(t)\sin(2\pi f_c t)\big]$$

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Identify the parameters. $A_m = 5$ V, $A_c = 20$ V, $f_m = 1000$ Hz, $f_c = 100$ kHz.

The Hilbert transform of $A_m\cos(2\pi f_m t)$ is $A_m\sin(2\pi f_m t) = 5\sin(2\pi \cdot 1000\,t)$.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Apply the USB formula (use the **minus** sign):

$$s_{\text{USB}}(t) = \frac{(20)(5)}{2}\cos\big[2\pi(100{,}000+1000)t\big] = 50\cos(2\pi\cdot 101{,}000\,t)\;\text{V}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Verify — the output is a single sinusoid at $f_c + f_m = 101$ kHz, exactly the upper sideband.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Compute the average power delivered to a $50\;\Omega$ load:

$$P_T = \frac{V_{\text{rms}}^{2}}{R} = \frac{(B/\sqrt{2})^{2}}{R} = \frac{B^{2}}{2R}$$

where $B = 50$ V is the amplitude:

$$P_T = \frac{50^{2}}{2 \times 50} = \frac{2500}{100} = \mathbf{25\;\text{W}}$$

> Cross-check using the formula $P_T = \frac{A_c^{2}A_m^{2}}{8R} = \frac{(20)^{2}(5)^{2}}{8 \times 50} = \frac{10000}{400} = 25$ W ✓

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]**  
**(a)** With the help of a **block diagram**, explain the **Filter Method** of SSB generation. Why is the filter typically placed at a low intermediate frequency (IF) and not at the final RF? **(7 marks)**  
**(b)** Explain the **Hartley (Phase-Shift) Method** of SSB generation. What is its main practical limitation? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 (3 marks):** Block diagram and explanation of the **Filter Method**:

1. The message $m(t)$ and carrier $A_c\cos\omega_c t$ are fed into a **Balanced Modulator**, which produces a **DSB-SC** output (carrier suppressed, two sidebands present).
2. The DSB-SC signal is then passed through a **highly selective Band-Pass Filter (BPF)** tuned to pass only the desired sideband (USB or LSB).
3. The output of the BPF is the **SSB signal**.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Why place the filter at a **low IF**, not at final RF?

- Building an extremely **sharp, steep-skirted BPF** at high RF frequencies (e.g., 10–30 MHz) is **practically impossible** with realizable component tolerances — the filter would need a Q-factor in the millions.
- At a **low IF** (e.g., 455 kHz or 9 MHz), **crystal filters** or **mechanical filters** can achieve the required sharp transition band (a few hundred Hz wide) with realistic Q factors.
- The IF SSB is then **up-converted** in a second mixer to the final RF carrier frequency, where no further filtering is needed because the sideband is already isolated.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Block-level summary:

> Message $\rightarrow$ Balanced Modulator $\rightarrow$ DSB-SC $\rightarrow$ **BPF (at IF)** $\rightarrow$ SSB at IF $\rightarrow$ Up-conversion mixer $\rightarrow$ SSB at RF $\rightarrow$ Power Amplifier $\rightarrow$ Antenna.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 (3 marks):** Operation of the **Hartley (Phase-Shift) Method**:

1. The message $m(t)$ is split into **two paths**:  
   • Path 1 goes **directly** to Balanced Modulator 1 (BM1).  
   • Path 2 passes through a **wideband 90° Hilbert transformer** to produce $\hat{m}(t)$, then to BM2.
2. The carrier $A_c\cos\omega_c t$ is also split:  
   • Path 1 goes **directly** to BM1.  
   • Path 2 passes through a **90° phase shifter** to produce $A_c\sin\omega_c t$, fed to BM2.
3. BM1 output: $m(t)\cos\omega_c t$ (DSB-SC).  
   BM2 output: $\hat{m}(t)\sin\omega_c t$ (DSB-SC with message pre-shifted 90°).
4. The two outputs are **summed with appropriate polarity** — the unwanted sideband components add in opposite phase and **cancel**, leaving only the desired sideband.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** The mathematical result:

$$s_{\text{USB}}(t) = m(t)\cos\omega_c t - \hat{m}(t)\sin\omega_c t$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Main practical limitation:

> [!WARNING]  
> The method requires a **wideband 90° phase-shift network** (Hilbert transformer) that delivers **exactly 90° phase shift and unity gain over the entire message bandwidth** (e.g., 300 Hz to 3.4 kHz for voice). This is **extremely difficult to realize** in practice using analog components — small deviations in phase or amplitude from the ideal $\pm 90°$ and unity gain cause **incomplete sideband suppression** (typically only 30–40 dB, vs. 60+ dB required for high-quality SSB). This is why the **Weaver's third method** is preferred for wideband signals.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common places students lose marks in SSB problems:**
>
> 1. **Sign Error (USB vs. LSB):** Mixing up the $+$ and $-$ signs. Remember: **USB = minus sign**; **LSB = plus sign**. Examiners catch this immediately.
> 2. **Missing Hilbert Transform:** Writing the SSB expression as just $m(t)\cos\omega_c t$ is **wrong** — that is DSB-SC. You **must** include the $\hat{m}(t)\sin\omega_c t$ term.
> 3. **Bandwidth Confusion:** Many students write $B_T = 2f_m$ for SSB. The correct answer is $B_T = f_{m(\text{max})}$. This single error can cost **2 marks** in a 7-mark sub-question.
> 4. **Forgetting the $\frac{A_c}{2}$ factor:** The full SSB formula has a $\frac{A_c}{2}$ in front. Omitting it loses a mark.
> 5. **Demodulation — Ignoring the LPF:** Just writing $r(t) = s(t)\cos\omega_c t$ is incomplete. You must explicitly **low-pass filter** to obtain $v_o(t) = \frac{A_c}{4}m(t)$.
> 6. **Pilot carrier omitted in receiver:** When asked "how is SSB demodulated practically," students often describe the coherent detector but forget to mention the **pilot tone / PLL** for carrier synchronization.
> 7. **Confusing "SSB" with "VSB":** Vestigial Sideband (VSB) is **not** SSB — VSB retains a *small portion* (vestige) of the second sideband and is used in **TV video**, not voice.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — SSB Modulation**

- **Definition:** SSB = AM with **carrier suppressed + one sideband suppressed**; only **one sideband** is transmitted.
- **Bandwidth:** $B_T = f_{m(\text{max})}$ — **half** of DSB or AM.
- **Power Efficiency:** $\eta = 100\%$ (theoretically). All transmitted power lies in information.
- **Total Power (single tone):** $P_T = \dfrac{A_c^{2}A_m^{2}}{8R}$.
- **Demodulation:** **Coherent (synchronous) detection only** — envelope detection is impossible.
- **Demodulated Output:** $v_o(t) = \dfrac{A_c}{4}\,m(t)$.
- **Sideband Selection Rule:**  
  • $s(t) = \frac{A_c}{2}\big[m\cos\omega_c t - \hat{m}\sin\omega_c t\big] \Rightarrow$ **USB**  
  • $s(t) = \frac{A_c}{2}\big[m\cos\omega_c t + \hat{m}\sin\omega_c t\big] \Rightarrow$ **LSB**
- **Hilbert Transform property:** $\hat{M}(f) = -j\,\text{sgn}(f)\cdot M(f)$ — 90° shift in frequency domain.
- **Three generation methods:** (1) **Filter Method** [most common, uses BPF at low IF]; (2) **Phase-Shift (Hartley) Method** [uses 90° phase shifters + balanced modulators]; (3) **Weaver's Method** [two-stage modulation + LPF, avoids wideband Hilbert transformer].
- **Demodulation requires:** Strict **frequency and phase synchronization** — a few Hz of drift destroys voice intelligibility.
- **Practical mitigation:** Transmit a **low-level pilot carrier** (typically –20 dB below PEP) and use a **PLL** at the receiver.
- **Applications:** HF voice communication, amateur radio, maritime/aeronautical mobile, long-range telephony, **vestigial sideband** in analog TV.
- **Comparison snapshot —** SSB vs DSB-SC: **same SNR performance**, but **half the bandwidth** and **half the transmitted power** for the same message amplitude.
- **KTU Buzzwords to memorize:** Hilbert Transform, Analytic Signal, Image-Reject Mixing, Pilot Carrier, Phasor Diagram, Phase Discrimination, Vestigial Sideband.
- **Key KTU trick questions:**  
  • "What happens if you use an envelope detector on SSB?" — Output is distorted; the envelope is **not** $|m(t)|$ but a function of both $m(t)$ and $\hat{m}(t)$.  
  • "Can SSB transmit a DC component?" — **No** (a DC shift in $m(t)$ becomes a delta at the carrier, but the carrier is suppressed; the information is lost). This is why SSB is unsuitable for baseband data with DC — **use DSB-SC with a pilot instead**.  
  • "Why is voice the dominant SSB signal?" — Voice has **no significant DC component** and naturally fades the sub-300 Hz range, making it ideal for SSB.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Comparison of Spectrum
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET864 - ELECTRONIC COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Comparison of Spectrum

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Signal Spectrum?

In electronic communication, the **spectrum** of a signal is the distribution of its signal amplitude (or power) as a function of frequency. Mathematically, it is the **magnitude of the Fourier Transform** of the time-domain signal.

$$X(f) = \mathcal{F}\{x(t)\} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-j2\pi f t}\, dt$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - PEEET864 Module 1):**
> A clear understanding of the frequency spectrum is foundational to all communication theory. Every modulation and multiplexing technique is ultimately evaluated by *how it shapes the spectrum* of the transmitted signal.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Rainbow of Signals"

Imagine sunlight (a white beam) passing through a prism. The prism splits the white light into a **rainbow** — each color represents a different frequency (red = low frequency, violet = high frequency). The resulting colorful band is the **spectrum** of white light.

Similarly, every electrical signal (audio, video, data) is essentially a "mixture" of many pure sinusoidal tones of different frequencies. The **spectrum analyzer** acts like the prism — it splits our electrical signal into its constituent frequencies and displays their amplitudes.

- A **pure single-tone sinusoid** → produces a *single colored line* (a single frequency spike).
- A **human voice** → produces a *wide rainbow* (frequencies from roughly **300 Hz to 3400 Hz**).
- A **music track** → produces an *even wider rainbow* (20 Hz to 20 kHz).

## 1.3 Why Compare Spectra?

Different communication schemes (AM, FM, PM, PAM, PWM, PPM) place the **information signal** at different regions of the frequency axis and distribute power in different ways. Comparing spectra allows us to evaluate:

| Comparison Parameter | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|
| **Bandwidth (BW)** | Spectrum occupancy → number of channels in a band |
| **Power Distribution** | Carrier power vs sideband power → efficiency |
| **Spectral Shape** | Line spectrum vs continuous spectrum |
| **Sideband Symmetry** | Upper vs lower sideband structure |
| **Out-of-Band Emission** | Adjacent channel interference |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Perspective:**
> When a question asks "Compare the spectra of AM and FM", the examiner expects a *tabular or point-wise* comparison covering: bandwidth, power, number of sidebands, mathematical expression, and bandwidth formula. Always use a comparison table for full marks.

## 1.4 Types of Signals Whose Spectra We Compare

In Module 1 of PEEET864, the typical signals whose spectra are compared are:

1. **Continuous Wave (CW)** — unmodulated carrier
2. **Amplitude Modulation (AM / DSB-FC)**
3. **Double Sideband Suppressed Carrier (DSB-SC)**
4. **Single Sideband (SSB)**
5. **Frequency Modulation (FM)**
6. **Phase Modulation (PM)**
7. **Pulse Modulation** (PAM, PWM, PPM) — a brief overview

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Spectrum of a Continuous Wave (CW) Signal

A pure CW signal is mathematically:

$$c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

Its Fourier transform yields **two impulse functions** in the frequency domain — at $+f_c$ and $-f_c$, each of weight $A_c/2$.

$$C(f) = \frac{A_c}{2}\left[\delta(f - f_c) + \delta(f + f_c)\right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> A CW signal occupies **zero bandwidth** (in the limit) and has all its power concentrated at a single frequency $f_c$. It carries no information — that is why we must *modulate* it.

## 2.2 Spectrum of an AM (DSB-FC) Signal

The standard AM signal is:

$$s_{AM}(t) = A_c\left[1 + k_a m(t)\right]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

Expanding using Euler's identity, its spectrum is:

$$S_{AM}(f) = \frac{A_c}{2}\left[\delta(f - f_c) + \delta(f + f_c)\right] + \frac{A_c k_a}{2}\left[M(f - f_c) + M(f + f_c)\right]$$

The spectrum has three components:
- **Carrier impulses** at $\pm f_c$ (delta functions)
- **Upper Sideband (USB)** centered at $+f_c$, occupying $[f_c - W, f_c + W]$
- **Lower Sideband (LSB)** centered at $-f_c$, occupying $[-f_c - W, -f_c + W]$

**Total Bandwidth:** $BW_{AM} = 2W$ (where $W$ = message bandwidth)

## 2.3 Spectrum of a DSB-SC Signal

$$s_{DSB-SC}(t) = A_c\, m(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

The spectrum is:

$$S_{DSB-SC}(f) = \frac{A_c}{2}\left[M(f - f_c) + M(f + f_c)\right]$$

Key difference from AM: **No carrier component**. Only two sidebands remain, giving **100% power efficiency**.

**Total Bandwidth:** $BW_{DSB-SC} = 2W$

## 2.4 Spectrum of an SSB Signal

SSB is generated by suppressing one sideband (either USB or LSB) of DSB-SC. The spectrum consists of only **one** sideband, so:

$$BW_{SSB} = W$$

**Power efficiency:** 100% (no carrier, no redundant sideband).

## 2.5 Spectrum of an FM Signal

An FM signal is defined by a time-varying **frequency deviation** rather than amplitude variation:

$$s_{FM}(t) = A_c \cos\left(2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_{0}^{t} m(\tau)\, d\tau\right)$$

Using the **Bessel function expansion** for a single-tone message $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$:

$$s_{FM}(t) = A_c \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n(\beta) \cos\left[2\pi(f_c + n f_m) t\right]$$

where $\beta = \dfrac{k_f A_m}{f_m}$ is the **modulation index**.

The spectrum contains:
- A carrier at $f_c$ with amplitude $J_0(\beta)$
- Infinite pairs of sidebands at $f_c \pm nf_m$ with amplitudes $J_n(\beta)$
- In practice, sidebands with $J_n(\beta) < 0.04$ are neglected (Carson's rule applies).

**Total Bandwidth (Carson's Rule):**

$$BW_{FM} = 2(\beta + 1) f_m = 2(\Delta f + f_m)$$

## 2.6 Spectrum of a PM Signal

A PM signal is:

$$s_{PM}(t) = A_c \cos\left[2\pi f_c t + k_p\, m(t)\right]$$

The spectrum is **mathematically identical in form** to FM, but the modulation index for PM is:

$$\beta_{PM} = k_p \cdot \max \vert m(t) \vert$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Key Difference between FM and PM Spectra:**
> - In **FM**, the *frequency deviation* $\Delta f$ is independent of $f_m$. Increasing $f_m$ decreases $\beta$.
> - In **PM**, the *phase deviation* $\beta$ is independent of $f_m$. Increasing $f_m$ increases the bandwidth proportionally.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Signal / Modulation | Time-Domain Expression | Spectrum Composition | Bandwidth Formula | Power Efficiency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **CW (unmodulated)** | $A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | Two impulses at $\pm f_c$ | $\approx 0$ | N/A (no info) |
| **AM (DSB-FC)** | $A_c[1 + k_a m(t)]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | Carrier + USB + LSB | $2W$ | $\dfrac{\mu^2}{2 + \mu^2}$ |
| **DSB-SC** | $A_c m(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | USB + LSB (no carrier) | $2W$ | 100% |
| **SSB** | $A_c m(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) \mp A_c \hat{m}(t)\sin(2\pi f_c t)$ | Only USB or only LSB | $W$ | 100% |
| **FM** | $A_c \cos\!\left(2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f\!\int m(\tau)d\tau\right)$ | Carrier + infinite sidebands (Bessel) | $2(\Delta f + f_m)$ | ~100% (theoretical) |
| **PM** | $A_c \cos[2\pi f_c t + k_p m(t)]$ | Same form as FM | $2(\beta_{PM}+1) f_m$ | ~100% |
| **PAM (Natural)** | Sampled pulse train modulated by message | Message spectrum replicated at $f_s, 2f_s, \ldots$ | $2W$ (baseband) | N/A |
| **PWM** | Pulse width varies with message | Wide spectrum due to sharp edges | $> 2W$ | N/A |
| **PPM** | Pulse position varies with message | Similar to PWM | $> 2W$ | N/A |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Spectrum comparison is the *backbone* of every regulatory body (e.g., **FCC, ITU**). Channel allocation, interference management, and link budget design all start from the spectral signatures discussed above.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Spectrum of a CW Signal

**Given:**

$$c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Step 1:** Apply Euler's identity.

$$c(t) = \frac{A_c}{2}\left[e^{j2\pi f_c t} + e^{-j2\pi f_c t}\right]$$

**Step 2:** Take the Fourier Transform. Recall $\mathcal{F}\{e^{j2\pi f_0 t}\} = \delta(f - f_0)$.

$$C(f) = \frac{A_c}{2}\left[\delta(f - f_c) + \delta(f + f_c)\right]$$

**Step 3:** Interpret the result. The signal has **all its energy concentrated at exactly two frequencies**: $+f_c$ and $-f_c$. Hence the spectrum is a pair of delta spikes.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Spectrum of an AM Signal (Single-Tone Message)

**Given:** Message $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$, modulation index $\mu = k_a A_m$.

The AM signal is:

$$s_{AM}(t) = A_c[1 + \mu \cos(2\pi f_m t)]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Step 1:** Expand the bracket.

$$s_{AM}(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + A_c \mu \cos(2\pi f_m t)\cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Step 2:** Apply the trigonometric identity $2\cos A \cos B = \cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)$ to the second term.

$$s_{AM}(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + \frac{A_c \mu}{2}\cos[2\pi(f_c - f_m)t] + \frac{A_c \mu}{2}\cos[2\pi(f_c + f_m)t]$$

**Step 3:** Identify the spectral components:
- Carrier at $f_c$ with amplitude $A_c$
- Lower Side Frequency (LSF) at $f_c - f_m$ with amplitude $A_c\mu/2$
- Upper Side Frequency (USF) at $f_c + f_m$ with amplitude $A_c\mu/2$

**Step 4:** Take the Fourier Transform.

$$S_{AM}(f) = \frac{A_c}{2}\left[\delta(f - f_c) + \delta(f + f_c)\right] + \frac{A_c \mu}{4}\left[\delta(f - (f_c - f_m)) + \delta(f + (f_c - f_m))\right] + \frac{A_c \mu}{4}\left[\delta(f - (f_c + f_m)) + \delta(f + (f_c + f_m))\right]$$

**Conclusion:** The single-tone AM spectrum has **three positive-frequency lines** (and three mirrored on the negative axis): the carrier and two side frequencies separated by $\pm f_m$ from $f_c$. The total bandwidth is $2f_m$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Spectrum of an FM Signal (Single-Tone Message)

**Given:** Message $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$, frequency sensitivity $k_f$ Hz/V.

The FM signal is:

$$s_{FM}(t) = A_c \cos\left[2\pi f_c t + \frac{k_f A_m}{f_m}\sin(2\pi f_m t)\right]$$

**Step 1:** Define the modulation index $\beta = \dfrac{k_f A_m}{f_m} = \dfrac{\Delta f}{f_m}$, where $\Delta f$ is the peak frequency deviation.

$$s_{FM}(t) = A_c \cos[2\pi f_c t + \beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)]$$

**Step 2:** Use the complex exponential form of cosine.

$$s_{FM}(t) = A_c\, \text{Re}\left\{e^{j2\pi f_c t} \cdot e^{j\beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)}\right\}$$

**Step 3:** Use the **Jacobi-Anger expansion**:

$$e^{j\beta \sin\theta} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n(\beta) e^{jn\theta}$$

Substituting $\theta = 2\pi f_m t$:

$$e^{j\beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n(\beta) e^{jn 2\pi f_m t}$$

**Step 4:** Combine with the carrier term.

$$s_{FM}(t) = A_c\, \text{Re}\left\{\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n(\beta) e^{j2\pi(f_c + n f_m) t}\right\}$$

$$s_{FM}(t) = A_c \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n(\beta) \cos[2\pi(f_c + n f_m) t]$$

**Step 5:** Each $n$ corresponds to a spectral component at frequency $f_c + nf_m$ with amplitude $J_n(\beta)$. Since $n$ runs from $-\infty$ to $+\infty$, the spectrum has **infinitely many sidebands** in principle.

**Step 6:** Apply **Carson's Rule** to compute the *effective* bandwidth, retaining only those $n$ for which $\vert J_n(\beta) \vert \geq 0.04$:

$$BW_{FM} = 2(\beta + 1) f_m = 2(\Delta f + f_m)$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Side by Side

| Property | CW | AM (DSB-FC) | DSB-SC | SSB | FM | PM |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Number of Sidebands (single tone)** | 0 | 2 | 2 | 1 | $\infty$ (in theory) | $\infty$ (in theory) |
| **Carrier present?** | Yes (1) | Yes (large) | No | No | Yes ($J_0$ only) | Yes ($J_0$ only) |
| **Bandwidth (single tone)** | $\sim 0$ | $2f_m$ | $2f_m$ | $f_m$ | $2(\beta+1)f_m$ | $2(\beta+1)f_m$ |
| **Power in carrier (% of total)** | 100% | $\frac{1}{1 + \mu^2/2}$ | 0% | 0% | depends on $\beta$ | depends on $\beta$ |
| **Power efficiency** | N/A | Low ($\leq 33.3\%$) | 100% | 100% | High | High |
| **SNR improvement over AM** | N/A | 1 (baseline) | 1 | 1 | $\frac{3}{2}\beta^2$ | similar to FM |
| **Susceptibility to noise** | N/A | High | High | High | Low | Low |
| **Line vs Continuous Spectrum** | Line | Line | Continuous | Continuous | Line | Line |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** For full marks on a "Compare AM and FM spectra" question, always include: (1) mathematical expression of each spectrum, (2) bandwidth formulas, (3) a comparison table, (4) one sentence on the practical implication of each difference (e.g., "FM has higher bandwidth but better noise immunity").

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — FM Bandwidth Calculation

**Problem:** An FM broadcast station has $f_m^{\max} = 15$ kHz and maximum frequency deviation $\Delta f = 75$ kHz. Compute the modulation index and the bandwidth.

**Solution:**

$$\beta = \frac{\Delta f}{f_m^{\max}} = \frac{75}{15} = 5$$

$$BW_{FM} = 2(\beta + 1) f_m^{\max} = 2(5 + 1)(15) = 180 \text{ kHz}$$

This matches the **standard FM broadcast allocation of 200 kHz per channel** (with 20 kHz guard band).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

The following Mermaid block renders a functional block-level comparison chart of the spectral occupancy of CW, AM, DSB-SC, SSB, and FM signals along a common frequency axis.
