<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PEEET864 Module 1]**

**Q: Define the frequency spectrum of a signal. Why is the comparison of spectra important in communication systems?**

**Model Answer:**

The **frequency spectrum** of a signal is the distribution of its amplitude or power as a function of frequency. Mathematically, it is the magnitude of the Fourier Transform of the time-domain signal:

$$X(f) = \left\vert \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-j2\pi f t} dt \right\vert$$

Comparison of spectra is important because it reveals:
1. The **bandwidth** required for transmission.
2. The **power distribution** between the carrier and sidebands.
3. The **spectral efficiency** (information rate per Hz).
4. The **susceptibility to noise and interference**.
5. The **channel allocation requirements** in a multi-user system.

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEEET864 Module 1]**

**Q: Compare the bandwidth of AM, DSB-SC, and SSB signals carrying the same message of bandwidth $W$.**

**Model Answer:**

| Modulation Type | Bandwidth |
|---|---|
| AM (DSB-FC) | $BW = 2W$ |
| DSB-SC | $BW = 2W$ |
| SSB | $BW = W$ |

Thus, **SSB is the most bandwidth-efficient**, requiring only half the spectrum of AM or DSB-SC. AM and DSB-SC have the same bandwidth, but AM wastes additional power on the carrier.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, *every* Part B question provides a choice between two questions. The two questions below are mutually exclusive alternatives a student can select.

---

### Question B1 – Option A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, PEEET864 Module 1]**

**Q: With the help of a single-tone modulating signal, derive the expression for the AM signal and sketch its frequency spectrum. Compare the spectra of AM, DSB-SC, and SSB signals in terms of bandwidth, power distribution, and transmission efficiency.**

#### (a) Derivation of AM Signal and its Spectrum (7 Marks)

**Given:** $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$, modulation index $\mu = k_a A_m$.

**Step 1:** Write the AM signal.

$$s_{AM}(t) = A_c[1 + \mu \cos(2\pi f_m t)]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Step 2:** Expand the product.

$$s_{AM}(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + A_c \mu \cos(2\pi f_m t)\cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Step 3:** Apply $2\cos A \cos B = \cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)$.

$$s_{AM}(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + \frac{A_c \mu}{2}\cos[2\pi(f_c - f_m)t] + \frac{A_c \mu}{2}\cos[2\pi(f_c + f_m)t]$$

**Step 4:** Take the Fourier Transform.

$$S_{AM}(f) = \frac{A_c}{2}[\delta(f-f_c) + \delta(f+f_c)] + \frac{A_c \mu}{4}[\delta(f - (f_c - f_m)) + \delta(f + (f_c - f_m))] + \frac{A_c \mu}{4}[\delta(f - (f_c + f_m)) + \delta(f + (f_c + f_m))]$$

**Spectral sketch (description):** Three positive-frequency spectral lines: a tall line (amplitude $A_c/2$) at $f_c$, and two shorter lines (amplitude $A_c\mu/4$) at $f_c - f_m$ and $f_c + f_m$. Mirror image appears on the negative frequency axis.

#### (b) Comparison Table — AM, DSB-SC, SSB (7 Marks)

| Parameter | AM (DSB-FC) | DSB-SC | SSB |
|---|---|---|---|
| Carrier present | Yes (large) | No | No |
| Number of sidebands | 2 (USB + LSB) | 2 (USB + LSB) | 1 (USB or LSB) |
| Bandwidth | $2W$ | $2W$ | $W$ |
| Power in carrier | $\frac{P_t}{1 + \mu^2/2}$ | 0 | 0 |
| Total sideband power | $P_t \cdot \frac{\mu^2/2}{1 + \mu^2/2}$ | $P_t$ | $P_t$ |
| Transmission efficiency | $\frac{\mu^2}{2 + \mu^2}$ | 100% | 100% |
| Receiver complexity | Simple (envelope) | Complex (coherent) | Very complex |
| Application | Broadcast radio | TV video, telemetry | HF voice, amateur radio |

**Conclusion:** SSB > DSB-SC > AM in terms of spectrum and power efficiency, but the order reverses in receiver complexity.

---

### Question B1 – Option B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PEEET864 Module 1]**

**Q: Derive the expression for the spectrum of a single-tone FM signal using the Bessel function. Apply Carson's rule to find the bandwidth of an FM signal with $f_m = 10$ kHz and $\Delta f = 50$ kHz. Compare the spectra of FM and PM signals.**

#### (a) Derivation of FM Spectrum and Carson's Rule (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** FM signal with single-tone message $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$:

$$s_{FM}(t) = A_c \cos\!\left[2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_0^t A_m \cos(2\pi f_m \tau) d\tau\right]$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate the integral.

$$\int_0^t A_m \cos(2\pi f_m \tau) d\tau = \frac{A_m}{2\pi f_m} \sin(2\pi f_m t)$$

**Step 3:** Substitute and define $\beta = \dfrac{k_f A_m}{f_m}$.

$$s_{FM}(t) = A_c \cos[2\pi f_c t + \beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)]$$

**Step 4:** Express as real part of complex exponential.

$$s_{FM}(t) = A_c\, \text{Re}\left\{e^{j2\pi f_c t} \cdot e^{j\beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)}\right\}$$

**Step 5:** Apply the Jacobi-Anger identity.

$$e^{j\beta \sin\theta} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n(\beta) e^{jn\theta}$$

$$s_{FM}(t) = A_c \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n(\beta) \cos[2\pi(f_c + n f_m) t]$$

**Step 6:** Apply Carson's Rule with $f_m = 10$ kHz, $\Delta f = 50$ kHz, hence $\beta = 5$.

$$BW_{FM} = 2(\beta + 1) f_m = 2(5 + 1)(10) = 120 \text{ kHz}$$

#### (b) Comparison — FM vs PM Spectrum (7 Marks)

| Property | FM | PM |
|---|---|---|
| Modulating parameter | Frequency deviation $\Delta f$ | Phase deviation $\Delta \phi$ |
| Modulation index formula | $\beta = \dfrac{\Delta f}{f_m}$ | $\beta_{PM} = k_p \cdot \max \vert m(t) \vert$ |
| Dependency on $f_m$ | $\beta \propto 1/f_m$ | $\beta_{PM}$ independent of $f_m$ |
| Bandwidth vs $f_m$ | $BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m)$; grows linearly with $f_m$ | $BW = 2(\beta_{PM}+1) f_m$; grows proportionally to $f_m$ |
| Spectrum shape | Bessel-weighted sidebands | Bessel-weighted sidebands (same form) |
| Phase deviation | Indirect via frequency integration | Direct |
| Noise immunity | Excellent (better than AM) | Comparable to FM |
| Applications | FM radio, TV audio | Digital phase-shift keying, synthesizers |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often confuse the **modulation index** formulas for FM ($\beta = \Delta f / f_m$) and PM ($\beta = k_p |m(t)|_{\max}$). Writing the wrong formula will cost **2 full marks**.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** In Carson's rule, students frequently write $BW = 2\beta f_m$ instead of $2(\beta+1)f_m$. The "+1" is **mandatory** — losing it costs 1 mark.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** In comparison questions, students list parameters but forget to **directly compare** them side-by-side. The examiner expects explicit phrases like "FM has X, while PM has Y" — vague statements lose 2–3 marks.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Sketches of the FM spectrum must show **multiple Bessel-weighted sidebands** (not just two). A bare two-line spectrum will be marked as AM, not FM, costing the spectrum-sketch sub-part entirely.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Comparison of Spectrum**

- A **CW signal** has a spectrum of two **impulses** at $\pm f_c$ — zero bandwidth, no information.
- An **AM signal** has a **carrier + two sidebands** (USB and LSB); bandwidth $= 2W$.
- A **DSB-SC signal** removes the carrier but keeps both sidebands; bandwidth $= 2W$, 100% power efficient.
- An **SSB signal** keeps only one sideband; bandwidth $= W$, 100% power efficient.
- An **FM signal** has a carrier and **infinitely many sidebands** whose amplitudes are given by **Bessel functions** $J_n(\beta)$.
- **Carson's rule:** $BW_{FM} = 2(\beta + 1) f_m = 2(\Delta f + f_m)$ — this is the **authoritative formula** in every KTU paper.
- The **modulation index** for FM is $\beta = \Delta f / f_m$; for PM it is $\beta_{PM} = k_p |m(t)|_{\max}$.
- **FM and PM spectra are mathematically identical in form** (Bessel expansion), but they differ in *how the modulation index depends on the message frequency*.
- The **transmission efficiency** order: SSB = DSB-SC (100%) > FM ≈ PM > AM (≤ 33.3% for $\mu=1$).
- The **bandwidth** order: SSB $(W)$ < AM = DSB-SC $(2W)$ < FM = PM $(2(\beta+1)f_m)$.
- The **noise immunity** order: AM (worst) < DSB-SC = SSB < FM ≈ PM (best).
- The **receiver complexity** order: AM (simplest) < DSB-SC < SSB < FM ≈ PM (most complex).
- For a **single-tone message**, the AM spectrum is a *line spectrum* of three lines, while for a general message $m(t)$, it becomes a *continuous* spectrum consisting of shifted copies of $M(f)$.
- **Guard bands** between adjacent channels in commercial FM broadcast account for the 20 kHz gap (channel spacing = 200 kHz, BW = 150 kHz with $\Delta f = 75$ kHz).
- Always **state assumptions** (e.g., single-tone vs general message) before writing the spectrum expression — examiners award marks for clarity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power and Efficiency of All the Three Variants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET864 - ELECTRONIC COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Power and Efficiency of All the Three Variants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Amplitude Modulation (AM)** systems, the term **"Three Variants"** refers to the three fundamental techniques used to translate a low-frequency message signal into a bandpass (RF) signal. Each variant differs in **how the carrier and sidebands are transmitted**, leading to dramatically different **power distribution** and **transmission efficiency ($\eta$)** values.

The three variants are:

1. **DSB-FC (Double Sideband – Full Carrier)** : Conventional AM
2. **DSB-SC (Double Sideband – Suppressed Carrier)** : Balanced Modulator output
3. **SSB-SC (Single Sideband – Suppressed Carrier)** : Hilbert Transform / Filter Method

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> Students are expected to derive the **total transmitted power**, **carrier power**, **sideband power**, and **transmission efficiency** for each of the three AM variants. Numerical problems on power and efficiency are **frequently asked (high weightage)** in the End Semester Examination (ESE).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Delivery Truck" Intuition

Imagine you run a courier company delivering a **single important letter (the message)** to a distant city:

| Variant | Analogy (How the letter is sent) | Power Wasted? |
|---|---|---|
| **DSB-FC** | You send a **huge empty truck (carrier)** carrying **two copies of the letter** (USB + LSB) for safety. The truck consumes a lot of fuel even though only one copy is needed! | **YES — heavily** |
| **DSB-SC** | You remove the giant truck and just send **two identical copies of the letter** on a smaller, efficient vehicle. | **NO carrier waste** |
| **SSB-SC** | You send **only ONE copy of the letter** (either USB or LSB) on the smallest possible vehicle. The receiver can reconstruct the original from this single copy. | **MAXIMUM efficiency** |

This simple picture exactly mirrors what happens in real transmitters:
- The **carrier** in DSB-FC carries **no information** — it is pure overhead.
- The **two sidebands** in DSB carry **redundant information** — one is the mirror image of the other.
- **SSB** removes this redundancy to save nearly **75 % of the transmitted power**.

---

## 1.3 Standard Physical Metrics (Remember These Constants)

- **Modulation Index** of AM: $0 \le m_a \le 1$ (for distortionless transmission).
- **Maximum theoretical efficiency of DSB-FC**: $\eta_{max} = 33.33\,\%$ at $m_a = 1$.
- **Efficiency of DSB-SC and SSB-SC**: $\eta = 100\,\%$ (in theory, since the carrier is suppressed).
- **Sideband power relation**: For the same message, **SSB uses exactly half the power of DSB-SC**, which itself uses far less than DSB-FC.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world fact:** Commercial AM broadcast stations (DSB-FC) are notoriously power-inefficient. This is why **long-distance aeronautical, maritime, and military HF communication** almost universally uses **SSB-SC** — it gives the same range using only ~1/6th of the transmitter power.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Setup for All Three Variants

Let the message signal be a single-tone sinusoid for simplicity:
$$m(t) = A_m \cos(\omega_m t)$$
and the high-frequency carrier be:
$$c(t) = A_c \cos(\omega_c t)$$

Define:
- **Modulation index**: $m_a = \dfrac{A_m}{A_c}$
- **Carrier power (into 1 Ω load)**: $P_c = \dfrac{A_c^2}{2}$
- **Sideband power per sideband**: $P_{SB} = \dfrac{m_a^2 A_c^2}{8} = \dfrac{m_a^2}{4} P_c$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners expect students to clearly state these **assumptions** before starting the power derivation. Losing these marks is a common pitfall (see Section 5).

---

## 2.2 Variant-Wise Operational Logic

### A) DSB-FC (Double Sideband – Full Carrier) — Conventional AM
The transmitted signal is:
$$s_{DSB-FC}(t) = A_c \cos(\omega_c t) + \frac{m_a A_c}{2}\cos((\omega_c - \omega_m)t) + \frac{m_a A_c}{2}\cos((\omega_c + \omega_m)t)$$

**Logic steps:**
- Step 1: Carrier is transmitted at **full power** $P_c$.
- Step 2: Two sidebands (USB + LSB) are transmitted symmetrically.
- Step 3: Total power = carrier + 2 × sideband power.
- Step 4: Efficiency is limited because the carrier carries **no information**.

### B) DSB-SC (Double Sideband – Suppressed Carrier)
The transmitted signal is:
$$s_{DSB-SC}(t) = A_c \cos(\omega_c t) \cdot m(t) = \frac{m_a A_c}{2}\cos((\omega_c - \omega_m)t) + \frac{m_a A_c}{2}\cos((\omega_c + \omega_m)t)$$

**Logic steps:**
- Step 1: Carrier is **completely suppressed** (removed via balanced modulator).
- Step 2: Only two sidebands (USB + LSB) are transmitted.
- Step 3: All transmitted power is **information-bearing**.
- Step 4: Efficiency = **100 %** in theory.

### C) SSB-SC (Single Sideband – Suppressed Carrier)
The transmitted signal (Upper Sideband example):
$$s_{SSB-SC}(t) = \frac{m_a A_c}{2}\cos((\omega_c + \omega_m)t)$$

**Logic steps:**
- Step 1: Carrier is suppressed.
- Step 2: **Only ONE sideband** (USB or LSB) is transmitted.
- Step 3: Power is exactly **half** of DSB-SC.
- Step 4: Efficiency = **100 %**, with the **smallest possible bandwidth** (BW = $W$ instead of $2W$).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this table — it is the **single most tested concept** in Module 1.

| Quantity | DSB-FC (AM) | DSB-SC | SSB-SC |
|---|---|---|---|
| Carrier Power, $P_c$ | $\dfrac{A_c^2}{2}$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| Power in one SB, $P_{SB}$ | $\dfrac{m_a^2 A_c^2}{8}$ | $\dfrac{m_a^2 A_c^2}{8}$ | $\dfrac{m_a^2 A_c^2}{8}$ |
| Total Sideband Power, $P_{SB,tot}$ | $\dfrac{m_a^2 A_c^2}{4}$ | $\dfrac{m_a^2 A_c^2}{4}$ | $\dfrac{m_a^2 A_c^2}{8}$ |
| **Total Transmitted Power, $P_t$** | $\dfrac{A_c^2}{2}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{m_a^2}{2}\right)$ | $\dfrac{m_a^2 A_c^2}{4}$ | $\dfrac{m_a^2 A_c^2}{8}$ |
| **Useful Power (Sidebands only), $P_u$** | $\dfrac{m_a^2 A_c^2}{4}$ | $\dfrac{m_a^2 A_c^2}{4}$ | $\dfrac{m_a^2 A_c^2}{8}$ |
| **Transmission Efficiency, $\eta$** | $\dfrac{m_a^2}{2 + m_a^2}\times 100\,\%$ | $100\,\%$ | $100\,\%$ |
| Max $\eta$ (at $m_a=1$) | $33.33\,\%$ | $100\,\%$ | $100\,\%$ |
| Bandwidth | $2W$ | $2W$ | $W$ |
| Real-world example | AM radio broadcast | Color TV chrominance, QAM | HF communication, amateur radio |

> **Note on notation:** Absolute value / vertical bar usage is avoided inside table cells to prevent markdown rendering issues.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Broadcast engineers** choose DSB-FC because **envelope detection** with a simple diode is cheap — the inefficiency is the price of cheap receivers.
- **Telephone trunk lines** and **stereo FM subcarriers** use DSB-SC for power economy.
- **Maritime and military HF radios** use SSB-SC because **spectrum is scarce** and **battery power is precious** on ships/aircraft.
- The 6-dB power advantage of SSB over DSB-SC translates to a **factor of 4** in transmitter final-stage power dissipation savings.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation used below:** Load resistance $R = 1\,\Omega$ is assumed (standard KTU convention). For arbitrary $R$, divide each result by $R$. Carrier amplitude = $A_c$, modulation index = $m_a$, message amplitude = $A_m = m_a A_c$.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — DSB-FC (Conventional AM) Power and Efficiency

### Step 1: Time-domain expression of conventional AM
A message signal $m(t) = A_m\cos(\omega_m t)$ modulates a carrier $c(t) = A_c\cos(\omega_c t)$. By definition of AM:
$$s(t) = [A_c + m(t)]\cos(\omega_c t)$$

Substituting $m(t)$:
$$s(t) = A_c\cos(\omega_c t) + A_m\cos(\omega_m t)\cos(\omega_c t)$$

### Step 2: Expand using the trigonometric product-to-sum identity
Using the identity $\cos A \cos B = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)]$:
$$s(t) = A_c\cos(\omega_c t) + \frac{A_m}{2}\cos((\omega_c - \omega_m)t) + \frac{A_m}{2}\cos((\omega_c + \omega_m)t)$$

### Step 3: Identify the three spectral components
- Component 1: $A_c\cos(\omega_c t)$ — the **carrier**
- Component 2: $\dfrac{A_m}{2}\cos((\omega_c - \omega_m)t)$ — **Lower Sideband (LSB)**
- Component 3: $\dfrac{A_m}{2}\cos((\omega_c + \omega_m)t)$ — **Upper Sideband (USB)**

### Step 4: Compute the power of each component (into 1 Ω)
For a sinusoid $A\cos(\omega t)$, average power = $A^2/2$.

Therefore:
- **Carrier power:**
$$P_c = \frac{A_c^2}{2}$$
- **Power in one sideband (LSB or USB):**
$$P_{SB} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{A_m}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{A_m^2}{8}$$
- **Total sideband power (USB + LSB):**
$$P_{SB,tot} = 2 \times P_{SB} = \frac{A_m^2}{4}$$

### Step 5: Express everything in terms of $P_c$ and $m_a$
Using $A_m = m_a A_c$, we get $A_m^2 = m_a^2 A_c^2$. Substituting:
$$P_{SB} = \frac{m_a^2 A_c^2}{8} = \frac{m_a^2}{4} \cdot \frac{A_c^2}{2} = \frac{m_a^2}{4}\,P_c$$

$$P_{SB,tot} = \frac{m_a^2 A_c^2}{4} = \frac{m_a^2}{2}\,P_c$$

### Step 6: Total transmitted power
$$P_t = P_c + P_{SB,tot} = \frac{A_c^2}{2} + \frac{m_a^2 A_c^2}{4}$$

Factor out $A_c^2/2$:
$$P_t = \frac{A_c^2}{2}\left[1 + \frac{m_a^2}{2}\right]$$

Equivalently, in terms of $P_c$:
$$P_t = P_c\left[1 + \frac{m_a^2}{2}\right]$$

### Step 7: Useful (information-bearing) power
Only the sidebands carry information:
$$P_u = P_{SB,tot} = \frac{m_a^2 A_c^2}{4} = \frac{m_a^2}{2}\,P_c$$

### Step 8: Transmission efficiency
Efficiency is defined as the ratio of useful (sideband) power to total transmitted power:
$$\eta_{DSB-FC} = \frac{P_u}{P_t} = \frac{\dfrac{m_a^2 A_c^2}{4}}{\dfrac{A_c^2}{2}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{m_a^2}{2}\right)}$$

Cancel $A_c^2/4$ from numerator and denominator:
$$\eta_{DSB-FC} = \frac{m_a^2}{2 + m_a^2}$$

Expressed as a percentage:
$$\boxed{\eta_{DSB-FC} = \frac{m_a^2}{2 + m_a^2}\times 100\,\%}$$

### Step 9: Maximum possible efficiency
Substitute $m_a = 1$ (the highest allowed modulation index for distortionless AM):
$$\eta_{max} = \frac{1^2}{2 + 1^2} = \frac{1}{3} = 33.33\,\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always state that the **maximum efficiency of conventional AM is 33.33 %**, regardless of transmitter power, antenna, or load. This is a classic 2-mark short-answer question.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — DSB-SC Power and Efficiency

### Step 1: Time-domain expression
In a balanced (ring) modulator, the carrier is multiplied by the message:
$$s_{DSB-SC}(t) = A_c \cos(\omega_c t) \cdot m(t) = A_c A_m \cos(\omega_c t)\cos(\omega_m t)$$

### Step 2: Expand using product-to-sum
$$s_{DSB-SC}(t) = \frac{A_c A_m}{2}\cos((\omega_c - \omega_m)t) + \frac{A_c A_m}{2}\cos((\omega_c + \omega_m)t)$$

### Step 3: Identify components
- **No carrier term** — the carrier has been suppressed.
- Two sidebands (LSB + USB), each of amplitude $A_c A_m / 2$.

### Step 4: Power of each sideband
$$P_{SB} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{A_c A_m}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{A_c^2 A_m^2}{8} = \frac{m_a^2 A_c^2}{8} = \frac{m_a^2}{4}\,P_c$$

### Step 5: Total transmitted power
$$P_t = 2 \times P_{SB} = \frac{A_c^2 A_m^2}{4} = \frac{m_a^2 A_c^2}{4}$$

### Step 6: Useful power
In DSB-SC, **all transmitted power is useful** (no carrier):
$$P_u = P_t = \frac{m_a^2 A_c^2}{4}$$

### Step 7: Transmission efficiency
$$\boxed{\eta_{DSB-SC} = \frac{P_u}{P_t} = 1 = 100\,\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition check:** Even though 100 % of the power is "useful" in DSB-SC, only half the information is unique — the two sidebands are mirror images. SSB-SC removes this redundancy.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — SSB-SC Power and Efficiency

### Step 1: Time-domain expression (USB case)
After bandpass filtering removes one sideband:
$$s_{SSB-SC}(t) = \frac{A_c A_m}{2}\cos((\omega_c + \omega_m)t)$$

(Or with LSB: $\dfrac{A_c A_m}{2}\cos((\omega_c - \omega_m)t)$.)

### Step 2: Power of the single sideband
$$P_{SB} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{A_c A_m}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{A_c^2 A_m^2}{8} = \frac{m_a^2 A_c^2}{8}$$

### Step 3: Total transmitted power
Only one sideband is transmitted:
$$P_t = P_{SB} = \frac{m_a^2 A_c^2}{8}$$

### Step 4: Useful power
All transmitted power is information-bearing:
$$P_u = P_t = \frac{m_a^2 A_c^2}{8}$$

### Step 5: Transmission efficiency
$$\boxed{\eta_{SSB-SC} = \frac{P_u}{P_t} = 1 = 100\,\%}$$

### Step 6: Power comparison (very important for KTU problems)
For the **same modulation index and carrier amplitude**:
$$\frac{P_{t,\,SSB}}{P_{t,\,DSB-SC}} = \frac{1}{2}, \qquad \frac{P_{t,\,SSB}}{P_{t,\,DSB-FC}} = \frac{m_a^2}{4(1 + m_a^2/2)} = \frac{m_a^2}{4 + 2m_a^2}$$

At $m_a = 1$: $\dfrac{P_{t,SSB}}{P_{t,DSB-FC}} = \dfrac{1}{6}$. **SSB uses only 1/6th the power of conventional AM!**

---

## 3.4 Numerical Reference Matrix (For Quick Verification)

Assume $A_c = 10\,\text{V}$, $m_a = 1$, $R = 1\,\Omega$:

| Quantity | DSB-FC | DSB-SC | SSB-SC |
|---|---|---|---|
| $P_c$ | $50\,\text{W}$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| $P_{SB}$ (each) | $12.5\,\text{W}$ | $12.5\,\text{W}$ | $12.5\,\text{W}$ |
| $P_{SB,tot}$ | $25\,\text{W}$ | $25\,\text{W}$ | $12.5\,\text{W}$ |
| $P_t$ | $75\,\text{W}$ | $25\,\text{W}$ | $12.5\,\text{W}$ |
| $P_u$ | $25\,\text{W}$ | $25\,\text{W}$ | $12.5\,\text{W}$ |
| $\eta$ | $33.33\,\%$ | $100\,\%$ | $100\,\%$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Spectral Power Distribution — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1[Message Signal m of t] --> node2[Modulator Block]
    nodeA[Carrier c of t] --> node2
    node2 --> node3{Variant Selector}
    node3 -->|Conventional AM| node4[DSB FC Output]
    node3 -->|Balanced Mod| node5[DSB SC Output]
    node3 -->|BPF Filter| node6[SSB SC Output]
    node4 --> node7[Power Splitter: Carrier 67 percent plus Sidebands 33 percent]
    node5 --> node8[Power Splitter: USB 50 percent plus LSB 50 percent]
    node6 --> node9[Power Splitter: One sideband 100 percent]
    node7 --> node10[Efficiency Stage: 33.33 percent Max]
    node8 --> node11[Efficiency Stage: 100 percent]
    node9 --> node12[Efficiency Stage: 100 percent Lowest Power]
```

## 4.2 Power Flow Block Diagram (Comparative View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph P_DSBFC[DSB FC Conventional AM]
      A1[Total Power Pt] --> B1[Carrier Power Pc]
      A1 --> C1[Sideband Power USB]
      A1 --> D1[Sideband Power LSB]
    end
    subgraph P_DSBSC[DSB SC Balanced Modulator]
      A2[Total Power Pt] --> C2[USB Power]
      A2 --> D2[LSB Power]
    end
    subgraph P_SSBSC[SSB SC Filter Method]
      A3[Total Power Pt] --> E3[Single Sideband Power USB or LSB]
    end
```

## 4.3 Frequency-Domain Spectral Diagram (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph F_AM[DSB FC Spectrum]
        F1[Carrier Spike at fc] --- F2[LSB at fc minus fm] --- F3[USB at fc plus fm]
    end
    subgraph F_DSBSC[DSB SC Spectrum]
        G1[No Carrier] --- G2[LSB at fc minus fm] --- G3[USB at fc plus fm]
    end
    subgraph F_SSBSC[SSB SC Spectrum]
        H1[No Carrier] --- H2[Only USB at fc plus fm]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> The block diagrams above map the **functional flow of power** in each AM variant — from the modulator output down to the radiated signal — exactly as KTU board examiners expect students to illustrate in long-answer derivations.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Similar]**
*Define transmission efficiency of an AM system. Derive the maximum theoretical efficiency of conventional DSB-FC (AM).*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Transmission efficiency $\eta$ is defined as the ratio of **useful power** (i.e., the total sideband power, which contains the information) to the **total transmitted power** (carrier + sidebands):
$$\eta = \frac{P_{\text{sidebands}}}{P_{\text{total}}} = \frac{P_{SB,tot}}{P_c + P_{SB,tot}}$$

For conventional AM:
$$\eta = \frac{m_a^2}{2 + m_a^2}$$

Maximum occurs at the largest allowable modulation index $m_a = 1$:
$$\eta_{max} = \frac{1^2}{2 + 1^2} = \frac{1}{3} = 33.33\,\%$$

Hence the **maximum theoretical transmission efficiency of conventional AM is 33.33 %**. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Similar]**
*Why is the efficiency of DSB-SC 100 % even though two sidebands are transmitted? Justify.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In DSB-SC, the carrier is completely suppressed by the balanced modulator. Hence **no power is wasted** in transmitting a non-information-bearing component. Although two sidebands are present, both **carry information** (they are individually useful signals, even though they contain mirror-image information). Therefore, all transmitted power is useful, giving:
$$\eta_{DSB-SC} = \frac{P_{SB,tot}}{P_t} = 1 = 100\,\%$$

The 100 % efficiency is *theoretical* — practical values are slightly less due to modulator losses, filtering losses, and amplifier inefficiency. **(3 marks)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Full derivation of all three variants*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **total transmitted power** and **transmission efficiency** of a conventional AM (DSB-FC) system transmitting a single-tone message signal. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the **same message and carrier amplitudes**, derive the total power and efficiency for **DSB-SC** and **SSB-SC** systems, and compare all three variants in a tabular form. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1: Mathematical model** *(1 mark)*
Let $m(t) = A_m \cos(\omega_m t)$ and $c(t) = A_c \cos(\omega_c t)$. Conventional AM:
$$s(t) = A_c[1 + m_a\cos(\omega_m t)]\cos(\omega_c t), \quad m_a = A_m/A_c$$

**Step 2: Expansion** *(1 mark)*
$$s(t) = A_c\cos(\omega_c t) + \frac{A_m}{2}\cos((\omega_c - \omega_m)t) + \frac{A_m}{2}\cos((\omega_c + \omega_m)t)$$

**Step 3: Component powers** *(2 marks)*
$$P_c = \frac{A_c^2}{2}, \qquad P_{SB} = \frac{A_m^2}{8} = \frac{m_a^2 A_c^2}{8}$$

**Step 4: Total power** *(1 mark)*
$$P_t = P_c + 2P_{SB} = \frac{A_c^2}{2}\left[1 + \frac{m_a^2}{2}\right]$$

**Step 5: Efficiency** *(1 mark)*
$$\eta = \frac{2P_{SB}}{P_t} = \frac{m_a^2}{2 + m_a^2}\times 100\,\%$$

**Step 6: Conclusion** *(1 mark)*
Maximum $\eta = 33.33\,\%$ at $m_a = 1$.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**DSB-SC** *(2 marks)*
- $s(t) = A_c A_m \cos(\omega_c t)\cos(\omega_m t) = \dfrac{A_c A_m}{2}[\cos((\omega_c - \omega_m)t) + \cos((\omega_c + \omega_m)t)]$
- $P_t = \dfrac{m_a^2 A_c^2}{4}$, $\eta = 100\,\%$.

**SSB-SC** *(2 marks)*
- $s(t) = \dfrac{A_c A_m}{2}\cos((\omega_c + \omega_m)t)$
- $P_t = \dfrac{m_a^2 A_c^2}{8}$, $\eta = 100\,\%$.

**Comparison Table** *(3 marks)*

| Parameter | DSB-FC | DSB-SC | SSB-SC |
|---|---|---|---|
| Carrier Power | $A_c^2/2$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| Total Power | $\frac{A_c^2}{2}(1 + m_a^2/2)$ | $m_a^2 A_c^2 / 4$ | $m_a^2 A_c^2 / 8$ |
| Efficiency | $\frac{m_a^2}{2+m_a^2}\times 100\,\%$ | $100\,\%$ | $100\,\%$ |
| Bandwidth | $2W$ | $2W$ | $W$ |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Numerical + Comparative Analysis*

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model]**

**(a)** An AM transmitter delivers **10 kW** to a 50 Ω antenna when modulated to its **maximum permissible level**. Calculate the **(i)** carrier power, **(ii)** total sideband power, and **(iii)** transmission efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the same transmitter is reconfigured to operate as **DSB-SC** and then as **SSB-SC** (same carrier amplitude, same $m_a = 1$), compute the new total power and efficiency. Comment on the practical significance. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**Given:** $P_t = 10\,\text{kW}$, $m_a = 1$ (maximum), $R = 50\,\Omega$.

**(i) Carrier power** *(3 marks)*
$$P_t = P_c\left(1 + \frac{m_a^2}{2}\right) = P_c\left(1 + \frac{1}{2}\right) = 1.5\,P_c$$
$$\Rightarrow P_c = \frac{10\,\text{kW}}{1.5} = 6.667\,\text{kW}$$

**(ii) Total sideband power** *(2 marks)*
$$P_{SB,tot} = P_t - P_c = 10 - 6.667 = 3.333\,\text{kW}$$
Or equivalently, $P_{SB,tot} = \dfrac{m_a^2}{2}P_c = \dfrac{1}{2}(6.667) = 3.333\,\text{kW}$.

**(iii) Efficiency** *(2 marks)*
$$\eta = \frac{P_{SB,tot}}{P_t} = \frac{3.333}{10} = 33.33\,\%$$

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

Since the carrier amplitude is unchanged, $P_c = 6.667\,\text{kW}$, and $A_c^2 = 2P_c R = 2 \times 6667 \times 50$ — but we work directly with the **$P_c$** and **$m_a$** form.

**DSB-SC:** *(3 marks)*
$$P_t = \frac{m_a^2}{2}\,P_c = \frac{1}{2} \times 6.667 = 3.333\,\text{kW}$$
$$\eta = 100\,\%$$

**SSB-SC:** *(3 marks)*
$$P_t = \frac{m_a^2}{4}\,P_c = \frac{1}{4} \times 6.667 = 1.667\,\text{kW}$$
$$\eta = 100\,\%$$

**Practical significance (1 mark):**
The same coverage / signal quality can be achieved with **only 1.667 kW** in SSB-SC versus **10 kW** in DSB-FC — a **6× reduction** in transmitter power. This is why SSB is mandatory in long-distance HF, aeronautical, and maritime communication where power and spectrum are at a premium.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to assume $R = 1\,\Omega$** explicitly — loss of 1 mark.
> 2. **Mixing up the formulas for $P_{SB,tot}$** between variants — SSB has only **one** sideband, not two.
> 3. **Quoting $\eta = 100\,\%$ for SSB/DSB-SC without justifying** that "useful power" = "total power" since the carrier is suppressed.
> 4. **Not stating the assumption $m_a = 1$** when computing $\eta_{max}$ in DSB-FC.
> 5. **Failing to mention that 100 % efficiency is theoretical** — real systems have modulator and amplifier losses.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Three Variants of AM:** DSB-FC, DSB-SC, SSB-SC.
- **DSB-FC total power:** $P_t = \dfrac{A_c^2}{2}\left(1 + \dfrac{m_a^2}{2}\right)$.
- **DSB-SC total power:** $P_t = \dfrac{m_a^2 A_c^2}{4}$ — **no carrier.**
- **SSB-SC total power:** $P_t = \dfrac{m_a^2 A_c^2}{8}$ — **only one sideband.**
- **DSB-FC efficiency:** $\eta = \dfrac{m_a^2}{2 + m_a^2}$, **maximum = 33.33 %** at $m_a = 1$.
- **DSB-SC efficiency:** $\eta = 100\,\%$ (theoretical).
- **SSB-SC efficiency:** $\eta = 100\,\%$ (theoretical).
- **Power ratio SSB : DSB-FC = 1 : 6** (at $m_a = 1$) — the most quoted KTU result.
- **Bandwidth:** DSB-FC = DSB-SC = $2W$; SSB-SC = $W$.
- **KtU high-weightage numericals:** Always assume single-tone $m(t) = A_m\cos(\omega_m t)$ unless specified otherwise; assume $R = 1\,\Omega$.
- **Practical takeaway:** AM broadcast = DSB-FC (cheap receiver); HF/military = SSB-SC (power economy).
- **Common mistake to avoid:** Do **not** confuse $P_{SB}$ (per sideband) with $P_{SB,tot}$ (sum of both sidebands) when writing final answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Amplitude Modulator Circuits - Balanced Modulator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET864 - ELECTRONIC COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Amplitude Modulator Circuits - Balanced Modulator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Balanced Modulator** is a non-linear signal processing circuit that produces the **product of two input signals** (typically a message signal $m(t)$ and a carrier signal $c(t)$) while **completely suppressing the carrier component** at the output. The resultant signal is a **Double Sideband Suppressed Carrier (DSB-SC)** waveform, formally defined by the KTU 2024 syllabus as a circuit whose output is proportional to the instantaneous product of the modulating signal and the carrier, with the carrier nulled by symmetric cancellation.

Mathematically, the ideal output is:

$$s_{DSB-SC}(t) = A_c \cdot m(t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

The amplitude spectrum contains **only the upper sideband (USB) and lower sideband (LSB)** symmetrically placed about $f_c$, with **zero spectral component at $f_c$** itself.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A balanced modulator is the foundational building block for generating **DSB-SC, SSB, and QAM** signals. It is also the heart of every synchronous (coherent) demodulator and is a high-weightage topic in ESE Module 1.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine two people pushing a swing:
- A **conventional AM transmitter** is like one person continuously pushing the swing **AND** another person just holding a heavy stone (the carrier) on the swing at all times. The stone does not swing but consumes a lot of energy.
- A **balanced modulator** is like a *smart seesaw*: the two people push in **opposite directions** with equal force. The stone (carrier) is **balanced out** and disappears, while the **actual swinging motion (sidebands — the information)** doubles in clarity. You transmit only what is useful — the swinging — and burn far less power.

Another analogy: It is a **subtractor for the carrier** but a **multiplier for the information**. A common-mode carrier is rejected; the differential-mode message is preserved.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

- **Carrier suppression ratio:** typically **40 dB to 60 dB** in a well-designed IC balanced modulator (e.g., MC1496, AD534).
- **Operating frequencies:** Audio to several hundred MHz (for IC versions).
- **Power efficiency:** Up to **100%** (vs. 33.3% max for conventional AM with $\mu = 1$).
- **Bandwidth:** Identical to conventional AM, i.e., $BW = 2 W$, where $W$ is the message bandwidth.

> [!NOTE]
> **Carrier Power Wasted in Conventional AM:** In a 100% modulated AM wave, the carrier carries **2/3 of the total transmitted power** while carrying **zero information**. The balanced modulator eliminates this wastage.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Do We Need a Balanced Modulator?

A conventional AM signal is:

$$s_{AM}(t) = A_c[1 + k_a m(t)]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

Expanding:

$$s_{AM}(t) = A_c\cos(2\pi f_c t) + A_c k_a m(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

The first term is the **pure carrier** (no information), and the second is the **DSB-SC** (the useful part). A balanced modulator generates **only the second term**, hence the name *balanced* — it balances out the carrier.

### 2.2 Working Principle — Symmetric Cancellation

The balanced modulator uses **two identical non-linear (or switching) devices** in a symmetric configuration. When the same carrier is fed to both devices in **opposite phase (180° apart)** and the message is fed **in phase**, the carrier components in the output **cancel** due to destructive interference, while the message-modulated sidebands **add constructively**.

### 2.3 Common Topologies Studied in KTU 2024

1. **Diode Ring (Lattice) Balanced Modulator** — uses 4 diodes as a switching ring driven by a large carrier.
2. **FET/BJT Balanced Modulator** — uses matched transistor pairs (e.g., emitter-coupled pair).
3. **IC Balanced Modulator** — e.g., **MC1496, AD633, AD534** — Gilbert Cell multiplier architecture.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Description / Units** |
|---|---|---|
| Ideal Output (Time Domain) | $s(t) = A_c \cdot m(t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | DSB-SC waveform; Volts |
| Single-tone Output | $s(t) = \dfrac{A_c A_m}{2}\big[\cos 2\pi(f_c-f_m)t + \cos 2\pi(f_c+f_m)t\big]$ | Two sidebands only; no carrier |
| Carrier Suppression Ratio (CSR) | $\text{CSR} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_{\text{carrier}}}{A_{\text{sideband}}}\right)$ | In decibels (dB) |
| Power Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{\text{sidebands}}}{P_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$ | Up to 100% for DSB-SC |
| Modulation Index (DSB-SC) | $m = k_a A_m$ | Dimensionless |
| Bandwidth | $BW = 2 W$ | Hz (same as AM) |
| Square-wave Carrier Fourier Series | $c(t) = \dfrac{4}{\pi}\displaystyle\sum_{n=1,3,5,\dots}^{\infty}\dfrac{(-1)^{(n-1)/2}}{n}\cos(2\pi n f_c t)$ | Used in diode ring analysis |
| Diode Ring Output (Unfiltered) | $s(t) = m(t)\cdot c(t) = \dfrac{4}{\pi}\displaystyle\sum_{n=1,3,5,\dots}\dfrac{(-1)^{(n-1)/2}}{n}m(t)\cos(2\pi n f_c t)$ | Contains odd harmonics of $f_c$ |
| Output Power (DSB-SC, single tone) | $P_{DSB} = \dfrac{A_c^2 A_m^2}{8 R}$ | Watts across load $R$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical LaTeX Note for Tables:** In the table above, vertical bars are intentionally rendered using `\dfrac` and explicit parentheses, **never** the bare pipe character `\vert x \vert`, to preserve Markdown table integrity.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **DSB-SC and SSB transmitters** for long-distance HF communication (maritime, military).
- **Synchronous detectors** in color TV (chrominance demodulation uses balanced multipliers with 3.58 MHz subcarrier).
- **Quadrature Amplitude Modulation (QAM)** modems — the I/Q mixers in cable modems, Wi-Fi, and 4G/5G are balanced modulators.
- **Spectral efficiency boosters** in satellite uplinks (where power is premium).
- **RF signal generators and lab test equipment.**

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Single-Tone DSB-SC Output

**Given:**
- Modulating signal: $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$
- Carrier signal: $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$

**Step 1 — Ideal Multiplication Output:**

The ideal balanced modulator multiplies the two inputs:

$$s(t) = m(t) \cdot c(t)$$

$$s(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t) \cdot A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

$$s(t) = A_c A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Product-to-Sum Trigonometric Identity:**

The standard identity is:

$$\cos(\alpha)\cos(\beta) = \frac{1}{2}\big[\cos(\alpha - \beta) + \cos(\alpha + \beta)\big]$$

Substituting $\alpha = 2\pi f_c t$ and $\beta = 2\pi f_m t$:

$$\cos(2\pi f_c t)\cos(2\pi f_m t) = \frac{1}{2}\big[\cos(2\pi(f_c - f_m)t) + \cos(2\pi(f_c + f_m)t)\big]$$

**Step 3 — Final DSB-SC Expression:**

$$\boxed{\,s_{DSB-SC}(t) = \frac{A_c A_m}{2}\big[\cos 2\pi(f_c - f_m)t + \cos 2\pi(f_c + f_m)t\big]\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The expression contains **no $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ term** — the carrier is mathematically absent, confirming the balanced modulator's defining property.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Power Efficiency of DSB-SC

**Step 1 — Total Power of DSB-SC Across Load $R$:**

The DSB-SC signal is the sum of two equal-amplitude sinusoids at $f_c - f_m$ and $f_c + f_m$, each of amplitude $A_c A_m / 2$. The power of one sinusoid of amplitude $A$ across $R$ is $A^2 / (2R)$.

$$P_{LSB} = \frac{(A_c A_m/2)^2}{2R} = \frac{A_c^2 A_m^2}{8R}$$

$$P_{USB} = \frac{A_c^2 A_m^2}{8R}$$

**Step 2 — Total DSB-SC Power:**

$$P_{DSB-SC} = P_{LSB} + P_{USB} = \frac{A_c^2 A_m^2}{4R}$$

**Step 3 — Power Efficiency:**

In DSB-SC, **100% of the transmitted power lies in the sidebands** (since the carrier is zero):

$$\eta_{DSB-SC} = \frac{P_{\text{sidebands}}}{P_{\text{total}}} \times 100\% = 100\%$$

**Comparison:** For conventional AM with modulation index $\mu = k_a A_m$:

$$P_{AM,\text{total}} = \frac{A_c^2}{2R}\left[1 + \frac{\mu^2}{2}\right]$$

$$P_{\text{carrier}} = \frac{A_c^2}{2R}$$

$$\eta_{AM} = \frac{\mu^2}{2 + \mu^2} \times 100\%$$

For $\mu = 1$ (100% modulation), $\eta_{AM} = 33.3\%$. DSB-SC is **3× more efficient**.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Diode Ring Balanced Modulator

This is the most analytically important circuit for KTU. Four matched diodes $D_1, D_2, D_3, D_4$ form a ring. The carrier is a large-amplitude square wave that switches the diodes ON/OFF. The message is injected through a center-tapped transformer.

**Step 1 — Carrier as a Switching Function:**

The large carrier drives the diodes into hard switching. The effective switching function is a symmetric square wave of unit amplitude:

$$k(t) = \begin{cases} +1, & \text{when } c(t) > 0 \\ -1, & \text{when } c(t) < 0 \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — Fourier Series of the Switching Function:**

A symmetric square wave of unit amplitude and frequency $f_c$ has the Fourier expansion:

$$k(t) = \frac{4}{\pi}\sum_{n=1,3,5,\dots}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^{(n-1)/2}}{n} \cos(2\pi n f_c t)$$

Expanding the first few terms:

$$k(t) = \frac{4}{\pi}\left[\cos(2\pi f_c t) - \frac{1}{3}\cos(2\pi \cdot 3 f_c t) + \frac{1}{5}\cos(2\pi \cdot 5 f_c t) - \cdots\right]$$

**Step 3 — Output Voltage of the Ring Modulator:**

The output of the diode ring is the message multiplied by the switching function:

$$s(t) = m(t) \cdot k(t) = m(t) \cdot \frac{4}{\pi}\sum_{n=1,3,5,\dots}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^{(n-1)/2}}{n} \cos(2\pi n f_c t)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Single-Tone Message $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$:**

$$s(t) = \frac{4 A_m}{\pi}\sum_{n=1,3,5,\dots}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^{(n-1)/2}}{n} \cos(2\pi f_m t)\cos(2\pi n f_c t)$$

**Step 5 — Apply Product-to-Sum Identity:**

$$\cos(2\pi f_m t)\cos(2\pi n f_c t) = \frac{1}{2}\big[\cos 2\pi(n f_c - f_m)t + \cos 2\pi(n f_c + f_m)t\big]$$

Therefore:

$$s(t) = \frac{2 A_m}{\pi}\sum_{n=1,3,5,\dots}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^{(n-1)/2}}{n}\big[\cos 2\pi(n f_c - f_m)t + \cos 2\pi(n f_c + f_m)t\big]$$

**Step 6 — Interpret the Spectrum:**

For $n=1$ (fundamental carrier):

$$\text{Component at } f_c: \quad \frac{2A_m}{\pi}\big[\cos 2\pi(f_c - f_m)t + \cos 2\pi(f_c + f_m)t\big]$$

This is the **desired DSB-SC signal** centered at $f_c$.

For $n=3, 5, 7, \dots$, **unwanted sideband pairs** appear at $3f_c \pm f_m$, $5f_c \pm f_m$, etc., with diminishing amplitude ($1/n$).

**Step 7 — Filtering:**

A **bandpass filter (BPF)** centered at $f_c$ with bandwidth $2W$ removes all harmonics beyond $n=1$, yielding the **pure DSB-SC signal**:

$$s_{DSB-SC}(t) = \frac{2 A_m}{\pi}\big[\cos 2\pi(f_c - f_m)t + \cos 2\pi(f_c + f_m)t\big]$$

### 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| **Aspect** | **Conventional AM** | **DSB-SC (Balanced Modulator)** |
|---|---|---|
| Carrier Present? | Yes | **No (suppressed)** |
| Output Equation | $A_c[1+k_a m(t)]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | $A_c m(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ |
| Spectrum at $f_c$ | Impulse of weight $A_c/2$ | **Zero** |
| Max Power Efficiency | 33.3% ($\mu = 1$) | **100%** |
| Demodulation | Envelope detector | **Synchronous detector** |
| Information in envelope? | Yes | **No — phase reversals** |
| Bandwidth | $2W$ | $2W$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Balanced Modulator System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Message Signal m of t] --> MIX1[Multiplier Stage A]
    C[Carrier cos 2 pi fc t] --> MIX1
    C --> PHASE[Phase Splitter 180 deg]
    PHASE --> MIX2[Multiplier Stage B]
    A --> MIX2
    MIX1 --> SUM[Summer Node]
    MIX2 --> SUM
    SUM --> OUT[Output DSB SC Signal]
    OUT --> BPF[Bandpass Filter fc BW 2W]
    BPF --> FINAL[Clean DSB SC Output]
```

### 4.2 Diode Ring Modulator — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT[Input Section]
        M1[Message m of t via Center Tap Transformer]
        C1[Large Carrier c of t to Diode Ring]
    end

    subgraph RING[Diode Ring Lattice]
        D1[Diode D1 Top Left]
        D2[Diode D2 Bottom Left]
        D3[Diode D3 Top Right]
        D4[Diode D4 Bottom Right]
    end

    subgraph SWITCH[Switching Logic]
        S1[Positive Half Cycle D1 D3 ON]
        S2[Negative Half Cycle D2 D4 ON]
    end

    subgraph OUTPATH[Output Section]
        OUT1[Output Transformer Secondary]
        OUT2[Spectrum f_c plus minus f_m]
        OUT3[Odd Harmonics 3f_c 5f_c ...]
        BPF1[BPF centered at f_c]
        OUT4[DSB SC Output]
    end

    M1 --> RING
    C1 --> RING
    C1 --> SWITCH
    SWITCH --> RING
    RING --> OUT1
    OUT1 --> OUT2
    OUT1 --> OUT3
    OUT3 --> BPF1
    OUT2 --> BPF1
    BPF1 --> OUT4
```

### 4.3 Frequency Domain Mapping — DSB-SC Spectrum

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DOMAIN[Frequency Axis]
        F1[At f_c minus f_m LSB] --> F2[At f_c NO CARRIER]
        F2 --> F3[At f_c plus f_m USB]
    end

    AXIS[Frequency Domain Visualization] --> DOMAIN
    NOTE[Carrier component is COMPLETELY suppressed unlike conventional AM] --> F2
```

### 4.4 Signal Flow — From Message to DSB-SC

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MSG[Modulating Signal m of t] --> MULT[Multiplication by Carrier]
    CAR[Carrier A_c cos 2 pi fc t] --> MULT
    MULT --> PROD[Product m of t times c of t]
    PROD --> SPEC[Two Sidebands Only]
    SPEC --> ZERO[Zero Carrier at f_c]
    SPEC --> TX[Transmit via Antenna with 100% Efficiency]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Practical Note:** In lab experiments using the MC1496 IC, students measure the carrier suppression by displaying the output on a spectrum analyzer and verifying that the spectral line at $f_c$ is at least **40 dB below** the sidebands.

---
