<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: Define Balanced Modulator. Mention its significance.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A balanced modulator is a circuit that produces the **product of two input signals** (message and carrier) while **suppressing the carrier component** at the output, yielding a **Double Sideband Suppressed Carrier (DSB-SC)** signal. *(1 mark)* Its significance lies in the fact that it **eliminates the carrier** which carries no information, thereby improving **power efficiency to 100%** (compared to 33.3% in conventional AM at 100% modulation) and forms the **fundamental building block for SSB, QAM, and synchronous detection systems**. *(2 marks)*

#### **Question 2: State any three applications of balanced modulators.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**
1. **Generation of DSB-SC and SSB signals** in communication transmitters for power-efficient long-distance transmission.
2. **Quadrature Amplitude Modulation (QAM)** in digital modems for cable, DSL, Wi-Fi, and cellular systems (as I/Q mixers).
3. **Synchronous (coherent) demodulation** in color television receivers for recovering the I and Q chrominance components from the 3.58 MHz subcarrier.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — July 2024]

**Q. (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **balanced modulator using diode ring modulator**. Derive the expression for the output voltage, and show that the carrier component is suppressed. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Circuit Description (2 marks):**
A diode ring balanced modulator uses **four matched diodes** $D_1, D_2, D_3, D_4$ connected in a ring (lattice) configuration. Two center-tapped transformers are used: one to inject the **modulating signal $m(t)$** into the ring through the center tap of the primary, and one to drive the **large carrier $c(t)$** into the ring. The output is taken from a third transformer.

**Step 2 — Working (2 marks):**
The carrier amplitude is made sufficiently large so that it acts as a **switching signal** that turns two diagonally opposite diodes ON and the other two OFF in each half-cycle.
- **Positive half cycle of carrier:** $D_1$ and $D_3$ are ON, $D_2$ and $D_4$ are OFF. The output is $+m(t)$.
- **Negative half cycle of carrier:** $D_2$ and $D_4$ are ON, $D_1$ and $D_3$ are OFF. The output is $-m(t)$.

Thus the ring acts as a **chopper / multiplier** with the polarity switched by the carrier.

**Step 3 — Derivation (3 marks):**
The output is the product of the message and a square-wave switching function $k(t)$:

$$s(t) = m(t) \cdot k(t)$$

The Fourier series of $k(t)$ is:

$$k(t) = \frac{4}{\pi}\sum_{n=1,3,5,\dots}^{\infty}\frac{(-1)^{(n-1)/2}}{n}\cos(2\pi n f_c t)$$

For $m(t) = A_m\cos(2\pi f_m t)$, and using product-to-sum:

$$s(t) = \frac{2A_m}{\pi}\sum_{n=1,3,5,\dots}^{\infty}\frac{(-1)^{(n-1)/2}}{n}\big[\cos 2\pi(nf_c - f_m)t + \cos 2\pi(nf_c + f_m)t\big]$$

The component at $n=1$ is the desired DSB-SC at $f_c \pm f_m$. The higher-order terms at $3f_c \pm f_m$, $5f_c \pm f_m$, … are removed by a **bandpass filter** centered at $f_c$:

$$s_{DSB-SC}(t) = \frac{2A_m}{\pi}\big[\cos 2\pi(f_c - f_m)t + \cos 2\pi(f_c + f_m)t\big]$$

**Step 4 — Carrier Suppression (1 mark):**
The **coefficient of $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ is identically zero** in the output. The carrier component is therefore completely suppressed, leaving only the sidebands — confirming the balanced operation.

---

**Q. (b)** A balanced modulator has a modulating signal $m(t) = 4\cos(2\pi \times 1000\,t)$ and a carrier $c(t) = 10\cos(2\pi \times 10^5\,t)$. **(i)** Write the expression for the DSB-SC output. **(ii)** Calculate the total transmitted power across a 50 Ω load. **(iii)** Compare the power efficiency with conventional AM at 100% modulation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) DSB-SC Output Expression (2 marks):**

Using the identity $\cos A \cos B = \tfrac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)]$:

$$s(t) = 4 \cdot 10 \cdot \cos(2\pi \cdot 1000\,t) \cdot \cos(2\pi \cdot 10^5 t)$$

$$s(t) = 40 \cdot \frac{1}{2}\big[\cos 2\pi(10^5 - 1000)t + \cos 2\pi(10^5 + 1000)t\big]$$

$$\boxed{\,s(t) = 20\big[\cos(2\pi \cdot 99{,}000\,t) + \cos(2\pi \cdot 101{,}000\,t)\big]\,}$$

**(ii) Total Power Across 50 Ω (3 marks):**

Each sideband has amplitude $A_{sb} = 20$ V. Power per sideband:

$$P_{sb} = \frac{A_{sb}^2}{2R} = \frac{20^2}{2 \cdot 50} = \frac{400}{100} = 4 \text{ W}$$

Total DSB-SC power:

$$P_{DSB-SC} = 2 \times 4 = 8 \text{ W}$$

**(iii) Comparison with Conventional AM (2 marks):**

For conventional AM with $\mu = 1$ (100% modulation):

$$P_{carrier} = \frac{A_c^2}{2R} = \frac{10^2}{2 \cdot 50} = 1 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{sidebands} = \frac{A_c^2 \mu^2}{4R} = \frac{100 \cdot 1}{200} = 0.5 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{AM,\,total} = 1.5 \text{ W}$$

$$\eta_{AM} = \frac{0.5}{1.5} \times 100\% = 33.3\%$$

For DSB-SC: $\eta_{DSB-SC} = 100\%$.

**Conclusion:** DSB-SC delivers the **same sideband power (0.5 W equivalent for matched modulation index)** while using less than half the total power, or alternatively delivers **far more sideband power** for the same total power, giving a 3× efficiency improvement.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**Q. (a)** Explain the **transistor (BJT/FET) balanced modulator** with a circuit diagram. Derive the expression for the output and show how carrier suppression is achieved. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Circuit Topology (2 marks):**
A transistor balanced modulator uses **two matched BJTs (or JFETs)** configured as a **long-tailed pair** (emitter-coupled or source-coupled). The message signal $m(t)$ is applied to the **common emitter tail** (or as differential input), and the carrier $c(t) = A_c\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ is applied **differentially** to the two bases (sources). The output is taken **differentially** between the two collectors (drains).

**Step 2 — Working Principle (2 marks):**
The matched pair exhibits a **square-law (or tanh) transfer characteristic** in the small-signal regime. The differential output contains the product of the message and the carrier. Because the two transistors are perfectly matched and the carrier is fed 180° out of phase, the **common-mode carrier component cancels** at the differential output, while the **differential message-modulated term adds constructively**.

**Step 3 — Derivation (3 marks):**
For a square-law device with $i_d = a v_{gs} + b v_{gs}^2$, and inputs $v_1 = m(t) + c(t)$ and $v_2 = m(t) - c(t)$:

$$i_1 = a[m+c] + b[m+c]^2$$

$$i_2 = a[m-c] + b[m-c]^2$$

Differential output current:

$$i_{out} = i_1 - i_2 = 2a c(t) + 4b\,m(t) c(t)$$

The first term is the residual carrier (suppressed by symmetric matching), and the second is the **desired DSB-SC product**:

$$s_{DSB-SC}(t) = 4b \cdot m(t) \cdot A_c\cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

For $m(t) = A_m\cos(2\pi f_m t)$:

$$s_{DSB-SC}(t) = 2b A_c A_m \big[\cos 2\pi(f_c - f_m)t + \cos 2\pi(f_c + f_m)t\big]$$

The carrier at $f_c$ is **completely cancelled** due to the symmetric differential topology.

---

**Q. (b)** With reference to the **diode ring balanced modulator**: **(i)** Explain why the output contains odd harmonics of the carrier. **(ii)** A carrier of 1 MHz drives the ring, and a 5 kHz tone is the modulating signal. Sketch the output spectrum (before filtering) up to 5 MHz. **(iii)** What is the role of the bandpass filter? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Origin of Odd Harmonics (2 marks):**
The diode ring driven by a large carrier behaves as a **multiplier with a square-wave switching function**, not a pure sinusoid. The Fourier series of a symmetric square wave contains **only odd harmonics of the fundamental** ($f_c, 3f_c, 5f_c, 7f_c, \dots$) with amplitudes $\propto 1/n$. When this switching function multiplies the message, the spectrum is **translated to each odd harmonic**, producing sideband pairs at $nf_c \pm f_m$ for odd $n$ only.

**(ii) Spectrum Sketch (3 marks):**
For $f_c = 1$ MHz and $f_m = 5$ kHz, the output spectrum contains sideband pairs at:

| **Harmonic ($n$)** | **Center Frequency** | **Sidebands** | **Relative Amplitude** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 MHz | 0.995 MHz, 1.005 MHz | $4/\pi \approx 1.273$ |
| 3 | 3 MHz | 2.995 MHz, 3.005 MHz | $4/(3\pi) \approx 0.424$ |
| 5 | 5 MHz | 4.995 MHz, 5.005 MHz | $4/(5\pi) \approx 0.255$ |

**ASCII Spectrum Sketch:**

```
Amplitude
   |
1.27|         X       X                              (1 MHz ± 5 kHz)
   |
0.42|                        X         X              (3 MHz ± 5 kHz)
   |
0.25|                                    X     X      (5 MHz ± 5 kHz)
   |
   +--------------------------------------------> f (Hz)
       0.995  1.005    2.995  3.005    4.995  5.005 (MHz)
```

**(iii) Role of the Bandpass Filter (2 marks):**
The BPF is centered at $f_c = 1$ MHz with bandwidth $2W = 10$ kHz. It **passes only the $n=1$ sideband pair** (at 0.995 and 1.005 MHz) and **rejects** all higher-order sideband pairs at $3f_c \pm f_m$, $5f_c \pm f_m$, etc., yielding a **clean DSB-SC signal** at the output. The filter's roll-off must be sharp enough to reject the $3f_c \pm f_m$ component which lies at 3 MHz — well separated, so a simple LC BPF is sufficient.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of $\frac{1}{2}$** in the product-to-sum expansion — students write $s(t) = A_c A_m[\cos(f_c-f_m) + \cos(f_c+f_m)]$ instead of $A_c A_m / 2 [\cdots]$. This costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Not explicitly stating "the carrier is suppressed"** in the conclusion. KTU examiners award the final synthesis mark only for the suppression statement.
> 3. **Confusing DSB-SC with SSB** — DSB-SC has BOTH sidebands; SSB has only ONE. Students often write "single sideband" in DSB-SC answers.
> 4. **Omitting units** in numerical problems (W, V, Ω). KTU strictly deducts for missing units.
> 5. **Power calculation error** — many students compute $P = A^2 / R$ (incorrect) instead of $A^2 / (2R)$ for sinusoids.
> 6. **Diode ring analysis pitfall** — students forget the Fourier expansion of the square wave and just write a single cosine. Always state the switching function $k(t)$ and its Fourier series.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Balanced Modulator** = product of message and carrier with **carrier suppressed** → yields **DSB-SC**.
- **Output:** $s(t) = A_c m(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t)$.
- **Single-tone DSB-SC:** $s(t) = \frac{A_c A_m}{2}[\cos 2\pi(f_c - f_m)t + \cos 2\pi(f_c + f_m)t]$.
- **Power efficiency of DSB-SC = 100%** (vs. 33.3% max for conventional AM at $\mu = 1$).
- **Bandwidth** of DSB-SC = $2W$ (same as AM).
- **Demodulation** requires a **synchronous (coherent) detector** — envelope detector fails because the envelope of DSB-SC is $\vert m(t) \vert$, not $m(t)$.
- **Three main types:** Diode Ring (switching), BJT/FET Pair (differential), IC Gilbert Cell (e.g., MC1496).
- **Diode ring output (unfiltered)** contains sideband pairs at **odd harmonics** of $f_c$: $f_c \pm f_m$, $3f_c \pm f_m$, $5f_c \pm f_m$, …
- **BPF centered at $f_c$** removes higher harmonics to give clean DSB-SC.
- **Square-wave Fourier series** is essential: $k(t) = \frac{4}{\pi}\sum_{n=1,3,5,\dots} \frac{(-1)^{(n-1)/2}}{n}\cos(2\pi n f_c t)$.
- **Carrier suppression ratio (CSR)** of a good IC balanced modulator: 40–60 dB.
- **Applications:** SSB generation (with filter/phasing method), QAM modems, color TV demodulators, coherent detectors, spectrum analyzers.
- **Key trigonometric identity to memorize:** $\cos A \cos B = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)]$.
- **KTU 2024 weightage:** This topic is a **favourite 14-mark question** in Module 1 of PEEET864 — expect either a diode ring derivation or a numerical on power/spectrum.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: AM Demodulators - Envelope Detector
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET864 - ELECTRONIC COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** AM Demodulators - Envelope Detector

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Envelope Detector** (also known as a **Diode Detector** or **Peak Detector**) is the simplest and most widely used AM demodulation circuit, consisting of a **half-wave rectifier** (diode) followed by a **first-order low-pass RC filter**. It recovers the original baseband (modulating) signal by tracing the instantaneous peak amplitude (envelope) of the incoming Amplitude Modulated (AM) wave, making it a non-coherent, asynchronous detector.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The envelope detector operates on the principle that the envelope of an AM wave IS the modulating signal (provided modulation index $m_a \le 1$). It is the de-facto demodulator in commercial AM broadcast receivers due to its simplicity, low cost, and lack of requirement for carrier synchronization.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are **measuring the height of ocean waves** rolling onto a beach using a **floating ball connected to a marker on a vertical pole**:
- As a wave rises, the ball floats up and the marker goes up.
- As the wave dips, the ball (and marker) gently slide down due to gravity — but **slowly**, because of friction.
- The marker essentially traces a smooth line along the **upper outline (envelope)** of the wavy water surface.
- That smooth line represents the **original message** (the tide or wave pattern), not the high-frequency oscillation of individual waves.

In an envelope detector:
- The **diode** acts like the ball → it only lets the AM signal "rise" (conducts on positive peaks).
- The **capacitor** stores charge quickly (like the ball floating up).
- The **resistor** lets the capacitor discharge slowly (like friction/gravity), holding the voltage near the peak until the next positive cycle recharges it.

### 1.3 Key Physical Quantities & Standard Metrics

The following constants and parameters govern envelope detector operation:

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value / Unit |
|--------|----------|----------------------|
| $f_c$ | Carrier frequency | **535 kHz – 1605 kHz** (AM broadcast) |
| $f_m$ | Modulating frequency | **30 Hz – 15 kHz** (audio) |
| $m_a$ | Modulation index | **0 ≤ $m_a$ ≤ 1** (no overmodulation) |
| $R$ | Detector load resistor | **10 kΩ – 100 kΩ** |
| $C$ | Filter capacitor | **0.001 µF – 0.01 µF** |
| $V_D$ | Diode forward drop | **≈ 0.3 V (Ge)** or **≈ 0.7 V (Si)** |
| $\tau = RC$ | Time constant | Must satisfy: $\frac{1}{\omega_c} \ll RC \ll \frac{1}{\omega_m}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **In practice**, **germanium diodes** are preferred over silicon diodes in envelope detectors because their lower forward voltage drop ($V_D \approx 0.3$ V vs $0.7$ V) minimizes signal loss and distortion for small AM signals (e.g., in radio receivers).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Circuit Topology & Component Roles

A standard envelope detector consists of three core elements:

1. **Diode (D)** — typically a germanium point-contact diode (e.g., 1N34A / OA90). It rectifies the AM wave, allowing only positive half-cycles to pass.
2. **Capacitor (C)** — charges rapidly to the peak of each positive half-cycle of the carrier.
3. **Load Resistor (R)** — provides a discharge path for the capacitor when the diode is OFF.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

**Step 1: Diode ON (Positive half-cycle of carrier envelope)**
- When the incoming AM voltage $v_{in}(t)$ exceeds the capacitor voltage $v_o(t)$ by the diode drop $V_D$, the diode is forward-biased and conducts.
- The capacitor charges **almost instantaneously** (limited only by diode forward resistance $r_d$) toward the peak of the AM waveform.

**Step 2: Diode OFF (After the carrier peak)**
- Once $v_{in}(t)$ falls below $v_o(t) + V_D$, the diode becomes reverse-biased and is cut off.
- The capacitor then discharges **slowly** through resistor R, with time constant $\tau = RC$.
- This discharge occurs over many carrier cycles but should NOT extend over many modulating cycles.

**Step 3: Recharge**
- On the next positive half-cycle of the carrier, when $v_{in}(t)$ again exceeds the capacitor voltage, the diode turns ON and quickly tops up the capacitor voltage to the new peak.

**Step 4: Resulting Output**
- The capacitor voltage $v_o(t)$ approximates the **upper envelope** of the AM signal, which is the modulating waveform.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---------|---------------|
| 1 | $v_{AM}(t) = V_c \left[1 + m_a \cos(\omega_m t)\right] \cos(\omega_c t)$ | Standard AM signal expression |
| 2 | $v_{env}(t) = V_c \left[1 + m_a \cos(\omega_m t)\right]$ | Ideal envelope (for $0 \le m_a \le 1$) |
| 3 | $\tau = RC$ | Detector time constant |
| 4 | $\frac{1}{\omega_c} \ll RC \ll \frac{1}{\omega_m}$ | Design constraint (carrier vs. modulating freq.) |
| 5 | $f_c \gg \frac{1}{2\pi RC} \gg f_m$ | Frequency-domain form of the design rule |
| 6 | $v_{ripple,pp} \approx \frac{V_c \cdot m_a}{f_c \cdot RC}$ | Approximate peak-to-peak ripple voltage |
| 7 | $V_{DC,output} = V_c$ | Average DC output level |
| 8 | $v_{o,ac}(t) = V_c \cdot m_a \cos(\omega_m t)$ | Recovered AC modulating signal |
| 9 | $V_{min,envelope} = V_c (1 - m_a)$ | Minimum envelope voltage |
| 10 | Diagonal clipping: occurs if $RC \cdot \frac{d v_{env}}{dt} > V_c m_a \omega_m$ | Condition for diagonal clipping onset |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:** Always replace absolute value bars $\vert x \vert$ with $\left\vert x \right\vert$ in your answer scripts to avoid markdown rendering issues. Use $\omega = 2\pi f$ for all angular frequency conversions.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Envelope detectors are the **backbone of commercial AM radio receivers** — every cheap pocket radio, car radio, and simple crystal radio set uses one. Their applications extend to:

- **AM broadcast demodulation** (MW/SW bands)
- **Signal-strength indicators (RSSI)** in superheterodyne receivers
- **Peak detection in instrumentation** (e.g., audio level meters)
- **AC voltmeters and signal tracers**
- **Simple ASK (Amplitude Shift Keying) demodulation** in low-cost RF tags
- **Optical communication receivers** (when signal-to-noise ratio is high)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why so popular?** Envelope detectors require **no local oscillator, no phase-locked loop, and no carrier synchronization**. They are passive (or nearly so) and work directly off the antenna — making them ideal for low-power, low-cost applications.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Recovered Output Voltage

Let the AM input signal be:

$$v_{AM}(t) = V_c \left[1 + m_a \cos(\omega_m t)\right] \cos(\omega_c t)$$

The **upper envelope** is defined as the positive peak trajectory of the carrier:

$$v_{env}(t) = V_c \left[1 + m_a \cos(\omega_m t)\right]$$

During diode conduction, the capacitor charges to nearly the peak value (minus diode drop $V_D$):

$$v_o(t) \approx v_{env}(t) - V_D$$

After blocking the DC component using a coupling capacitor (typical in cascaded receiver stages), the recovered AC modulating signal is:

$$v_{o,ac}(t) = V_c \cdot m_a \cos(\omega_m t)$$

This is exactly the original message signal (scaled by $V_c$), confirming successful demodulation.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Ripple Voltage (Quantitative)

During diode OFF intervals, the capacitor discharges exponentially. If the discharge interval spans a small fraction $\Delta t$ of the carrier period $T_c$, the capacitor voltage drops by:

$$\Delta V = \frac{V_c \cdot m_a \cdot \Delta t}{RC}$$

The maximum discharge occurs when the input is at its negative peak of the modulating envelope. The peak-to-peak ripple over one carrier period $T_c = 1/f_c$ is approximately:

$$v_{ripple,pp} \approx \frac{V_c \cdot m_a}{f_c \cdot RC}$$

A more rigorous derivation considers the discharge from the peak to the next conduction point. Starting from the envelope value $V_c[1 + m_a \cos(\omega_m t)]$, the capacitor voltage during discharge is:

$$\begin{aligned}
v_C(t) &= v_{env}(t_0) \cdot e^{-(t - t_0)/RC} \\
&\approx v_{env}(t_0) \left[1 - \frac{(t - t_0)}{RC}\right] \quad \text{(for small } (t-t_0)/RC \text{)}
\end{aligned}$$

The voltage drop over one carrier period is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta V &= v_{env}(t_0) - v_{env}(t_0) \cdot e^{-T_c/RC} \\
&\approx \frac{V_c[1 + m_a \cos(\omega_m t_0)] \cdot T_c}{RC} \\
&= \frac{V_c[1 + m_a \cos(\omega_m t_0)]}{f_c \cdot RC}
\end{aligned}$$

The **worst-case ripple** (at maximum envelope) is therefore:

$$v_{ripple,pp,max} \approx \frac{V_c(1 + m_a)}{f_c \cdot RC}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Condition to Avoid Diagonal Clipping

Diagonal clipping occurs when the discharge slope of the RC circuit is **steeper than the descending slope of the envelope**. The envelope slope is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{d v_{env}}{dt} &= -V_c \cdot m_a \cdot \omega_m \sin(\omega_m t)
\end{aligned}$$

The maximum negative slope magnitude occurs at $\sin(\omega_m t) = 1$:

$$\left\vert \frac{d v_{env}}{dt} \right\vert_{max} = V_c \cdot m_a \cdot \omega_m$$

The capacitor discharge slope magnitude (immediately after a peak) is:

$$\left\vert \frac{d v_C}{dt} \right\vert = \frac{v_C}{RC}$$

For no diagonal clipping, we require:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{v_C}{RC} &\le \left\vert \frac{d v_{env}}{dt} \right\vert_{max} \\
\frac{V_c(1 - m_a)}{RC} &\le V_c \cdot m_a \cdot \omega_m \\
RC &\ge \frac{1 - m_a}{m_a \cdot \omega_m}
\end{aligned}$$

This gives the **lower bound** on $RC$:

$$RC \ge \frac{1 - m_a}{m_a \cdot \omega_m}$$

Combined with the upper bound from the ripple criterion ($RC \ll 1/\omega_m$), the **complete design inequality** becomes:

$$\frac{1 - m_a}{m_a \cdot \omega_m} \le RC \ll \frac{1}{\omega_m}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Detector Time Constant Effects

| Condition | Effect on Output | Practical Consequence |
|-----------|------------------|----------------------|
| $RC \ll 1/\omega_c$ | Capacitor discharges within one carrier cycle | Output is **rectified carrier**, no demodulation |
| $1/\omega_c \ll RC \ll 1/\omega_m$ | **Ideal operation** | Clean envelope recovery, low ripple |
| $RC \approx 1/\omega_m$ | Capacitor cannot follow envelope variations | **Diagonal clipping** (positive peak distortion) |
| $RC \gg 1/\omega_m$ | Capacitor holds voltage too long | Output "flattens" — severe distortion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** In numerical problems, ALWAYS check both the **upper bound** ($RC \ll 1/\omega_m$) and **lower bound** ($RC \ge (1-m_a)/(m_a \omega_m)$) of the time constant. Marks are often lost by students who verify only one condition.

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Style)

**Problem:** An AM signal has $V_c = 10$ V, $f_c = 1$ MHz, $f_m = 5$ kHz, $m_a = 0.8$. An envelope detector uses $R = 10$ kΩ and $C = 0.005$ µF. Verify whether the detector works without distortion.

**Solution:**

Compute time constant:

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau = RC &= 10 \times 10^3 \times 0.005 \times 10^{-6} \\
&= 5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s} = 50 \; \mu s
\end{aligned}$$

Check upper bound:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{\omega_m} &= \frac{1}{2\pi \times 5000} \\
&= 3.18 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s} = 31.8 \; \mu s
\end{aligned}$$

Here $\tau = 50 \; \mu s$ is **NOT** $\ll 31.8 \; \mu s$. The upper bound is **violated**, indicating potential **diagonal clipping**.

Check lower bound:

$$\begin{aligned}
RC_{min} &= \frac{1 - m_a}{m_a \cdot \omega_m} \\
&= \frac{1 - 0.8}{0.8 \times 2\pi \times 5000} \\
&= \frac{0.2}{25132.7} \\
&= 7.96 \; \mu s
\end{aligned}$$

Since $\tau = 50 \; \mu s > 7.96 \; \mu s$, the lower bound is satisfied.

**Conclusion:** The detector will suffer from **diagonal clipping**. To fix, reduce $C$ to about $0.002$ µF, giving $\tau = 20 \; \mu s$, which satisfies both bounds.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Envelope Detector — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[AM Signal Input] --> B[Diode Rectifier]
    B --> C[Capacitor Filter]
    C --> D[Load Resistor R]
    D --> E[Envelope Output]
    D --> F[DC Blocking Capacitor]
    F --> G[Recovered Modulating Signal]

    H[Time Constant Constraint] --> C
    I[Diode Drop Compensation] --> B
    J[Modulation Index Limit] --> A
```

### 4.2 Detailed Operational State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    state1: AM Input Rising
    state2: Diode ON Capacitor Charging
    state3: AM Input Falling Below Peak
    state4: Diode OFF Capacitor Discharging through R
    state5: Next Carrier Peak Reached

    state1 --> state2 : Vin exceeds Vc plus Vd
    state2 --> state3 : Vin reaches local maximum
    state3 --> state4 : Vin drops below Vc plus Vd
    state4 --> state5 : New carrier cycle peak
    state5 --> state2 : Diode turns ON again
```

### 4.3 Functional Signal Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT[AM Signal Stage]
        A1[Carrier Vc cos wc t]
        A2[Modulating Signal 1 plus ma cos wm t]
        A3[Product yields AM Wave]
    end

    subgraph DETECT[Envelope Detector]
        B1[Diode Half Wave Rectifier]
        B2[RC Low Pass Filter]
        B3[Diode Forward Drop Vd]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Stage]
        C1[DC Component Vc]
        C2[AC Component Vc ma cos wm t]
        C3[High Frequency Ripple]
    end

    A1 --> A3
    A2 --> A3
    A3 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B3 --> B1
    B2 --> C1
    B2 --> C2
    B2 --> C3
```

### 4.4 Distortion Analysis Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Envelope Detector Operation] --> B{Is RC time constant correct?}
    B -->|Too Small| C[Excessive Ripple on Output]
    B -->|Too Large| D[Diagonal Clipping Distortion]
    B -->|Within Bounds| E[Clean Envelope Recovery]

    D --> F[Negative Peak Affected]
    D --> G[Slope of Discharge exceeds Envelope Slope]

    C --> H[Ripple Frequency equals Carrier Frequency]

    E --> I[Modulating Signal Faithfully Recovered]

    A --> J{Is ma within limits?}
    J -->|ma greater than 1| K[Envelope Distortion at Source]
    J -->|ma less than or equal to 1| E
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define an envelope detector. State the condition on the time constant RC for proper operation.*

**Model Answer:**
An envelope detector is a simple AM demodulator circuit consisting of a diode followed by a parallel RC filter. It recovers the modulating signal by tracing the peak amplitude (envelope) of the AM wave.

For proper operation, the time constant must satisfy:

$$\frac{1}{\omega_c} \ll RC \ll \frac{1}{\omega_m}$$

where $\omega_c$ is the carrier angular frequency and $\omega_m$ is the modulating angular frequency. The lower limit ensures the capacitor does not discharge between carrier cycles, and the upper limit ensures the capacitor can follow the envelope variations.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
*List two advantages and two disadvantages of the envelope detector.*

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages:**
1. Simple, cheap, and requires no local oscillator or carrier synchronization.
2. Works well for strong AM signals with low distortion; ideal for commercial AM broadcast receivers.

**Disadvantages:**
1. Severe distortion for weak signals (diode drop becomes significant relative to signal).
2. Not suitable for SSB or DSB-SC signals (which lack a carrier envelope) — requires coherent detection instead.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain the operation of an envelope detector with a neat circuit diagram. Derive the conditions on the time constant RC for distortionless demodulation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An AM signal with carrier frequency $f_c = 455$ kHz and modulating signal $f_m = 5$ kHz is demodulated by an envelope detector with $R = 4.7$ kΩ and $C = 0.01$ µF. Calculate the maximum permissible modulation index to avoid diagonal clipping. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

The envelope detector consists of a diode D in series with a parallel RC combination across the load.

**Operation:**
- During the positive half-cycle of the carrier, when $v_{in} > v_C + V_D$, the diode conducts, and the capacitor charges rapidly to the peak value.
- After the peak, when $v_{in}$ falls below $v_C + V_D$, the diode is OFF, and the capacitor discharges slowly through R.
- This charging and discharging over many carrier cycles results in a capacitor voltage that closely follows the envelope of the AM signal.

**Derivation of conditions:**

For the capacitor to NOT discharge significantly between carrier cycles:

$$RC \gg \frac{1}{f_c} \quad \Rightarrow \quad RC \gg \frac{1}{\omega_c} \tag{1}$$

For the capacitor to follow the envelope variation (no diagonal clipping):

$$RC \ll \frac{1}{f_m} \quad \Rightarrow \quad RC \ll \frac{1}{\omega_m} \tag{2}$$

Combining both:

$$\frac{1}{\omega_c} \ll RC \ll \frac{1}{\omega_m}$$

**Model Solution to (b):**

To avoid diagonal clipping, the condition is:

$$RC \ge \frac{1 - m_a}{m_a \cdot \omega_m}$$

Compute RC:

$$\begin{aligned}
RC &= 4.7 \times 10^3 \times 0.01 \times 10^{-6} \\
&= 4.7 \times 10^{-8} \text{ s}
\end{aligned}$$

Compute $\omega_m$:

$$\omega_m = 2\pi \times 5000 = 31415.9 \text{ rad/s}$$

Substitute:

$$\begin{aligned}
4.7 \times 10^{-8} &\ge \frac{1 - m_a}{m_a \times 31415.9} \\
4.7 \times 10^{-8} \times m_a \times 31415.9 &\ge 1 - m_a \\
1.4765 \times 10^{-3} \cdot m_a &\ge 1 - m_a \\
m_a (1 + 1.4765 \times 10^{-3}) &\ge 1 \\
m_a &\ge \frac{1}{1.0014765} \\
m_a &\ge 0.9985
\end{aligned}$$

**Answer:** $m_{a, max} \approx 0.9985$ (just under unity)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a)** With the help of input and output waveforms, explain the working of an envelope detector. Mention the cause and effect of diagonal clipping. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An envelope detector is used to demodulate an AM wave with $V_c = 20$ V, $m_a = 0.6$, $f_c = 800$ kHz. The detector load is $R = 10$ kΩ. Calculate the maximum value of C such that diagonal clipping does not occur for $f_m = 5$ kHz. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Working Principle:**

The AM signal is applied to a series diode with a parallel RC circuit. During each positive half-cycle of the carrier, the diode conducts and charges the capacitor to nearly the peak voltage. Between positive peaks, the capacitor discharges through R, holding its voltage close to the envelope.

**Waveforms:**

- Input: AM wave with sinusoidal envelope.
- Capacitor voltage: Sawtooth-like waveform hugging the upper envelope.
- Output (after DC blocking): Pure modulating sinusoid at $\omega_m$.

**Diagonal Clipping — Cause and Effect:**

**Cause:** When the discharge slope of the RC circuit is **steeper** than the descending slope of the AM envelope (i.e., $RC$ is too large), the capacitor cannot follow the rapid envelope descent near the negative peak of the modulating signal.

**Effect:** The output voltage "plateaus" or "clips" near the negative peak, producing **diagonal distortion** in the recovered signal — audible as a buzzing or muffled tone in audio demodulation.

**Model Solution to (b):**

For no diagonal clipping:

$$RC \le \frac{1 - m_a}{m_a \cdot \omega_m}$$

Compute $\omega_m$:

$$\omega_m = 2\pi \times 5000 = 31415.9 \text{ rad/s}$$

Substitute $R = 10$ kΩ and $m_a = 0.6$:

$$\begin{aligned}
10 \times 10^3 \times C &\le \frac{1 - 0.6}{0.6 \times 31415.9} \\
10^4 \cdot C &\le \frac{0.4}{18849.6} \\
10^4 \cdot C &\le 2.122 \times 10^{-5} \\
C &\le 2.122 \times 10^{-9} \text{ F} \\
C &\le 2.122 \text{ nF}
\end{aligned}$$

**Answer:** $C_{max} \approx 2.12$ nF

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to verify the UPPER bound** of the time constant — students often only check $RC \ll 1/\omega_m$ and lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing diagonal clipping with negative peak clipping** — diagonal clipping is due to a large $RC$, while negative peak clipping is due to AC coupling in cascaded stages (different cause, different analysis).
> 3. **Using silicon diode equations in envelope detector problems** — always assume ideal diode or $V_D = 0.3$ V (germanium) unless specified.
> 4. **Mixing up $f$ and $\omega$** in numerical substitutions — remember to multiply by $2\pi$ when using $\omega_m$ in formulas.
> 5. **Omitting the units in the final answer** — KTU evaluators deduct marks for unitless numerical answers in derivations.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Envelope Detector**

- [x] An **envelope detector** = Diode + Parallel RC filter — the simplest AM demodulator.
- [x] It traces the **upper envelope** of the AM wave, which IS the modulating signal (for $m_a \le 1$).
- [x] **Design rule:** $\frac{1}{\omega_c} \ll RC \ll \frac{1}{\omega_m}$ — strict double inequality.
- [x] **Germanium diodes** are preferred (low $V_D \approx 0.3$ V) over silicon ($V_D \approx 0.7$ V).
- [x] **Diagonal clipping** occurs when $RC$ is too large — slope of discharge exceeds envelope slope.
- [x] **Condition to avoid diagonal clipping:** $RC \ge \frac{1 - m_a}{m_a \cdot \omega_m}$ (lower bound on $RC$).
- [x] **Excessive ripple** occurs when $RC$ is too small — output contains carrier frequency.
- [x] **Peak-to-peak ripple:** $V_{ripple,pp} \approx \frac{V_c \cdot m_a}{f_c \cdot RC}$.
- [x] **Recovered AC output:** $v_{o,ac}(t) = V_c \cdot m_a \cos(\omega_m t)$.
- [x] **Not suitable for SSB/DSB-SC** — these have no envelope; require synchronous detection.
- [x] **Used in:** AM broadcast radios, ASK demodulators, signal-strength meters, crystal sets.
- [x] **Negative peak clipping** is a separate phenomenon (caused by AC coupling), NOT diagonal clipping.
- [x] Always state both the **upper and lower bounds** in derivations to secure full KTU marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Frequency and Phase Modulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET864 - ELECTRONIC COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Frequency and Phase Modulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Frequency Modulation (FM) — Formal Definition

**Frequency Modulation (FM)** is a continuous-wave angle modulation technique in which the **instantaneous frequency** of the sinusoidal carrier wave is varied in direct proportion to the instantaneous amplitude of the modulating (message) signal, while the carrier amplitude is held strictly constant.

Mathematically, if the message signal is $m(t)$ and the carrier is $c(t) = A_c \cos(\omega_c t)$, the FM signal is expressed as:

$$s_{FM}(t) = A_c \cos\left( \omega_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau)\, d\tau \right)$$

where $k_f$ (Hz/V) is the **frequency sensitivity** of the modulator, a constant that determines how much the carrier frequency deviates per volt of the message signal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** FM and PM are classified under **Angle Modulation** because the information is carried in the *angle* (phase argument) of the carrier, not its amplitude. Both FM and PM are special cases of the general angle modulation equation.

### 1.2 Phase Modulation (PM) — Formal Definition

**Phase Modulation (PM)** is the angle modulation technique in which the **instantaneous phase** of the carrier is varied in direct proportion to the instantaneous amplitude of the modulating signal $m(t)$. The general PM signal is:

$$s_{PM}(t) = A_c \cos\left( \omega_c t + k_p\, m(t) \right)$$

where $k_p$ (radians/volt) is the **phase sensitivity** constant of the phase modulator.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bicycle Wheel" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** Imagine a bicycle wheel spinning at a constant rotational speed — this is the unmodulated carrier. Now, when you speak into a microphone:
>
> - In **Amplitude Modulation (AM)**, the *size* (radius) of the wheel pulsates with your voice. Loud voice = big wheel; soft voice = small wheel.
> - In **Frequency Modulation (FM)**, the *spinning speed* of the wheel changes with your voice. A loud sound makes the wheel spin faster (higher frequency); a soft sound slows it down.
> - In **Phase Modulation (PM)**, the *angular position* of a marked point on the wheel jumps forward or backward depending on your voice amplitude.
>
> Crucially, in both FM and PM, the **wheel size (amplitude) never changes** — only the rotational behaviour does. This is why FM and PM are far more noise-immune than AM: random electrical noise mostly perturbs amplitude, which FM/PM receivers simply ignore.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics in FM/PM

- **Maximum frequency deviation ($\Delta f$):** the peak excursion of the instantaneous frequency from the carrier frequency $\omega_c$ (in Hz).
- **Modulation index ($\beta$):** a dimensionless quantity, defined as $\beta = \dfrac{\Delta f}{f_m}$ for FM and $\beta_p = k_p A_m$ (in radians) for PM.
- **Carson's Rule Bandwidth ($BW$):** the practical transmission bandwidth, $BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2(\beta + 1)f_m$.
- **Frequency sensitivity $k_f$:** measured in **Hz per volt (Hz/V)**.
- **Phase sensitivity $k_p$:** measured in **radians per volt (rad/V)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 General Angle Modulation Framework

Both FM and PM emerge from one parent equation. Let the message be $m(t)$ and let the carrier be $A_c \cos(\omega_c t)$. Define the **instantaneous phase** $\theta_i(t)$ such that:

$$s(t) = A_c \cos\bigl(\theta_i(t)\bigr)$$

The **instantaneous angular frequency** is the time derivative of the phase:

$$\omega_i(t) = \frac{d\theta_i(t)}{dt}$$

This single relation links FM and PM — one integrates, the other differentiates.

### 2.2 Logical Steps — How FM Works

1. The message signal $m(t)$ enters the FM modulator.
2. The modulator produces an instantaneous frequency $\omega_i(t) = \omega_c + 2\pi k_f\, m(t)$ — directly proportional to $m(t)$.
3. To obtain the phase argument, **integrate** the instantaneous frequency: $\theta_i(t) = \omega_c t + 2\pi k_f \int m(\tau)\, d\tau$.
4. Feed this $\theta_i(t)$ into the cosine carrier: $s_{FM}(t) = A_c \cos\bigl(\theta_i(t)\bigr)$.
5. The constant $k_f$ (Hz/V) sets the sensitivity: $k_f$ large = strong frequency swing per volt of $m(t)$.

### 2.3 Logical Steps — How PM Works

1. The message $m(t)$ enters the PM modulator.
2. The modulator directly shifts the phase by $k_p\, m(t)$ radians: $\theta_i(t) = \omega_c t + k_p\, m(t)$.
3. The instantaneous frequency becomes the derivative: $\omega_i(t) = \omega_c + k_p\, \dfrac{dm(t)}{dt}$.
4. Notice the key insight: **PM is FM of the derivative of the message**; equivalently, **FM is PM of the integral of the message**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Connection:** In any exam question, if a circuit block diagram shows an **integrator** followed by a **PM modulator**, the output is functionally equivalent to an **FM modulator** of the original message. Conversely, a **differentiator** feeding a **FM modulator** gives **PM**. This is one of the most frequently tested facts in KTU ESE.

### 2.4 Modulation Index in Detail

For a single-tone message $m(t) = A_m \cos(\omega_m t)$:

- **FM modulation index:** $\beta_{FM} = \dfrac{k_f A_m}{f_m} = \dfrac{\Delta f}{f_m}$ (dimensionless).
- **PM modulation index:** $\beta_{PM} = k_p A_m$ (radians, but treated as dimensionless for bandwidth).

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Distinction:** In FM, the modulation index is **inversely proportional to the message frequency $f_m$** (higher pitch → smaller $\beta$). In PM, the modulation index is **independent of $f_m$** (it depends only on $k_p$ and $A_m$). This is the deepest difference between the two systems.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|------|---------|
| 1 | FM signal | $s(t) = A_c \cos\!\left(\omega_c t + 2\pi k_f \int m(\tau)\,d\tau\right)$ | Volt | Carrier amplitude $A_c$ is **constant** |
| 2 | PM signal | $s(t) = A_c \cos\!\left(\omega_c t + k_p\, m(t)\right)$ | Volt | Phase is **directly** proportional to $m(t)$ |
| 3 | Inst. freq. (FM) | $\omega_i(t) = \omega_c + 2\pi k_f\, m(t)$ | rad/s | Linear in $m(t)$ |
| 4 | Inst. freq. (PM) | $\omega_i(t) = \omega_c + k_p\, \dfrac{dm(t)}{dt}$ | rad/s | Linear in $dm/dt$ |
| 5 | Freq. deviation (FM) | $\Delta f = k_f\, A_m$ | Hz | Peak deviation from $f_c$ |
| 6 | Freq. deviation (PM) | $\Delta f = k_p\, A_m\, f_m$ | Hz | Depends on $f_m$ also |
| 7 | FM mod. index | $\beta = \Delta f \,/\, f_m$ | dimensionless | Decreases with $f_m$ |
| 8 | PM mod. index | $\beta_p = k_p A_m$ | rad | Independent of $f_m$ |
| 9 | Carson's rule BW | $BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2(\beta+1)f_m$ | Hz | $\approx 98\%$ power rule |
| 10 | Narrowband FM BW | $BW \approx 2 f_m$ | Hz | Valid when $\beta \ll 1$ |
| 11 | Wideband FM BW | $BW \approx 2 \Delta f$ | Hz | Valid when $\beta \gg 1$ |
| 12 | PM Carson's BW | $BW = 2(\Delta\omega/\omega_m + 1)\, f_m$ | Hz | $\Delta\omega = k_p A_m \omega_m$ |

### 2.6 Real-World Utility

- **FM Broadcast (88–108 MHz):** High-fidelity radio transmission, $\Delta f = 75$ kHz, $f_m \le 15$ kHz, $\beta = 5$.
- **PM in Digital Modems:** Used as the basis for **PSK (Phase Shift Keying)** and **QPSK** in satellite and Wi-Fi communications.
- **FM in Two-Way Radios:** Police, taxi, and marine VHF use narrowband FM (NBFM, $\beta < 1$).
- **PM in Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR):** Linear FM (chirp) pulses are generated using PM techniques.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the FM Equation from First Principles

**Starting point:** Let the message be a single-tone $m(t) = A_m \cos(\omega_m t)$.

**Step 1 — Define the desired instantaneous frequency.**
In FM, the frequency must vary linearly with $m(t)$:

$$\omega_i(t) = \omega_c + 2\pi k_f\, A_m \cos(\omega_m t)$$

**Step 2 — Identify the frequency deviation term.**
The peak deviation $\Delta\omega = 2\pi k_f A_m$ occurs at the peak of $m(t)$, so $\omega_i(t) = \omega_c + \Delta\omega \cos(\omega_m t)$.

**Step 3 — Integrate to obtain the instantaneous phase.**
Since $\theta_i(t) = \int \omega_i(t)\, dt + \phi_0$ (with zero initial phase), we get:

$$\theta_i(t) = \omega_c t + \Delta\omega \int \cos(\omega_m t)\, dt = \omega_c t + \frac{\Delta\omega}{\omega_m} \sin(\omega_m t)$$

**Step 4 — Recognise the modulation index.**
Define $\beta = \dfrac{\Delta\omega}{\omega_m} = \dfrac{\Delta f}{f_m}$. Then:

$$\theta_i(t) = \omega_c t + \beta \sin(\omega_m t)$$

**Step 5 — Form the FM signal.**

$$s_{FM}(t) = A_c \cos\!\left( \omega_c t + \beta \sin(\omega_m t) \right)$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the PM Equation from First Principles

**Starting point:** With the same message $m(t) = A_m \cos(\omega_m t)$, PM demands the phase shift to be proportional to $m(t)$:

$$\theta_i(t) = \omega_c t + k_p A_m \cos(\omega_m t) = \omega_c t + \beta_p \cos(\omega_m t)$$

**Step 1 — Form the PM signal directly.**

$$s_{PM}(t) = A_c \cos\!\left( \omega_c t + \beta_p \cos(\omega_m t) \right)$$

**Step 2 — Derive the instantaneous frequency by differentiation.**

$$\omega_i(t) = \frac{d\theta_i}{dt} = \omega_c - \beta_p \omega_m \sin(\omega_m t)$$

**Step 3 — Identify the peak frequency deviation.**

$$\Delta\omega = \beta_p\, \omega_m = k_p A_m \omega_m \quad\Rightarrow\quad \Delta f = k_p A_m f_m$$

This confirms that in PM, $\Delta f$ depends on $f_m$ — a key KTU-asked distinction.

### 3.3 Equivalence Derivation: FM ⇔ PM

Starting with an FM modulator whose input is $m(t)$:

$$s_{FM}(t) = A_c \cos\!\left( \omega_c t + 2\pi k_f \int m(\tau)\, d\tau \right)$$

Compare with a PM modulator whose input is $m_1(t) = k \int m(\tau)\, d\tau$:

$$s_{PM}(t) = A_c \cos\!\left( \omega_c t + k_p\, m_1(t) \right) = A_c \cos\!\left( \omega_c t + k_p\, k \int m(\tau)\, d\tau \right)$$

If we set $k_p k = 2\pi k_f$, both signals are identical. Hence:

$$\boxed{\text{PM modulator preceded by an integrator} \equiv \text{FM modulator}}$$

By symmetric reasoning, an **FM modulator preceded by a differentiator** is equivalent to a **PM modulator**.

### 3.4 Derivation of Carson's Rule Bandwidth

The FM signal is **not** bandlimited — theoretically it has infinite sidebands. We need a practical 98%-power bandwidth rule.

**Step 1 — Bessel-function expansion of FM.**
Using the identity $\cos(\beta \sin\omega_m t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n(\beta) \cos(n\omega_m t)$ and $\sin(\beta \sin\omega_m t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n(\beta) \sin(n\omega_m t)$, the FM spectrum contains components at $\omega_c \pm n\omega_m$ with amplitudes $J_n(\beta)$.

**Step 2 — Identify significant sidebands.**
For a modulation index $\beta$, sidebands with order $n > \beta + 1$ carry negligible power ($J_n(\beta) < 0.04$ for $n > \beta + 1$).

**Step 3 — Compute the total bandwidth.**
The highest significant sideband is at $f_c + (\beta + 1)f_m$. The lowest significant sideband is at $f_c - (\beta + 1)f_m$. Therefore:

$$BW = 2(\beta + 1) f_m$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $\beta = \Delta f / f_m$.**

$$BW = 2\!\left(\frac{\Delta f}{f_m} + 1\right) f_m = 2(\Delta f + f_m)$$

This is **Carson's rule**. It captures approximately **98 % of the total signal power** — the universal KTU-approved engineering rule of thumb.

### 3.5 Narrowband vs. Wideband FM Limit

**Case 1: $\beta \ll 1$ (Narrowband FM).**
Carson's rule gives $BW \approx 2 f_m$ — same as AM. NBFM is essentially AM with a phase shift, and the two spectra look almost identical.

**Case 2: $\beta \gg 1$ (Wideband FM).**
Carson's rule gives $BW \approx 2 \beta f_m = 2 \Delta f$. The bandwidth is set by the deviation, not the message bandwidth.

### 3.6 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A 10 kHz message with peak amplitude 4 V frequency-modulates a 100 MHz carrier. Given $k_f = 15$ kHz/V, find $\Delta f$, $\beta$, and Carson's bandwidth.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Frequency deviation.**
$\Delta f = k_f A_m = 15 \text{ kHz/V} \times 4 \text{ V} = 60 \text{ kHz}$.

**Step 2 — Modulation index.**
$\beta = \dfrac{\Delta f}{f_m} = \dfrac{60 \text{ kHz}}{10 \text{ kHz}} = 6$.

**Step 3 — Carson's rule bandwidth.**
$BW = 2(\beta + 1) f_m = 2 \times 7 \times 10 \text{ kHz} = 140 \text{ kHz}$.

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**
Since $\beta = 6 \gg 1$, this is **wideband FM** (WBFM), typical of high-fidelity broadcast. The signal occupies 140 kHz around 100 MHz.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Indirect FM Generation (Armstrong Method)

The Armstrong method is the most tested FM generation technique in KTU examinations. It uses a **double-balanced mixer + multiplier chain** to convert a narrowband FM signal into wideband FM.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Message m of t] --> B[Integrator]
    B --> C[NBPM Modulator]
    D[Crystal Oscillator f1] --> C
    C --> E[Mixed with f2]
    F[Oscillator f2] --> E
    E --> G[BPF at difference freq]
    G --> H[Frequency Multiplier x n1]
    H --> I[Mixed with f3]
    J[Oscillator f3] --> I
    I --> K[BPF at sum freq]
    K --> L[Frequency Multiplier x n2]
    L --> M[WBFM Output at fc]
```

**Explanation of the Armstrong flow:**
- An **integrator** converts the message $m(t)$ into a form suitable for a phase modulator, making the chain behave as an **FM generator**.
- A **crystal oscillator** provides the high frequency stability required for broadcast.
- A **frequency multiplier** ($\times\, n_1 \times n_2$) raises both the carrier frequency and the deviation simultaneously, transforming NBFM into WBFM.
- A **final mixer** shifts the spectrum to the desired carrier frequency $f_c$.

### 4.2 Block Diagram — FM Demodulator (Frequency Discriminator)

The Foster–Seeley and Ratio detectors are classic FM demodulators. The block logic is shown below.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[FM Input s of t] --> B[Limiter]
    B --> C[Bandpass Filter]
    C --> D[Frequency Discriminator]
    D --> E[Envelope Detector]
    E --> F[Lowpass Filter]
    F --> G[Recovered Message m of t]
    D --> H[AFC Feedback]
    H --> B
```

**Explanation of the demodulator flow:**
- The **limiter** removes any residual amplitude noise — a critical noise-immunity advantage of FM over AM.
- The **frequency discriminator** converts frequency variation $\Delta f$ into amplitude variation (slope detector action).
- The **envelope detector** then recovers the message envelope.
- **Automatic Frequency Control (AFC)** feedback corrects slow carrier drift.

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Functional Equivalence of FM and PM

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Message m of t] --> B[Integrator]
    B --> C[PM Modulator]
    C --> D[Equivalent FM Output]
    E[Message m of t] --> F[FM Modulator]
    F --> G[Equivalent PM Output]
    H[Message m of t] --> I[Differentiatort]
    I --> J[FM Modulator]
    J --> K[Equivalent PM Output]
```

This functional map illustrates the three fundamental equivalences tested in KTU:

1. **Integrator → PM modulator = FM modulator**
2. **Differentiator → FM modulator = PM modulator**
3. These identities allow designers to build whichever modulator (FM or PM) is cheaper, then synthesize the other by adding a passive RC integrator or differentiator at the input.

### 4.4 Spectrum Comparison Diagram (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A[AM Spectrum]
        A1[Carrier at fc] --- A2[USB at fc plus fm] --- A3[LSB at fc minus fm]
    end
    subgraph B[NBFM Spectrum with small beta]
        B1[Carrier at fc] --- B2[USB at fc plus fm] --- B3[LSB at fc minus fm]
    end
    subgraph C[WBFM Spectrum with large beta]
        C1[Carrier at fc] --- C2[Sidebands up to fc plus beta fm] --- C3[Sidebands down to fc minus beta fm]
    end
```

**Reading guide:**
- **AM** has only 2 sidebands.
- **NBFM ($\beta \ll 1$)** has essentially 2 sidebands — looks like AM, but the carrier phase differs.
- **WBFM ($\beta \gg 1$)** spreads into a wide forest of sidebands, as predicted by Carson's rule.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Frequency Modulation and Phase Modulation. Show that FM and PM are special cases of angle modulation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition of FM:** Frequency Modulation is a type of angle modulation in which the instantaneous frequency of the carrier is varied in proportion to the amplitude of the message signal $m(t)$, while the carrier amplitude remains constant. The FM signal is given by:

$$s_{FM}(t) = A_c \cos\!\left( \omega_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau)\, d\tau \right)$$

**Definition of PM:** Phase Modulation is a type of angle modulation in which the instantaneous phase of the carrier is varied in proportion to the amplitude of the message signal $m(t)$. The PM signal is given by:

$$s_{PM}(t) = A_c \cos\!\left( \omega_c t + k_p\, m(t) \right)$$

**Proof of special-case status:** The general angle modulation equation is $s(t) = A_c \cos(\theta_i(t))$ where $\theta_i(t) = \omega_c t + \phi(t)$. Setting $\phi(t) = k_p m(t)$ recovers PM, and setting $\phi(t) = 2\pi k_f \int m(\tau) d\tau$ recovers FM. Hence both are special cases of the angle modulation framework. *(1 mark for the unified form, 1 mark each for FM and PM expressions.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is Carson's rule? For an FM system with $\Delta f = 30$ kHz and $f_m = 5$ kHz, compute the transmission bandwidth.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Carson's rule gives the transmission bandwidth of an FM (or PM) signal that contains approximately 98 % of the total signal power. The rule is:

$$BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2(\beta + 1) f_m$$

**Calculation (2 marks):**
- Given: $\Delta f = 30$ kHz, $f_m = 5$ kHz.
- Modulation index: $\beta = \Delta f / f_m = 30 / 5 = 6$.
- Bandwidth: $BW = 2 \times (30 + 5) = 70$ kHz.

**Conclusion:** The FM signal requires a transmission bandwidth of **70 kHz**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – July 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of necessary mathematical expressions, explain the generation of an FM signal using the **Armstrong indirect method**. Show how NBFM is converted to WBFM using frequency multipliers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sinusoidal message $m(t) = 5\cos(2\pi \times 500\,t)$ is used to frequency modulate a 25 MHz carrier. The frequency sensitivity is $k_f = 20$ kHz/V. Determine:
  (i) Frequency deviation $\Delta f$
  (ii) Modulation index $\beta$
  (iii) Carson's rule bandwidth
  (iv) Average transmitted power if $A_c = 10$ V across a 50 Ω load. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Armstrong Indirect FM Generation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Motivation (1 mark).** Direct FM generation (using a VCO) suffers from poor carrier stability because the carrier frequency drifts with temperature and component ageing. The Armstrong method bypasses this by using a **highly stable crystal oscillator** to derive the carrier, then synthesising wideband FM from a narrowband phase modulator through frequency multiplication and mixing.

**Step 2 — Block flow (2 marks).** The standard Armstrong chain is:
1. **Integrate** the message $m(t)$ to produce $m_1(t) = \int m(\tau) d\tau$.
2. Apply $m_1(t)$ to a **narrowband phase modulator (NBPM)** driven by a crystal oscillator at $f_1$ (typically a few hundred kHz). The output is an **NBFM** signal centred at $f_1$.
3. **Mix** this NBFM with a local oscillator at $f_2$ to shift the spectrum; the difference frequency is selected by a **bandpass filter**.
4. Pass the result through a **frequency multiplier** of ratio $n_1$ to raise both the carrier and the deviation.
5. **Mix** again with an oscillator at $f_3$ and select the sum frequency by a second BPF.
6. Pass through a final multiplier of ratio $n_2$ to reach the final carrier $f_c$ and deviation $\Delta f$.

**Step 3 — Frequency relations (2 marks).** If the NBPM deviation is $\Delta f_1$ at carrier $f_1$, after multiplier $n_1$ the deviation becomes $n_1 \Delta f_1$ and the carrier becomes $n_1 f_1$. After the second mixer (sum selected), the deviation is unchanged (mixing is linear) and the carrier becomes $n_1 f_1 + f_3$. After the final multiplier $n_2$:

$$f_c = n_2(n_1 f_1 + f_3), \qquad \Delta f = n_1 n_2 \Delta f_1$$

**Step 4 — Deviation amplification (2 marks).** A single VCO would struggle to produce $\Delta f = 75$ kHz at 100 MHz with stability better than $\pm 2$ kHz. The Armstrong chain uses a small initial deviation (say $\Delta f_1 = 25$ Hz) and amplifies it by $n_1 n_2 = 75{,}000 / 25 = 3000$ via the multipliers, while the crystal oscillator guarantees long-term stability. This is why the Armstrong method is the universal choice for commercial FM broadcast transmitters.

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:** $m(t) = 5\cos(2\pi \times 500\,t)$ V, $f_c = 25$ MHz, $k_f = 20$ kHz/V.

**Step 1 (1.5 marks) — Identify message parameters.** $A_m = 5$ V, $f_m = 500$ Hz.

**Step 2 (1.5 marks) — Compute frequency deviation.**
$\Delta f = k_f \cdot A_m = 20 \text{ kHz/V} \times 5 \text{ V} = 100$ kHz.

**Step 3 (1.5 marks) — Compute modulation index.**
$\beta = \dfrac{\Delta f}{f_m} = \dfrac{100{,}000}{500} = 200$.

**Step 4 (1.5 marks) — Compute Carson's bandwidth.**
$BW = 2(\beta + 1) f_m = 2 \times 201 \times 500 = 201$ kHz. Alternatively, $BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2(100{,}000 + 500) = 201$ kHz.

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Average power.**
For an FM signal the amplitude is constant ($A_c = 10$ V), so the average power is:

$$P = \frac{A_c^2}{2 R} = \frac{10^2}{2 \times 50} = 1 \text{ W}$$

**Final Answers:** $\Delta f = 100$ kHz, $\beta = 200$, $BW = 201$ kHz, $P = 1$ W.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Derive the relationship between the **modulation indices of FM and PM**. Prove that an **integrator followed by a PM modulator is functionally equivalent to an FM modulator**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An angle-modulated signal is described by:
$$s(t) = 10\cos\!\left(2\pi \times 10^8\, t + 5\sin(2\pi \times 10^3\, t)\right)$$
Determine whether it is FM or PM, and calculate:
  (i) Carrier frequency
  (ii) Modulation index
  (iii) Frequency deviation
  (iv) Carson's rule bandwidth. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Relationship between FM and PM (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Write the standard FM and PM equations.**

$$s_{FM}(t) = A_c \cos\!\left( \omega_c t + 2\pi k_f \int m(\tau)\, d\tau \right)$$

$$s_{PM}(t) = A_c \cos\!\left( \omega_c t + k_p\, m(t) \right)$$

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Equivalence construction.**
Consider a PM modulator with an integrator preceding it. The integrator output is:

$$m_I(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau)\, d\tau$$

The PM modulator then produces:

$$s(t) = A_c \cos\!\left( \omega_c t + k_p\, m_I(t) \right) = A_c \cos\!\left( \omega_c t + k_p \int m(\tau)\, d\tau \right)$$

Setting $k_p = 2\pi k_f$, this is identical to the FM equation. Hence **integrator + PM = FM**.

**Step 3 (1.5 marks) — Equivalence construction for FM → PM.**
By symmetric reasoning, a differentiator placed before an FM modulator yields PM. Mathematically:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\!\left[2\pi k_f \int m(\tau) d\tau \right] = 2\pi k_f\, m(t)$$

so the FM modulator with differentiated input becomes $A_c \cos(\omega_c t + 2\pi k_f \cdot m(t))$ — exactly the PM form.

**Step 4 (1.5 marks) — Modulation-index relationship.**
Let $m(t) = A_m \cos(\omega_m t)$.

- FM index: $\beta_{FM} = \dfrac{k_f A_m}{f_m}$.
- PM index: $\beta_{PM} = k_p A_m$.

If the same modulator is used alternately as FM and as PM, the indices are related by:

$$\beta_{FM} = \frac{\beta_{PM}}{2\pi f_m / (2\pi)} = \frac{\beta_{PM}}{f_m} \cdot (\text{scaling constant})$$

In general, $\beta_{FM} = \dfrac{\Delta f_{PM}}{f_m}$ and $\beta_{PM} = \dfrac{\Delta f_{FM}}{f_m}$ when the same physical circuit is re-purposed.

**Part (b) — Identify FM/PM and compute parameters (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1.5 marks) — Identify the modulation type.**
The phase argument contains $\sin(2\pi \times 10^3 t)$, which is a *sinusoid of the message variable*. Recall:
- FM with $m(t) = A_m \cos(\omega_m t)$ gives phase $\beta \sin(\omega_m t)$.
- PM with the same message gives phase $\beta_p \cos(\omega_m t)$.

Since the phase term is **sinusoidal (sin)** rather than **cosine**, the signal is **FM** with message $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi \times 10^3 t)$.

**Step 2 (1.5 marks) — Carrier frequency.**
From the equation, $\omega_c = 2\pi \times 10^8$, so $f_c = 10^8$ Hz = **100 MHz**.

**Step 3 (1.5 marks) — Modulation index.**
The coefficient of $\sin(2\pi \times 10^3 t)$ is the FM modulation index: $\beta = 5$.

**Step 4 (1.5 marks) — Frequency deviation.**
$\Delta f = \beta \cdot f_m = 5 \times 1000 = 5$ kHz.

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Carson's rule bandwidth.**
$BW = 2(\beta + 1) f_m = 2 \times 6 \times 1000 = 12$ kHz.

**Final Answers:** FM, $f_c = 100$ MHz, $\beta = 5$, $\Delta f = 5$ kHz, $BW = 12$ kHz.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the integration in FM:** A very common error is writing the FM equation without the integral of $m(t)$. KTU examiners specifically deduct 1–2 marks if the integral sign is missing.
> 2. **Mixing $\Delta f$ units:** $\Delta f$ must be in **Hz**, not rad/s. Students who write $\Delta\omega = k_f A_m$ and then forget to convert to $\Delta f = \Delta\omega / 2\pi$ lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Carson's rule confusion:** Students sometimes write $BW = 2(\beta + 1) f_c$ using the *carrier* instead of $f_m$. Always use the *message* frequency.
> 4. **Identifying FM vs. PM from the equation:** If the phase term is a **sine** function of the message, it is **FM**. If it is a **cosine** function, it is **PM** (or vice versa, depending on the message convention). Examiners check this carefully.
> 5. **Power calculation in FM:** Many students incorrectly think FM power depends on $\beta$. It does not — FM is a constant-envelope signal, so $P = A_c^2 / 2R$ is always valid.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **FM varies the carrier's *frequency*** in proportion to $m(t)$; **PM varies the carrier's *phase*** in proportion to $m(t)$.
- Both FM and PM are **constant-envelope** modulations, giving superior noise immunity over AM.
- The **modulation index** of FM is $\beta = \Delta f / f_m$ and is **inversely proportional to $f_m$**; the modulation index of PM is $\beta_p = k_p A_m$ and is **independent of $f_m$**.
- **Carson's rule bandwidth:** $BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2(\beta + 1) f_m$ — the single most tested formula in KTU Module 2.
- **Functional equivalence:** Integrator + PM modulator ≡ FM modulator; Differentiator + FM modulator ≡ PM modulator.
- **Armstrong indirect method** uses a crystal oscillator + NBPM + frequency multipliers to produce stable, wide-deviation WBFM.
- **Average power of FM/PM** is independent of $\beta$ and equals $A_c^2 / 2R$.
- **Noise immunity of FM** improves with $\beta$ at the cost of bandwidth — the famous **bandwidth–SNR trade-off** quantified by Carson's rule.
- **Limiter** is a critical front-end block in any FM receiver to suppress amplitude noise.
- **Frequency discriminator** (Foster–Seeley or Ratio detector) converts frequency variation back to baseband amplitude variation for envelope detection.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Narrow and Wide Band FM and their Spectra
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET864 - ELECTRONIC COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Narrow and Wide Band FM and their Spectra

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition of Frequency Modulation (FM)

**Frequency Modulation (FM)** is an angle modulation technique in which the instantaneous frequency of the carrier signal is varied in proportion to the instantaneous amplitude of the modulating (message) signal, while the amplitude of the carrier remains constant.

The standard mathematical expression for a single-tone FM signal is:

$$s(t) = A_c \cos\left(2\pi f_c t + \beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)\right)$$

where:
- $A_c$ = Amplitude of the carrier
- $f_c$ = Carrier frequency
- $f_m$ = Modulating signal frequency
- $\beta$ = Modulation index of FM
- $k_f$ = Frequency sensitivity of the modulator (Hz/volt)
- $A_m$ = Amplitude of the modulating signal

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modulation Index ($\beta$):** The ratio of frequency deviation ($\Delta f$) to the modulating frequency ($f_m$) is called the modulation index of FM.
>
> $$\beta = \frac{\Delta f}{f_m} = \frac{k_f A_m}{2\pi f_m}$$
>
> **Frequency Deviation ($\Delta f$):** The maximum shift in the instantaneous frequency of the FM carrier from its resting (carrier) frequency.

---

### 1.2 Narrowband FM (NBFM) – Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Narrowband FM (NBFM)** is defined as the type of Frequency Modulation in which the modulation index $\beta$ is very small, typically $\beta \ll 1$ (often $\beta < 0.5$ or, in some textbooks, $\beta \leq 0.5$).
>
> In this regime, the frequency deviation $\Delta f$ is very small compared to the modulating frequency $f_m$, so the bandwidth of the FM signal is approximately equal to **twice the modulating frequency** (similar to AM).

---

### 1.3 Wideband FM (WBFM) – Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Wideband FM (WBFM)** is the type of Frequency Modulation in which the modulation index $\beta$ is large, typically $\beta \gg 1$ (often $\beta > 0.5$, and in commercial broadcasting $\beta \approx 5$).
>
> The bandwidth of WBFM is much larger than that of AM or NBFM, and is governed by **Carson's rule**.

---

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy – Plain English Intuition

**Imagine a Swing (Pendulum):**
- Think of a swing that someone is pushing rhythmically.
- The swing is like our **carrier wave** oscillating at a constant base frequency.
- Now, if the pusher gives **very small, gentle pushes** at random times, the swing's frequency changes only slightly — this is **Narrowband FM**. The overall motion looks almost like a regular swing with tiny wobbles.
- If the pusher gives **huge, energetic pushes**, the swing's frequency changes dramatically — speeding up and slowing down a lot — this is **Wideband FM**. The motion becomes much more complex and "spread out" in terms of how far it swings each second.
- The carrier **amplitude never changes** in FM (the swing's max swing-height stays the same), but the rate at which it oscillates (frequency) dances to the music of the modulating signal.

**Another Analogy – Guitar Tuning:**
- A guitar string is your carrier. If you slightly bend the string (NBFM), it produces a small change in pitch.
- If you stretch the string vigorously (WBFM), the pitch swings wildly, producing many harmonics.

---

### 1.5 Key Distinction Highlight

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **single most important parameter** that distinguishes NBFM from WBFM is the **modulation index $\beta$**:
> - NBFM : $\beta \ll 1$ (low-deviation, low-bandwidth FM)
> - WBFM : $\beta \gg 1$ (high-deviation, high-bandwidth FM, as in FM radio broadcasting)
>
> Both NBFM and WBFM have **constant envelope** (constant carrier amplitude), which is a defining feature of all angle modulation systems.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation

The instantaneous frequency of an FM signal is given by:

$$f_i(t) = f_c + k_f \cdot m(t)$$

The total phase is:

$$\phi(t) = 2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau) d\tau$$

The FM signal is therefore:

$$s(t) = A_c \cos\left[2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau) d\tau\right]$$

For a single-tone message $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau) d\tau = \frac{A_m}{2\pi f_m} \sin(2\pi f_m t)$$

Thus:

$$s(t) = A_c \cos\left[2\pi f_c t + \frac{k_f A_m}{f_m} \sin(2\pi f_m t)\right] = A_c \cos\left[2\pi f_c t + \beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)\right]$$

---

### 2.2 Narrowband FM – Analytical Treatment

When $\beta \ll 1$, we use the small-angle approximations:
- $\cos(\beta \sin \omega_m t) \approx 1$
- $\sin(\beta \sin \omega_m t) \approx \beta \sin \omega_m t$

So:

$$s(t) \approx A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) - \beta A_c \sin(2\pi f_c t) \sin(2\pi f_m t)$$

This expands via trigonometric identity to:

$$s(t) \approx A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) - \frac{\beta A_c}{2}\left[\cos 2\pi(f_c - f_m)t - \cos 2\pi(f_c + f_m)t\right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The NBFM spectrum consists of a **carrier** at $f_c$ and **two sidebands** at $f_c \pm f_m$ — exactly the same structure as AM! However, the lower sideband is **inverted (180° phase-shifted)** compared to AM, which is why NBFM is sometimes called a "phase-reversed AM".

---

### 2.3 Wideband FM – Analytical Treatment

For WBFM, the small-angle approximation fails. We use the Bessel function expansion:

$$\cos\left[\beta \sin \omega_m t\right] = J_0(\beta) + 2\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} J_{2n}(\beta) \cos(2n \omega_m t)$$

$$\sin\left[\beta \sin \omega_m t\right] = 2\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} J_{2n+1}(\beta) \sin((2n+1) \omega_m t)$$

The FM signal becomes:

$$s(t) = A_c \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n(\beta) \cos\left[2\pi(f_c + n f_m)t\right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The WBFM spectrum theoretically contains an **infinite number of sidebands** at frequencies $f_c \pm n f_m$, with amplitudes governed by **Bessel functions of the first kind $J_n(\beta)$**. In practice, we only keep the sidebands whose amplitude exceeds a small threshold (e.g., 1% or 0.01 of the carrier).

---

### 2.4 Carson's Rule (Bandwidth Estimation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Carson's Rule:** The transmission bandwidth of an FM signal is:
>
> $$BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2 f_m (\beta + 1)$$
>
> This rule includes approximately **98% of the total signal power** and is universally used in KTU examinations.

For **NBFM** ($\beta \ll 1$): $BW \approx 2 f_m$
For **WBFM** ($\beta \gg 1$): $BW \approx 2 \Delta f = 2 \beta f_m$

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Unit / Condition** |
|---|---|---|
| Modulation Index $\beta$ | $\beta = \dfrac{\Delta f}{f_m} = \dfrac{k_f A_m}{2\pi f_m}$ | Dimensionless, $\beta \ge 0$ |
| Frequency Deviation $\Delta f$ | $\Delta f = k_f A_m / (2\pi)$ | Hz |
| NBFM Bandwidth | $BW_{NBFM} \approx 2 f_m$ | Hz (when $\beta \ll 1$) |
| WBFM Bandwidth (Carson's Rule) | $BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2 f_m(\beta + 1)$ | Hz |
| FM Signal Equation | $s(t) = A_c \cos[2\pi f_c t + \beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)]$ | Volts |
| NBFM Approximation | $s(t) \approx A_c \cos \omega_c t - \beta A_c \sin \omega_c t \sin \omega_m t$ | Volts |
| WBFM Spectral Component | $A_c J_n(\beta) \cos[2\pi(f_c + n f_m)t]$ | Volts |
| Average Power of FM | $P_{avg} = \dfrac{A_c^2}{2}$ | Watts (constant, independent of $\beta$) |
| Phase Modulation Index $\beta_p$ | $\beta_p = k_p A_m$ | Radians |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Pipe Symbol:** All table entries use $\vert$ or $\mid$ representation for absolute values, and we use $/2$ for halves to avoid breaking the markdown table structure.

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **NBFM** is used in **two-way radio communication, walkie-talkies, marine communication (VHF), and police/ambulance radio** because it requires **narrow bandwidth** (similar to AM) but provides **better noise immunity** than AM.
- **WBFM** is used in **commercial FM radio broadcasting (88–108 MHz band)** where high-fidelity audio transmission is required, justifying the larger bandwidth. In India, the maximum frequency deviation is **75 kHz** with maximum modulating frequency of **15 kHz**, giving $\beta_{max} = 5$.
- FM is also used in **television sound transmission, satellite communication, radar, and telemetry**.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Narrowband FM (NBFM) Approximation

**Given:**
$$s(t) = A_c \cos\left[2\pi f_c t + \beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)\right]$$

**Step 1: Expand using trigonometric identity**
$$\cos(A + B) = \cos A \cos B - \sin A \sin B$$

Applied here with $A = 2\pi f_c t$ and $B = \beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)$:

$$s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)\cos(\beta \sin 2\pi f_m t) - A_c \sin(2\pi f_c t)\sin(\beta \sin 2\pi f_m t)$$

**Step 2: Apply the small-angle approximation (valid only for NBFM where $\beta \ll 1$)**

For very small angles, $\cos x \approx 1$ and $\sin x \approx x$. Since $\beta \ll 1$, the argument $\beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)$ is also small:

$$\cos(\beta \sin 2\pi f_m t) \approx 1$$

$$\sin(\beta \sin 2\pi f_m t) \approx \beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)$$

**Step 3: Substitute back**

$$s(t) \approx A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) \cdot 1 - A_c \sin(2\pi f_c t) \cdot \beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)$$

$$s(t) \approx A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) - \beta A_c \sin(2\pi f_c t) \sin(2\pi f_m t)$$

**Step 4: Apply the product-to-sum identity**

$$\sin X \sin Y = \frac{1}{2}\left[\cos(X - Y) - \cos(X + Y)\right]$$

Applied with $X = 2\pi f_c t$ and $Y = 2\pi f_m t$:

$$\sin(2\pi f_c t)\sin(2\pi f_m t) = \frac{1}{2}\left[\cos(2\pi(f_c - f_m)t) - \cos(2\pi(f_c + f_m)t)\right]$$

**Step 5: Final NBFM expression**

$$s(t) \approx A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) - \frac{\beta A_c}{2}\cos(2\pi(f_c - f_m)t) + \frac{\beta A_c}{2}\cos(2\pi(f_c + f_m)t)$$

**Interpretation:**
- **First term:** Carrier component of amplitude $A_c$ at frequency $f_c$.
- **Second term:** Lower Sideband (LSB) of amplitude $\beta A_c / 2$ at $f_c - f_m$ (with a **negative sign**, i.e., phase-inverted).
- **Third term:** Upper Sideband (USB) of amplitude $\beta A_c / 2$ at $f_c + f_m$ (positive sign).

> [!NOTE]
> Comparing with AM where both sidebands are **positive**, the NBFM has a **negative LSB** — this 180° phase difference is what gives FM its superior noise rejection (noise affects both sidebands equally in AM, but in FM, the noise-induced phase perturbations are translated into frequency changes that the discriminator ignores).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Wideband FM (WBFM) Spectrum using Bessel Functions

**Given:** $s(t) = A_c \cos[2\pi f_c t + \beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)]$

**Step 1: Use the complex exponential representation of cosine**

$$\cos(\theta) = \frac{e^{j\theta} + e^{-j\theta}}{2}$$

Therefore:

$$s(t) = \frac{A_c}{2}\left[e^{j(2\pi f_c t + \beta \sin 2\pi f_m t)} + e^{-j(2\pi f_c t + \beta \sin 2\pi f_m t)}\right]$$

**Step 2: Factor out the carrier term**

$$s(t) = \frac{A_c}{2}\left[e^{j 2\pi f_c t} \cdot e^{j\beta \sin 2\pi f_m t} + e^{-j 2\pi f_c t} \cdot e^{-j\beta \sin 2\pi f_m t}\right]$$

**Step 3: Apply the Bessel function identity (Jacobi-Anger expansion)**

For any real number $x$:

$$e^{j x \sin \theta} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n(x) e^{j n \theta}$$

So:

$$e^{j\beta \sin 2\pi f_m t} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n(\beta) e^{j 2\pi n f_m t}$$

And:

$$e^{-j\beta \sin 2\pi f_m t} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n(\beta) e^{-j 2\pi n f_m t}$$

(using the identity $J_{-n}(x) = (-1)^n J_n(x)$, but the structure remains valid)

**Step 4: Substitute back**

$$s(t) = \frac{A_c}{2}\left[e^{j 2\pi f_c t} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n(\beta) e^{j 2\pi n f_m t} + e^{-j 2\pi f_c t} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n(\beta) e^{-j 2\pi n f_m t}\right]$$

$$s(t) = \frac{A_c}{2} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n(\beta) \left[e^{j 2\pi(f_c + n f_m)t} + e^{-j 2\pi(f_c + n f_m)t}\right]$$

**Step 5: Convert back to cosine**

Using $\cos\theta = (e^{j\theta} + e^{-j\theta})/2$:

$$\boxed{s(t) = A_c \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n(\beta) \cos\left[2\pi(f_c + n f_m)t\right]}$$

**Step 6: Expand the first few terms to identify spectral components**

$$s(t) = A_c J_0(\beta) \cos(2\pi f_c t) \quad \text{(carrier)}$$
$$+ A_c J_1(\beta) \cos(2\pi(f_c + f_m)t) + A_c J_{-1}(\beta) \cos(2\pi(f_c - f_m)t) \quad \text{(1st sideband pair)}$$
$$+ A_c J_2(\beta) \cos(2\pi(f_c + 2f_m)t) + A_c J_{-2}(\beta) \cos(2\pi(f_c - 2f_m)t) \quad \text{(2nd sideband pair)}$$
$$+ \ldots \text{(infinite series)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Properties of Bessel Functions used in KTU exams:**
> 1. $J_n(\beta) = (-1)^n J_{-n}(\beta)$ — symmetry of even/odd order functions
> 2. $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n^2(\beta) = 1$ — power conservation (Parseval's theorem)
> 3. For small $\beta$: $J_0(\beta) \approx 1$, $J_1(\beta) \approx \beta/2$, $J_n(\beta) \approx 0$ for $n \ge 2$
> 4. Carrier vanishes when $J_0(\beta) = 0$, which occurs at $\beta \approx 2.405, 5.520, \ldots$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Bandwidth of FM using Carson's Rule

**Carson's rule is empirical**, derived from the observation that for an FM signal with modulation index $\beta$, the sideband amplitude $J_n(\beta)$ becomes negligibly small when $n > \beta + 1$.

**Number of significant sideband pairs (upper + lower):** $n = \beta + 1$

**Total number of significant sidebands (counting both sides):** $2(\beta + 1)$

**Frequency spacing between sidebands:** $f_m$

**Therefore, the bandwidth is:**

$$BW = 2(\beta + 1) f_m$$

**Substituting $\beta = \Delta f / f_m$:**

$$BW = 2\left(\frac{\Delta f}{f_m} + 1\right) f_m = 2(\Delta f + f_m)$$

$$\boxed{BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2 f_m (\beta + 1)}$$

**For NBFM ($\beta \ll 1$):**
$$BW \approx 2 f_m$$

**For WBFM ($\beta \gg 1$):**
$$BW \approx 2 \Delta f = 2 \beta f_m$$

---

### 3.4 Numerical Example (KTU Style Problem)

**Problem:** An FM broadcast transmitter has carrier frequency 100 MHz, modulating frequency 15 kHz, and frequency deviation 75 kHz. Find:
(a) Modulation index $\beta$
(b) Bandwidth by Carson's rule
(c) Number of significant sideband pairs

**Solution:**

(a) Modulation index:

$$\beta = \frac{\Delta f}{f_m} = \frac{75 \text{ kHz}}{15 \text{ kHz}} = 5$$

(b) Carson's rule bandwidth:

$$BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2(75 + 15) \text{ kHz} = 180 \text{ kHz}$$

(c) Number of significant sideband pairs:

$$n = \beta + 1 = 5 + 1 = 6 \text{ pairs} = 12 \text{ sidebands}$$
