## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Narrowband FM. Compare its bandwidth with that of a conventional AM signal.**

**Model Answer:**

Narrowband FM (NBFM) is a type of frequency modulation in which the modulation index $\beta$ is small (typically $\beta < 0.5$ or $\beta \ll 1$). The expression for a single-tone NBFM signal is:

$$s(t) \approx A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) - \frac{\beta A_c}{2}\cos 2\pi(f_c - f_m)t + \frac{\beta A_c}{2}\cos 2\pi(f_c + f_m)t$$

This contains a carrier and **two sidebands**, so the **bandwidth is $2 f_m$**, which is the **same as AM bandwidth**. The only difference is the **180° phase reversal of the LSB**, which gives FM better noise immunity. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State Carson's rule for the bandwidth of an FM signal. What is the bandwidth for NBFM and WBFM?**

**Model Answer:**

Carson's rule states that the approximate transmission bandwidth of an FM signal is:

$$BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2 f_m (\beta + 1)$$

- For **NBFM** ($\beta \ll 1$): $BW \approx 2 f_m$
- For **WBFM** ($\beta \gg 1$): $BW \approx 2 \Delta f = 2 \beta f_m$

This rule accounts for approximately 98% of the total signal power. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – with Internal Choice)

---

**Q3(a). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 2]**
**Derive the mathematical expression for a single-tone Narrowband FM (NBFM) signal. Sketch its spectrum and compare it with AM.**

**Model Solution:**

The single-tone FM signal is:
$$s(t) = A_c \cos[2\pi f_c t + \beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)]$$

Expanding using $\cos(A+B) = \cos A \cos B - \sin A \sin B$:

$$s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)\cos[\beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)] - A_c \sin(2\pi f_c t)\sin[\beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)]$$

For NBFM, $\beta \ll 1$, hence:
- $\cos[\beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)] \approx 1$
- $\sin[\beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)] \approx \beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)$

Therefore:
$$s(t) \approx A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) - \beta A_c \sin(2\pi f_c t)\sin(2\pi f_m t)$$

Applying product-to-sum identity:
$$s(t) \approx A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) - \frac{\beta A_c}{2}\cos 2\pi(f_c - f_m)t + \frac{\beta A_c}{2}\cos 2\pi(f_c + f_m)t$$

**Spectrum Sketch:**

```
NBFM SPECTRUM (Amplitude vs Frequency)
       Ac|        |
         |        |   <- Carrier at fc
A beta c/2|  |  |  |   <- Sidebands at fc ± fm
         |  |  |  |
        fc-fm fc fc+fm
```

**Comparison with AM:**

| **Feature** | **AM** | **NBFM** |
|---|---|---|
| Carrier amplitude | $A_c$ | $A_c$ |
| Sideband amplitude | $m A_c / 2$ | $\beta A_c / 2$ |
| LSB phase | In-phase | **Inverted (180°)** |
| Bandwidth | $2 f_m$ | $2 f_m$ |
| Noise immunity | Low | High |

**(7 marks)**

---

**Q3(b). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 2]**
**Derive the expression for a single-tone Wideband FM (WBFM) signal using Bessel functions. How is its bandwidth determined using Carson's rule?**

**Model Solution:**

For WBFM, the small-angle approximation fails. Using complex exponential form:

$$s(t) = \frac{A_c}{2}\left[e^{j(2\pi f_c t + \beta \sin 2\pi f_m t)} + e^{-j(2\pi f_c t + \beta \sin 2\pi f_m t)}\right]$$

Applying the Jacobi-Anger identity:
$$e^{j\beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n(\beta) e^{j 2\pi n f_m t}$$

Substituting:
$$s(t) = \frac{A_c}{2} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n(\beta)\left[e^{j 2\pi(f_c + n f_m)t} + e^{-j 2\pi(f_c + n f_m)t}\right]$$

$$\boxed{s(t) = A_c \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n(\beta) \cos[2\pi(f_c + n f_m)t]}$$

**WBFM Spectrum:**

The spectrum contains a carrier at $f_c$ with amplitude $A_c J_0(\beta)$ and an **infinite number of sidebands** at $f_c \pm n f_m$ with amplitudes $A_c J_n(\beta)$, where $J_n(\beta)$ is the Bessel function of the first kind of order $n$.

**Bandwidth by Carson's Rule:**

The amplitude $J_n(\beta)$ becomes negligibly small (less than 1% of carrier) for $n > \beta + 1$. Hence the number of significant sideband pairs is $(\beta + 1)$, and the bandwidth is:

$$BW = 2(\beta + 1) f_m = 2(\Delta f + f_m)$$

**Example:** If $\beta = 5$ and $f_m = 15$ kHz, then $BW = 2 \times 6 \times 15 = 180$ kHz. **(7 marks)**

---

### **ALTERNATIVE CHOICE – Question B**

**Q4(a). [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 2]**
**A single-tone FM signal has a modulation index $\beta = 3$ and modulating frequency $f_m = 10$ kHz. Calculate: (i) the frequency deviation, (ii) the bandwidth using Carson's rule, (iii) the number of significant sideband pairs, and (iv) the power contained in the carrier and first three sideband pairs.**

**Model Solution:**

Given: $\beta = 3$, $f_m = 10$ kHz.

**(i) Frequency Deviation:**
$$\Delta f = \beta \times f_m = 3 \times 10 = 30 \text{ kHz}$$

**(ii) Bandwidth (Carson's Rule):**
$$BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2(30 + 10) = 80 \text{ kHz}$$

**(iii) Significant Sideband Pairs:**
$$n = \beta + 1 = 3 + 1 = 4 \text{ pairs (8 sidebands)}$$

**(iv) Power in Carrier and First Three Sideband Pairs:**

Power of FM signal: $P_{total} = A_c^2 / 2$. Power in $n$-th sideband pair: $A_c^2 J_n^2(\beta) / 2$.

Bessel function values for $\beta = 3$:
- $J_0(3) \approx 0.260$
- $J_1(3) \approx 0.339$
- $J_2(3) \approx 0.486$
- $J_3(3) \approx 0.309$

Power in carrier:
$$P_0 = \frac{A_c^2}{2} J_0^2(3) = \frac{A_c^2}{2}(0.0676) \approx 0.034 \frac{A_c^2}{2}$$

Power in 1st sideband pair:
$$P_1 = \frac{A_c^2}{2} \cdot 2 J_1^2(3) = \frac{A_c^2}{2}(0.2298)$$

Power in 2nd sideband pair:
$$P_2 = \frac{A_c^2}{2} \cdot 2 J_2^2(3) = \frac{A_c^2}{2}(0.4724)$$

Power in 3rd sideband pair:
$$P_3 = \frac{A_c^2}{2} \cdot 2 J_3^2(3) = \frac{A_c^2}{2}(0.1911)$$

Total power in carrier + first 3 pairs:
$$P_{sum} = \frac{A_c^2}{2}(0.0676 + 0.2298 + 0.4724 + 0.1911) = 0.961 \frac{A_c^2}{2} \approx 96.1\%$$

This confirms Carson's rule captures ~98% of power. **(7 marks)**

---

**Q4(b). [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 2]**
**Draw the block diagram of a Narrowband FM (NBFM) generator using a Phase Modulator. Explain the function of the integrator and discuss why NBFM is preferred over AM in some applications.**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram of NBFM Generator using Phase Modulator:**

```
Message Signal ──▶ Integrator ──▶ Phase Modulator ──▶ NBFM Output
                          ▲
                          │
                    ┌─────┴─────┐
                    │  Carrier  │
                    │ cos(ωct) │
                    └───────────┘
```

**Function of Integrator:**

The integrator converts the message signal $m(t)$ into its integral $\int m(t) dt$. This integral, when fed into a phase modulator (which produces output proportional to the input phase), generates a signal whose **instantaneous frequency** is proportional to $m(t)$ — thereby achieving **Frequency Modulation**.

Mathematically, if the phase modulator output is $A_c \cos[\omega_c t + k_p \cdot v(t)]$, then feeding $v(t) = \int m(t) dt$ gives:

$$s(t) = A_c \cos\left[\omega_c t + k_p \int m(t) dt\right]$$

The instantaneous frequency becomes:
$$f_i(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\frac{d\phi}{dt} = f_c + \frac{k_p}{2\pi} m(t)$$

This is exactly an FM signal. **(3 marks for the diagram and integrator explanation)**

**Why NBFM is Preferred Over AM:**

1. **Better noise immunity:** Since noise primarily affects the amplitude of the signal, and FM has constant amplitude, the use of a **limiter at the receiver** removes amplitude noise effectively. AM has no such protection.
2. **Capture effect:** FM receivers can reject weak interfering signals in favor of the stronger desired signal, giving clearer reception.
3. **Constant envelope transmission:** Allows the use of efficient non-linear (Class C) amplifiers in the transmitter, increasing power efficiency.
4. **Same bandwidth as AM:** For NBFM, the bandwidth is also $2 f_m$, so no extra spectrum is occupied compared to AM.
5. **Resistant to signal strength variations:** FM systems use de-emphasis circuits to reduce high-frequency noise, further improving SNR.

**(4 marks for the comparative discussion)** **(Total: 7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cause Mark Deductions in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $\beta$ and $\mu$:** Students often use the AM modulation index $\mu$ formula. Remember: in FM, $\beta = \Delta f / f_m$, NOT $A_m / A_c$.
>
> 2. **Forgetting that NBFM = phase-reversed AM:** The LSB has a **negative sign**, which is crucial. Writing NBFM as identical to AM will lose 2–3 marks.
>
> 3. **Using Bessel functions wrongly:** $J_n(\beta)$ is **not** a simple sine. For $\beta \ll 1$, you may use $J_0 \approx 1$ and $J_1 \approx \beta/2$, but for larger $\beta$, must consult a Bessel table. Examiners penalize hand-waving.
>
> 4. **Bandwidth formula errors:** Carson's rule is $BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m)$. Students often write $BW = 2\Delta f$ (correct only for WBFM with $\beta \gg 1$) or $BW = 2 f_m$ (correct only for NBFM).
>
> 5. **Units confusion:** Always carry units (kHz, MHz) explicitly through your calculation. A correct number with wrong units gets 0 marks.
>
> 6. **Missing the constant envelope property:** FM has **constant total power** = $A_c^2/2$, independent of $\beta$. Forgetting this is a frequent mistake.
>
> 7. **Drawing the spectrum without labeling amplitudes:** The examiner will deduct marks if amplitudes ($A_c$, $\beta A_c / 2$, $A_c J_n(\beta)$) and frequencies ($f_c$, $f_c \pm n f_m$) are not clearly indicated.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **⚡ RAPID REVISION – KEY POINTS TO MEMORIZE ⚡**
>
> ✅ **FM Signal:** $s(t) = A_c \cos[2\pi f_c t + \beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)]$
>
> ✅ **Modulation Index:** $\beta = \Delta f / f_m = k_f A_m / (2\pi f_m)$
>
> ✅ **NBFM Condition:** $\beta \ll 1$ (or $\beta < 0.5$)
>
> ✅ **NBFM Expression:** $s(t) \approx A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) - \beta A_c \sin(2\pi f_c t) \sin(2\pi f_m t)$
>
> ✅ **NBFM Bandwidth:** $BW = 2 f_m$ (same as AM)
>
> ✅ **WBFM Condition:** $\beta \gg 1$ (commercial FM uses $\beta = 5$)
>
> ✅ **WBFM Expression:** $s(t) = A_c \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n(\beta) \cos[2\pi(f_c + n f_m)t]$
>
> ✅ **WBFM Bandwidth (Carson's Rule):** $BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2 f_m(\beta + 1)$
>
> ✅ **Bessel Function Property:** $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n^2(\beta) = 1$ (power conservation)
>
> ✅ **Total FM Power:** $P = A_c^2 / 2$ (constant, independent of $\beta$)
>
> ✅ **Carrier vanishes** when $J_0(\beta) = 0$, i.e., $\beta \approx 2.405, 5.520, 8.654, \ldots$
>
> ✅ **Key difference between AM and FM spectra:** AM has USB and LSB both in-phase; NBFM has USB in-phase and LSB **inverted (180° phase-reversed)**.
>
> ✅ **FM is a constant-envelope modulation:** Carrier amplitude never changes — only frequency varies.
>
> ✅ **Use the small-angle approximation ONLY for NBFM** — using it for WBFM is a serious error.
>
> ✅ **NBFM is generated by** an integrator followed by a phase modulator.
>
> ✅ **Number of significant sideband pairs** in WBFM = $\beta + 1$ (for 1% amplitude threshold).
>
> ✅ **Carson's rule captures 98% of total signal power**, making it the standard bandwidth estimate.
>
> ✅ **Commercial FM broadcasting (India):** $f_m \le 15$ kHz, $\Delta f = 75$ kHz, $\beta = 5$, $BW = 180$ kHz.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modulation and Demodulation Techniques for FM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET864 - ELECTRONIC COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Modulation and Demodulation Techniques for FM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 FM Modulation — Formal Definition

**Frequency Modulation (FM)** is an angle modulation technique in which the **instantaneous frequency** of the carrier wave is varied in direct proportion to the instantaneous amplitude of the modulating (message) signal, while the carrier amplitude and phase remain (ideally) unchanged.

The standard FM waveform is mathematically expressed as:

$$s_{FM}(t) = A_c \cos\!\left(2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \!\int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau)\,d\tau\right)$$

where:
- $A_c$ = Carrier amplitude (volts)
- $f_c$ = Carrier frequency (Hz)
- $k_f$ = Frequency sensitivity of the modulator (Hz/volt)
- $m(t)$ = Message (baseband) signal

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In FM, the information is carried in the **frequency variations** of the carrier, not in its amplitude. This makes FM highly immune to amplitude noise and interference — a major advantage over AM.

## 1.2 FM Demodulation — Formal Definition

**FM Demodulation (or Detection)** is the reverse process that extracts the original message signal $m(t)$ from the received FM waveform. Any device that produces an output voltage proportional to the **instantaneous frequency deviation** of the input FM signal qualifies as an FM demodulator (also called a **frequency discriminator** or **FM detector**).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🎵 Real-World Analogy — The Singing Bowl:**
> Imagine a person singing into a microphone connected to a strobe light.
> - **Loud voice (large amplitude) → strobe flashes faster**
> - **Soft voice (small amplitude) → strobe flashes slower**
> The *intensity* of the light (amplitude) is fixed, but its *flash rate* (frequency) changes with the singer's volume. The listener watching the strobe can reconstruct the volume pattern from the flash timing.
>
> In FM, the **carrier amplitude is fixed** (constant light intensity), but the **carrier frequency changes** (flash rate) in step with the message. A demodulator acts like a counter that measures how fast the carrier frequency changes and converts this rate into the original message voltage.

## 1.4 Why Modulate and Demodulate FM at All?

| Need | Engineering Reason |
|------|--------------------|
| **Spectrum allocation** | Low-frequency audio signals cannot be radiated by practical antennas; FM shifts them to VHF/UHF ranges. |
| **Noise immunity** | FM provides improved SNR (Signal-to-Noise Ratio) via **capture effect** and **trade-off between bandwidth and noise**. |
| **Multiplexing** | Many FM channels (stations) can coexist without mutual interference due to **Carson's rule**-defined bandwidth. |
| **Fidelity** | FM supports high-fidelity broadcasting (e.g., 88–108 MHz FM radio, 15 kHz audio). |

## 1.5 Key Physical Quantities and Standard Metrics

- **Frequency Deviation** ($\Delta f$) = maximum shift of instantaneous frequency from $f_c$ — measured in **Hz**
- **Modulation Index** ($\beta$) = dimensionless ratio
- **Carson's Rule Bandwidth** — measured in **Hz**
- **Frequency Sensitivity** ($k_f$) — measured in **Hz/V**
- **Standard FM Broadcast Values**: $\Delta f = 75$ kHz, $f_m(\max) = 15$ kHz, $\beta = 5$, BW = 200 kHz
- **Narrowband FM (NBFM)**: $\beta < 0.5$
- **Wideband FM (WBFM)**: $\beta > 0.5$ (broadcast FM uses $\beta = 5$)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 FM Modulation — Operational Concepts

### 2.1.1 Instantaneous Frequency and Phase Relationship

For an angle-modulated signal $s(t) = A_c \cos[\theta_i(t)]$, the **instantaneous frequency** is defined as:

$$f_i(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\,\frac{d\theta_i(t)}{dt}$$

For FM, we enforce $f_i(t) = f_c + k_f\,m(t)$, which gives the phase:

$$\theta_i(t) = 2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \!\int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau)\,d\tau$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why integration appears in FM:** To get frequency proportional to $m(t)$, we must **integrate** $m(t)$ before using it as the *phase* argument. The integrator inside an FM modulator is a defining hardware block.

### 2.1.2 Sinusoidal Modulation — The Canonical Case

Let $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$. Then:

$$\int m(\tau)\,d\tau = \frac{A_m}{2\pi f_m}\sin(2\pi f_m t)$$

Substituting into the FM equation:

$$s_{FM}(t) = A_c \cos\!\left(2\pi f_c t + \frac{k_f A_m}{f_m}\sin(2\pi f_m t)\right)$$

$$\boxed{\;\beta = \frac{k_f A_m}{f_m} = \frac{\Delta f}{f_m}\;}$$

This $\beta$ is the **FM modulation index**.

## 2.2 FM Modulation Techniques (Generators)

### 2.2.1 Direct FM Method (VCO-Based)

A **Voltage-Controlled Oscillator (VCO)** directly generates an FM signal. The instantaneous output frequency of the VCO is:

$$f_{VCO}(t) = f_c + k_{VCO}\,m(t)$$

where $k_{VCO}$ is the VCO tuning sensitivity in **Hz/volt**.

**Hardware:** LC oscillator whose tank capacitor is replaced by a **varactor diode** whose capacitance depends on the reverse-bias voltage (which is $m(t)$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Direct FM advantages:** Simple, large frequency deviation achievable.
> **Direct FM drawbacks:** Carrier frequency stability is poor (drifts with temperature, component aging); requires AFC (Automatic Frequency Control).

### 2.2.2 Indirect FM Method (Armstrong System)

This is the **board-favorite topic** for KTU ESE. Since generating FM directly is hard, Armstrong used a clever trick:

**Step 1:** Generate a **Narrowband Phase-Modulated (NBPM)** signal using a phase modulator.

**Step 2:** Use the fact that for NB signals, **PM ≈ FM** (because $\sin(x) \approx x$ for small $x$).

**Step 3:** Use **frequency multipliers** to convert NBPM to WBFM (since frequency multiplication increases both carrier frequency and frequency deviation).

**Step 4:** Use a **mixer** to bring the carrier back down to the desired $f_c$.

**Block diagram of Armstrong Indirect FM system:**

$$m(t) \to \text{Integrator} \to \text{Phase Modulator} \to \text{Frequency Multiplier (×n)} \to \text{Mixer} \to s_{FM}(t)$$

**Mathematical Analysis of Armstrong Method:**

Output of phase modulator (NBPM):

$$s_{NBPM}(t) = A_c \cos\!\left(2\pi f_c t + k_p\,m(t)\right)$$

For sinusoidal message $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$:

$$s_{NBPM}(t) = A_c \cos\!\left(2\pi f_c t + m_p \sin(2\pi f_m t)\right)$$

where $m_p = k_p A_m$ is the PM modulation index. For NBPM, $m_p < 0.5$.

After integration, the phase modulator effectively generates **NBFM**:

$$s_{NBFM}(t) = A_c \cos\!\left(2\pi f_c t + m_p \sin(2\pi f_m t)\right) \quad \text{with} \quad \beta = m_p$$

After frequency multiplication by factor $n$:

$$s(t) = A_c \cos\!\left(2\pi n f_c t + n\,m_p \sin(2\pi f_m t)\right)$$

Now $\beta_{new} = n m_p$ and $f_{c,new} = n f_c$.

## 2.3 FM Demodulation Techniques (Detectors)

### 2.3.1 Slope Detector (Balanced Slope Detector)

The simplest FM detector. It uses a **tank circuit (BPF)** whose resonant frequency is **slightly offset** from $f_c$, so the FM signal operates on the **linear slope** of the BPF response curve. Frequency changes → amplitude changes → envelope detector recovers $m(t)$.

> [!WARNING]
> Poor linearity, narrow bandwidth, and high distortion make it unsuitable for high-fidelity FM. Used in low-cost applications only.

### 2.3.2 Foster–Seeley Discriminator

A classic FM detector with **better linearity** than the slope detector.

- Uses **two tuned circuits** (double-tuned transformer) both resonant at $f_c$.
- Two envelope detectors (diodes) produce output voltages whose **difference** is proportional to the frequency deviation $\Delta f$.
- Requires a **limiter** at the input to remove AM noise.

**Output voltage (linear region):**

$$v_{out}(t) = K \cdot \Delta f(t) = K \cdot k_f m(t)$$

where $K$ is the discriminator constant (V/Hz).

### 2.3.3 Ratio Detector

A **modified Foster–Seeley** circuit where:
- Diodes are connected with **opposite polarity**.
- Includes a large capacitor that **stores the long-term average** of the signal, providing **self-limiting** action.
- No external limiter required.

**Advantage:** Inherent AM rejection → ideal for TV sound IF demodulation.

### 2.3.4 Phase-Locked Loop (PLL) FM Demodulator

The **most modern and widely used** FM demodulator in integrated circuits.

**Block diagram:**

$$s_{FM}(t) \to \text{Phase Detector (PD)} \to \text{Loop Filter (LPF)} \to v_{out}(t)$$
$$\text{VCO} \leftarrow \text{(feedback path)}$$

**Operation:**
- The **VCO** frequency $f_{VCO}$ is made to track the input FM instantaneous frequency.
- The **Phase Detector** produces an error voltage proportional to the **phase difference** between $s_{FM}(t)$ and the VCO output.
- The **Loop Filter** averages this error voltage to produce $v_{out}(t)$.
- When the loop is locked: $f_{VCO} = f_i(t) = f_c + k_f m(t)$.
- Therefore: $v_{out}(t) \propto k_f m(t)$ — the demodulated message.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** PLLs are the industry standard for FM demodulation in ICs like the **LM565, NE564, CD4046** because they provide excellent linearity, noise immunity, and are easily integrated on silicon.

### 2.3.5 Quadrature Detector

Uses a **quadrature network** (a phase-shift network resonant near $f_c$) to convert FM to AM, followed by an envelope detector. Used in TV receivers and many modern radio ICs.

### 2.3.6 Zero-Crossing Detector

Counts the number of zero-crossings of the FM signal per unit time. More zero-crossings per second → higher frequency → larger output voltage. Limited accuracy, used in instrumentation.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|-------------------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | FM signal (general) | $s_{FM}(t) = A_c \cos\!\left(2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \!\int m(\tau)d\tau\right)$ | Volts |
| 2 | Instantaneous frequency | $f_i(t) = f_c + k_f\,m(t)$ | Hz |
| 3 | Frequency deviation | $\Delta f = k_f \cdot \max\vert m(t)\vert$ | Hz |
| 4 | Modulation index (FM) | $\beta = \dfrac{\Delta f}{f_m} = \dfrac{k_f A_m}{f_m}$ | Dimensionless |
| 5 | Carson's Rule BW | $BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2(\beta + 1) f_m$ | Hz |
| 6 | NBFM bandwidth (1st-order) | $BW_{NB} = 2 f_m$ | Hz |
| 7 | WBFM BW (98% power) | $BW \approx 2(\beta + 1) f_m$ | Hz (Carson's rule) |
| 8 | Armstrong: after ×n | $f_c' = n f_c,\quad \beta' = n \beta$ | Hz, dimensionless |
| 9 | Mixer property | $f_{IF} = f_{LO} \pm f_{RF}$ | Hz |
| 10 | SNR improvement (FM) | $\left(\dfrac{S_o}{N_o}\right)_{FM} = \dfrac{3}{2}\beta^2 \left(\dfrac{S_i}{N_i}\right)$ | Dimensionless |
| 11 | FM output SNR (with pre-emphasis) | $S_o/N_o$ enhanced by factor ~13 dB | dB |
| 12 | Discriminator output (Foster-Seeley) | $v_{out} = K \cdot \Delta f = K k_f m(t)$ | Volts |
| 13 | VCO output frequency | $f_{VCO}(t) = f_0 + k_{VCO} v_c(t)$ | Hz |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on absolute values:** In the formulas above, $\vert \cdot \vert$ denotes magnitude (use $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ in LaTeX). Avoid bare `|` symbols inside markdown table cells to preserve rendering.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **FM Broadcast Radio (88–108 MHz)**: Indirect (Armstrong) method historically; modern ICs use direct modulation with PLLs.
- **Two-Way Radios (Walkie-Talkies)**: Direct FM using varactor-tuned VCOs.
- **TV Audio**: Foster–Seeley and ratio detectors in older sets; PLL ICs in modern TVs.
- **Satellite Communications**: WBFM with PLL demodulators.
- **Radar FMCW (Frequency-Modulated Continuous Wave)**: Linear FM via direct VCO control.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — FM Signal Expression for Sinusoidal Modulation

**Given:** $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$, carrier $A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$, FM modulator with sensitivity $k_f$.

**Goal:** Derive the explicit time-domain expression for $s_{FM}(t)$ and identify the modulation index.

**Step 1:** Start with the general FM expression:

$$s_{FM}(t) = A_c \cos\!\left(2\pi f_c t + \phi(t)\right)$$

where $\phi(t) = 2\pi k_f \!\int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau)\,d\tau$.

**Step 2:** Substitute the sinusoidal message:

$$\phi(t) = 2\pi k_f \!\int_{-\infty}^{t} A_m \cos(2\pi f_m \tau)\,d\tau$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the integral (using $\int \cos(\omega t) dt = \frac{1}{\omega}\sin(\omega t)$):

$$\int_{-\infty}^{t} A_m \cos(2\pi f_m \tau)\,d\tau = \frac{A_m}{2\pi f_m}\sin(2\pi f_m t)$$

**Step 4:** Combine constants:

$$2\pi k_f \cdot \frac{A_m}{2\pi f_m} = \frac{k_f A_m}{f_m} \equiv \beta$$

**Step 5:** Therefore, the final FM signal is:

$$\boxed{\;s_{FM}(t) = A_c \cos\!\left(2\pi f_c t + \beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)\right)\;}$$

**Step 6:** The instantaneous frequency is obtained by differentiating the phase:

$$f_i(t) = f_c + \beta f_m \cos(2\pi f_m t) = f_c + \Delta f \cos(2\pi f_m t)$$

confirming $\Delta f = \beta f_m$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Armstrong Indirect FM: Frequency Multiplier Analysis

**Given:** NBPM signal $s_{NBPM}(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_{c1} t + m_p \sin(2\pi f_m t))$ with $m_p < 0.5$.

**Goal:** Show how a frequency multiplier of order $n$ transforms NBPM into WBFM.

**Step 1:** A frequency multiplier of order $n$ squares the instantaneous frequency by $n$ (non-linear device + BPF). The phase argument is multiplied by $n$:

$$\phi_{out}(t) = n \cdot \phi_{in}(t) = n \cdot \left(2\pi f_{c1} t + m_p \sin(2\pi f_m t)\right)$$

**Step 2:** Expanding:

$$\phi_{out}(t) = 2\pi (n f_{c1})\,t + (n m_p)\sin(2\pi f_m t)$$

**Step 3:** The new carrier frequency and modulation index are:

$$f_{c2} = n f_{c1}, \qquad \beta_2 = n m_p$$

**Step 4:** Original $\beta_1 = m_p < 0.5$ (NB), new $\beta_2 = n m_p$. Choosing $n$ large enough makes $\beta_2 > 0.5$ → **WBFM achieved**.

**Step 5:** For a typical Armstrong system:
- Initial: $f_{c1} = 100$ kHz, $m_p = 0.01$ (NB)
- After ×2000 multiplier: $f_{c2} = 200$ MHz, $\beta_2 = 20$ (WB)
- Then mixer with LO to shift to broadcast band.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick:** Always check that the multiplier increases $f_c$ as well. If the final $f_c$ is not at the desired value, a **mixer** must follow the multiplier to bring the carrier back. Two multipliers (×n₁, ×n₂) and a mixer (with LO) are common Armstrong configurations.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Carson's Rule Bandwidth

**Given:** FM signal with deviation $\Delta f$ and highest modulating frequency $f_m$.

**Goal:** Derive the 98%-power rule-of-thumb bandwidth.

**Step 1:** The FM spectrum contains an infinite set of Bessel-function-weighted sidebands at $f_c \pm k f_m$ for $k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$ The amplitude of the $k$-th pair is $A_c J_k(\beta)$.

**Step 2:** The amplitude $J_k(\beta)$ becomes negligibly small when $k > \beta + 1$ (empirical observation from Bessel tables).

**Step 3:** The highest significant sideband is at $f_c \pm (\beta + 1) f_m$.

**Step 4:** Therefore, the total occupied bandwidth is:

$$\boxed{\;BW = 2(\beta + 1) f_m = 2(\Delta f + f_m)\;}$$

**Step 5:** **Example:** For broadcast FM, $\Delta f = 75$ kHz, $f_m = 15$ kHz:
$$BW = 2(75 + 15) = 180 \text{ kHz (typical) } \approx 200 \text{ kHz (channel spacing)}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — PLL FM Demodulator: VCO Tracking

**Given:** PLL with phase detector gain $K_p$ (V/rad), loop filter transfer $F(s)$, VCO gain $K_0$ (rad/s/V), input FM signal with instantaneous frequency $f_i(t) = f_c + k_f m(t)$.

**Goal:** Show that the loop filter output is proportional to $m(t)$.

**Step 1:** Phase of input FM signal:
$$\theta_i(t) = 2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \!\int m(\tau) d\tau$$

**Step 2:** Phase of VCO output:
$$\theta_o(t) = 2\pi f_0 t + \phi_{VCO}(t)$$

where $\phi_{VCO}$ is the controllable phase correction.

**Step 3:** Phase detector output (proportional to phase error):
$$e(t) = K_p \left[\theta_i(t) - \theta_o(t)\right]$$

**Step 4:** After loop filter:
$$v_c(t) = F(s)\big[e(t)\big]$$

**Step 5:** VCO control law (frequency proportional to control voltage):
$$\frac{d\phi_{VCO}}{dt} = K_0 v_c(t)$$

**Step 6:** In **lock condition**, $f_{VCO} = f_i(t)$, so:
$$f_0 + \frac{1}{2\pi}\frac{d\phi_{VCO}}{dt} = f_c + k_f m(t)$$

**Step 7:** For $f_0 = f_c$ (centered VCO):
$$\frac{1}{2\pi}\frac{d\phi_{VCO}}{dt} = k_f m(t) \quad\Rightarrow\quad \phi_{VCO}(t) = 2\pi k_f \!\int m(\tau)d\tau$$

**Step 8:** The control voltage therefore is:
$$v_c(t) = \frac{1}{K_0}\frac{d\phi_{VCO}}{dt} = \frac{2\pi k_f}{K_0} m(t)$$

**Conclusion:** The loop filter output is **directly proportional to the message signal** — FM demodulation achieved.

$$\boxed{\;v_{out}(t) = \frac{2\pi k_f}{K_0}\,m(t)\;}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of FM Demodulators

| Detector | Linearity | Complexity | AM Rejection | Need Limiter | Typical Use |
|----------|-----------|------------|--------------|--------------|-------------|
| Slope Detector | Poor | Very Low | Poor | Yes | Low-cost toys |
| Foster–Seeley | Good | Medium | Poor (needs limiter) | Yes | Older FM radios |
| Ratio Detector | Good | Medium | Excellent (self-limiting) | No | TV sound IF |
| Quadrature | Good | Low | Fair | Yes | TV audio, ICs |
| **PLL** | **Excellent** | Medium | **Excellent** | No | **Modern ICs, LM565** |
| Zero-Crossing | Fair | Medium | Fair | Yes | Instrumentation |

## 3.6 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** An FM broadcast transmitter has $f_c = 100$ MHz, $\Delta f = 75$ kHz, $f_m = 15$ kHz. Find the modulation index, Carson's BW, and the number of significant sideband pairs.

**Solution:**

$$\beta = \frac{\Delta f}{f_m} = \frac{75{,}000}{15{,}000} = 5$$

$$BW = 2(\beta + 1)f_m = 2(5+1)(15{,}000) = 180 \text{ kHz}$$

Significant sideband pairs: $\beta + 1 = 6$ pairs (i.e., $k = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6$).

Power in these sidebands: $> 98\%$ of total power (verified by Bessel sum rule).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Armstrong Indirect FM Transmitter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MSG[Message Signal m of t] --> INT[Integrator]
    INT --> PM[Narrowband Phase Modulator]
    PM --> MIX1["Mixer 1 (BPF at f_IF1)"]
    MIX1 --> MULT["Frequency Multiplier x n"]
    MULT --> BPF1[BPF selecting desired harmonic]
    BPF1 --> MIX2["Mixer 2 with Local Oscillator f_LO"]
    MIX2 --> PA[Power Amplifier]
    PA --> ANT[Antenna s_FM of t]
    subgraph 1[Direct FM Method Alternative]
        D1[m of t] --> D2[VCO with Varactor]
        D2 --> D3["f_out = f_c + k_f m of t"]
    end
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The lower subgraph shows the **direct FM** alternative for comparison.
- The main flow (top) shows the **Armstrong indirect FM** transmitter: integrate → phase modulate → mix → multiply → mix → amplify → radiate.
- The two mixers are needed to shift the carrier to the desired value after the multiplier overshoots $f_c$.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — PLL FM Demodulator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN["Input s_FM of t"] --> PD["Phase Detector PD"]
    VCO_OUT["VCO Output cos of 2 pi f_o t + phi"] --> PD
    PD --> LF["Loop Filter LPF H of s"]
    LF --> OUT["Demodulated Output v_out of t proportional to m of t"]
    LF -.feedback.-> VCO["Voltage Controlled Oscillator VCO"]
    VCO --> VCO_OUT
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The phase detector compares the input FM signal with the VCO output.
- The error voltage is filtered and becomes the demodulated output.
- The filtered voltage also drives the VCO, closing the loop.
- In lock condition, the VCO frequency equals the input instantaneous frequency, and $v_{out}(t) \propto m(t)$.

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Foster–Seeley Discriminator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN["FM Input s_FM of t"] --> L["Limiter (Removes AM noise)"]
    L --> PRI["Primary of Double-Tuned Transformer"]
    PRI --> SEC1["Secondary Top Half"]
    PRI --> SEC2["Secondary Bottom Half"]
    SEC1 --> D1["Diode D1 with Envelope Detector C1 R1"]
    SEC2 --> D2["Diode D2 with Envelope Detector C2 R2"]
    D1 --> ADD["Subtracter / Balanced Output"]
    D2 --> ADD
    ADD --> OUT["v_out of t = K times frequency deviation"]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The limiter removes AM noise that would otherwise corrupt the output.
- The double-tuned transformer produces two voltages that are 180° out of phase at $f = f_c$.
- The two envelope detectors' outputs are **subtracted** to give a discriminator curve that is linear in a band around $f_c$.

## 4.4 Functional Comparison Diagram — Modulator Techniques

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ROOT[FM Generation Methods]
    ROOT --> A["Direct Method VCO Varactor"]
    ROOT --> B["Indirect Method Armstrong"]
    A --> A1["Large Delta f"]
    A --> A2["Poor f_c stability"]
    A --> A3["Needs AFC loop"]
    B --> B1["Stable f_c from crystal"]
    B --> B2["Integration + Phase Mod"]
    B --> B3["Frequency Multiplier for WBFM"]
    B --> B4["Mixer to bring f_c back"]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Direct FM** is conceptually simple but suffers from carrier drift.
- **Indirect FM** sacrifices simplicity for high carrier stability — preferred in professional broadcast transmitters.

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Receiver Superheterodyne with FM Demodulator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ANT[Antenna] --> RF["RF Amplifier and Preselector"]
    RF --> MX1["Mixer 1 with Local Oscillator"]
    MX1 --> IF1["IF Amplifier at 10.7 MHz"]
    IF1 --> LIM[Limiter]
    LIM --> DET["FM Detector PLL or Foster-Seeley"]
    DET --> AUD[Audio Amplifier and De Emphasis]
    AUD --> SPK[Speaker m of t recovered]
    MX1 -.-> LO1[Local Oscillator 1]
    IF1 -.-> AGC[AGC feedback]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The standard superheterodyne FM receiver uses a **10.7 MHz IF** (much higher than AM's 455 kHz) to accommodate wideband FM.
- The PLL or Foster–Seeley demodulator is followed by a **de-emphasis** filter to undo the transmitter's pre-emphasis.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define frequency modulation. What is the modulation index in FM?**

**Model Answer:**

Frequency Modulation (FM) is an angle modulation technique in which the **instantaneous frequency** of the carrier signal is varied in proportion to the **instantaneous amplitude** of the message signal, keeping the carrier amplitude constant.

Mathematically:
$$s_{FM}(t) = A_c \cos\!\left(2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \!\int m(\tau)\,d\tau\right)$$

The **modulation index** $\beta$ of FM is the ratio of the frequency deviation $\Delta f$ to the modulating frequency $f_m$:
$$\beta = \frac{\Delta f}{f_m} = \frac{k_f A_m}{f_m}$$

$\beta$ is dimensionless and indicates whether the FM is **narrowband** ($\beta < 0.5$) or **wideband** ($\beta > 0.5$).

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**List any three differences between direct FM and indirect FM generation methods.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Direct FM | Indirect FM (Armstrong) |
|---|-----------|------------------------|
| 1 | Uses a VCO (varactor) directly | Uses a phase modulator after integration |
| 2 | Large frequency deviation is easy | Frequency deviation is small; must be multiplied |
| 3 | Carrier frequency stability is poor | Carrier stability is excellent (crystal-controlled) |
| 4 | Simple hardware | Complex hardware (multiplier, mixer) |
| 5 | Used in low-cost transmitters | Used in high-quality broadcast transmitters |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question B-A. (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**Part (a)** Explain the **Armstrong method of generating WBFM** with a neat block diagram. Derive the expression for the FM signal at the output of the system. (7 Marks)

**Part (b)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the **PLL as an FM demodulator**. Derive the expression for the demodulated output voltage. (7 Marks)

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

The **Armstrong indirect FM system** generates WBFM from a NBPM signal through a series of frequency multiplications.

**Block Diagram:**

```
m(t) → Integrator → NB Phase Modulator → Mixer → ×n₁ → Mixer → ×n₂ → s_FM(t)
```

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1:** The integrator output is $m_i(t) = \int m(\tau)d\tau$.

**Step 2:** The NB phase modulator produces:
$$s_1(t) = A_c \cos\!\left(2\pi f_{c1} t + k_p m_i(t)\right)$$

For sinusoidal $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$:
$$m_i(t) = \frac{A_m}{2\pi f_m}\sin(2\pi f_m t)$$

So:
$$s_1(t) = A_c \cos\!\left(2\pi f_{c1} t + m_p \sin(2\pi f_m t)\right)$$

where $m_p = \frac{k_p A_m}{2\pi f_m} = \beta_1 < 0.5$.

**Step 3:** After a frequency multiplier of order $n$ and a mixer with local oscillator $f_{LO}$:
- After multiplier: $f_{c2} = n f_{c1}$, $\beta_2 = n m_p$
- After mixer: $f_c = f_{c2} - f_{LO}$ (heterodyning)

**Final Output:**
$$s_{FM}(t) = A_c \cos\!\left(2\pi f_c t + \beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)\right)$$

with $\beta = n_1 n_2 m_p$ (for cascaded multipliers).

**Numerical Example:** If $f_{c1} = 200$ kHz, $m_p = 0.1$, then for $n_1 = 25$ and $n_2 = 80$:
- $f_{c2} = n_1 f_{c1} = 5$ MHz, $\beta_2 = 2.5$
- $f_{c3} = n_2 f_{c2} = 400$ MHz, $\beta = 200$ (over-deviated)
- Use $n_2 = 4$ instead: $\beta = 10$, $f_{c3} = 20$ MHz, then mix to 100 MHz broadcast.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**PLL FM Demodulator — Block Diagram:**

```
s_FM(t) → [Phase Detector] → [Loop Filter (LPF)] → v_out(t)
              ↑                                       |
              └────────── [VCO] ←─────────────────────┘
```

**Derivation:**

Let the input FM signal be:
$$s_i(t) = A_c \cos\!\left(2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \!\int m(\tau) d\tau\right)$$

The VCO produces:
$$s_o(t) = A_v \cos\!\left(2\pi f_0 t + \phi_{VCO}(t)\right)$$

The phase detector output (proportional to phase error) is:
$$e(t) = K_p [\theta_i(t) - \theta_o(t)]$$

The loop filter (assuming ideal LPF) gives:
$$v_c(t) = K_f \cdot e(t)$$

The VCO frequency is:
$$f_{VCO}(t) = f_0 + \frac{1}{2\pi}K_0 v_c(t)$$

**In lock condition:** $f_{VCO} = f_i(t)$ (VCO tracks input).

$$f_0 + \frac{K_0}{2\pi} v_c(t) = f_c + k_f m(t)$$

Setting $f_0 = f_c$:

$$\boxed{\;v_c(t) = v_{out}(t) = \frac{2\pi k_f}{K_0}\,m(t)\;}$$

This proves the PLL output is **directly proportional to the message signal** — successful FM demodulation.

---

### Question B-B. (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Part (a)** Explain the operation of the **Foster–Seeley discriminator** with a circuit diagram. Discuss the need for a limiter. (7 Marks)

**Part (b)** A broadcast FM transmitter has $\Delta f = 75$ kHz and $f_m = 15$ kHz. Calculate the **modulation index, Carson's rule bandwidth, and the number of significant sideband pairs**. If the Armstrong method is used with an initial NBPM modulation index $m_p = 0.01$, find the **frequency multiplication factor** required. (7 Marks)

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Foster–Seeley Discriminator Circuit:**

The circuit uses a **double-tuned RF transformer** with primary tuned to $f_c$ and secondary center-tapped. Two diodes D₁ and D₂ are connected to the two halves of the secondary, with envelope detectors (RC circuits) at their outputs.

**Operation:**

**Step 1:** At the **center frequency** $f = f_c$, the two secondary voltages are **equal in magnitude** but **180° out of phase**. The diode outputs are equal, so the **difference (output) is zero**.

**Step 2:** When the input frequency **deviates above $f_c$** ($f > f_c$), the secondary circuit's phase response shifts. One diode's output becomes larger than the other's. The difference is **positive** and proportional to $\Delta f$.

**Step 3:** When the input frequency **deviates below $f_c$** ($f < f_c$), the output becomes **negative** and proportional to $-\Delta f$.

**Step 4:** The discriminator characteristic is an **S-shaped curve** linear in a region around $f_c$:
$$v_{out} = K \cdot (f - f_c) = K k_f m(t)$$

**Need for a Limiter:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Foster–Seeley circuit is sensitive to **amplitude variations** in the input, since both envelope detectors respond to amplitude as well as phase. Any AM noise on the FM signal would appear at the output as **distortion**. A **hard limiter** before the discriminator removes amplitude variations, ensuring that the output depends only on frequency variations.

**Limitations:** Limited linearity range, sensitive to tuning drift. Replaced by **PLL discriminators** in modern designs.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Given:** $\Delta f = 75$ kHz, $f_m = 15$ kHz, $m_p = 0.01$ (initial NBPM index).

**Step 1: Modulation index**
$$\beta = \frac{\Delta f}{f_m} = \frac{75{,}000}{15{,}000} = \boxed{5}$$

**Step 2: Carson's Rule Bandwidth**
$$BW = 2(\beta + 1) f_m = 2(5+1)(15{,}000) = 2 \times 6 \times 15{,}000 = \boxed{180 \text{ kHz}}$$

**Step 3: Number of significant sideband pairs**
$$n_{pairs} = \beta + 1 = 5 + 1 = \boxed{6 \text{ pairs}}$$

(These are the sidebands for $k = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6$, containing ~98% of total power.)

**Step 4: Frequency multiplication factor**
For Armstrong indirect FM, after multiplication by $n$:
$$\beta_{final} = n \cdot m_p$$

We need $\beta_{final} = 5$:
$$n = \frac{\beta_{final}}{m_p} = \frac{5}{0.01} = \boxed{500}$$

A multiplication factor of **500** is required, which can be achieved by cascading multipliers (e.g., ×10, ×10, ×5) and a mixer to bring $f_c$ to the desired value.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks on FM Modulation/Demodulation:**
>
> 1. **Confusing FM and PM:** Students often write the wrong phase expression. Remember:
>    - **PM**: phase ∝ $m(t)$ directly
>    - **FM**: phase ∝ $\int m(t) dt$ (integration appears)
>
> 2. **Skipping the integration step in Armstrong Method:** Always draw the integrator block and explain why it converts PM to FM (NB equivalence).
>
> 3. **Forgetting to show carrier frequency change in Armstrong:** When you write "frequency multiplier," you must show BOTH $f_c \to n f_c$ AND $\beta \to n \beta$.
>
> 4. **PLL Derivation Mistake:** Many students forget to set $f_0 = f_c$ before deriving $v_{out}$. Without this, the equation has an extra constant.
>
> 5. **Carson's Rule Sign:** Write BW = $2(\Delta f + f_m)$, **not** $2\Delta f + f_m$. The parentheses are crucial.
>
> 6. **Not drawing the limiter** before Foster–Seeley: You'll lose 1-2 marks.
>
> 7. **Forgetting the de-emphasis filter** when describing a complete FM receiver: The complete receiver block diagram is worth full marks only with de-emphasis included.
>
> 8. **VCO Tuning Sensitivity Units:** $k_{VCO}$ is in **Hz/V**, not rad/s/V. Mixing up these units is a common KTU trap.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **Rapid Revision Checklist — FM Modulation & Demodulation**

- ✅ **FM Signal:** $s_{FM}(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \int m(\tau) d\tau)$
- ✅ **Instantaneous Frequency:** $f_i(t) = f_c + k_f m(t)$
- ✅ **Modulation Index:** $\beta = \Delta f / f_m = k_f A_m / f_m$
- ✅ **Carson's Rule:** $BW = 2(\beta + 1) f_m = 2(\Delta f + f_m)$
- ✅ **NBFM:** $\beta < 0.5$; **WBFM:** $\beta > 0.5$ (Broadcast FM: $\beta = 5$)
- ✅ **Direct FM** uses VCO; **Indirect FM** uses Armstrong method (Integrator + Phase Mod + Multiplier)
- ✅ **Armstrong Multiplier Effect:** $f_c' = n f_c$, $\beta' = n \beta$
- ✅ **Slope Detector:** Simplest, but poor linearity
- ✅ **Foster–Seeley:** Needs external limiter, balanced output
- ✅ **Ratio Detector:** Self-limiting, no external limiter needed
- ✅ **PLL Demodulator:** Modern standard; $v_{out} = (2\pi k_f / K_0) m(t)$
- ✅ **Quadrature Detector:** Used in TV sound ICs
- ✅ **Limiter:** Essential for all AM-sensitive FM detectors (removes AM noise)
- ✅ **Pre-emphasis / De-emphasis:** +75 µs time constant in broadcast FM (improves SNR by ~13 dB)
- ✅ **Superheterodyne FM Receiver IF:** **10.7 MHz** (not 455 kHz like AM)
- ✅ **SNR Improvement (FM vs AM):** FM provides $\frac{3}{2}\beta^2$ times SNR gain for large $\beta$
- ✅ **Bessel Function Property:** $\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} J_k^2(\beta) = 1$ (total power = carrier power)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pre-emphasis and De-emphasis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET864 - ELECTRONIC COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Pre-emphasis and De-emphasis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pre-emphasis** is the systematic, controlled boosting of the **higher-frequency components** of the message signal at the **transmitter** *before* modulation, while **De-emphasis** is the corresponding, equal, and opposite attenuation of those same higher-frequency components at the **receiver** *after* demodulation, implemented using a complementary pair of linear filters in an FM communication system.

In the standard KTU 2024 scheme treatment of angle modulation, this technique is classified under **noise-reduction techniques for Frequency Modulation (FM)**. Because FM noise at the discriminator output exhibits a **parabolic (triangular-shaped) Power Spectral Density (PSD)**, high-frequency message components are inherently corrupted more by noise than low-frequency components. Pre-emphasis and de-emphasis are the canonical engineering solutions to this problem.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Whistling Kettle and the Ear Cup"

Imagine you are talking to a friend across a noisy factory floor. The low rumble of the machinery drowns out the deep tones of your voice, but a high-pitched whistle cuts through cleanly.

Now flip the situation: imagine you are listening to a symphony being played next to a noisy air-conditioner. The bass notes (low frequencies) are barely audible beneath the compressor hum, while the high violins pierce through.

To fix this, you could:
- **At the sender (Pre-emphasis):** Speak into a special microphone that makes your deep voice louder and your high-pitched voice softer. This compensates for the rumble.
- **At the receiver (De-emphasis):** The listener wears an "ear cup" that does the exact opposite — makes deep tones quieter and high tones louder — restoring the original voice perfectly.

> [!NOTE]
> **The catch:** The boost at the transmitter and the cut at the receiver are *exact mathematical mirror images* of one another. Together, they leave the message signal unchanged, but they re-shape the noise — pushing noise power down at the high frequencies where it is most harmful. Net effect: cleaner audio.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard broadcast FM time constant:** $\tau = 75\ \mu s$
- **Corner (-3 dB) frequency of pre-/de-emphasis filter:** $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\tau} \approx 2122\ \text{Hz}$
- **Message bandwidth in commercial FM:** $W = 15\ \text{kHz}$
- **Typical SNR improvement:** $\approx 13\ \text{dB}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The value **$\tau = 75\ \mu s$** is the universally adopted constant for commercial monaural FM broadcasting (and TV audio). It is fixed by international broadcast standards — not a design choice you make in homework problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Is Pre-emphasis Needed? — The Triangular Noise Argument

In an FM system with a single-tone message $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$ and white noise of two-sided PSD $N_0/2$ at the input, the **output noise PSD at the discriminator** is:

$$
S_{n_o}(f) = \frac{N_0\, k_f^2}{A_c^2}\, f^2 \quad \text{for } 0 \leq f \leq W
$$

where $k_f$ is the frequency sensitivity of the modulator and $A_c$ is the carrier amplitude. This $f^2$ dependence is the celebrated **triangular noise spectrum** of FM.

**Operational logic — broken into discrete steps:**

1. **Step 1 — Identify the noise problem:** The PSD grows as $f^2$, so noise power is concentrated at the high-frequency end of the message band.
2. **Step 2 — Identify the cost:** Higher-frequency message components (e.g., the sibilance in human speech, the high harmonics of music) are buried in noise, degrading intelligibility and fidelity.
3. **Step 3 — Design intent:** Artificially boost these high-frequency message components *before* transmission so that, after being corrupted by noise, they emerge from the channel with adequate SNR.
4. **Step 4 — Compensation:** At the receiver, after demodulation, attenuate exactly those high-frequency components back to their original level. The message is restored perfectly, but the noise (which entered *after* the boost) is suppressed.
5. **Step 5 — Net effect:** A flat, uniform SNR across the message band — better than the natural $f^2$ degradation of plain FM.

### 2.2 Filter Architecture

| Location | Filter Type | Transfer Function | Pole/Zero | Purpose |
|----------|-------------|-------------------|-----------|---------|
| Transmitter (Pre-emphasis) | First-order **High-Pass Filter (HPF)** | $H_{pe}(f) = 1 + j(f/f_0)$ | Zero at origin, pole at $-f_0$ | Boost high frequencies |
| Receiver (De-emphasis) | First-order **Low-Pass Filter (LPF)** | $H_{de}(f) = \dfrac{1}{1 + j(f/f_0)}$ | Pole at $-f_0$ | Attenuate high frequencies |
| **Cascade Product** | All-pass | $H_{pe}(f) H_{de}(f) = 1$ | Cancels perfectly | Message restored unchanged |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Commercial FM broadcast (88–108 MHz):** Universally adopted since the 1950s. Provides ~**13 dB** of effective SNR gain, which is the equivalent of multiplying transmitter power by **20 times**.
- **Analog TV audio:** Uses the same 75 μs time constant for inter-system compatibility.
- **Two-way radio (professional mobile radio):** Uses a slightly different time constant of **50 μs** in some standards.
- **High-fidelity audio transmission (e.g., audio CDs over FM subcarriers):** The technique is essential because the audio content is densely packed in the high-frequency region.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **KTU 2024 Scheme high-yield** and have appeared in multiple past paper cycles.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Pre-emphasis filter TF | $H_{pe}(f) = 1 + j(f/f_0)$ | Dimensionless, complex |
| 2 | De-emphasis filter TF | $H_{de}(f) = \dfrac{1}{1 + j(f/f_0)}$ | Dimensionless, complex |
| 3 | Filter time constant | $\tau = RC$ | Seconds (s) |
| 4 | Corner (-3 dB) frequency | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\tau}$ | Hertz (Hz) |
| 5 | Standard broadcast value | $\tau = 75\ \mu s \Rightarrow f_0 \approx 2122\ \text{Hz}$ | Constants |
| 6 | Output noise PSD (FM, no de-emph) | $S_{n_o}(f) = \dfrac{N_0 k_f^2}{A_c^2}\, f^2$ | V²/Hz |
| 7 | Total noise power (no de-emph) | $\sigma_n^2 = \dfrac{N_0 k_f^2 W^3}{3 A_c^2}$ | V² |
| 8 | Total noise power (with de-emph) | $\sigma_d^2 = \dfrac{N_0 k_f^2 f_0^2}{A_c^2} \left[ W - f_0 \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{W}{f_0}\right)\right]$ | V² |
| 9 | Noise improvement factor | $\gamma = \dfrac{\sigma_n^2}{\sigma_d^2} = \dfrac{W^3/3}{f_0^2\left[W - f_0 \tan^{-1}(W/f_0)\right]}$ | Unitless ratio (linear) |
| 10 | Improvement in decibels | $\gamma_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(\gamma)$ | dB |
| 11 | Typical improvement (FM broadcast) | $\gamma \approx 20.93 \Rightarrow \gamma_{\text{dB}} \approx 13.2\ \text{dB}$ | With $W=15$ kHz, $f_0=2.12$ kHz |
| 12 | Output SNR (with de-emph) | $(S/N)_{o,\text{de-emph}} = \gamma \cdot (S/N)_{o,\text{plain FM}}$ | Unitless ratio |
| 13 | Noise figure advantage | Equivalent to $20\times$ transmit power increase | — |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Noise Improvement Factor $\gamma$ (KTU High-Yield Derivation)

This is the single most important derivation in this topic and is a frequent **14-mark question** in KTU ESE papers.

**Starting Point:** The FM discriminator output noise PSD (one-sided form):

$$
S_{n_o}(f) = \kappa f^2, \quad 0 \le f \le W
$$

where $\kappa = \dfrac{N_0 k_f^2}{A_c^2}$ is a constant for a given system.

#### Case A — Without De-emphasis (Plain FM)

The total mean-square noise power at the discriminator output is obtained by integrating the PSD over the message band $0$ to $W$:

$$
\sigma_n^2 = \int_0^W S_{n_o}(f)\, df = \int_0^W \kappa f^2\, df
$$

Evaluating the integral step-by-step:

$$
\sigma_n^2 = \kappa \left[ \frac{f^3}{3} \right]_0^W
$$

$$
\sigma_n^2 = \frac{\kappa W^3}{3}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Without de-emphasis, the noise power scales as the **cube** of the message bandwidth $W$. This cubic dependence is the fundamental reason why wideband FM is so noise-sensitive.

#### Case B — With De-emphasis Filter

The de-emphasis network is a first-order RC low-pass filter with magnitude-squared response:

$$
\mid H_{de}(f) \mid^2 = \frac{1}{1 + (f/f_0)^2}
$$

The filtered noise power becomes:

$$
\sigma_d^2 = \int_0^W S_{n_o}(f)\, \mid H_{de}(f) \mid^2 df = \int_0^W \frac{\kappa f^2}{1 + (f/f_0)^2}\, df
$$

**Substitution** $u = f/f_0 \Rightarrow f = f_0 u, \quad df = f_0\, du$:

Limits transform: $f=0 \Rightarrow u=0$; $f=W \Rightarrow u = W/f_0$.

$$
\sigma_d^2 = \int_0^{W/f_0} \frac{\kappa (f_0 u)^2}{1 + u^2}\, f_0\, du
$$

$$
\sigma_d^2 = \kappa f_0^3 \int_0^{W/f_0} \frac{u^2}{1 + u^2}\, du
$$

**Algebraic simplification of the integrand:**

$$
\frac{u^2}{1 + u^2} = \frac{(1 + u^2) - 1}{1 + u^2} = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + u^2}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\int \frac{u^2}{1 + u^2}\, du = \int 1\, du - \int \frac{1}{1 + u^2}\, du = u - \tan^{-1}(u) + C
$$

**Back-substituting the limits:**

$$
\sigma_d^2 = \kappa f_0^3 \left[ u - \tan^{-1}(u) \right]_0^{W/f_0}
$$

$$
\sigma_d^2 = \kappa f_0^3 \left[ \frac{W}{f_0} - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{W}{f_0}\right) - 0 \right]
$$

$$
\sigma_d^2 = \kappa f_0^2 \left[ W - f_0 \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{W}{f_0}\right) \right]
$$

#### The Improvement Factor

By definition, $\gamma$ is the ratio of noise power *without* de-emphasis to noise power *with* de-emphasis:

$$
\gamma = \frac{\sigma_n^2}{\sigma_d^2} = \frac{\kappa W^3 / 3}{\kappa f_0^2 \left[ W - f_0 \tan^{-1}(W/f_0) \right]}
$$

Since $\kappa$ cancels:

$$
\boxed{\;\gamma = \frac{W^3 \,/\, 3}{f_0^2 \left[ W - f_0 \tan^{-1}(W/f_0) \right]}\;}
$$

**Numerical evaluation for the standard KTU problem** (broadcast FM, $W = 15$ kHz, $f_0 = 2.122$ kHz):

Step 1 — Compute the ratio $W/f_0$:

$$
\frac{W}{f_0} = \frac{15}{2.122} = 7.07
$$

Step 2 — Compute $\tan^{-1}(7.07)$:

$$
\tan^{-1}(7.07) = 1.430\ \text{rad} \quad (\approx 81.9^{\circ})
$$

Step 3 — Compute the denominator term:

$$
W - f_0 \tan^{-1}(W/f_0) = 15 - (2.122)(1.430) = 15 - 3.034 = 11.966\ \text{kHz}
$$

Step 4 — Compute the denominator:

$$
f_0^2 \times 11.966 = (2.122)^2 \times 11.966 = 4.503 \times 11.966 = 53.88
$$

Step 5 — Compute the numerator:

$$
W^3 / 3 = (15)^3 / 3 = 3375 / 3 = 1125
$$

Step 6 — Final ratio:

$$
\gamma = \frac{1125}{53.88} \approx 20.88
$$

Step 7 — In decibels:

$$
\gamma_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(20.88) \approx 13.2\ \text{dB}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip:** The numerical answer of $\approx 13\ \text{dB}$ is the standard textbook result. Always carry at least **3 significant figures** in intermediate steps, and remember to convert your final ratio to dB using the $10\log_{10}$ formula.

### 3.2 Derivation of Pre-emphasis Transfer Function

The pre-emphasis filter is a first-order RC high-pass filter. Its transfer function in the frequency domain is:

$$
H_{pe}(f) = \frac{R}{R + \dfrac{1}{j2\pi f C}} = \frac{j2\pi f RC}{1 + j2\pi f RC}
$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $j2\pi f_0$ where $f_0 = 1/(2\pi RC)$:

$$
H_{pe}(f) = \frac{j(f/f_0)}{1 + j(f/f_0)} = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + j(f/f_0)}
$$

This is equivalently written as a single zero/pole form:

$$
H_{pe}(f) = 1 + j(f/f_0) \quad \text{(when cascaded with a flat-reference block)}
$$

**Verification of the cancellation property:** Multiplying the pre-emphasis and de-emphasis TFs:

$$
H_{pe}(f) \cdot H_{de}(f) = \left( 1 + j\frac{f}{f_0} \right) \cdot \frac{1}{1 + j(f/f_0)} = 1
$$

Thus, the message signal is transmitted through both networks **unmodified in amplitude and phase** — the boost is exactly undone.

### 3.3 SNR Comparison Summary

| Quantity | Without Pre/De-emphasis | With Pre/De-emphasis |
|----------|-------------------------|----------------------|
| Output noise PSD | $\kappa f^2$ (triangular) | $\kappa f^2 / (1 + (f/f_0)^2)$ (flattened at high $f$) |
| Total noise power | $\kappa W^3 / 3$ | $\kappa f_0^2 [W - f_0 \tan^{-1}(W/f_0)]$ |
| Output message power | Unchanged | Unchanged (filters cancel) |
| Output SNR | $S_o / (\kappa W^3/3)$ | $\gamma \cdot S_o / (\kappa W^3/3)$ |
| Net change | Baseline | **Multiplied by $\gamma \approx 21$** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Complete FM System with Pre-emphasis and De-emphasis

```mermaid

    PE --> MOD["FM Modulator"]
    MOD --> TX_ANT["Transmitter Antenna"]

    CH --> RX_ANT["Receiver Antenna"]
    RX_ANT --> FMIF["FM Demodulator Discriminator"]
    FMIF --> DE["De-emphasis Filter H_de(f)"]
    DE --> OUT["Recovered Message m_out(t)"]

    NOISE_BEFORE["Noise PSD Triangular f squared"] -.shapes.-> FMIF
    NOISE_AFTER["Noise PSD Flattened by de-emphasis"] -.decreased.-> DE
```

```mermaid
    B --> C["Boost High Frequencies at Transmitter using Pre-emphasis HPF"]

    D --> E["Demodulate at Receiver"]
```
