<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define pre-emphasis and de-emphasis. Why are they required in FM systems?*

**Model Answer:**
Pre-emphasis is the boosting of higher-frequency components of the message signal at the FM transmitter using a high-pass filter with transfer function $H_{pe}(f) = 1 + j(f/f_0)$, while de-emphasis is the complementary attenuation of those components at the receiver using a low-pass filter with transfer function $H_{de}(f) = 1/(1 + j(f/f_0))$, where $f_0 = 1/(2\pi\tau)$.

**Why required:** In an FM system, the noise PSD at the discriminator output varies as $f^2$, so high-frequency message components experience a much poorer SNR. Pre-emphasis boosts them before transmission, and de-emphasis restores them at the receiver while simultaneously suppressing the high-frequency noise. The net effect is a **flatter SNR across the message band** and an improvement of approximately **13 dB** in commercial broadcast FM.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is the standard time constant used for pre-emphasis and de-emphasis in commercial FM broadcasting? State the corresponding corner frequency.*

**Model Answer:**
The standard time constant is $\tau = 75\ \mu s$, which yields a corner (-3 dB) frequency of:

$$
f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\tau} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 75 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 2122\ \text{Hz} \approx 2.12\ \text{kHz}
$$

This value is fixed by international broadcast standards for compatibility between all commercial FM transmitters and receivers.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> In derivation problems (especially the $\gamma$ formula), students frequently:
> 1. **Forget to convert to decibels** at the end — KTU examiners explicitly demand the dB form for full marks.
> 2. **Skip the substitution step** $u = f/f_0$ — losing 2 marks for not showing the integral transformation.
> 3. **Confuse the integration limits** — the upper limit is $W/f_0$, NOT $W$.
> 4. **Drop the constant $\kappa$** — it must explicitly appear and then cancel in the ratio.
> 5. **Use wrong transfer function** — many students write $H_{de}(f) = 1/(1 + (f/f_0)^2)$ (magnitude only) when the derivation needs $\vert H_{de}(f)\vert^2$ inside the integral.
> Always show ALL algebraic steps; partial credit is heavily tied to visible work in derivations.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **noise improvement factor $\gamma$** in an FM system employing a first-order de-emphasis filter with time constant $\tau$. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a commercial FM broadcast system with message bandwidth $W = 15$ kHz and pre-emphasis time constant $\tau = 75\ \mu s$, calculate the **noise improvement factor in dB** and comment on the practical significance of this value. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — State the FM noise PSD at discriminator output** (1 mark):
Assuming white Gaussian noise of two-sided PSD $N_0/2$ at the receiver input, the one-sided noise PSD at the FM discriminator output is:

$$
S_{n_o}(f) = \frac{N_0\, k_f^2}{A_c^2}\, f^2 = \kappa f^2, \quad 0 \le f \le W
$$

where $\kappa = N_0 k_f^2 / A_c^2$ is a system constant.

**Step 2 — Total noise power without de-emphasis** (1 mark):

$$
\sigma_n^2 = \int_0^W \kappa f^2\, df = \kappa \left[ \frac{f^3}{3} \right]_0^W = \frac{\kappa W^3}{3}
$$

**Step 3 — Magnitude-squared response of the first-order de-emphasis filter** (1 mark):
The de-emphasis filter is a first-order RC low-pass filter with:

$$
\mid H_{de}(f) \mid^2 = \frac{1}{1 + (f/f_0)^2}, \quad f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}
$$

**Step 4 — Total noise power with de-emphasis** (2 marks):

$$
\sigma_d^2 = \int_0^W \frac{\kappa f^2}{1 + (f/f_0)^2}\, df
$$

Using substitution $u = f/f_0$:

$$
\sigma_d^2 = \kappa f_0^3 \int_0^{W/f_0} \frac{u^2}{1+u^2}\, du = \kappa f_0^3 \left[ u - \tan^{-1}(u) \right]_0^{W/f_0}
$$

$$
\sigma_d^2 = \kappa f_0^2 \left[ W - f_0 \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{W}{f_0}\right) \right]
$$

**Step 5 — Define and compute the improvement factor** (2 marks):

$$
\boxed{\;\gamma = \frac{\sigma_n^2}{\sigma_d^2} = \frac{W^3/3}{f_0^2 \left[ W - f_0 \tan^{-1}(W/f_0) \right]}\;}
$$

**Assumptions used:** white Gaussian channel noise; flat message signal spectrum (worst case); message bandlimited to $W$; first-order de-emphasis filter.

---

**(b) Numerical evaluation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute corner frequency** (1 mark):

$$
f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi \tau} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 75 \times 10^{-6}} = 2122\ \text{Hz}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $W/f_0$** (1 mark):

$$
\frac{W}{f_0} = \frac{15000}{2122} = 7.07
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\tan^{-1}(W/f_0)$** (1 mark):

$$
\tan^{-1}(7.07) = 1.43\ \text{rad}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute denominator term** (1 mark):

$$
W - f_0 \tan^{-1}(W/f_0) = 15000 - 2122 \times 1.43 = 15000 - 3034 = 11966\ \text{Hz}
$$

**Step 5 — Form the ratio** (1 mark):

$$
\gamma = \frac{(15000)^3 / 3}{(2122)^2 \times 11966} = \frac{1.125 \times 10^{12}}{5.39 \times 10^{10}} \approx 20.88
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to dB** (1 mark):

$$
\gamma_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(20.88) = 13.2\ \text{dB}
$$

**Step 7 — Practical significance comment** (1 mark):
A **13.2 dB improvement** is equivalent to multiplying the transmitter power by a factor of approximately **20**, or extending the broadcast range by ~30%, with no increase in transmitter power or bandwidth. This is precisely why pre-emphasis/de-emphasis has been a universal feature of FM broadcasting since the 1950s.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Alternative]**

**(a)** With the help of a **block diagram**, explain the operation of an FM communication system employing pre-emphasis at the transmitter and de-emphasis at the receiver. Show mathematically that the **cascaded response** of the two filters is unity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **de-emphasis filter output noise power** $\sigma_d^2$ in terms of $W$ and $f_0$, and explain how it differs from the noise power in a plain FM system. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**(a) Block diagram and unity cascade property (7 marks):**

**Block diagram explanation (4 marks):**

The system consists of the following blocks in cascade:

1. **Message source** $m(t)$ → fed into the **pre-emphasis network** (a simple RC high-pass filter with capacitor in series and resistor to ground, or equivalent active HPF) with transfer function $H_{pe}(f) = 1 + j(f/f_0)$.
2. **Pre-emphasized signal** → fed into the **FM modulator** (VCO or Armstrong indirect modulator).
3. **Modulated FM signal** $s(t)$ → transmitted over the **channel with additive white Gaussian noise** $n_w(t)$.
4. **Received signal** → fed into the **FM demodulator** (frequency discriminator or PLL).
5. **Discriminator output** → fed into the **de-emphasis network** (a first-order RC low-pass filter with resistor in series and capacitor to ground) with transfer function $H_{de}(f) = 1/(1 + j(f/f_0))$.
6. **De-emphasized output** → recovered message $\hat{m}(t) \approx m(t)$.

**Unity cascade proof (3 marks):**

$$
H_{pe}(f) \cdot H_{de}(f) = \left[ 1 + j\frac{f}{f_0} \right] \cdot \frac{1}{1 + j(f/f_0)}
$$

$$
= \frac{1 + j(f/f_0)}{1 + j(f/f_0)} = 1
$$

Therefore, for the message signal:

$$
M_{\text{out}}(f) = M(f) \cdot H_{pe}(f) \cdot H_{de}(f) = M(f) \cdot 1 = M(f)
$$

The message is transmitted **unaffected** in both amplitude and phase. However, noise entering the channel passes through only the de-emphasis filter and is therefore suppressed above $f_0$.

---

**(b) Derivation of $\sigma_d^2$ and comparison (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Write the noise PSD** (1 mark):

$$
S_{n_o}(f) = \kappa f^2
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the de-emphasis filter** (1 mark):

$$
\sigma_d^2 = \int_0^W S_{n_o}(f) \mid H_{de}(f) \mid^2 df = \int_0^W \frac{\kappa f^2}{1 + (f/f_0)^2}\, df
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and integrate** (3 marks):

Using $u = f/f_0$:

$$
\sigma_d^2 = \kappa f_0^3 \int_0^{W/f_0} \frac{u^2}{1 + u^2}\, du
$$

Decomposing the integrand:

$$
\frac{u^2}{1 + u^2} = 1 - \frac{1}{1+u^2}
$$

$$
\sigma_d^2 = \kappa f_0^3 \left[ u - \tan^{-1}(u) \right]_0^{W/f_0} = \kappa f_0^2 \left[ W - f_0 \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{W}{f_0}\right) \right]
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with plain FM** (2 marks):
Plain FM: $\sigma_n^2 = \kappa W^3 / 3$, which is the noise power integrated over the full triangular spectrum.

With de-emphasis: The high-frequency portion of the triangular spectrum is suppressed by the $(1 + (f/f_0)^2)$ factor, so $\sigma_d^2 < \sigma_n^2$. The ratio $\gamma = \sigma_n^2/\sigma_d^2 > 1$ represents the noise reduction achieved. The reduction is most pronounced when $W \gg f_0$, which is exactly the case in commercial FM ($W/f_0 \approx 7$).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Pre-emphasis & De-emphasis**

- **Core definition:** Pre-emphasis = boost HF at TX; De-emphasis = attenuate HF at RX. Filters are exact complements.
- **Filter types:** Pre-emphasis is HPF; De-emphasis is LPF. Both are first-order RC networks.
- **Cascade property:** $H_{pe}(f) \cdot H_{de}(f) = 1$ — message signal is unchanged end-to-end.
- **Standard time constant:** $\tau = 75\ \mu s$ for commercial FM broadcasting (NOT a design choice — it is fixed by international standards).
- **Corner frequency:** $f_0 = 1/(2\pi\tau) \approx 2122\ \text{Hz}$.
- **Why needed:** FM discriminator output noise PSD grows as $f^2$ (triangular spectrum). HF message components suffer more noise.
- **Noise PSD without de-emphasis:** $S_{n_o}(f) = (N_0 k_f^2/A_c^2) f^2$.
- **Total noise power without de-emphasis:** $\sigma_n^2 = \kappa W^3/3$ (cubic in bandwidth).
- **Total noise power with de-emphasis:** $\sigma_d^2 = \kappa f_0^2 [W - f_0 \tan^{-1}(W/f_0)]$.
- **Improvement factor formula:** $\gamma = \dfrac{W^3/3}{f_0^2[W - f_0 \tan^{-1}(W/f_0)]}$.
- **Typical improvement:** $\gamma \approx 21$ (linear) $\approx 13\ \text{dB}$ for $W = 15$ kHz, $\tau = 75\ \mu s$.
- **Practical equivalence:** 13 dB SNR gain is equivalent to a 20× increase in transmitter power.
- **Derivation steps to remember:**
  1. Write $\sigma_n^2 = \int \kappa f^2 df$ (no filter).
  2. Multiply by $\mid H_{de}(f)\vert^2$ for filtered case.
  3. Substitute $u = f/f_0$.
  4. Decompose $u^2/(1+u^2) = 1 - 1/(1+u^2)$.
  5. Integrate to get $u - \tan^{-1}(u)$.
  6. Take ratio for $\gamma$.
- **Output message signal:** Unchanged in amplitude because filters cancel; only noise is reduced.
- **Common mistake 1:** Forgetting the dB conversion at the end.
- **Common mistake 2:** Wrong integration limits (use $W/f_0$, not $W$).
- **Common mistake 3:** Using $H_{de}$ instead of $\mid H_{de}\vert^2$ inside the noise integral.
- **Common mistake 4:** Confusing pre-emphasis (HPF) with de-emphasis (LPF) at the wrong end of the system.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: FM Transmitter and Receiver
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET864 - ELECTRONIC COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** FM Transmitter and Receiver

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 FM Transmitter — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **FM Transmitter (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> An **FM (Frequency Modulation) Transmitter** is a radio frequency (RF) communication system that encodes an information-bearing baseband message signal into a high-frequency carrier wave by varying its **instantaneous frequency** in direct proportion to the amplitude of the modulating signal, while keeping the carrier's amplitude and phase relatively constant. The transmitted signal is mathematically expressed as:
> $$s(t) = A_c \cos\left(2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_{0}^{t} m(\tau)\, d\tau\right)$$
> where $A_c$ is the carrier amplitude, $f_c$ is the carrier frequency, $k_f$ is the frequency sensitivity (Hz/volt), and $m(t)$ is the message signal.

### 1.2 FM Receiver — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FM Receiver (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> A **Frequency Modulation (FM) Receiver** is a superheterodyne radio receiver designed to demodulate FM signals by down-converting the incoming RF signal to a fixed **Intermediate Frequency (IF) of 10.7 MHz** (the standard KTU/Kerala-broadcast value), amplifying it through cascaded IF stages, amplitude-limiting it, and then extracting the original message signal using a **frequency discriminator** (Foster–Seeley, Ratio detector, or PLL).

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing at the edge of a swimming pool holding a rope tied to a floating ball.

- **Amplitude Modulation (AM)** would be like moving the ball up and down (varying the *height* of the wave) while keeping the speed of waves constant.
- **Frequency Modulation (FM)** is like changing **how fast the waves hit the ball** (the *frequency* of the wiggles) — when you wiggle the rope vigorously, the frequency of the ripples on the water surface increases. The information is hidden in the **rate of change**, not in the height.

A real-world FM transmitter works in much the same way: the **microphone voltage** is the "wiggling force" that pushes and pulls the frequency of a high-frequency carrier (typically **88 MHz – 108 MHz** for commercial broadcast FM). The louder you speak into the microphone, the more the carrier's frequency deviates from its rest value.

For the receiver, think of a **tuning fork**: you strike a fork vibrating at **440 Hz** in a noisy room, and your ear can pick it out because the brain is naturally good at detecting *frequency* patterns. An FM receiver is an electronic version of this — it locks onto the *frequency* of the incoming carrier and discards any amplitude variations caused by noise. This is precisely why FM is more immune to lightning, ignition noise, and static than AM.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Example:** All India Radio (AIR) FM Gold (93.5 MHz, Kerala), Radio Mango 91.9 MHz, and club FM 94.3 MHz all use FM transmitters in the VHF Band-II (88–108 MHz) defined by international broadcast standards.

### 1.4 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| FM Broadcast Band (Worldwide) | **88 MHz – 108 MHz** |
| Channel Spacing | **200 kHz** |
| Maximum Frequency Deviation ($\Delta f$) | **±75 kHz** |
| Maximum Modulating Frequency ($f_m$) | **15 kHz** |
| FM Intermediate Frequency (IF) | **10.7 MHz** |
| Pre-emphasis Time Constant ($\tau$) | **75 µs** |
| Carson's Rule Bandwidth factor | **$\beta_{CR} = \Delta f + f_m$** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Block-Level Architecture of an FM System

An FM communication system consists of **three major subsystems**:

1. **FM Transmitter** — Generates the modulated RF signal.
2. **Channel** — Free-space propagation (typically VHF/UHF).
3. **FM Receiver** — Demodulates the message signal at the user end.

---

### 2.2 FM Transmitter — Two Principal Methods of Frequency Modulation

#### Method A: Direct FM Transmitter (Reactance Modulator)

- The modulating signal **directly** varies the frequency of the carrier oscillator (e.g., by changing the capacitance of a varactor diode in an LC tank).
- **Drawback:** Carrier frequency stability is poor because any drift in the LC oscillator is interpreted as modulation.

#### Method B: Indirect FM Transmitter — The Armstrong System

- The modulating signal first phase-modulates a **low-frequency crystal-controlled carrier** (e.g., at 100 kHz).
- A **frequency multiplier chain** (doublers + triplers) is then used to raise both the carrier frequency and the frequency deviation to broadcast levels.
- **Advantage:** Excellent carrier stability because the crystal oscillator is the reference.

---

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Operation of an Armstrong FM Transmitter

The Armstrong indirect FM transmitter is a **standard KTU 2024 syllabus topic** and consists of the following cascaded blocks:

| # | Block | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Crystal Oscillator** | Generates a highly stable carrier $f_1$ (typically 100 kHz) |
| 2 | **Balanced Modulator** | Produces DSB-SC output (suppresses carrier) |
| 3 | **Phase Shifter (90°)** | Provides quadrature carrier for phase modulator |
| 4 | **Phase Modulator** | Combines message with carrier to produce NBFM |
| 5 | **Frequency Multipliers** (×2, ×3 etc.) | Boost frequency deviation $\Delta f$ and carrier $f_c$ |
| 6 | **Mixer + Local Oscillator** | Up-converts frequency to the final RF band |
| 7 | **Power Amplifier (PA)** | Boosts output power for transmission via antenna |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Concept — Frequency Multipliers:**
> A frequency multiplier of order $n$ increases both the carrier frequency and the frequency deviation by the same factor $n$.
> - New Carrier: $f_c' = n \cdot f_c$
> - New Deviation: $\Delta f' = n \cdot \Delta f$

The **modulation index** is preserved by a frequency multiplier:
$$\beta = \frac{\Delta f}{f_m} = \frac{n \cdot \Delta f}{n \cdot f_m}$$

---

### 2.4 FM Receiver — Superheterodyne Architecture

The **FM superheterodyne receiver** (invented by Edwin Armstrong, 1918) is the universal architecture. Its cascaded blocks are:

| # | Block | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **RF Amplifier (LNA)** | Selects desired station, rejects image frequency |
| 2 | **Mixer + Local Oscillator** | Translates RF to fixed **IF of 10.7 MHz** |
| 3 | **IF Amplifier (cascaded)** | Provides most of the gain and adjacent-channel selectivity |
| 4 | **Limiter** | Removes amplitude variations (AM noise), produces constant-amplitude IF |
| 5 | **FM Discriminator (Detector)** | Converts frequency variations back to voltage (message) |
| 6 | **De-emphasis Network** | Restores flat frequency response (75 µs) |
| 7 | **Audio Amplifier** | Drives loudspeaker |
| 8 | **AFC (Automatic Frequency Control)** | Locks LO to incoming carrier to minimize drift |

> [!TIP]
> **Image Frequency Rejection:**
> For an FM receiver with IF = 10.7 MHz and a station at $f_s$, the image frequency is $f_{image} = f_s + 2 \times 10.7$ MHz. The RF amplifier's preselector must reject this image — failure to do so results in a "ghost station" appearing at the same dial position.

---

### 2.5 FM Demodulator (Discriminator) — The Detection Stage

The Limiter output is a **constant-amplitude IF signal with frequency variation**. The job of the discriminator is to convert this **frequency variation into voltage variation**. Common methods:

#### A. Slope Detector
- A tuned circuit is deliberately **detuned** so its amplitude response varies linearly with frequency in the region of interest.
- The signal is passed through the detuned tank, producing AM, which is then envelope-detected.

#### B. Balanced Slope Detector
- Two slope detectors working in push-pull on opposite sides of the IF center frequency.
- Sum of outputs is the demodulated audio.

#### C. Foster–Seeley Discriminator
- Uses a **double-tuned RF transformer** with a center-tapped secondary and a phase-shift network.
- Output voltage is proportional to the frequency deviation around the IF center.
- **Most commonly asked in KTU exams.**

#### D. Ratio Detector
- A modified Foster–Seeley with a built-in large capacitor providing **inherent AM limiting**.
- Does not require a separate limiter stage.

#### E. PLL (Phase-Locked Loop) Detector
- A modern, mathematically elegant detector using a VCO, phase detector, and loop filter.
- The VCO tracks the incoming frequency, and the control voltage to the VCO is the recovered message.

---

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is a high-density revision of every formula KTU examiners expect for FM transmitter/receiver numericals. **Memorize these equations.**

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta f = k_f \cdot \vert m(t) \vert_{max}$ | Frequency deviation in Hz |
| 2 | $\beta = \dfrac{\Delta f}{f_m}$ | Modulation index of FM (also called *deviation ratio*) |
| 3 | $BW_{Carson's} = 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2(\beta + 1) f_m$ | Carson's rule for 98% power |
| 4 | $BW_{Bessel} = 2(\beta + 1) f_m$ (for $\beta < 2$); $2(\beta + 2) f_m$ (general) | Bessel-based rule of thumb |
| 5 | $\text{SNR}_{FM} = \dfrac{3}{2} \beta^2 (\text{SNR}_{AM, baseband})$ | FM SNR advantage over AM |
| 6 | $S_{FM,out} = \dfrac{A_c^2 \beta^2}{2}$ | Output SNR of FM |
| 7 | $f_{IF} = \vert f_{LO} - f_{RF} \vert$ | Mixer IF relation |
| 8 | $f_{image} = f_{RF} + 2 f_{IF}$ | Image frequency |
| 9 | $f_c' = n \cdot f_c$ ; $\Delta f' = n \cdot \Delta f$ | Effect of ×n frequency multiplier |
| 10 | $\Delta f_{total} = n_1 n_2 \dots n_k \cdot \Delta f_{0}$ | Cascade of multipliers |
| 11 | $f_{c,final} = \dfrac{n_1 n_2 \dots n_k f_{c,0} \pm f_{LO}}{m}$ | After mixing + multiplication |
| 12 | $N_{eff} = N_0 \cdot BW$ | Effective noise power at receiver input |
| 13 | $S_i = \dfrac{A_c^2}{2}$ | Input signal power of FM |
| 14 | $NF = \dfrac{SNR_{in}}{SNR_{out}}$ | Noise Figure of receiver |
| 15 | $\tau = 75\,\mu s$ ; $f_{3dB} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \tau} \approx 2.122\,\text{kHz}$ | Pre-emphasis / De-emphasis time constant |
| 16 | $\dfrac{S_o}{N_o} = \dfrac{3 A_c^2 k_f^2 \overline{m^2(t)}}{8 \pi^2 N_0 f_m^3}$ | FM output SNR (single-tone message) |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Broadcasting:** FM radio (88–108 MHz) for high-fidelity music.
- **Two-way Radio:** Police, taxi, marine (typically 150–174 MHz, 450–470 MHz).
- **TV Audio:** The sound carrier of analog TV used FM.
- **Mobile Radio:** Early cellular systems (AMPS, NMT) used FM.
- **Radar:** Chirp radar uses wideband linear FM (LFMCW).
- **Wireless Mics & Walkie-Talkies:** Narrowband FM (NBFM) with $\beta < 2$.
- **Satellite Telemetry:** FM-FM multiplexing used in early spacecraft.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Frequency Multiplier Effect on Deviation and Modulation Index

**Problem Statement (KTU Pattern):**
> An Armstrong indirect FM transmitter uses a crystal oscillator at $f_1 = 100$ kHz. The phase modulator produces a peak phase deviation of $\Delta \phi = 0.1$ rad for a message of $f_m = 5$ kHz. A chain of multipliers (×2 → ×3 → ×2 → ×3) is used before mixing with a local oscillator of $f_{LO} = 79.4$ MHz. Find the final carrier frequency, the final frequency deviation, and the final modulation index.

**Given Data:**
- $f_1 = 100$ kHz
- $\Delta \phi_{peak} = 0.1$ rad
- $f_m = 5$ kHz
- Multipliers: ×2, ×3, ×2, ×3 (in cascade)

**Step 1 — Initial Modulation Index of NBFM**
For a phase modulator, the modulation index is the peak phase deviation:
$$\beta_0 = \Delta \phi_{peak} = 0.1$$

**Step 2 — Initial Frequency Deviation**
The frequency deviation is related to modulation index and message frequency:
$$\beta_0 = \frac{\Delta f_0}{f_m} \implies \Delta f_0 = \beta_0 \cdot f_m = 0.1 \times 5\,\text{kHz} = 0.5\,\text{kHz} = 500\,\text{Hz}$$

**Step 3 — Total Frequency Multiplication Factor**
Cascading multipliers multiplies the multiplication factors:
$$n_{total} = 2 \times 3 \times 2 \times 3 = 36$$

**Step 4 — Carrier Frequency After Multipliers**
$$f_{c,mult} = n_{total} \cdot f_1 = 36 \times 100\,\text{kHz} = 3.6\,\text{MHz}$$

**Step 5 — Frequency Deviation After Multipliers**
$$\Delta f_{mult} = n_{total} \cdot \Delta f_0 = 36 \times 500\,\text{Hz} = 18\,\text{kHz}$$

**Step 6 — Mixer Up-Conversion**
Mixing with $f_{LO} = 79.4$ MHz (taking the **sum** to get to broadcast band):
$$f_{c,final} = f_{c,mult} + f_{LO} = 3.6 + 79.4 = 83.0\,\text{MHz}$$
Mixing does **not** change the frequency deviation, so:
$$\Delta f_{final} = \Delta f_{mult} = 18\,\text{kHz}$$

**Step 7 — Final Modulation Index**
$$\beta_{final} = \frac{\Delta f_{final}}{f_m} = \frac{18\,\text{kHz}}{5\,\text{kHz}} = 3.6$$

**Verification — Modulation Index Invariance**
A frequency multiplier of order $n$ multiplies both deviation and message frequency by $n$, leaving $\beta$ unchanged. In the mixer stage, the message frequency (and thus $\beta$) is also invariant. Hence:
$$\beta_{final} = \beta_0 = 0.1 \text{ (before multiplication) ... but the above calculation shows } 3.6!$$

**Correction Note for Students:**
The modulation index is **not** preserved in the original phase-modulator form, because the phase modulator output is **NBFM (narrowband)**, where $\Delta f_0 = \beta_0 \cdot f_m$ already uses the message frequency. After frequency multiplication, the carrier goes up by $n$, but $f_m$ is still $5$ kHz (we do **not** multiply the message). Hence:
$$\beta_{final} = \frac{n \cdot \Delta f_0}{f_m} = n \cdot \beta_0 = 36 \times 0.1 = 3.6 \;\checkmark$$

This is the **crucial KTU exam point** — the modulation index *increases* by the multiplier factor because the multiplier amplifies deviation but not the message frequency.

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Carson's Rule Bandwidth

**Derivation Goal:** Show that $BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m)$ contains 98% of the total FM power.

**Step 1 — Bessel Function Power Distribution**
For an FM signal with modulation index $\beta$, the amplitude of the $n^{th}$ sideband pair is $J_n(\beta)$ (Bessel function of the first kind, order $n$). The total power in the carrier and all sidebands is constant:
$$P_{total} = \frac{A_c^2}{2} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n^2(\beta) = \frac{A_c^2}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Significant Sidebands**
A Bessel function $J_n(\beta)$ is "negligible" when $n > \beta + 1$. So the highest significant sideband order is approximately $n_{max} = \beta + 1$.

**Step 3 — Bandwidth from Highest Sideband**
The $n_{max}^{th}$ sideband is at frequency $f_c + n_{max} f_m = f_c + (\beta + 1) f_m$. The total occupied bandwidth (one side up, one side down) is:
$$BW = 2 \times n_{max} \times f_m = 2(\beta + 1) f_m$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $\beta = \Delta f / f_m$**
$$BW = 2\left(\frac{\Delta f}{f_m} + 1\right) f_m = 2(\Delta f + f_m)$$

This is **Carson's Rule**, denoted as $BW_{CR}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Carson's Rule Bandwidth is the most cited KTU numerical formula for FM.** Always remember:
> $$BW_{Carson's} = 2(\Delta f + f_m)$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation — Foster–Seeley Discriminator Output

**Setup:** A double-tuned transformer with primary and secondary tuned to $f_c$ (the IF center frequency, e.g., 10.7 MHz). The secondary is center-tapped, and a tertiary winding is added in series with a capacitor $C_3$ to provide a 90° reference voltage at $f_c$.

**Step 1 — Primary Voltage**
$$e_1 = E_1 \cos(\omega t)$$

**Step 2 — Secondary Voltage (center-tapped, 180° between halves)**
$$e_2 = E_2 \sin(\omega t - 90°) \quad \text{(in quadrature with primary at resonance)}$$

**Step 3 — Reference Voltage from Tertiary**
The tertiary voltage is in phase with the primary (or shifted by 90° for the discriminator):
$$e_3 = E_3 \cos(\omega t)$$

**Step 4 — Diodes D1 and D2 detect the sum and difference**
The two diode outputs are:
$$v_{d1} \propto \vert e_3 + \tfrac{e_2}{2} \vert \quad \text{and} \quad v_{d2} \propto \vert e_3 - \tfrac{e_2}{2} \vert$$

**Step 5 — Net Discriminator Output**
$$v_o = v_{d1} - v_{d2} \propto \frac{e_2}{e_3}$$

**Step 6 — Linear Approximation Near $f_c$**
For small deviations $\delta f = f - f_c$:
$$v_o \approx K \cdot \delta f$$

where $K$ is the discriminator constant (in V/Hz). This is the **demodulated audio voltage**.

> [!TIP]
> The Foster–Seeley S-curve is an **S-shaped characteristic** centered at $f_c$. The linear region is typically $\pm 100$ kHz around the 10.7 MHz IF center.

---

### 3.4 Derivation — Image Frequency and RF Tuner Selectivity

**Problem:** An FM receiver is tuned to $f_{RF} = 98.3$ MHz. The IF is 10.7 MHz.

**Step 1 — Local Oscillator Frequency (high-side injection)**
$$f_{LO} = f_{RF} + f_{IF} = 98.3 + 10.7 = 109.0\,\text{MHz}$$

**Step 2 — Image Frequency**
The image is the frequency that, when mixed with $f_{LO}$, also produces $f_{IF}$:
$$f_{image} = f_{LO} + f_{IF} = 109.0 + 10.7 = 119.7\,\text{MHz}$$
or equivalently:
$$f_{image} = f_{RF} + 2 f_{IF} = 98.3 + 21.4 = 119.7\,\text{MHz} \;\checkmark$$

**Step 3 — Required RF Preselector Rejection**
The RF amplifier must reject $f_{image} = 119.7$ MHz while passing $f_{RF} = 98.3$ MHz. This requires a high-Q bandpass filter with at least **40 dB rejection** at $\pm 21.4$ MHz offset.

### 3.5 Summary of Numerical Relationships

| Quantity | Effect of ×n Multiplier | Effect of Mixer |
|---|---|---|
| Carrier Frequency $f_c$ | Multiplied by $n$ | Shifted by $f_{LO}$ (sum or difference) |
| Frequency Deviation $\Delta f$ | Multiplied by $n$ | **Unchanged** |
| Message Frequency $f_m$ | **Unchanged** | **Unchanged** |
| Modulation Index $\beta$ | Multiplied by $n$ | **Unchanged** |
| Total Power | **Unchanged** | **Unchanged** |
| Phase $\phi(t)$ | Multiplied by $n$ (PM→FM property) | **Unchanged** |

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*List any three advantages of FM over AM.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — allocate one per point):**

1. **Better noise immunity** — FM is less affected by amplitude noise (e.g., lightning, ignition, thermal noise) because information is in frequency, not amplitude. The limiter stage removes amplitude perturbations before detection.
2. **Improved Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** — FM offers an SNR improvement of $3\beta^2(\beta+1)$ over AM, where $\beta$ is the modulation index. For $\beta = 5$, this is a 22 dB advantage.
3. **Capture Effect** — An FM receiver naturally suppresses weaker co-channel or interfering stations; only the strongest signal is demodulated. This effectively reduces cross-talk.
4. *(Bonus 4th point)*: FM can support higher fidelity audio (15 kHz vs ~4.5 kHz in AM broadcast) due to wider bandwidth.

**Marks Distribution (3):** 1 mark for each correctly stated advantage with one-line explanation.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Define pre-emphasis and de-emphasis. Why is the time constant 75 µs?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Pre-emphasis** is a high-pass filtering (with $\tau = RC = 75\,\mu s$) applied to the message signal at the **transmitter** before FM modulation, which boosts the high-frequency audio components (above 2.122 kHz).
- **De-emphasis** is the inverse low-pass filtering applied at the **receiver** after FM demodulation, which restores the original flat spectrum.
- The 75 µs time constant is an **international standard** chosen so that $f_{3dB} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \tau} \approx 2.122$ kHz, which corresponds to the **thermal noise corner** in FM systems. Above this frequency, the noise spectral density rises linearly (triangular noise), and pre-emphasis compensates by boosting the signal at the same rate, yielding a **flat output SNR** across the audio band.

**Marks Distribution (3):** 1 mark for each of: pre-emphasis definition, de-emphasis definition, and the 75 µs rationale.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Two full 14-mark questions, each with two sub-parts of 7 marks. Internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of an **Armstrong indirect FM transmitter**. Show how a chain of frequency multipliers is used to achieve the required frequency deviation for commercial FM broadcasting (75 kHz) from an initial NBFM deviation of 25 Hz.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An Armstrong FM transmitter has a message signal $f_m = 5$ kHz. The phase modulator produces a peak phase deviation of $\Delta \phi = 0.25$ rad. A frequency multiplier chain of ×2, ×3, ×2, and ×3 is used. The crystal oscillator frequency is 200 kHz. The mixer LO is 90.2 MHz. Calculate the final carrier frequency, final frequency deviation, and the final modulation index.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-A (a)**

**Block Diagram:** (See Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram — reproduce in answer sheet as a labeled rectangular block diagram.)

**Operation — Step by Step (1 mark per step, capped at 5 marks):**

1. The **crystal oscillator** generates a highly stable low-frequency carrier $f_1$ (e.g., 200 kHz).
2. The message signal $m(t)$ is fed to a **phase modulator**, which uses a 90° phase-shifted carrier from a **balanced modulator** to produce **NBFM** (narrowband FM).
3. The NBFM output is passed through a **cascaded frequency multiplier chain** (×2, ×3, ×2, ×3 in this case). Each multiplier of order $n$ increases the carrier frequency and frequency deviation by factor $n$, but leaves the message frequency unchanged.
4. **Total multiplication factor:** $n = 2 \times 3 \times 2 \times 3 = 36$.
5. The multiplied FM signal is then **mixed** with a local oscillator to shift the carrier into the **88–108 MHz broadcast band**.
6. The **power amplifier** boosts the signal, and the antenna radiates it.

**Numerical Demonstration of Multiplier Effect (2 marks):**

Given initial deviation $\Delta f_0 = 25$ Hz and $f_m = 15$ kHz:
- Initial modulation index: $\beta_0 = \dfrac{25}{15000} = 1.667 \times 10^{-3}$ (NBFM)
- After total multiplication by 36:
  - $\Delta f_{final} = 36 \times 25 = 900$ Hz
- To reach 75 kHz, additional multiplier stage(s) are required.
- A ×100 multiplier (achievable via ×2, ×5, ×10, or higher) would give $\Delta f = 25 \times 100 = 2500$ Hz — still not enough.
- A ×3000 total multiplication would yield $\Delta f = 25 \times 3000 = 75$ kHz ✓ — hence the multiplier chain must have a combined gain of 3000 (e.g., ×2, ×3, ×2, ×3, ×5, ×5 in practice).

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
The Armstrong transmitter uses a low-deviation NBFM generated at a stable low frequency, then **systematically multiplies** the deviation up to broadcast specifications, preserving carrier stability through the crystal reference.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-A (b)**

**Given:**
- $f_m = 5$ kHz
- $\Delta \phi = 0.25$ rad
- Multipliers: ×2, ×3, ×2, ×3 → $n_{total} = 36$
- $f_1 = 200$ kHz
- $f_{LO} = 90.2$ MHz

**Step 1 — Initial Modulation Index**
$$\beta_0 = \Delta \phi = 0.25$$

**Step 2 — Initial Frequency Deviation**
$$\Delta f_0 = \beta_0 \cdot f_m = 0.25 \times 5\,\text{kHz} = 1.25\,\text{kHz}$$

**Step 3 — Carrier Frequency After Multiplication**
$$f_{c,mult} = 36 \times 200\,\text{kHz} = 7.2\,\text{MHz}$$

**Step 4 — Deviation After Multiplication**
$$\Delta f_{mult} = 36 \times 1.25\,\text{kHz} = 45\,\text{kHz}$$

**Step 5 — Carrier After Mixing (using sum)**
$$f_{c,final} = f_{c,mult} + f_{LO} = 7.2 + 90.2 = 97.4\,\text{MHz}$$

**Step 6 — Final Deviation (mixer does not change deviation)**
$$\Delta f_{final} = 45\,\text{kHz}$$

**Step 7 — Final Modulation Index**
$$\beta_{final} = \frac{\Delta f_{final}}{f_m} = \frac{45\,\text{kHz}}{5\,\text{kHz}} = 9$$

**Verification:**
$$\beta_{final} = n_{total} \cdot \beta_0 = 36 \times 0.25 = 9 \;\checkmark$$

**Final Answer:**
- $f_{c,final} = 97.4$ MHz ✓
- $\Delta f_{final} = 45$ kHz ✓
- $\beta_{final} = 9$ ✓

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Draw the block diagram of a **superheterodyne FM receiver** and explain the function of each block. Why is the Intermediate Frequency (IF) chosen to be **10.7 MHz** specifically for FM?

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a circuit diagram, explain the working of the **Foster–Seeley discriminator** for FM detection. Compare it with the **ratio detector**.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-B (a)**

**Block Diagram:** (Reproduce the Mermaid diagram from Section 4.2 as a labeled sketch.)

**Functions of Each Block (1 mark per block, capped at 5 marks):**

| Block | Function |
|---|---|
| **Antenna** | Captures the FM RF signal from free space |
| **RF Amplifier** | Selects the desired station using a tunable bandpass filter; amplifies the weak RF signal; rejects the **image frequency** |
| **Mixer + Local Oscillator** | Converts the incoming RF to a fixed IF (10.7 MHz) using a heterodyning process |
| **IF Amplifier** | Provides most of the receiver gain and adjacent-channel selectivity; operates at fixed 10.7 MHz for stable design |
| **Limiter** | Removes amplitude variations (AM noise) and produces a constant-amplitude IF signal |
| **FM Discriminator** | Converts frequency variations in the IF back to audio voltage |
| **De-emphasis** | Applies the 75 µs low-pass filter to restore flat audio response |
| **Audio Amplifier** | Boosts the recovered audio to drive a loudspeaker |

**Why IF = 10.7 MHz? (2 marks):**

1. **Attenuation vs. frequency trade-off:** Below 10 MHz, atmospheric and man-made noise is high; above 30 MHz, image frequency rejection becomes difficult due to RF amplifier limitations. 10.7 MHz is a **sweet spot**.
2. **Standardization:** Worldwide convention (CCIR/FCC/ETSI) for FM broadcast reception.
3. **Adjacent channel rejection:** 10.7 MHz IF allows 200 kHz channel spacing with good selectivity.
4. **Image frequency** $= f_{RF} + 2 \times 10.7$ MHz — easily rejected by RF preselector.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-B (b)**

**Foster–Seeley Circuit Description (3 marks):**

The Foster–Seeley discriminator consists of a **double-tuned IF transformer** with:
- A **primary winding** (L1) tuned to the IF center frequency (10.7 MHz).
- A **center-tapped secondary winding** (L2) also tuned to 10.7 MHz.
- A **tertiary winding** (L3) tightly coupled to L1, providing a reference voltage via capacitor C3.
- Two diodes (D1, D2) connected to the ends of the center-tapped secondary.
- Two load capacitors (C1, C2) and load resistors (R1, R2).

**Working (2 marks):**
- At the **center frequency** $f_c$, the voltages induced in the two halves of L2 are equal in magnitude but **opposite in phase** with respect to the tertiary voltage. The diode currents are equal, and the net output $v_o = v_{d1} - v_{d2} = 0$.
- When the **input frequency deviates above** $f_c$, one diode conducts more than the other, producing a **positive output voltage**.
- When the **input frequency deviates below** $f_c$, the polarity reverses, giving a **negative output voltage**.
- The output vs. frequency characteristic is an **S-shaped curve** centered at 10.7 MHz, with a **linear region** of approximately ±100 kHz — well beyond the ±75 kHz deviation of broadcast FM.

**Comparison with Ratio Detector (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Foster–Seeley | Ratio Detector |
|---|---|---|
| AM rejection | Requires external limiter | **Inherent AM limiting** (large C across load) |
| Output amplitude | Higher (sum of diode outputs) | Lower (ratio of diode outputs) |
| Complexity | Simpler | Slightly more complex (extra diode, large C) |
| Sensitivity | Higher | Slightly lower |
| Use | Commercial FM broadcast | Low-cost FM receivers |

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
The Foster–Seeley is preferred where high audio output and external limiting are acceptable. The ratio detector is preferred for cost-effective consumer FM radios due to its built-in AM rejection.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU FM Transmitter/Receiver Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting that mixers do NOT change frequency deviation** — only carrier frequency shifts. Students often erroneously multiply $\Delta f$ by the mixer factor.
> 2. **Confusing message frequency and carrier frequency** — the message frequency $f_m$ is **not** multiplied by the frequency multiplier. Only the carrier and deviation are.
> 3. **Using AM bandwidth formula** $2 f_m$ instead of **Carson's Rule** $2(\Delta f + f_m)$ for FM bandwidth — a classic 2-mark loss.
> 4. **Modulation index invariance misconception** — students claim $\beta$ is invariant under frequency multiplication. The correct statement: $\beta$ is invariant *per multiplier* for the same input signal, but in Armstrong transmitters, the message frequency is held fixed, so the **modulation index increases** by the multiplier factor.
> 5. **Image frequency sign error** — the image is $f_{RF} + 2 f_{IF}$ (high-side injection), not $f_{RF} - 2 f_{IF}$. Check whether the local oscillator is above or below the RF.
> 6. **Pre-emphasis at TX, De-emphasis at RX** — reversed placement is a 1-mark deduction.
> 7. **Limiter must come BEFORE the discriminator** — the limiter is at IF (10.7 MHz), and the discriminator is the next stage. Reversing this order is a structural error worth 1 mark.
> 8. **Carson's Rule gives 98% power** — students sometimes state "100% power", which is wrong. Bessel function analysis shows that exactly 98% of the power lies within Carson's bandwidth.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **FM Transmitter (Armstrong):** Crystal Oscillator → Balanced Modulator → 90° Phase Shifter → Phase Modulator → Frequency Multipliers (×2, ×3...) → Mixer with LO → Power Amplifier → Antenna.
- [x] **Frequency Multiplier Effect:** Multiplies $f_c$ and $\Delta f$ by $n$. Does **not** multiply $f_m$.
- [x] **Mixer Effect:** Shifts $f_c$ by $f_{LO}$. Does **not** change $\Delta f$ or $f_m$.
- [x] **FM Receiver Blocks:** Antenna → RF Amp → Mixer → IF Amp (10.7 MHz) → Limiter → Discriminator → De-emphasis → Audio Amp → Speaker.
- [x] **IF Frequency:** 10.7 MHz (standard for FM broadcast).
- [x] **Image Frequency:** $f_{image} = f_{RF} + 2 f_{IF}$ (high-side LO injection).
- [x] **Carson's Rule:** $BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m)$. **Most cited KTU formula.**
- [x] **Modulation Index:** $\beta = \dfrac{\Delta f}{f_m}$. Increases by factor $n$ in a ×n frequency multiplier (for fixed $f_m$).
- [x] **Pre-emphasis / De-emphasis:** Time constant = **75 µs**; $f_{3dB} \approx 2.122$ kHz.
- [x] **Foster–Seeley:** S-curve response, linear for ±100 kHz, requires external limiter.
- [x] **Ratio Detector:** Built-in limiting via large capacitor, no separate limiter needed.
- [x] **PLL Detector:** Modern alternative using VCO + phase detector + loop filter.
- [x] **Capture Effect:** FM receivers naturally reject weaker co-channel interferers.
- [x] **SNR Improvement over AM:** $3\beta^2(\beta+1)$ (or equivalently $\frac{3}{2}\beta^2$ for narrowband).
- [x] **FM Broadcast Band:** 88–108 MHz; Channel spacing = 200 kHz; $\Delta f_{max} = 75$ kHz; $f_{m,max} = 15$ kHz.
- [x] **Direct FM** uses varactor diode / reactance modulator; **Indirect FM (Armstrong)** uses phase modulation + multipliers.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** For 14-mark long questions, **always** (1) draw a labeled block diagram first, (2) state the formula you will use, (3) substitute values, (4) show unit conversions explicitly, and (5) box your final answer. Examiners reward clarity as much as correctness.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Noise in Receivers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET864 - ELECTRONIC COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Noise in Receivers ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition In the context of electronic communication receivers, **Noise** refers to any unwanted electrical disturbance or random fluctuation that interferes with the desired information-bearing signal, degrading the quality of the received message. In a communication receiver, noise originates from both **internal sources** (components within the receiver itself) and **external sources** (the channel, atmosphere, and man-made interference). The study of noise in receivers is fundamental to determining the minimum detectable signal level, the **signal-to-noise ratio (SNR)** at the demodulator output, and ultimately the overall **bit-error-rate (BER)** or fidelity of the recovered information. ### 1.2 Key Categories of Noise in Receivers 1. **Internal Noise**: Generated within the receiver circuitry - **Thermal (Johnson) Noise**: Due to random motion of charge carriers in resistors. - **Shot Noise**: Due to discrete nature of charge carriers crossing a potential barrier. - **Flicker (1/f) Noise**: Dominant at low frequencies, semiconductor-related. - **Transit-Time Noise**: In semiconductor devices at high frequencies. 2. **External Noise**: Picked up by the antenna - **Atmospheric Noise**: Lightning, thunderstorms, electrostatic discharge. - **Extraterrestrial (Galactic) Noise**: Cosmic background radiation, solar noise. - **Man-Made (Industrial) Noise**: Ignition systems, motors, fluorescent lamps. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For Module 2, the emphasis is on **internal noise** generated within the receiver stages, the calculation of **noise figure (NF)** and **equivalent noise temperature (Te)**, and the application of **Friis' formula** for cascaded receiver stages. External noise is generally treated as part of the antenna temperature in a system-level noise budget. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are trying to listen to your friend whisper a secret message in a crowded, noisy marketplace. The whisper represents the **desired signal**, while the chatter of the crowd, honking vehicles, and blaring music represent **noise**. Even if your friend's voice is loud enough, the noise makes it hard to "decode" the secret. Similarly, a receiver must extract a weak signal (often in microvolts) buried in electrical noise. The "intelligence" with which the receiver front-end is designed determines whether the secret (information) is recovered or lost. **Noise Figure** is essentially a measure of how much "extra noise" the receiver itself adds to the input signal, making the listener's job harder. ### 1.4 Standard Noise Metrics - **k** = Boltzmann's Constant = **1.38 × 10-23 J/K** - **T0** = Standard Reference Temperature = **290 K** - **Noise Bandwidth (Bn)** in Hz - **Noise Figure (NF)** in dB - **Equivalent Noise Temperature (Te)** in Kelvin (K) > [!IMPORTANT] > The **KTU 2024 Scheme** expects students to master conversions between noise factor (F) and noise figure (NF) in dB, and between noise factor and equivalent noise temperature using the relation **Te = T0(F − 1)**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Breakdown of Receiver Noise Sources A communication receiver typically consists of an **antenna**, followed by an **RF amplifier (LNA)**, **mixer**, **IF amplifier**, and **demodulator**. Each stage contributes noise. Let us analyze them step by step. - **Step 1: Antenna Noise Capture** The antenna intercepts both the desired signal and external noise. The total noise power available from a matched source at temperature T is given by the **Nyquist relation**: $$P_n = kTB_n$$ where $B_n$ is the noise bandwidth in Hz. - **Step 2: Thermal (Johnson) Noise** Generated by any passive resistive element. The open-circuit RMS noise voltage across a resistor R is: $$\overline{v_n^2} = 4kTBR$$ This is **frequency-independent** (white noise) up to approximately $10^{12}$ Hz. - **Step 3: Shot Noise** Occurs in active devices due to the discrete nature of current flow. The mean-square shot noise current is: $$\overline{i_n^2} = 2qI_{DC}B$$ where $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C is the electron charge and $I_{DC}$ is the DC bias current. - **Step 4: Flicker (1/f) Noise** Power spectral density is inversely proportional to frequency. Dominant below a few kHz. Critical in oscillators and DC amplifiers but negligible in RF front-ends. - **Step 5: Noise Figure (F) of a Two-Port Network** The **Noise Factor (F)** is defined as: $$F = \frac{\text{Input SNR}}{\text{Output SNR}} = \frac{S_i/N_i}{S_o/N_o}$$ For a matched system at standard temperature $T_0 = 290$ K: $$F = \frac{N_o}{G \cdot kT_0 B}$$ where $G$ is the available power gain of the network. - **Step 6: Equivalent Noise Temperature (Te)** An alternative metric, particularly useful in satellite and microwave receivers: $$T_e = T_0(F - 1)$$ Or inversely: $$F = 1 + \frac{T_e}{T_0}$$ - **Step 7: Friis' Formula for Cascaded Stages** For N cascaded stages with gains $G_1, G_2, ..., G_N$ and noise factors $F_1, F_2, ..., F_N$, the **overall noise factor** is: $$F_{total} = F_1 + \frac{F_2 - 1}{G_1} + \frac{F_3 - 1}{G_1 G_2} + \cdots + \frac{F_N - 1}{G_1 G_2 \cdots G_{N-1}}$$ In terms of noise temperatures: $$T_{e,total} = T_{e1} + \frac{T_{e2}}{G_1} + \frac{T_{e3}}{G_1 G_2} + \cdots$$ - **Step 8: System Noise Temperature** Including antenna temperature $T_{ant}$: $$T_{sys} = T_{ant} + T_{e,total}$$ ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Symbol | Meaning | Formula | Unit | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $P_n$ | Available noise power | $P_n = kT B_n$ | W | | $\overline{v_n^2}$ | Mean-square thermal noise voltage | $\overline{v_n^2} = 4kTBR$ | V² | | $\overline{i_n^2}$ | Mean-square shot noise current | $\overline{i_n^2} = 2qI_{DC}B$ | A² | | $F$ | Noise Factor (linear) | $F = (S_i/N_i) / (S_o/N_o)$ | Dimensionless | | $NF$ | Noise Figure (dB) | $NF = 10 \log_{10}(F)$ | dB | | $T_e$ | Equivalent Noise Temperature | $T_e = T_0(F - 1)$ | K | | $F_{total}$ | Friis' Formula | $F_1 + \sum (F_i - 1)/\prod G_{prev}$ | Dimensionless | | $T_{e,total}$ | Total Noise Temperature | $T_{e1} + \sum T_{ei}/\prod G_{prev}$ | K | | $SNR_o$ | Output SNR | $SNR_i / F$ (for matched system) | dB | | $T_{sys}$ | System Noise Temperature | $T_{ant} + T_{e,total}$ | K | | $G$ | Antenna / System Gain | $G = 10 \log_{10}(g)$ | dBi | | $C/N$ | Carrier-to-Noise Ratio | $C/N = EIRP + G/T_{sys} - k - B - L$ | dB | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Examination Note**: In KTU problems, noise figure is almost always given in **dB**. Before applying Friis' formula, you MUST convert dB noise figures to linear noise factors using $F = 10^{NF/10}$. Marks are frequently lost when students use dB values directly in the Friis equation. ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Satellite Ground Stations**: Use cryogenically cooled LNAs with $T_e \approx 20$ K to detect signals weaker than $-150$ dBm. - **Cellular Base Stations**: Noise figure budget is carefully optimized (NF $\approx$ 2–3 dB) to maximize cell-edge coverage. - **Radar Receivers**: Determines minimum detectable range. Lower NF directly translates to longer detection range. - **Software Defined Radio (SDR)**: Front-end LNA design is dominated by noise figure considerations. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Friis' Formula for Noise Factor Consider two cascaded two-port networks, $N_1$ and $N_2$, with available power gains $G_1$ and $G_2$, and noise factors $F_1$ and $F_2$ respectively. Let the input be terminated in a matched source at standard temperature $T_0 = 290$ K. **Step 1: Input Noise Power from Source** The available noise power from the matched source at $T_0$ over bandwidth $B$ is: $$N_i = kT_0B$$ **Step 2: Output Noise from the Source Alone (No Internal Noise)** If both networks were noiseless, the output noise power would simply be the amplified input noise: $$N_{o,ideal} = G_1 G_2 \cdot kT_0 B$$ **Step 3: Output Noise with Internal Noise Contributions** The actual output noise includes: - Noise added by $N_1$: $G_1 G_2 (F_1 - 1)kT_0 B$ - Noise added by $N_2$: $G_2 (F_2 - 1)kT_0 B$ Therefore, the **total output noise** is: $$N_{o,total} = G_1 G_2 kT_0 B + G_1 G_2(F_1 - 1)kT_0 B + G_2(F_2 - 1)kT_0 B$$ **Step 4: Factor the Expression** Factoring out $G_1 G_2 kT_0 B$: $$N_{o,total} = G_1 G_2 kT_0 B \left[1 + (F_1 - 1) + \frac{F_2 - 1}{G_1}\right]$$ **Step 5: Simplify the Bracket** $$1 + F_1 - 1 + \frac{F_2 - 1}{G_1} = F_1 + \frac{F_2 - 1}{G_1}$$ **Step 6: Apply Definition of Overall Noise Factor** By definition, the overall noise factor is the ratio of actual output noise to the noise output if the network were noiseless: $$F_{total} = \frac{N_{o,total}}{G_1 G_2 kT_0 B} = F_1 + \frac{F_2 - 1}{G_1}$$ **Step 7: Extend to N Cascaded Stages** Generalizing to $N$ stages by induction: $$F_{total} = F_1 + \frac{F_2 - 1}{G_1} + \frac{F_3 - 1}{G_1 G_2} + \cdots + \frac{F_N - 1}{G_1 G_2 \cdots G_{N-1}}$$ This is the celebrated **Friis' Formula** for noise factor in cascaded systems. ### 3.2 Derivation: Relation Between Noise Factor and Equivalent Noise Temperature Consider a two-port network with noise factor $F$ at the standard reference temperature $T_0$. The output noise power is: $$N_o = F \cdot G \cdot kT_0 B$$ We can alternatively model the same network as a noiseless network of gain $G$ preceded by a source of equivalent noise temperature $T_e$. The output noise is then: $$N_o = G \cdot k(T_0 + T_e)B = G \cdot kT_0 B + G \cdot kT_e B$$ Equating the two expressions for $N_o$: $$F \cdot G \cdot kT_0 B = G \cdot kT_0 B + G \cdot kT_e B$$ Dividing both sides by $G \cdot kB$: $$F \cdot T_0 = T_0 + T_e$$ Solving for $T_e$: $$T_e = T_0(F - 1)$$ And inversely: $$F = 1 + \frac{T_e}{T_0}$$ ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Problem:** A receiver consists of three cascaded stages. Stage 1: LNA with gain 20 dB and NF = 1.5 dB. Stage 2: Mixer with gain 6 dB and NF = 8 dB. Stage 3: IF amplifier with gain 30 dB and NF = 12 dB. Calculate the overall noise figure in dB and the overall equivalent noise temperature. **Solution:** **Step 1: Convert dB Gains to Linear** $$G_1 = 10^{20/10} = 100$$ $$G_2 = 10^{6/10} \approx 3.981$$ $$G_3 = 10^{30/10} = 1000$$ **Step 2: Convert dB Noise Figures to Linear Noise Factors** $$F_1 = 10^{1.5/10} = 10^{0.15} \approx 1.4125$$ $$F_2 = 10^{8/10} = 10^{0.8} \approx 6.3096$$ $$F_3 = 10^{12/10} = 10^{1.2} \approx 15.8489$$ **Step 3: Apply Friis' Formula** $$F_{total} = F_1 + \frac{F_2 - 1}{G_1} + \frac{F_3 - 1}{G_1 G_2}$$ Substituting values: $$F_{total} = 1.4125 + \frac{6.3096 - 1}{100} + \frac{15.8489 - 1}{100 \times 3.981}$$ $$F_{total} = 1.4125 + \frac{5.3096}{100} + \frac{14.8489}{398.1}$$ $$F_{total} = 1.4125 + 0.05310 + 0.03730$$ $$F_{total} \approx 1.5029$$ **Step 4: Convert Overall Noise Figure to dB** $$NF_{total} = 10 \log_{10}(1.5029) \approx 1.77 \text{ dB}$$ **Step 5: Calculate Equivalent Noise Temperature** $$T_{e,total} = T_0(F_{total} - 1) = 290 \times (1.5029 - 1) = 290 \times 0.5029 \approx 145.84 \text{ K}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Observation:** The LNA dominates the noise budget. Even though stages 2 and 3 have much higher noise figures (8 dB and 12 dB), their contribution is divided by the large gain of the preceding stage. This is why a low-noise front-end amplifier is the most critical component in any sensitive receiver. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Cascaded Receiver Noise Model (Friis' Formula Visualization) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Antenna
T_ant = 50 K] --> B[LNA Stage 1
G1 = 20 dB
NF1 = 1.5 dB
Te1 = 35 K] B --> C[Mixer Stage 2
G2 = 6 dB
NF2 = 8 dB
Te2 = 1543 K] C --> D[IF Amplifier Stage 3
G3 = 30 dB
NF3 = 12 dB
Te3 = 4305 K] D --> E[Demodulator
Output SNR] subgraph Noise_Budget["Friis Noise Contribution"] F1["Stage 1: 1.4125
Dominant"] --- F2["Stage 2: 0.0531
Divided by G1"] F2 --- F3["Stage 3: 0.0373
Divided by G1xG2"] end ``` ### 4.2 Noise Figure vs. Noise Temperature Conversion Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Given: NF in dB] --> B[F linear = 10 to the power NF/10] B --> C{Need Te or F?} C -->|Equivalent Temp| D[Te = T0 times F minus 1] C -->|Noise Factor| E[F_total via Friis Formula] D --> F[Output: Te in Kelvin] E --> G[Sum: F1 + F2-1/G1 + ...] G --> H[Convert back: NF = 10 log10 F] H --> I[Output: NF in dB] ``` ### 4.3 Block Diagram of Receiver Internal Noise Sources ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Internal_Sources["INTERNAL NOISE SOURCES"] T1[Thermal Noise
v_n squared = 4kTBR] T2[Shot Noise
i_n squared = 2qI DC B] T3[Flicker 1/f Noise
PSD proportional to 1/f] T4[Transit Time Noise
at microwave frequencies] end subgraph External_Sources["EXTERNAL NOISE SOURCES"] E1[Atmospheric Noise
lightning, ESD] E2[Galactic Noise
cosmic microwave BG] E3[Man-Made Noise
industrial, ignition] end T1 --> N[Total Receiver Noise Floor] T2 --> N T3 --> N T4 --> N E1 --> N E2 --> N E3 --> N N --> Q[Quantified by:
Noise Figure NF
Noise Temperature Te] Q --> R[Receiver Sensitivity Limit] ``` ### 4.4 SNR Degradation Through Cascaded Receiver Stages ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Input Signal Si
Input Noise Ni = kT0B] --> B{Stage 1
F1} B --> C[So1 = G1 x Si
No1 = G1 x F1 x Ni] C --> D{Stage 2
F2} D --> E[So2 = G2 x So1
No2 = G2 x F2 x No1] E --> F[SNR degrades
at each stage] F --> G[Output SNR
Si/Ni x 1/F_total] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *Define noise figure of a receiver. What is its significance in communication system design?* **Model Answer:** **Noise Figure (NF)** is defined as the ratio of the input signal-to-noise ratio to the output signal-to-noise ratio of a receiver, expressed in decibels: $$NF = 10 \log_{10}(F) = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{S_i/N_i}{S_o/N_o}\right)$$ **Significance:** 1. It quantifies the noise added by the receiver itself. 2. A lower NF implies a more sensitive receiver capable of detecting weaker signals. 3. It determines the **minimum detectable signal** for a given output SNR requirement. 4. In cascaded systems, Friis' formula uses NF to predict the overall receiver noise performance. 5. It is a critical specification for LNA design in satellite, radar, and cellular systems. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2023]** *What is meant by equivalent noise temperature? How is it related to noise factor?* **Model Answer:** **Equivalent Noise Temperature (Te)** is defined as the temperature at which a matched source would have to be maintained to produce the same output noise power as that generated by the internal noise of the receiver. **Relation to Noise Factor:** $$T_e = T_0(F - 1) \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad F = 1 + \frac{T_e}{T_0}$$ where $T_0 = 290$ K is the standard reference temperature. **Advantages of using Te:** 1. Easier to add noise contributions in cascaded low-noise systems (e.g., satellite receivers). 2. More intuitive for ultra-low-noise designs where NF $\approx$ 1 dB. 3. Simplifies calculation of system noise temperature: $T_{sys} = T_{ant} + T_{e,total}$. --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **Q. (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]** *Derive the expression for the overall noise figure of two cascaded networks using Friis' formula.* **Model Solution:** Let two two-port networks $N_1$ and $N_2$ have available power gains $G_1$ and $G_2$ and noise factors $F_1$ and $F_2$. The source is matched at temperature $T_0 = 290$ K. **Step 1: Input noise power from source** $$N_i = kT_0 B$$ **Step 2: Output noise from source only (ideal, no internal noise)** $$N_{o,ideal} = G_1 G_2 kT_0 B$$ **Step 3: Add internal noise from $N_1$ and $N_2$** The internal noise of $N_1$ referred to its input is $(F_1 - 1)kT_0 B$, and similarly for $N_2$. After amplification through the subsequent stages: $$N_{o,total} = G_1 G_2 kT_0 B + G_1 G_2(F_1 - 1)kT_0 B + G_2(F_2 - 1)kT_0 B$$ **Step 4: Factor out $G_1 G_2 kT_0 B$** $$N_{o,total} = G_1 G_2 kT_0 B \left[1 + (F_1 - 1) + \frac{F_2 - 1}{G_1}\right]$$ **Step 5: Apply definition of overall noise factor** $$F_{total} = \frac{N_{o,total}}{G_1 G_2 kT_0 B} = F_1 + \frac{F_2 - 1}{G_1}$$ **Generalization to N stages:** $$F_{total} = F_1 + \frac{F_2 - 1}{G_1} + \frac{F_3 - 1}{G_1 G_2} + \cdots + \frac{F_N - 1}{G_1 G_2 \cdots G_{N-1}}$$ --- **Q. (b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam - June 2024]** *A receiver consists of an LNA (G = 15 dB, NF = 2 dB) followed by a mixer (G = -6 dB, NF = 8 dB) and an IF amplifier (G = 25 dB, NF = 10 dB). Determine the overall noise figure in dB and the overall equivalent noise temperature.* **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Convert dB values to linear** Gains: $$G_1 = 10^{15/10} = 31.623$$ $$G_2 = 10^{-6/10} = 0.2512$$ $$G_3 = 10^{25/10} = 316.23$$ Noise Factors: $$F_1 = 10^{2/10} = 1.5849$$ $$F_2 = 10^{8/10} = 6.3096$$ $$F_3 = 10^{10/10} = 10$$ **Step 2: Apply Friis' formula** $$F_{total} = F_1 + \frac{F_2 - 1}{G_1} + \frac{F_3 - 1}{G_1 G_2}$$ $$F_{total} = 1.5849 + \frac{6.3096 - 1}{31.623} + \frac{10 - 1}{31.623 \times 0.2512}$$ $$F_{total} = 1.5849 + \frac{5.3096}{31.623} + \frac{9}{7.943}$$ $$F_{total} = 1.5849 + 0.1679 + 1.1330 = 2.886$$ **Step 3: Overall noise figure in dB** $$NF_{total} = 10 \log_{10}(2.886) = 4.60 \text{ dB}$$ **Step 4: Equivalent noise temperature** $$T_{e,total} = 290 \times (2.886 - 1) = 290 \times 1.886 = 547.0 \text{ K}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students mistakenly use the noise figure in dB directly in the Friis equation. The formula REQUIRES linear noise factors. Failure to convert $NF_{2} = 8$ dB into $F_2 = 6.31$ will lead to a completely wrong answer. **Marks Deducted: 3-4 out of 7.** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **Q. (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam - Dec 2021]** *Explain the various types of internal noise generated in a communication receiver. Derive the expression for thermal noise voltage across a resistor.* **Model Solution:** **Types of Internal Noise:** 1. **Thermal (Johnson) Noise:** Random motion of electrons in conductors and resistors. It is white (frequency-independent up to ~1 THz) and Gaussian-distributed. RMS voltage: $\sqrt{4kTBR}$. 2. **Shot Noise:** Occurs in BJTs, diodes, and tubes due to the discrete nature of charge carriers. Mean-square current: $2qI_{DC}B$. Increases with DC bias current. 3. **Flicker (1/f) Noise:** Dominant below 1 kHz. PSD $\propto 1/f^{\alpha}$, where $\alpha \approx 1$. Caused by traps and imperfections in semiconductors. 4. **Transit-Time Noise:** Occurs when transit time of carriers through a device is comparable to the signal period. Important above few GHz. 5. **Partition Noise:** Occurs in BJTs where current splits between base and collector terminals randomly. **Derivation of Thermal Noise Voltage (Nyquist Theorem):** Consider a resistor R connected in a closed circuit. Electrons undergo random thermal motion. Using the principle of equipartition of energy: **Step 1:** The average thermal energy per degree of freedom is $\frac{1}{2}kT$. For a one-dimensional harmonic oscillator, total energy = $kT$ (kinetic + potential). **Step 2:** Consider the resistor as a series RLC circuit. The average energy stored in the inductor and capacitor at resonance equals $kT$: $$\frac{1}{2}L\overline{i^2} = \frac{1}{2}C\overline{v^2} = \frac{1}{2}kT$$ **Step 3:** The real part of the impedance of a parallel RLC at resonance relates voltage to current. After integration over frequency: $$\overline{v_n^2} = 4kTBR$$ where $B$ is the measurement bandwidth in Hz. --- **Q. (b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam - May 2023]** *An antenna with equivalent noise temperature 45 K is connected to a receiver with overall noise temperature 180 K. Find: (i) the overall system noise temperature, (ii) the overall noise figure, and (iii) the output SNR if the input signal power is -90 dBm and the bandwidth is 1 MHz.* **Model Solution:** **Step 1: System Noise Temperature** $$T_{sys} = T_{ant} + T_{e,total} = 45 + 180 = 225 \text{ K}$$ **Step 2: Overall Noise Figure** $$F = 1 + \frac{T_e}{T_0} = 1 + \frac{180}{290} = 1.6207$$ $$NF = 10 \log_{10}(1.6207) = 2.10 \text{ dB}$$ **Step 3: Input Noise Power** $$N_i = kT_0 B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 290 \times 10^6 = 4.002 \times 10^{-15} \text{ W}$$ $$N_i \text{ in dBm} = 10 \log_{10}(4.002 \times 10^{-15}) + 30 = -143.98 \text{ dBm}$$ **Step 4: Input SNR** $$SNR_i = P_{sig} - N_i = -90 - (-143.98) = 53.98 \text{ dB}$$ **Step 5: Output SNR** $$SNR_o = SNR_i - NF = 53.98 - 2.10 = 51.88 \text{ dB}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** In part (iii), students often forget to convert the noise power into dBm before subtracting. Use $N_i$(dBm) $= 10 \log_{10}(kT_0 B) + 30$ dBm. Also, ensure the bandwidth is in Hz (here 1 MHz = $10^6$ Hz), not kHz. **Marks Deducted: 2 out of 7** for unit mistakes. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Noise Factor (F)** is a **linear, dimensionless** quantity; **Noise Figure (NF)** is in **dB**. Always convert dB to linear before applying Friis' formula. - **Friis' Formula** shows that the **first stage (LNA) dominates** the overall noise performance. High gain in early stages suppresses noise from later stages. - **Thermal Noise** voltage: $\overline{v_n^2} = 4kTBR$. **Shot Noise** current: $\overline{i_n^2} = 2qI_{DC}B$. Both are white (frequency-independent). - **Equivalent Noise Temperature**: $T_e = T_0(F - 1)$, with $T_0 = 290$ K. Preferred in low-noise satellite/microwave receiver design. - **System Noise Temperature**: $T_{sys} = T_{ant} + T_{e,total}$. Total noise referred to receiver input. - **Output SNR** $= SNR_i - NF$ (all in dB) for a matched receiver. - **Cascaded Noise Factor** in linear domain: $$F_{total} = F_1 + \frac{F_2 - 1}{G_1} + \frac{F_3 - 1}{G_1 G_2} + \cdots$$ - **Flicker (1/f) noise** is dominant at low frequencies (below 1 kHz); negligible in RF front-ends. - **Receiver Sensitivity** is the minimum input signal power for a given output SNR: $$S_{min} = -174 \text{ dBm/Hz} + 10 \log_{10}(B) + NF + SNR_{min}$$ - Always state **units clearly** in derivations: k in J/K, T in K, B in Hz, R in Ω, $I_{DC}$ in A. - The **−174 dBm/Hz** figure is the noise floor at 290 K over 1 Hz bandwidth and is the foundation of all link budget calculations.

topics.title: Noise Figures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET864 - ELECTRONIC COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Noise Figures # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In the context of electronic communication receivers and amplifiers, the **Noise Figure (NF)** is a quantitative figure-of-merit that measures the degradation of the **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** caused by a component or system, such as an amplifier, mixer, or complete receiver chain. It is the single most important metric used by communication engineers to evaluate the "cleanness" with which a device processes weak signals buried in thermal noise. Mathematically, the **Noise Factor ($F$)** is defined as the ratio of the input signal-to-noise ratio to the output signal-to-noise ratio of a two-port network: $$F = \frac{S_i / N_i}{S_o / N_o}$$ The **Noise Figure** is simply the Noise Factor expressed in logarithmic (decibel) form: $$NF = 10 \log_{10}(F) \quad \text{(in dB)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Distinction (Frequently Tested)** > - **Noise Factor ($F$)** → Dimensionless ratio (linear scale, always $\geq 1$). > - **Noise Figure ($NF$)** → Expressed in **decibels (dB)** (always $\geq 0$ dB). > - An ideal, noise-free device has $F = 1$ and $NF = 0$ dB. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Whisper in a Crowded Room" Imagine you are trying to hear a friend whisper a secret in a quiet library. Your ear (the receiver) can detect the whisper clearly because the background is silent. Now imagine the same whisper in a crowded cafeteria with loud music. The whisper is *drowned out* by the noise. Your ear, in this case, has a *high* noise figure relative to the noisy environment. Now, suppose your friend uses a cheap, hissy megaphone. The megaphone amplifies the whisper, but it also adds a loud "hiss" of its own. Even though the signal is louder, the *quality* is worse because the megaphone added noise. This is exactly what happens in a real electronic amplifier — it amplifies the signal **and** adds its own internal noise. The **Noise Figure** quantifies exactly how much extra noise the device adds relative to the pristine input. A device with a **low NF (e.g., 1–2 dB)** is like a high-fidelity, silent megaphone — it adds almost no hiss. A device with a **high NF (e.g., 10+ dB)** is like a cheap, buzzy megaphone — it ruins the listening experience. ## 1.3 Why Noise Figure Matters in Communication Systems In any radio receiver chain (antenna → LNA → mixer → IF amplifier → demodulator), the signal becomes progressively weaker as it travels through lossy cables and passive components. The **first active stage** (usually the **Low-Noise Amplifier, LNA**) is the most critical: any noise it adds is amplified by all subsequent stages. This is why LNAs are engineered to have the **lowest possible NF** in the entire receiver. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Reference Temperature:** > The IEEE standard reference temperature is **$T_0 = 290 \text{ K}$** (approximately 16.85 °C, typical room temperature). This is the temperature at which thermal noise power is defined as $N = k T_0 B$, where $k$ is Boltzmann's constant ($1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K) and $B$ is the bandwidth in Hz. ## 1.4 Noise Temperature — An Equivalent Way to Express Noise For very low-noise devices (especially satellite and space communication receivers), engineers prefer to express noise performance in terms of **Effective Input Noise Temperature ($T_e$)** rather than Noise Figure. The two are directly related: $$T_e = T_0 (F - 1)$$ This temperature represents the *hypothetical* increase in the input source temperature that would produce the same amount of output noise as the device itself generates. A lower $T_e$ means a cleaner, less noisy device. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Detailed Analytical Breakdown — The Logic of Noise Figure To understand *why* noise figure is defined as $S_i/N_i$ divided by $S_o/N_o$, consider what happens inside a two-port network: **Step 1 — The Input Stage** A signal $S_i$ arrives at the input along with thermal noise $N_i = k T_0 B$ generated by the source resistance. The input SNR is therefore $SNR_i = S_i / N_i$. **Step 2 — The Amplification Process** The network amplifies the signal by its power gain $G$. So the signal at the output is $S_o = G \cdot S_i$. **Step 3 — The Internal Noise Addition** The network also has internal noise sources (thermal agitation in resistors, shot noise in transistors, flicker noise, etc.) that add a noise power $N_a$ to the output. The total output noise becomes $N_o = G \cdot N_i + N_a$. **Step 4 — The Output SNR** The output SNR degrades because $N_a$ has been added: $SNR_o = G \cdot S_i / (G \cdot N_i + N_a) < SNR_i$. **Step 5 — Defining the Noise Factor** Dividing the input SNR by the output SNR gives a number that is always $\geq 1$, equal to 1 only when $N_a = 0$ (ideal noiseless device). > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Why" Behind the Definition:** > Noise Figure is *not* a measure of how much noise a device produces in absolute terms. It is a *relative* measure of how much the SNR is degraded. A device that adds a lot of noise but has high gain may still have a moderate NF; conversely, a passive attenuator with $NF = \text{its attenuation in dB}$ adds no noise of its own but degrades SNR by absorbing signal power. ## 2.2 Friis' Formula — Noise in Cascaded Systems In any real communication receiver, multiple stages are connected in cascade. The **Friis' Formula for Noise** (derived by Harald Friis in 1944) gives the **overall noise factor** of a cascade of $n$ stages: $$F_{total} = F_1 + \frac{F_2 - 1}{G_1} + \frac{F_3 - 1}{G_1 G_2} + \frac{F_4 - 1}{G_1 G_2 G_3} + \cdots + \frac{F_n - 1}{G_1 G_2 \cdots G_{n-1}}$$ Where: - $F_1, F_2, \ldots, F_n$ are the noise factors of stages 1 through $n$. - $G_1, G_2, \ldots, G_{n-1}$ are the available power gains of stages 1 through $n-1$. - The noise factor of the *last* stage's noise contribution is divided by the product of all *preceding* gains. **Physical Interpretation:** The first stage dominates the overall noise performance because its noise is multiplied by all subsequent gains. Subsequent stages are progressively less important because their noise contributions are attenuated by the gains of the stages that come before them. This is the fundamental reason why **LNA design is the most noise-critical task in receiver engineering**. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Units / Range | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Noise Factor (Linear) | $F = \dfrac{S_i / N_i}{S_o / N_o}$ | Dimensionless, $F \geq 1$ | Quantifies SNR degradation | | 2 | Noise Figure (dB) | $NF = 10 \log_{10}(F)$ | dB, $NF \geq 0$ dB | Practical figure-of-merit | | 3 | Noise Factor from added noise | $F = 1 + \dfrac{N_a}{G \cdot N_i}$ | Dimensionless | Used in derivations | | 4 | Effective Noise Temperature | $T_e = T_0 (F - 1)$ | Kelvin (K) | Used in satellite/radio astronomy | | 5 | Friis' Cascade Formula | $F_{tot} = F_1 + \sum_{k=2}^{n} \dfrac{F_k - 1}{\prod_{j=1}^{k-1} G_j}$ | Dimensionless | Multi-stage receiver analysis | | 6 | Friis' in Noise Temperature form | $T_{e,total} = T_{e1} + \dfrac{T_{e2}}{G_1} + \dfrac{T_{e3}}{G_1 G_2} + \cdots$ | Kelvin | Cleaner for very low-noise systems | | 7 | Passive device NF | $NF_{\text{passive}} = L \text{ (dB)} = -10 \log_{10}(G)$ | dB | Lossy cables, filters, attenuators | | 8 | Total system temperature | $T_{sys} = T_{ant} + T_e$ | Kelvin | Link budget calculations | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Reminder for Passive Components:** > A passive device (cable, filter, attenuator) with power loss $L$ (where $L > 1$ in linear terms) has a noise factor $F = L$ and noise figure $NF = 10 \log_{10}(L)$ in dB. This is the *only* case where the loss in dB and NF in dB are numerically equal. ## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering - **Satellite Ground Stations:** Engineers use $T_e$ instead of $NF$ because LNAs in satellite receivers have $T_e$ values as low as 20–50 K. - **Cellular Base Stations:** The LNA at the tower-top must have $NF < 1.5$ dB to meet 5G NR sensitivity requirements. - **Radio Astronomy:** The entire system temperature $T_{sys}$ (sky + antenna + receiver) determines the minimum detectable signal. - **Cable TV Distribution:** Each long coaxial cable run degrades SNR; engineers use Friis' formula to budget how many amplifiers can be cascaded before signal quality becomes unacceptable. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Noise Factor from the Two-Port Noise Model **Starting Premise:** Consider a noisy two-port amplifier with available power gain $G$, input signal power $S_i$, and input noise power $N_i = k T_0 B$ from the source at reference temperature $T_0 = 290$ K. **Step 1 — Express the output signal power.** The signal at the output is the input signal multiplied by the gain: $$S_o = G \cdot S_i$$ **Step 2 — Express the output noise power in two parts.** The total output noise consists of (a) the amplified input noise plus (b) the internally generated noise $N_a$: $$N_o = G \cdot N_i + N_a$$ **Step 3 — Substitute into the Noise Factor definition.** By definition: $$F = \frac{S_i / N_i}{S_o / N_o}$$ Substituting $S_o = G S_i$ and $N_o = G N_i + N_a$: $$F = \frac{S_i / N_i}{G \cdot S_i / (G \cdot N_i + N_a)}$$ **Step 4 — Simplify the fraction.** $$F = \frac{S_i}{N_i} \cdot \frac{G \cdot N_i + N_a}{G \cdot S_i}$$ Cancel $S_i$ in numerator and denominator: $$F = \frac{G \cdot N_i + N_a}{G \cdot N_i}$$ **Step 5 — Split the numerator.** $$F = \frac{G \cdot N_i}{G \cdot N_i} + \frac{N_a}{G \cdot N_i}$$ **Step 6 — Final form.** $$F = 1 + \frac{N_a}{G \cdot N_i}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation:** This shows that $F = 1$ exactly when $N_a = 0$ (ideal noiseless amplifier). Any real device has $N_a > 0$, hence $F > 1$. The quantity $G \cdot N_i$ in the denominator is the *amplified input noise* — this is the "benchmark" against which the device's own added noise $N_a$ is compared. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Friis' Formula for Two Cascaded Stages **Starting Premise:** Two amplifiers in cascade. Stage 1 has gain $G_1$ and noise factor $F_1$. Stage 2 has gain $G_2$ and noise factor $F_2$. **Step 1 — Write each stage's output noise.** For Stage 1, using the result of Derivation 1: $$N_{o1} = G_1 \cdot N_i \cdot F_1$$ For Stage 2, its input noise is whatever comes out of Stage 1, and it adds its own noise: $$N_{o2} = G_2 \cdot N_{o1} \cdot F_2$$ **Step 2 — Combine by substitution.** $$N_{o2} = G_2 \cdot (G_1 \cdot N_i \cdot F_1) \cdot F_2$$ $$N_{o2} = G_1 G_2 \cdot N_i \cdot F_1 F_2$$ **Step 3 — Compare with the definition of total noise factor.** The total noise factor of the cascade is defined as the ratio $F_{total} = N_{o2} / (G_{total} \cdot N_i)$, where $G_{total} = G_1 G_2$: $$F_{total} = \frac{G_1 G_2 \cdot N_i \cdot F_1 F_2}{G_1 G_2 \cdot N_i}$$ $$F_{total} = F_1 F_2$$ **Step 4 — Show why this simple product form is misleading.** The above gives the *apparent* simple product, but the noise factor of Stage 2 must be referenced to the *same input temperature* $T_0$. The issue arises when we want to express Stage 2's noise factor in terms of the *overall* system. The standard Friis' form arises when we keep Stage 2's noise factor referenced to the original source, not to the output of Stage 1. Re-derive by keeping $F_2$ as the noise factor of Stage 2 *alone* (with input at $T_0$): $$N_{o2} = G_2 \cdot (G_1 N_i) + G_2 \cdot N_{a2}$$ where $N_{a2} = (F_2 - 1) G_2 N_i$ is the noise *added* by Stage 2 (since $F_2 = 1 + N_{a2}/(G_2 N_i)$). Wait — careful: For Stage 2 considered in isolation, $N_{a2} = (F_2 - 1) \cdot G_2 \cdot N_i$ (since its input would be $N_i$ if standalone). So the total output: $$N_{o2} = G_1 G_2 N_i + G_2 (F_2 - 1) G_2 N_i$$ **Step 5 — Simplify carefully.** $$N_{o2} = G_1 G_2 N_i + G_2^2 (F_2 - 1) N_i$$ $$N_{o2} = G_1 G_2 N_i \left[ 1 + \frac{G_2 (F_2 - 1)}{G_1} \right]$$ **Step 6 — Divide by $G_1 G_2 N_i$ to get the total noise factor.** $$F_{total} = 1 + \frac{G_2 (F_2 - 1)}{G_1}$$ **Step 7 — Reinterpret by adding Stage 1's contribution.** For Stage 1, $F_1 = 1 + N_{a1}/(G_1 N_i)$, so $N_{a1} = (F_1 - 1) G_1 N_i$. The amplified version of $N_{a1}$ at the output is $G_2 N_{a1} = G_2 (F_1 - 1) G_1 N_i = G_1 G_2 (F_1 - 1) N_i$. Adding this to the previous $N_{o2}$ expression (which already included Stage 1's amplified input noise $G_1 G_2 N_i$ but we need to add Stage 1's added noise as well): $$N_{o2,total} = G_1 G_2 N_i + G_1 G_2 (F_1 - 1) N_i + G_2^2 (F_2 - 1) N_i$$ $$N_{o2,total} = G_1 G_2 N_i \left[ F_1 + \frac{G_2 (F_2 - 1)}{G_1} \right]$$ **Step 8 — Final form for two stages.** $$F_{total} = F_1 + \frac{F_2 - 1}{G_1}$$ This is the Friis' formula for $n = 2$ stages.
