## 1.3 What is a Receiver?

A **receiver** is the electronic system that captures the transmitted signal from the channel, separates it from noise and other unwanted signals, demodulates it, and delivers the original message to the user (loudspeaker, display, data port).

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Mail Sorting Office"

Think of the receiver as a **post office** that processes mail:
- The **mailbox antenna** receives thousands of letters (signals + noise + interference).
- The **filter section** (RF + IF) sorts letters by sender (frequency selection).
- The **demodulator** opens the letter and extracts the actual content.
- The **amplifier** makes the message strong enough to read/listen.
- The **AGC** is like a smart manager that adjusts sensitivity so the office doesn't get overwhelmed by loud mail or miss quiet mail.

## 1.4 Types of Receivers (KTU Focus)

1. **Tuned Radio Frequency (TRF) Receiver** — Early design; multiple tunable stages; suffered from poor selectivity and instability.
2. **Superheterodyne Receiver** — The **industry standard** and the most-tested KTU topic.
3. **Direct Conversion (Zero-IF) Receiver** — Used in modern software-defined radios.
4. **Digital IF Receiver** — Used in SDR systems with DSP.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** The **Superheterodyne Receiver** (invented by Edwin Armstrong, 1918) is the single most important receiver architecture in your syllabus. Expect direct questions on its block diagram, image frequency, and choice of IF.

## 1.5 Key Performance Metrics of a Receiver

| Metric | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Selectivity** | Ability to select the desired signal while rejecting adjacent channel signals. |
| **Sensitivity** | Minimum input signal strength (in dBm or µV) the receiver can detect with acceptable SNR. |
| **Fidelity** | Ability to reproduce the modulating signal without distortion. |
| **Noise Figure (NF)** | Measure of SNR degradation by the receiver. |
| **Dynamic Range** | Ratio between largest and smallest input signals the receiver can handle. |
| **Image Rejection Ratio (IRR)** | Ability to reject image frequency signals. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Values:** Receiver sensitivity is typically measured in **dBm** (decibels relative to 1 mW). A good communication receiver has sensitivity in the range of **−100 dBm to −120 dBm**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Channel Models

### 2.1.1 The Additive White Gaussian Noise (AWGN) Channel
The simplest and most widely used model. The received signal is:

$$Y(t) = X(t) + n(t)$$

where $n(t)$ is white Gaussian noise with two-sided Power Spectral Density $\frac{N_0}{2}$ W/Hz.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "White"?** Because its PSD is constant across all frequencies — analogous to white light containing all visible frequencies.

### 2.1.2 The Fading Channel (Multipath Channel)
When the signal reaches the receiver via multiple paths (reflections, diffraction), the received signal is:

$$Y(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} a_i X(t - \tau_i) e^{j\phi_i} + n(t)$$

where $a_i$, $\tau_i$, and $\phi_i$ are the amplitude, delay, and phase of the $i$-th path.

**Types of Fading:**
- **Slow Fading / Shadowing** — Caused by large obstacles (buildings, hills).
- **Fast Fading** — Caused by multipath with rapidly changing relative phases.
- **Rayleigh Fading** — When no LOS path exists.
- **Rician Fading** — When a strong LOS path dominates.

### 2.1.3 The Band-Limited Channel
Any real channel has a finite bandwidth $B$ Hz. The output is:

$$Y(t) = X(t) * h(t) + n(t)$$

where $h(t)$ is the channel's impulse response and $*$ denotes convolution.

## 2.2 Shannon's Channel Capacity Theorem

The **maximum achievable data rate** over a noisy band-limited channel is given by **Claude Shannon's (1948) theorem**:

$$C = B \log_2 \left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \;\; \text{bits/second}$$

Where:
- $C$ = Channel capacity (bits/sec)
- $B$ = Channel bandwidth (Hz)
- $S$ = Signal power (W)
- $N$ = Noise power (W) = $N_0 B$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Shannon's theorem gives a **theoretical upper bound** — it tells you what is *possible*, not how to achieve it. Practical codes (Turbo, LDPC, Polar) approach this limit.

## 2.3 Noise in Communication Channels

### 2.3.1 Thermal Noise (Johnson–Nyquist Noise)
Generated by the random motion of electrons in conductors. Available noise power:

$$N = kTB$$

Where:
- $k$ = Boltzmann's constant = **$1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K**
- $T$ = Absolute temperature (Kelvin)
- $B$ = Bandwidth (Hz)

### 2.3.2 Noise Figure (NF) of a Receiver

$$F = \frac{\text{SNR}_{\text{in}}}{\text{SNR}_{\text{out}}} = \frac{(S/N)_i}{(S/N)_o}$$

Expressed in decibels: $\text{NF (dB)} = 10 \log_{10}(F)$.

### 2.3.3 Friis' Formula for Noise (Cascaded Stages)

$$F_{\text{total}} = F_1 + \frac{F_2 - 1}{G_1} + \frac{F_3 - 1}{G_1 G_2} + \cdots + \frac{F_n - 1}{G_1 G_2 \cdots G_{n-1}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Friis matters:** The **first stage** of a receiver dominates the noise performance. Always put a **Low-Noise Amplifier (LNA)** right after the antenna.

## 2.4 Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)

For a line-of-sight link of distance $d$ meters operating at frequency $f$ Hz:

$$\text{FSPL (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 32.44 \;\; \text{(dB, with } d \text{ in km, } f \text{ in MHz)}$$

$$\text{FSPL (linear)} = \left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{4\pi d f}{c}\right)^2$$

Where $c$ = **$3 \times 10^8$ m/s** (speed of light).

## 2.5 Friis Transmission Equation

Received power in a free-space link:

$$P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$$

Where $P_t$ = transmit power, $G_t$, $G_r$ = antenna gains.

## 2.6 The Superheterodyne Receiver — Deep Theory

### 2.6.1 Why Superheterodyne?

The TRF receiver had three problems:
1. **Gain variation** across the band (tuning all RF stages together is hard).
2. **Insufficient selectivity** at high frequencies.
3. **Instability** (oscillation due to multi-stage tuned amplifiers at RF).

The superheterodyne solves these by converting the incoming RF signal to a **fixed lower frequency** called the **Intermediate Frequency (IF)** using a **mixer** and **local oscillator**.

### 2.6.2 The Mixing Process

A mixer is essentially a multiplier followed by a low-pass filter. Given:
- RF input: $v_{RF}(t) = A \cos(2\pi f_s t)$
- LO input: $v_{LO}(t) = \cos(2\pi f_o t)$

The mixer output contains sum and difference frequencies:

$$v_{RF} \cdot v_{LO} = \frac{A}{2} \left[\cos 2\pi(f_o + f_s)t + \cos 2\pi(f_o - f_s)t\right]$$

After low-pass filtering, we retain the **difference frequency**:

$$f_{IF} = \vert f_o - f_s \vert$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Choice of LO Frequency:** The LO can be placed either **above** the signal ($f_o = f_s + f_{IF}$) or **below** ($f_o = f_s - f_{IF}$). This duality creates the famous **image frequency problem**.

### 2.6.3 Image Frequency

When the LO is set to $f_o = f_s + f_{IF}$, an unwanted signal at frequency:

$$f_{IM} = f_s + 2 f_{IF} = f_o + f_{IF}$$

will also produce an output at $f_{IF}$ after mixing. This is the **image frequency**.

**Example (KTU Classic):** If $f_s = 1000$ kHz and $f_{IF} = 455$ kHz:
- LO: $f_o = 1000 + 455 = 1455$ kHz
- Image: $f_{IM} = 1455 + 455 = 1910$ kHz

An unwanted broadcast at 1910 kHz would also be received and interfere.

### 2.6.4 Image Rejection Ratio (IRR)

The IRR depends on the **Q-factor of the RF stage** before the mixer:

$$\text{IRR (dB)} \approx 20 \log_{10}\left[Q \cdot \frac{f_s}{f_{IF}}\right]$$

**Key Insight:** Higher IF → Lower image rejection. Lower IF → Better image rejection but poorer adjacent channel selectivity. **Trade-off.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard IF Values:** AM broadcast → **455 kHz**; FM broadcast → **10.7 MHz**; TV → **38.9 MHz (video)**.

### 2.6.5 Automatic Gain Control (AGC)

Modern superheterodyne receivers include an AGC loop that adjusts the RF/IF amplifier gain so that the output remains nearly constant despite input signal level variations (fading, near-far problem).

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ | Shannon channel capacity | bits/sec |
| 2 | $N = kTB$ | Thermal noise power | Watts |
| 3 | $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ | Boltzmann constant | J/K |
| 4 | $F = (S/N)_i / (S/N)_o$ | Noise figure | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\text{FSPL} = (4\pi d/\lambda)^2$ | Free-space path loss (linear) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $P_r = P_t G_t G_r (\lambda/4\pi d)^2$ | Friis transmission | Watts |
| 7 | $f_{IF} = \vert f_o - f_s \vert$ | Intermediate frequency | Hz |
| 8 | $f_{IM} = f_s + 2 f_{IF}$ | Image frequency (LO above) | Hz |
| 9 | $P_r (\text{dBm}) = P_t + G_t + G_r - \text{FSPL} - L_c$ | Link budget | dBm |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cellular Networks:** Superheterodyne receivers are used in every base station and mobile phone.
- **Satellite Communication:** Double-conversion superheterodyne is used in VSAT terminals.
- **Radar Systems:** Coherent superheterodyne receivers enable Doppler processing.
- **Software-Defined Radio (SDR):** Modern SDR replaces analog IF with digital sampling.
- **Optical Fiber:** Uses PIN/APD photodiodes followed by low-noise transimpedance amplifiers (Friis in action).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Shannon's Channel Capacity

**Starting Point:** A band-limited AWGN channel with bandwidth $B$ Hz and signal power $S$, noise power $N = N_0 B$.

**Step 1 — Information per Sample:**
If we transmit $M$ distinguishable amplitude levels in the presence of Gaussian noise, the number of bits per symbol (Huffman/M-ary signalling) is bounded by:

$$\log_2(M) \le \frac{1}{2}\log_2\left(\frac{S + N}{N}\right)$$

**Reasoning:** The noise adds a Gaussian uncertainty of variance $\sigma^2 = N_0 B / 2$. The maximum number of distinguishable levels within a signal swing $\sqrt{S}$ is approximately $\sqrt{S}/\sigma$.

**Step 2 — Sampling Theorem Application:**
A band-limited signal of bandwidth $B$ can be represented by $2B$ samples per second (Nyquist rate). So the bits per second is:

$$C = 2B \cdot \frac{1}{2} \log_2\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Simplification:**

$$\boxed{C = B \log_2\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \;\; \text{bits/second}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $N = N_0 B$:**

$$C = B \log_2\left(1 + \frac{S}{N_0 B}\right)$$

This shows that as bandwidth $B \to \infty$, $C$ approaches the asymptotic limit $\frac{S}{N_0} \log_2(e)$ bits/sec.

## 3.2 Derivation of Image Frequency

**Given:** Signal frequency $f_s$, Intermediate frequency $f_{IF}$, Local oscillator above signal: $f_o = f_s + f_{IF}$.

**Step 1 — Mixer Operation:**
A mixer multiplies the input spectrum with the LO. The output of an ideal mixer has frequencies:

$$f_{\text{out}} = \vert f_{\text{in}} \pm f_o \vert$$

**Step 2 — Desired Output:**
We want $f_{\text{out}} = f_{IF}$:

$$f_{IF} = f_o - f_s \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; f_o = f_s + f_{IF}$$

**Step 3 — Find Image:**
Suppose an interfering signal exists at $f_{IM}$. The mixer output for this signal is:

$$f_{\text{out}} = f_{IM} - f_o$$

For this to also equal $f_{IF}$ (interfering with our desired signal):

$$f_{IM} - f_o = f_{IF} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; f_{IM} = f_o + f_{IF}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $f_o$:**

$$\boxed{f_{IM} = f_s + 2 f_{IF}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Friis Transmission Equation

**Setup:** Isotropic transmit antenna radiates power $P_t$ uniformly in all directions. The power flux density at distance $d$ is:

$$S_d = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^2} \;\; \text{W/m}^2$$

**Step 1 — Gain of Transmit Antenna:**
A directive antenna with gain $G_t$ focuses energy, so the power flux density becomes:

$$S_d = \frac{P_t G_t}{4\pi d^2}$$

**Step 2 — Receive Antenna Effective Aperture:**
The receive antenna intercepts power over its effective area $A_e$, where:

$$A_e = \frac{G_r \lambda^2}{4\pi}$$

**Step 3 — Received Power:**

$$P_r = S_d \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t G_t}{4\pi d^2} \cdot \frac{G_r \lambda^2}{4\pi}$$

**Step 4 — Final Form:**

$$\boxed{P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2}$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Examples (KTU Style)

### Example 1: Channel Capacity
A channel has $B = 4$ kHz and SNR = 30 dB.

**Solution:**
$\text{SNR (linear)} = 10^{30/10} = 1000$

$$C = 4000 \cdot \log_2(1 + 1000) = 4000 \cdot \log_2(1001) \approx 4000 \cdot 9.97 = 39{,}880 \text{ bps}$$

### Example 2: Image Frequency
$f_s = 1200$ kHz, $f_{IF} = 455$ kHz, LO above signal.

**Solution:**
$f_o = 1200 + 455 = 1655$ kHz
$f_{IM} = 1200 + 2(455) = 1200 + 910 = 2110$ kHz

### Example 3: Free-Space Path Loss
Find FSPL at $d = 10$ km, $f = 900$ MHz.

**Solution:**

$$\text{FSPL} = 20 \log_{10}(10) + 20 \log_{10}(900) + 32.44$$
$$= 20 + 59.08 + 32.44 = 111.52 \text{ dB}$$

### Example 4: Friis Link Budget
$P_t = 10$ W, $G_t = G_r = 20$ dBi, $d = 50$ km, $f = 2$ GHz, $L_c = 3$ dB (cable loss).

**Solution:**
$\lambda = c/f = 3 \times 10^8 / 2 \times 10^9 = 0.15$ m
$\text{FSPL} = (4\pi \cdot 50000 / 0.15)^2 = (4.19 \times 10^6)^2 = 1.755 \times 10^{13}$
$\text{FSPL (dB)} = 10 \log_{10}(1.755 \times 10^{13}) = 132.44$ dB
$P_t$ (dBW) = 10 dBW
$G_t + G_r$ = 40 dBi
$P_r$ (dBW) = 10 + 40 − 132.44 − 3 = **−85.44 dBW**

## 3.5 Derivation of Noise Figure for Cascaded Stages (Friis' Noise Formula)

**Setup:** Two stages with power gains $G_1, G_2$ and noise figures $F_1, F_2$.

**Step 1 — Output Noise Power:**
The total output noise referred to the output is:

$$N_{\text{out}} = F_1 G_1 G_2 N_i + (F_2 - 1) G_2 N_i$$

**Step 2 — Output Signal Power:**

$$S_{\text{out}} = G_1 G_2 S_i$$

**Step 3 — Total Noise Figure:**

$$F_{\text{total}} = \frac{S_i / N_i}{S_{\text{out}} / N_{\text{out}}} = \frac{S_i / N_i}{G_1 G_2 S_i / [F_1 G_1 G_2 N_i + (F_2 - 1) G_2 N_i]}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify:**

$$\boxed{F_{\text{total}} = F_1 + \frac{F_2 - 1}{G_1}}$$

**Generalization to $n$ stages:**

$$F_{\text{total}} = F_1 + \frac{F_2 - 1}{G_1} + \frac{F_3 - 1}{G_1 G_2} + \cdots + \frac{F_n - 1}{G_1 G_2 \cdots G_{n-1}}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Superheterodyne Receiver (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[ANTENNA] --> B[RF AMPLIFIER]
    B --> C[RF TUNED FILTER]
    C --> D[MIXER STAGE]
    E[LOCAL OSCILLATOR] --> D
    D --> F[IF AMPLIFIER]
    F --> G[IF BANDPASS FILTER]
    G --> H[DETECTOR DEMODULATOR]
    H --> I[AUDIO AMPLIFIER]
    I --> J[LOUDSPEAKER OUTPUT]
    F --> K[AGC CIRCUIT]
    K --> B
    K --> F
    style A fill:#FFD700
    style B fill:#87CEEB
    style C fill:#90EE90
    style D fill:#FFB6C1
    style E fill:#DDA0DD
    style F fill:#87CEEB
    style G fill:#90EE90
    style H fill:#FFB6C1
    style I fill:#87CEEB
    style J fill:#FFD700
    style K fill:#D3D3D3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Functional Flow:** The antenna captures all radio signals. The RF amplifier/filter provides initial gain and rough image rejection. The mixer beats the RF with the LO to produce a fixed IF. The IF stages provide most of the gain and selectivity. The detector extracts the modulating signal. The AGC monitors output and feeds back gain control.

## 4.2 Communication System Channel Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    SRC[Information Source] --> ENC[Source Encoder]
    ENC --> CHN_ENC[Channel Encoder]
    CHN_ENC --> MOD[Modulator]
    MOD --> TX_AMP[TX Power Amplifier]
    TX_AMP --> CHAN[CHANNEL MEDIUM]
    CHAN --> NOISE[Additive Noise and Interference]
    NOISE --> CHAN
    CHAN --> RX_AMP[RX Low Noise Amplifier]
    RX_AMP --> DEMOD[Demodulator]
    DEMOD --> CHN_DEC[Channel Decoder]
    CHN_DEC --> SRC_DEC[Source Decoder]
    SRC_DEC --> DST[Destination Output]
    style SRC fill:#FFD700
    style CHAN fill:#FF6347
    style NOISE fill:#FF6347
    style RX_AMP fill:#90EE90
    style DEMOD fill:#87CEEB
    style DST fill:#FFD700
```

## 4.3 Frequency Translation Process in Mixer (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph LR
    RF[RF Input fs] --> MIX[Mixer Multiplier]
    LO[Local Oscillator fo] --> MIX
    MIX --> SUM[Sum Frequency fo PLUS fs]
    MIX --> DIFF[Difference Frequency fo MINUS fs]
    SUM --> FILT[Low Pass Filter]
    DIFF --> FILT
    FILT --> IFOUT[IF Output fIF]
    style RF fill:#87CEEB
    style LO fill:#DDA0DD
    style MIX fill:#FFB6C1
    style SUM fill:#FFA07A
    style DIFF fill:#FFA07A
    style FILT fill:#90EE90
    style IFOUT fill:#FFD700
```

## 4.4 Channel Classification Tree (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    CH[Communication Channel] --> G[Guided Wired]
    CH --> U[Unguided Wireless]
    G --> TPC[Twisted Pair Cable]
    G --> CX[Coaxial Cable]
    G --> OF[Optical Fiber]
    G --> WG[Waveguide]
    U --> SW[Surface Wave]
    U --> SK[Skywave Ionospheric]
    U --> LOS[Line of Sight]
    U --> SP[Space Communication]
    SW --> AM[AM Radio Broadcast]
    SK --> SW2[Shortwave HF Radio]
    LOS --> MW[Microwave Links]
    LOS --> WFI[WiFi Cellular]
    LOS --> SAT[Satellite Uplink Downlink]
    SP --> DS[Deep Space Probes]
    style CH fill:#FFD700
    style G fill:#90EE90
    style U fill:#87CEEB
```

## 4.5 Receiver Comparison: TRF vs Superheterodyne (Block-Level Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    TRF[TRF Receiver Architecture] --> A1[RF Amp Stage 1]
    A1 --> A2[RF Amp Stage 2]
    A2 --> A3[RF Amp Stage 3]
    A3 --> DET_TRF[Detector]
    DET_TRF --> AUD_TRF[Audio Output]
    SUPER[Superheterodyne Architecture] --> B1[RF Amp and Filter]
    B1 --> B2[Mixer with LO]
    LO[Local Oscillator] --> B2
    B2 --> B3[IF Amp Multi Stage]
    B3 --> B4[IF Filter]
    B4 --> DET_SUP[Detector Demod]
    DET_SUP --> AUD_SUP[Audio Output]
    AGC[AGC Feedback Loop] -.-> B3
    AGC -.-> B1
    style TRF fill:#FFB6C1
    style SUPER fill:#90EE90
    style LO fill:#DDA0DD
    style AGC fill:#D3D3D3
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** Define the term **channel capacity**. State Shannon's channel capacity theorem.

**Model Answer:**
Channel capacity is the maximum rate at which information can be reliably transmitted over a communication channel. Shannon's theorem states that for a band-limited AWGN channel of bandwidth $B$ Hz with signal power $S$ and noise power $N$:

$$C = B \log_2\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \;\; \text{bits/second}$$

This gives the theoretical upper limit on achievable data rate with arbitrarily low error probability using optimal coding.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023]** What is meant by **image frequency** in a superheterodyne receiver?

**Model Answer:**
Image frequency is an unwanted input frequency that, after mixing with the local oscillator, produces the same intermediate frequency as the desired signal. When the LO is above the signal:

$$f_{IM} = f_s + 2 f_{IF}$$

An RF signal at $f_{IM}$, when mixed with the LO at $f_o = f_s + f_{IF}$, produces an output at $f_{IM} - f_o = f_{IF}$, interfering with the desired IF signal. The RF stage tuned circuit must reject this image.

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern Note:** Module 3 internal choice requires answering EITHER Question A OR Question B. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **superheterodyne receiver**. Discuss the function of each block. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes)

**Working Principle:**

1. **Antenna:** Captures the incoming RF signal from free space. All signals across the spectrum reach the antenna.
2. **RF Amplifier:** Provides initial amplification of weak signals. The noise figure of this stage dominates the receiver's overall noise performance.
3. **RF Tuned Filter:** Selects the desired RF band and provides initial **image rejection** before the mixer.
4. **Mixer:** Multiplies the RF input with the local oscillator signal. Produces sum and difference frequencies.
5. **Local Oscillator (LO):** Generates a tunable sinusoid whose frequency is offset from the RF by $f_{IF}$.
6. **IF Amplifier:** Provides most of the receiver gain at a fixed lower frequency. Easy to design for high gain and selectivity.
7. **IF Bandpass Filter:** A high-Q ceramic or crystal filter that defines the receiver's **selectivity** (adjacent channel rejection).
8. **Detector/Demodulator:** Extracts the original modulating signal from the IF carrier using envelope, synchronous, or FM detection.
9. **Audio Amplifier:** Boosts the demodulated signal to drive a loudspeaker.
10. **AGC Circuit:** Samples the IF output and feeds back a DC control voltage to vary the gain of RF/IF stages, keeping the output level nearly constant.

**Advantages over TRF:**
- Fixed IF simplifies high-gain, high-Q filter design.
- Easier to obtain uniform selectivity across the band.
- Better stability (no oscillation risk from cascaded RF stages).
- Better image rejection via RF pre-selector + IF filter.

---

**(b)** A superheterodyne receiver is tuned to receive a signal at **$f_s = 1200$ kHz**. The IF is **$455$ kHz** and the LO is above the signal frequency. Calculate: **(i)** Local oscillator frequency, **(ii)** Image frequency, **(iii)** Image rejection ratio if the Q-factor of the RF stage is **50**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Local Oscillator Frequency:**
Using $f_o = f_s + f_{IF}$:
$$f_o = 1200 + 455 = 1655 \text{ kHz}$$

**(ii) Image Frequency:**
Using $f_{IM} = f_s + 2 f_{IF}$:
$$f_{IM} = 1200 + 2(455) = 1200 + 910 = 2110 \text{ kHz}$$

**(iii) Image Rejection Ratio (IRR):**
Using the formula $\text{IRR} = 20 \log_{10}\left[Q \cdot \dfrac{f_s}{f_{IF}}\right]$:
$$\text{IRR} = 20 \log_{10}\left[50 \cdot \frac{1200}{455}\right] = 20 \log_{10}\left[50 \cdot 2.637\right] = 20 \log_{10}(131.87)$$
$$\text{IRR} = 20 \cdot 2.120 = 42.4 \text{ dB}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes:** Students often write $f_{IM} = f_s - 2f_{IF}$ when they assume LO below the signal. **Always state the LO position explicitly** before computing. Also, the IRR formula is approximate; if your textbook uses a different form, use that. The above is the standard KTU accepted version.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the different types of **communication channels** with suitable examples. Discuss the factors affecting channel performance. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Guided (Wired) Channels:**
- **Twisted Pair:** Two insulated copper wires twisted to cancel EMI. Used in telephone subscriber loops (bandwidth up to several MHz). Attenuation: ~0.5 dB/m at 1 MHz.
- **Coaxial Cable:** Central conductor with cylindrical shield. Bandwidth up to GHz. Used in cable TV, broadband internet.
- **Optical Fiber:** Glass/plastic core with cladding. Extremely low loss (< 0.2 dB/km), huge bandwidth (THz). Immune to EMI. Used in long-haul telecom, undersea cables, FTTH.
- **Waveguides:** Hollow metallic tubes for microwave frequencies (> 2 GHz).

**2. Unguided (Wireless) Channels:**
- **Surface Wave Propagation:** Waves follow Earth's curvature. Used in AM broadcast (535–1705 kHz), maritime communication.
- **Skywave Propagation:** HF radio (3–30 MHz) reflects off the ionosphere. Enables long-distance communication without satellites.
- **Line-of-Sight Propagation:** Frequencies above 30 MHz generally travel in straight lines. Used in FM broadcast, TV, cellular, Wi-Fi, satellite.
- **Space Communication:** Signals travel through vacuum/air with negligible absorption. Used in satellite links, deep space missions.

**Factors Affecting Channel Performance:**
- **Attenuation:** Signal power loss with distance.
- **Noise:** Thermal noise, atmospheric noise, man-made noise, galactic noise.
- **Fading:** Slow (shadowing) and fast (multipath) variations.
- **Bandwidth:** Limited by physical properties of the medium.
- **Interference:** Other users, co-channel and adjacent-channel.
- **Distortion:** Linear (frequency-dependent attenuation) and non-linear.
- **Propagation Delay and Delay Spread:** Critical for digital systems (ISI).

---

**(b)** A communication channel has a bandwidth of **$B = 10$ kHz**. The received signal power is **$S = 0.1$ mW** and the noise power in the channel is **$N = 1$ µW**. Calculate: **(i)** Channel capacity, **(ii)** Capacity if bandwidth is doubled, **(iii)** Capacity if SNR is doubled (with original bandwidth). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $B = 10$ kHz, $S = 0.1$ mW = $10^{-4}$ W, $N = 1$ µW = $10^{-6}$ W.

**SNR (linear):** $\dfrac{S}{N} = \dfrac{10^{-4}}{10^{-6}} = 100$ (which is 20 dB).

**(i) Channel Capacity:**

$$C = B \log_2\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) = 10{,}000 \cdot \log_2(101)$$
$$C = 10{,}000 \cdot 6.658 = 66{,}580 \text{ bps} \approx 66.58 \text{ kbps}$$

**(ii) Capacity with Doubled Bandwidth ($B' = 20$ kHz):**

$$C' = 20{,}000 \cdot \log_2(101) = 133{,}160 \text{ bps} \approx 133.16 \text{ kbps}$$

(Exactly double the original capacity.)

**(iii) Capacity with Doubled SNR (S/N = 200, B = 10 kHz):**

$$C'' = 10{,}000 \cdot \log_2(1 + 200) = 10{,}000 \cdot \log_2(201)$$
$$C'' = 10{,}000 \cdot 7.651 = 76{,}510 \text{ bps} \approx 76.51 \text{ kbps}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often miscalculate $\log_2(101)$. Use the conversion $\log_2(x) = \frac{\log_{10}(x)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{\log_{10}(x)}{0.301}$. Also, **doubling bandwidth doubles capacity** (linear relationship), but **doubling SNR increases capacity only logarithmically** — a crucial Shannon trade-off that examiners love to test.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Channels and Receivers:**
> 1. **Block diagram missing arrows or labels** — Deduct 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Wrong sign in image frequency** — Students compute $f_{IM} = f_s - 2f_{IF}$ instead of $f_s + 2f_{IF}$ when LO is above.
> 3. **Confusing SNR (linear) with SNR (dB)** — Always convert: $\text{SNR (dB)} = 10 \log_{10}(\text{SNR linear})$.
> 4. **Forgetting the AGC block** — Many students miss drawing the AGC feedback loop; examiners specifically look for it.
> 5. **Writing $\log$ instead of $\log_2$** in Shannon's formula — this gives wrong units.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ⭐ A **communication channel** is the physical medium that carries information from source to destination and is always corrupted by **noise, fading, and distortion**.
- ⭐ The **AWGN channel** is the simplest and most-used analytical model: $Y(t) = X(t) + n(t)$.
- ⭐ **Shannon's capacity theorem:** $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ — bandwidth linear, SNR logarithmic.
- ⭐ **Thermal noise:** $N = kTB$, with $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K.
- ⭐ **Free-space path loss** increases as the **square of distance and frequency**: $\text{FSPL} = (4\pi d/\lambda)^2$.
- ⭐ **Friis transmission equation:** $P_r = P_t G_t G_r (\lambda / 4\pi d)^2$.
- ⭐ **Friis' noise formula** (cascaded NF): the **first stage** dominates — use an **LNA** right after the antenna.
- ⭐ The **superheterodyne receiver** converts every incoming RF signal to a **fixed IF** via a **mixer + local oscillator** — this is the industry standard.
- ⭐ Standard IFs: **AM = 455 kHz**, **FM = 10.7 MHz**, **TV = 38.9 MHz**.
- ⭐ **Image frequency** (LO above signal): $f_{IM} = f_s + 2 f_{IF}$.
- ⭐ **Higher IF → better selectivity but worse image rejection** (trade-off!).
- ⭐ **AGC** maintains nearly constant output despite input signal variations.
- ⭐ **TRF receiver** is obsolete — superseded by superheterodyne due to better selectivity, stability, and uniformity of gain.
- ⭐ **Double conversion superheterodyne** uses two IFs (e.g., 10.7 MHz → 455 kHz) for very high-performance receivers.
- ⭐ **Noise figure (dB)** is the logarithmic measure of receiver noise degradation: $\text{NF (dB)} = 10 \log_{10}(F)$.
- ⭐ Remember to draw **all functional blocks with labelled arrows** in diagrams; missing AGC loop costs marks.
- ⭐ Always **state the LO position** (above or below) before computing image frequency.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sampling and Reconstruction of Analog Signals: Nyquist Sampling Theorem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET864 - ELECTRONIC COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Sampling and Reconstruction of Analog Signals: Nyquist Sampling Theorem # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Nyquist Sampling Theorem (Formal Statement):** > A **bandlimited** analog signal $x(t)$ whose **highest frequency component** is $W$ Hz can be **uniquely and perfectly reconstructed** from its samples, provided the sampling frequency $f_s$ satisfies the condition: > $$f_s \geq 2W_{\max}$$ > where $2W_{\max}$ is called the **Nyquist Rate** ($f_N$). **Key Terminology (Board-Exam Standard Vocabulary):** - **Analog Signal $x(t)$** — A continuous-time, continuous-amplitude signal defined for every instant of time. - **Sampling** — The process of converting a continuous-time signal into a discrete-time signal by recording its instantaneous amplitudes at **uniformly spaced intervals** $T_s$. - **Sampling Period ($T_s$)** — The uniform time interval between two consecutive samples, measured in **seconds (s)**. - **Sampling Frequency ($f_s$)** — The reciprocal of the sampling period, $f_s = 1/T_s$, measured in **Hertz (Hz)**. - **Nyquist Rate ($f_N = 2W$)** — The **minimum** sampling rate required to reconstruct the signal without distortion. - **Bandlimited Signal** — A signal whose Fourier spectrum is zero for all frequencies $\vert f \vert > W$. - **Aliasing** — The irreversible distortion that occurs when $f_s < 2W$ (i.e., high-frequency components impersonate as lower frequencies). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "How a Stroboscopic Movie Works" Imagine you are watching a ceiling fan rotating at **600 RPM (10 revolutions per second)**. A video camera records only **5 frames per second**. When the playback is shown, the fan appears to rotate **very slowly or even backwards!** Why? Because your eye is being fooled by insufficient "samples" of the motion. Now, if the camera records **30 frames per second** (which is much greater than $2 \times 10 = 20$ fps), the fan rotates naturally and smoothly. The camera has *satisfied the Nyquist criterion* for that motion. **The Lesson:** To faithfully capture a phenomenon (be it motion, sound, or voltage), you must record it at a rate **at least twice the fastest variation** present in it. Anything less causes **aliasing** — an irreversible illusion. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics > [!NOTE] > **Universal Constants & Conventions Used in Sampling Theory:** > - **Nyquist Rate:** $f_N = 2W$ Hz (minimum sampling rate) > - **Sampling Period:** $T_s = 1/f_s$ seconds > - **Guard Band Threshold:** $f_s \geq 2.2W$ is preferred in practical systems (anti-aliasing margin) > - **Standard Audio CD Sampling:** $f_s = 44.1$ kHz, protecting audio band up to $W = 20$ kHz > - **Standard Voice (Telephony) Sampling:** $f_s = 8$ kHz, protecting voice band up to $W = 4$ kHz # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three-Stage Operational Pipeline The complete process of converting an analog signal to digital and back involves three discrete, sequential stages: ### **Stage 1 — Anti-Aliasing Filtering (Pre-Sampling)** - **Why:** Real-world signals are rarely perfectly bandlimited. They contain out-of-band noise and spurious high-frequency components that, if not removed, will fold back into the baseband and corrupt the sampled signal. - **How:** A **low-pass filter (LPF)** with cutoff frequency $W_c \leq f_s/2$ is applied to $x(t)$ *before* sampling. - **Output:** A guaranteed bandlimited signal $x_{\text{LPF}}(t)$ with spectrum zero beyond $W_c$. ### **Stage 2 — Sampling (Continuous → Discrete)** - **Why:** To enable digital storage, transmission, and processing. - **How:** The filtered signal is multiplied by a **periodic impulse train** $\delta_{T_s}(t)$: $$x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\,\delta(t - nT_s)$$ - **Output:** A train of weighted impulses whose **areas (weights)** are the sample values $x(nT_s)$. ### **Stage 3 — Reconstruction (Discrete → Continuous)** - **Why:** To recover the original analog waveform for human listening/viewing or analog actuator control. - **How:** The sampled signal is passed through an **ideal low-pass filter** (reconstruction filter) of gain $T_s$ and cutoff $W$. - **Output:** A continuous-time signal $\hat{x}(t)$ that equals $x(t)$ exactly (if Nyquist condition is met). ## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | **Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Boundary / Critical Condition** | |---|---|---|---|---| | Sampling Frequency | $f_s$ | $1/T_s$ | Hz (Hertz) | Must satisfy $f_s \geq 2W$ | | Nyquist Rate | $f_N$ | $2W_{\max}$ | Hz (Hertz) | Minimum allowable $f_s$ | | Sampling Period | $T_s$ | $1/f_s$ | seconds (s) | $T_s \leq 1/(2W)$ | | Maximum Signal Frequency | $W$ | $f_s / 2$ | Hz | Upper bound of baseband spectrum | | Oversampling Factor | $k$ | $f_s / f_N$ | dimensionless | $k \geq 1$; $k = 1$ is critical | | Reconstruction Filter Gain | $G$ | $T_s$ | seconds (s) | Equal to sampling period | | Reconstruction Filter Cutoff | $f_c$ | $W$ | Hz | Must be $W \leq f_s/2$ | | Sinc Interpolation | $x(t)$ | $\sum_{n} x(nT_s)\, \mathrm{sinc}(2\pi W(t - nT_s))$ | volts (V) | Ideal reconstruction formula | | Anti-Aliasing Filter Cutoff | $f_c^{\text{AA}}$ | $f_s/2 - \Delta f$ | Hz | Practical margin $\Delta f$ included | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board-Exam Golden Rule:** Whenever a problem says *"the signal is sampled at $f_s$ Hz"*, the *implicit assumption* is that the signal's **maximum frequency** $W = f_s / 2$. Always state this assumption explicitly in your answer script. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Applications - **Digital Audio (Compact Disc):** $W = 20$ kHz audio band, $f_s = 44.1$ kHz (slight oversampling to ease filter design). - **Pulse Code Modulation (PCM) Telephony:** $W = 3.4$ kHz voice channel, $f_s = 8$ kHz. - **Digital Oscilloscopes & Digitizers:** Real-time sampling ADCs use $f_s \geq 5 \times W$ for accurate transient capture. - **Medical Imaging (MRI, CT):** k-space samples acquired under the Nyquist criterion to reconstruct artifact-free images. - **Software Defined Radio (SDR):** Direct RF sampling now feasible because ADCs reach $f_s$ in the GHz range. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Spectrum of a Sampled Signal **Problem Setup:** Let $x(t)$ be an analog signal with Fourier Transform $X(f)$ such that $X(f) = 0$ for $\vert f \vert > W$. The signal is sampled by a **periodic impulse train** $\delta_{T_s}(t)$ with sampling period $T_s = 1/f_s$. ### Step 1: Express the Sampling Impulse Train in the Time Domain The periodic impulse train is mathematically represented as an infinite sum of shifted Dirac delta functions: $$ \delta_{T_s}(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s) $$ ### Step 2: Form the Sampled Signal by Multiplication Multiplication in the time domain corresponds to convolution in the frequency domain. The sampled signal $x_s(t)$ is obtained by multiplying $x(t)$ with the impulse train: $$ x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot \delta_{T_s}(t) = x(t) \cdot \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s) $$ ### Step 3: Extract the Sample Values Using the **sifting property** of the Dirac delta, $x(t)\cdot \delta(t - nT_s) = x(nT_s)\,\delta(t - nT_s)$, we get: $$ x_s(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\,\delta(t - nT_s) $$ The result is a **train of impulses** whose areas (weights) are precisely the sample values $x(nT_s)$. ### Step 4: Take the Fourier Transform of the Impulse Train The Fourier series representation of the periodic impulse train is: $$ \delta_{T_s}(t) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{j2\pi k f_s t} $$ Therefore, its Fourier Transform is itself a **periodic impulse train in the frequency domain**: $$ \mathcal{F}\{\delta_{T_s}(t)\} = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta\!\left(f - kf_s\right) $$ ### Step 5: Apply the Multiplication-Convolution Property Multiplication in time $\Rightarrow$ Convolution in frequency, scaled by $1/T_s$: $$ X_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - kf_s) $$ ### Step 6: Interpret the Result — Spectral Replication The spectrum $X(f)$ of the original signal is **replicated** (copied) at every integer multiple of $f_s$. The baseband copy (centered at $f = 0$) and all shifted copies are scaled in amplitude by $1/T_s$. ### Step 7: Apply the Non-Aliasing Condition For the copies to **not overlap**, the gap between adjacent replicas must be non-negative. The original spectrum occupies $[-W, +W]$. The first replica is centered at $f_s$ and occupies $[f_s - W,\, f_s + W]$. Non-overlap requires: $$ f_s - W \;\geq\; W \quad \Longrightarrow \quad f_s \;\geq\; 2W $$ This is the **Nyquist Criterion**, derived rigorously. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** The factor $1/T_s$ is the amplitude scaling that occurs due to energy redistribution. The reconstruction filter must multiply by $T_s$ to recover the true amplitude of $x(t)$. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Reconstruction Using the Ideal Low-Pass Filter (Sinc Interpolation) **Problem Setup:** Given the samples $x(nT_s)$ of a bandlimited signal $x(t)$ (with $f_s \geq 2W$), derive the exact reconstruction formula. ### Step 1: Specify the Ideal Reconstruction Filter The ideal low-pass filter has the transfer function: $$ H(f) = \begin{cases} T_s, & \vert f \vert \leq W \\ 0, & \vert f \vert > W \end{cases} $$ The filter **passes** the baseband replica $[-W, +W]$ and **rejects** all higher replicas, while compensating for the $1/T_s$ amplitude scaling by its gain $T_s$. ### Step 2: Compute the Impulse Response via Inverse Fourier Transform The impulse response is the inverse Fourier Transform of $H(f)$: $$ h(t) = \int_{-W}^{W} T_s \cdot e^{j2\pi f t} \, df $$ ### Step 3: Evaluate the Integral Factor out the constant $T_s$ and integrate the complex exponential: $$ h(t) = T_s \left[ \frac{e^{j2\pi f t}}{j2\pi t} \right]_{-W}^{W} $$ ### Step 4: Substitute the Limits $$ h(t) = T_s \cdot \frac{e^{j2\pi W t} - e^{-j2\pi W t}}{j2\pi t} $$ ### Step 5: Apply Euler's Identity Using $\sin(\theta) = \frac{e^{j\theta} - e^{-j\theta}}{2j}$, the numerator simplifies to $2j\sin(2\pi W t)$: $$ h(t) = T_s \cdot \frac{2j \sin(2\pi W t)}{j2\pi t} = T_s \cdot \frac{\sin(2\pi W t)}{\pi t} $$ ### Step 6: Convert to the Normalized Sinc Function Using the normalized sinc definition $\mathrm{sinc}(x) = \frac{\sin(\pi x)}{\pi x}$, with $x = 2Wt$: $$ h(t) = 2W T_s \cdot \mathrm{sinc}(2W t) $$ Since $2W T_s = f_s / f_s \cdot 2W T_s$ and the canonical form is most useful with $T_s$ retained, we write: $$ h(t) = 2W T_s \,\mathrm{sinc}\!\left( \frac{t}{T_s/2} \right) \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad h(t) = \mathrm{sinc}(2W t) \quad \text{(for } f_s = 2W\text{)} $$ For the general case, the canonical sinc-interpolation kernel is: $$ h(t) = \mathrm{sinc}\!\left( \frac{t}{T_s} \right) $$ ### Step 7: Convolve the Sampled Signal with the Impulse Response The reconstructed signal is $\hat{x}(t) = x_s(t) * h(t)$: $$ \hat{x}(t) = \left[ \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\,\delta(t - nT_s) \right] * h(t) $$ ### Step 8: Apply the Convolution Property of Impulses The sifting property again yields: $$ \hat{x}(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\, h(t - nT_s) $$ ### Step 9: Substitute the Sinc Kernel — The Whittaker-Shannon Interpolation Formula $$ \boxed{\,x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\,\mathrm{sinc}\!\left( 2W(t - nT_s) \right)\,} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Board-Exam Equivalent Form:** Many KTU textbooks use the alternative form $\sum_n x(nT_s)\,\mathrm{sinc}(\pi f_s(t - nT_s))$. Both are mathematically identical; the argument differs only by a scaling convention. **Always check which form your module textbook uses and stick with it.** ### Step 10: Verification — At Sample Instants $t = mT_s$ The sinc function $\mathrm{sinc}(2W(mT_s - nT_s)) = \mathrm{sinc}(2(m-n)W/f_s)$ equals $\delta_{mn}$ (Kronecker delta) at sample instants, confirming that $\hat{x}(mT_s) = x(mT_s)$. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Quantitative Aliasing Error Bound **Problem Setup:** A signal $x(t) = \cos(2\pi f_0 t)$ is sampled at $f_s < 2f_0$. Determine the apparent (aliased) frequency $f_a$ observed after reconstruction. ### Step 1: Identify the Signal Frequency The highest frequency in the signal is $f_0$ Hz. ### Step 2: Write the Aliased Frequency Formula When $f_s < 2f_0$, the apparent frequency is: $$ f_a = \vert f_0 - k f_s \vert \quad \text{where } k = \left\lfloor \frac{f_0}{f_s} + \frac{1}{2} \right\rfloor $$ ### Step 3: Worked Numerical Example Let $f_0 = 70$ Hz, $f_s = 50$ Hz. Then $k = \lfloor 70/50 + 0.5 \rfloor = \lfloor 1.9 \rfloor = 1$. Hence $f_a = \vert 70 - 50 \vert = 20$ Hz. **Interpretation:** A 70 Hz tone, undersampled at 50 Hz, **looks like** a 20 Hz tone — this is aliasing. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often confuse *anti-aliasing* (analog filtering *before* sampling) with *reconstruction* filtering (analog filtering *after* sampling). The anti-aliasing filter is the **first** stage; the reconstruction filter is the **last** stage. They serve different purposes. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Complete Sampling & Reconstruction System The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the **end-to-end signal flow** from the original analog source $x(t)$ to the reconstructed analog output $\hat{x}(t)$, including the critical role of each filter. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Analog Source x of t] --> B[Anti Aliasing LPF
Cutoff f s over 2] B --> C[Bandlimited Signal x LPF of t] C --> D[Sampler Multiplier
x Impulse Train] D --> E[Sampled Signal x s of t
Train of Impulses] E --> F[Quantizer and Encoder
ADC Block] F --> G[Digital Storage or
Transmission Channel] G --> H[Decoder and
DAC Block] H --> I[Reconstruction LPF
Gain T s Cutoff W] I --> J[Reconstructed Signal x hat of t] J --> K[Analog Output Device
Speaker or Display] style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style B fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style D fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style F fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style G fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style H fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style I fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style K fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px ``` ## 4.2 Spectral Replications — Frequency Domain Visualization The following conceptual diagram illustrates the **spectrum replication** phenomenon in the frequency domain, showing both the compliant and aliasing cases. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph NONALIAS["NON ALIASING CASE f s GREATER THAN 2W"] N1[Baseband Copy of X of f
Centered at 0
Width 2W] -.Gap.-> N2[First Replica
Centered at f s
Width 2W] -.Gap.-> N3[Second Replica
Centered at 2 f s] end subgraph ALIASING["ALIASING CASE f s LESS THAN 2W"] A1[Baseband Copy
Centered at 0] --- A2[Overlapping First Replica
Centered at f s] A2 --- A3[Corrupted Baseband
Irreversible Overlap] end NONALIAS --- ALIASING style N1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style N2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style N3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style A1 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style A2 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style A3 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px ``` ## 4.3 State Transition Diagram — Sampling Decision Logic This flowchart captures the **decision-making logic** an engineer follows when designing a sampling system. ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Identify Maximum Frequency W in Signal] --> S2{Is Signal
Bandlimited} S2 -- NO --> S3[Apply Anti Aliasing LPF
with Cutoff f s over 2] S3 --> S4[New Effective W equals f s over 2] S2 -- YES --> S4 S4 --> S5[Choose Sampling Frequency f s
Must be GREATER THAN OR EQUAL TO 2W] S5 --> S6[Compute Sampling Period
T s equals 1 over f s] S6 --> S7[Design Reconstruction LPF
Cutoff W Gain T s] S7 --> S8[System Ready for
Digital Processing] style S1 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style S3 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style S5 fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style S8 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **State and explain the Nyquist Sampling Theorem. Mention the condition under which the original signal can be uniquely recovered from its samples.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Statement:** A bandlimited analog signal $x(t)$ having no frequency components above $W$ Hz can be completely recovered from its samples taken at a rate $f_s$ such that: > $$f_s \geq 2W$$ > The minimum sampling rate $f_N = 2W$ is called the **Nyquist Rate**. > > **Explanation:** When the signal is sampled using a periodic impulse train, its spectrum is replicated at every multiple of $f_s$. If $f_s \geq 2W$, the spectral replicas do not overlap, and a low-pass filter can isolate the baseband copy to perfectly reconstruct $x(t)$. > > **Recovery Condition:** The original signal can be uniquely recovered **if and only if** $f_s \geq 2W_{\max}$ and the reconstruction filter is an **ideal low-pass filter** with cutoff $W$ and gain $T_s$. --- ### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define aliasing. What is the remedy to avoid aliasing in practical communication systems?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Definition:** Aliasing is the **irreversible distortion** that occurs when an analog signal is sampled at a rate lower than the Nyquist rate ($f_s < 2W$). High-frequency components fold back into the baseband and become indistinguishable from legitimate low-frequency components. > > **Mathematical Statement:** A frequency $f_0 > f_s/2$ appears as an aliased frequency $f_a = \vert f_0 - k f_s \vert$ after sampling. > > **Remedies:** > 1. **Pre-sampling anti-aliasing filter:** A sharp low-pass filter with cutoff $f_c = f_s/2$ is applied before sampling to ensure the signal is bandlimited to $f_s/2$. > 2. **Oversampling:** Choosing $f_s$ comfortably above $2W$ (e.g., $f_s = 2.2W$) provides a guard band. > 3. **Bandlimited signal sources:** Using well-designed front-end analog filters. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Derive the condition for the Nyquist Sampling Theorem using the concept of spectrum replication in the frequency domain. Clearly state all mathematical steps. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An audio signal $x(t)$ has frequency components in the range $50$ Hz to $4$ kHz. It is sampled at a sampling frequency of $10$ kHz. Determine: (i) The Nyquist rate for this signal. (ii) Whether the chosen sampling frequency satisfies the Nyquist criterion. (iii) The maximum allowable sampling period. (iv) The bandwidth of the anti-aliasing filter required. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution for Question A:** #### Part (a) — Nyquist Theorem Derivation (7 marks) **Step 1:** Consider an analog signal $x(t)$ with Fourier Transform $X(f)$, bandlimited to $W$ Hz, i.e., $X(f) = 0$ for $\vert f \vert > W$. **Step 2:** The sampling impulse train is: $$ \delta_{T_s}(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s) $$ Its Fourier Series representation is: $$ \delta_{T_s}(t) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{j2\pi k f_s t} $$ **Step 3:** The sampled signal is $x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot \delta_{T_s}(t)$: $$ x_s(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\,\delta(t - nT_s) $$ **Step 4:** Taking the Fourier Transform and using the multiplication-convolution property: $$ X_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - kf_s) $$ **Step 5:** The spectrum $X(f)$ is **replicated** at $f = 0, \pm f_s, \pm 2f_s, \ldots$ with amplitude scaling $1/T_s$. **Step 6:** For **non-overlapping** replicas, the right edge of the baseband copy ($+W$) must be less than or equal to the left edge of the first replica ($f_s - W$): $$ f_s - W \geq W \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{f_s \geq 2W} $$ **Step 7:** Therefore, the **minimum sampling rate** is the **Nyquist rate** $f_N = 2W$. When this condition holds, the original signal is recovered by an ideal LPF of gain $T_s$ and cutoff $W$. --- #### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks) **Given:** $f_{\min} = 50$ Hz, $f_{\max} = 4$ kHz, $f_s = 10$ kHz. **(i) Nyquist Rate:** The maximum frequency is $W = 4$ kHz. $$ f_N = 2W = 2 \times 4 = 8 \text{ kHz} $$ **(ii) Nyquist Criterion Check:** Since $f_s = 10$ kHz $\geq f_N = 8$ kHz, the Nyquist criterion is **satisfied** with a margin of 2 kHz. No aliasing will occur. **(iii) Maximum Sampling Period:** $$ T_{s,\max} = \frac{1}{f_N} = \frac{1}{8000} = 125 \,\mu\text{s} $$ **(iv) Anti-Aliasing Filter Bandwidth:** The filter must pass signals up to $W = 4$ kHz and reject all frequencies above $f_s - W = 10 - 4 = 6$ kHz. The filter cutoff is therefore: $$ f_c = W = 4 \text{ kHz} $$ with a transition band spanning $4$ kHz to $6$ kHz. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain the process of reconstruction of an analog signal from its samples using an ideal low-pass filter. Derive the **Whittaker-Shannon interpolation formula** for reconstruction. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A bandlimited signal $x(t)$ has a maximum frequency of $5$ kHz. It is sampled at $f_s = 12$ kHz. (i) Compute the Nyquist rate. (ii) Verify if aliasing occurs. (iii) If a component at $7$ kHz is present in the original signal (unfiltered), determine the aliased frequency that appears after sampling. (iv) If the signal is reconstructed using an LPF of cutoff $5$ kHz, will the reconstruction be faithful? Justify. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution for Question B:** #### Part (a) — Reconstruction via Ideal LPF (7 marks) **Step 1:** The sampled signal spectrum is: $$ X_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - kf_s) $$ (Assuming $f_s \geq 2W$ to avoid overlap.) **Step 2:** The reconstruction filter has transfer function: $$ H(f) = \begin{cases} T_s, & \vert f \vert \leq W \\ 0, & \vert f \vert > W \end{cases} $$ **Step 3:** The impulse response is obtained by inverse Fourier transform: $$ h(t) = \int_{-W}^{W} T_s e^{j2\pi f t} df = 2W T_s \cdot \mathrm{sinc}(2W t) $$ **Step 4:** The reconstructed signal is the convolution of the sampled signal with $h(t)$: $$ \hat{x}(t) = x_s(t) * h(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s) \, h(t - nT_s) $$ **Step 5:** Substituting $h(t)$: $$ \boxed{\,x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\, \mathrm{sinc}\!\left[ 2W(t - nT_s) \right]\,} $$ This is the **Whittaker-Shannon Interpolation Formula** — the canonical result for ideal reconstruction. --- #### Part (b) — Aliasing Numerical Problem (7 marks) **Given:** $W = 5$ kHz, $f_s = 12$ kHz, spurious component at $7$ kHz. **(i) Nyquist Rate:** $$ f_N = 2W = 2 \times 5 = 10 \text{ kHz} $$ **(ii) Aliasing Check (Baseband Signal):** For the legitimate $5$ kHz signal: $f_s = 12$ kHz $\geq 10$ kHz. **No aliasing** of the baseband signal. **(iii) Aliased Frequency of 7 kHz Component:** Since $7$ kHz $> f_s/2 = 6$ kHz, this component **will alias**. Compute: $k = \lfloor 7/12 + 0.5 \rfloor = \lfloor 1.083 \rfloor = 1$. $$ f_a = \vert 7 - 1 \times 12 \vert = 5 \text{ kHz} $$ The 7 kHz tone appears as a **5 kHz tone** at the output. **(iv) Faithful Reconstruction?** **No**, reconstruction will **not** be faithful. The 7 kHz component folds into the baseband at 5 kHz, which lies inside the LPF passband ($0$ to $5$ kHz). It cannot be distinguished from any genuine 5 kHz content. An **anti-aliasing filter** with cutoff below $6$ kHz must be inserted *before* the sampler to prevent this. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on This Topic (Examiner Insights):** > > 1. **Confusing $f_s$ with $f_N$:** $f_N = 2W$ is the **minimum** allowable rate. $f_s$ is the **chosen** rate. They are not interchangeable; $f_s \geq f_N$. > 2. **Forgetting the $1/T_s$ amplitude scaling:** When writing $X_s(f)$, students often drop the $1/T_s$ factor. The reconstruction filter gain is $T_s$ to compensate. > 3. **Aliasing miscalculation:** When computing $f_a$, students forget to apply the absolute value $\vert \cdot \vert$. The result can be negative — the magnitude is the apparent frequency. > 4. **Confusing anti-aliasing with reconstruction filtering:** Anti-aliasing goes **before** the sampler; reconstruction goes **after** the DAC. They are at opposite ends of the system. > 5. **Sinc argument inconsistencies:** Some texts use $\mathrm{sinc}(2\pi W t)$, others use $\mathrm{sinc}(2W t)$. **Always verify the convention** with your module textbook. > 6. **No mention of bandlimited assumption:** A near-zero number of students explicitly state that $x(t)$ is **bandlimited**. This is a *required hypothesis* of the theorem. Examiners often deduct 1 mark for omitting it. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist > **Essential Things to Remember (Final-Day Revision Sheet):** > > - **Nyquist Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2W_{\max}$ is the necessary and sufficient condition for **unique, lossless** reconstruction. > - **Nyquist Rate:** $f_N = 2W$ — the **boundary** rate. Below this, aliasing is guaranteed. > - **Sampling Theorem Spectrum:** $X_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_k X(f - kf_s)$ — replication with $1/T_s$ scaling. > - **Whittaker-Shannon Reconstruction:** $x(t) = \sum_n x(nT_s)\,\mathrm{sinc}\!\left[ 2W(t - nT_s) \right]$. > - **Reconstruction Filter:** Ideal LPF, gain $T_s$, cutoff $W$. > - **Impulse Response:** $h(t) = 2W T_s \cdot \mathrm{sinc}(2W t)$. > - **Anti-Aliasing Filter:** Placed **before** the sampler; cutoff $f_s/2$. > - **Aliasing Formula:** $f_a = \vert f_0 - k f_s \vert$ where $k = \lfloor f_0/f_s + 1/2 \rfloor$. > - **Oversampling Factor:** $k = f_s / f_N \geq 1$. Practical systems use $k \approx 1.1$ to $2$. > - **Standard Sampling Rates:** Audio CD = 44.1 kHz; Telephony = 8 kHz; Professional Audio = 48 kHz. > - **Engineering Mantra:** *Sample fast, filter smart, reconstruct clean.*

topics.title: Ideal Reconstruction Filter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET864 - ELECTRONIC COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ideal Reconstruction Filter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Ideal Reconstruction Filter** is a theoretical, perfect low-pass filter (LPF) used at the receiver end of a sampled communication system to perfectly reconstruct the original continuous-time, bandlimited analog signal from its discrete-time samples. Mathematically, it is a filter whose frequency response is a **rectangular (brick-wall)** function that passes all frequency components from $-\pi/T_s$ to $+\pi/T_s$ (or equivalently, $-f_m$ to $+f_m$) with **unity gain** and **zero phase distortion**, while completely attenuating all frequencies outside this band.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Ideal Reconstruction Filter is the cornerstone of the **Sampling Theorem** (also called the **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem**). It operates in accordance with the **Interpolation Formula**, which states that a bandlimited signal can be perfectly reconstructed from its samples if the sampling frequency $f_s \geq 2f_m$, where $f_m$ is the maximum frequency present in the original signal.

Formally, the frequency response of the Ideal Reconstruction Filter is given by:

$$
H_R(f) = T_s \cdot \text{rect}\left(\frac{f}{2f_m}\right) = \begin{cases} T_s, & \vert f \vert \leq f_m \\ 0, & \vert f \vert > f_m \end{cases}
$$

where $f_m = f_s/2$ is the **Nyquist frequency** and $T_s = 1/f_s$ is the **sampling period**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — The "Stamp Collector & Photo Printer" Analogy:**
>
> Imagine you are watching a fast-spinning fan. The blades appear blurred, but if you take rapid snapshots (samples) using a high-speed camera, you can later "play them back" at the correct speed to recreate the illusion of motion. The **Ideal Reconstruction Filter** acts like a **magic "smoother"** placed after the camera: it takes the discrete snapshots and fills in all the gaps *between* the snapshots with mathematically perfect curves, producing a seamless, continuous video — exactly as if you had filmed without interruption.
>
> In engineering terms: **Sampling = taking discrete snapshots**; **Reconstruction = smoothing them into a continuous signal using a perfect low-pass filter.**

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Nyquist Rate:** $f_N = 2f_m$ — the minimum sampling rate to avoid **aliasing**.
- **Sampling Frequency:** $f_s \geq 2f_m$ Hz.
- **Filter Bandwidth:** $B = f_m = f_s/2$ Hz.
- **Filter Gain in Passband:** $T_s$ (the sampling period), to ensure proper amplitude scaling.
- **Transition Band:** **Zero** (instantaneous roll-off) — the defining feature of an *ideal* filter.
- **Passband Ripple:** **0 dB** (perfectly flat).
- **Stopband Attenuation:** **∞ dB** (perfect rejection).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** Always remember that the **gain of the ideal reconstruction filter is $T_s$**, not 1. This scaling factor is essential to ensure the reconstructed signal has the same amplitude as the original. Many students lose marks by writing "gain = 1."

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

The reconstruction process following sampling can be broken down into structured logical steps:

- **Step 1 — Sampling:** The original bandlimited analog signal $x(t)$ (with maximum frequency $f_m$) is sampled at regular intervals $T_s = 1/f_s$, producing the **impulse-sampled signal** $x_s(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\delta(t - nT_s)$.

- **Step 2 — Spectrum Replication:** In the frequency domain, sampling causes the spectrum $X(f)$ to be replicated at integer multiples of $f_s$, scaled by $1/T_s$. This yields the sampled spectrum:
$$
X_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - kf_s)
$$

- **Step 3 — Filtering for Reconstruction:** The Ideal Reconstruction Filter is applied with cutoff frequency $f_c = f_m = f_s/2$ to extract the **baseband replica** (i.e., $k = 0$) of the spectrum.

- **Step 4 — Amplitude Correction:** The filter multiplies the surviving spectrum by $T_s$ to undo the $1/T_s$ scaling introduced by sampling, restoring the original signal amplitude.

- **Step 5 — Output:** The output is the perfectly reconstructed signal $\hat{x}(t) = x(t)$, satisfying the **sampling theorem** equality.

### 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind the Process

> **Why an ideal filter?**
> Because any non-ideal filter (with a gradual roll-off) would distort the amplitude of frequency components near $f_m$ and allow spectral leakage between adjacent replicas, causing **aliasing artifacts** in the reconstructed signal. The ideal (brick-wall) response is the *only* filter that performs perfect reconstruction in theory.

> **How does it reconstruct in the time domain?**
> The impulse response of the ideal reconstruction filter is a **sinc function**. When convolved with the impulse train of samples, each sample gets "smeared" into a sinc pulse, and the superposition of all these sinc pulses reconstructs the original waveform — this is the essence of the **Whittaker–Shannon interpolation formula**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Frequency Response** | $H_R(f) = T_s \cdot \text{rect}\!\left(\frac{f}{2f_m}\right)$ | Brick-wall LPF, passband $0 \to f_m$ |
| 2 | **Impulse Response** | $h(t) = \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t}{T_s}\right) = \frac{\sin(\pi t / T_s)}{\pi t / T_s}$ | Time-domain sinc function |
| 3 | **Reconstruction Equation** | $x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\,\text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t - nT_s}{T_s}\right)$ | Whittaker–Shannon Interpolation |
| 4 | **Nyquist Rate** | $f_s = 2f_m$ | Minimum sampling rate (Hz) |
| 5 | **Nyquist Frequency** | $f_N = f_s/2 = f_m$ | Half the sampling rate (Hz) |
| 6 | **Sampling Period** | $T_s = 1/f_s$ | Time between samples (s) |
| 7 | **Sampled Spectrum** | $X_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - kf_s)$ | Periodic spectrum due to sampling |
| 8 | **Filter Gain in Passband** | $T_s$ | Ensures unity amplitude reconstruction |
| 9 | **Cutoff Frequency** | $f_c = f_s/2$ | Equals the signal's max frequency |
| 10 | **Group Delay** | $0$ (zero phase distortion) | All frequencies delayed equally |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note for KTU Students:** When writing the sinc function in your answer scripts, always use the **normalized form** $\text{sinc}(x) = \frac{\sin(\pi x)}{\pi x}$. Some textbooks (especially older Indian editions) use the unnormalized form $\frac{\sin x}{x}$. KTU typically follows the normalized form — state your convention clearly at the start of your answer.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Digital Audio Systems (CD Players):** Audio sampled at 44.1 kHz is reconstructed using a high-quality analog low-pass filter (a *practical approximation* of the ideal reconstruction filter) at the DAC output.
- **Software-Defined Radio (SDR):** Reconstruction filters are essential in digital-to-analog converters (DACs) for clean signal playback.
- **Medical Imaging (MRI, CT scans):** Image reconstruction from sampled k-space data uses 2D extensions of sinc-based interpolation.
- **Telecommunications:** Baseband reconstruction in modems and pulse-code modulation (PCM) systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Impulse Response $h(t)$ of the Ideal Reconstruction Filter

We start from the frequency-domain definition of the ideal reconstruction filter (a brick-wall low-pass filter) and apply the **inverse Fourier Transform** to obtain the time-domain impulse response.

**Step 1 — State the Frequency Response**

The frequency response of the ideal reconstruction filter is a rectangular function centered at $f = 0$, with width $2f_m$ and height $T_s$:

$$
H_R(f) = \begin{cases} T_s, & -f_m \leq f \leq f_m \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Inverse Fourier Transform**

The impulse response is the inverse Fourier transform of $H_R(f)$:

$$
h(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} H_R(f)\, e^{j2\pi f t}\, df
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Definition (Split the Integral Over the Passband)**

Since $H_R(f)$ is non-zero only over $[-f_m, f_m]$, the integral reduces to:

$$
h(t) = \int_{-f_m}^{f_m} T_s\, e^{j2\pi f t}\, df
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the Integral**

Factor out the constant $T_s$ and integrate:

$$
h(t) = T_s \left[ \frac{e^{j2\pi f t}}{j2\pi t} \right]_{-f_m}^{f_m}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the Limits**

$$
h(t) = T_s \cdot \frac{e^{j2\pi f_m t} - e^{-j2\pi f_m t}}{j2\pi t}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply Euler's Identity**

Using the identity $\sin(x) = \frac{e^{jx} - e^{-jx}}{2j}$:

$$
h(t) = T_s \cdot \frac{2j\sin(2\pi f_m t)}{j2\pi t} = T_s \cdot \frac{\sin(2\pi f_m t)}{\pi t}
$$

**Step 7 — Substitute $f_m = 1/(2T_s)$**

Recall that $f_m = f_s/2 = 1/(2T_s)$. Substituting:

$$
h(t) = T_s \cdot \frac{\sin\!\left(2\pi \cdot \frac{t}{2T_s}\right)}{\pi t} = T_s \cdot \frac{\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi t}{T_s}\right)}{\pi t}
$$

**Step 8 — Express in Standard sinc Form**

Reorganize to obtain the classical sinc form:

$$
h(t) = \frac{\sin(\pi t / T_s)}{\pi t / T_s} = \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t}{T_s}\right)
$$

> **Result:** The impulse response of the ideal reconstruction filter is a **sinc function**, which is symmetric about $t = 0$, has its peak value of 1 at $t = 0$, and has zero crossings at $t = nT_s$ for all non-zero integers $n$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Reconstruction (Interpolation) Formula

**Step 1 — Sampled Signal in Frequency Domain**

The sampled signal (impulse train of samples) has the spectrum:

$$
X_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - kf_s)
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Reconstruction Filter**

Multiplying $X_s(f)$ by the filter response $H_R(f)$ extracts only the $k = 0$ replica and scales it by $T_s$:

$$
\hat{X}(f) = X_s(f) \cdot H_R(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} X(f) \cdot T_s = X(f)
$$

**Step 3 — Take the Inverse Fourier Transform**

$$
\hat{x}(t) = x(t)
$$

**Step 4 — Time-Domain Equivalent via Convolution**

Since multiplication in frequency domain = convolution in time domain:

$$
\hat{x}(t) = x_s(t) * h(t) = \left[\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\,\delta(t - nT_s)\right] * h(t)
$$

**Step 5 — Use the Sifting Property of the Impulse**

Convolution with an impulse shifts the function:

$$
\hat{x}(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\, h(t - nT_s)
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute the Sinc Impulse Response**

$$
\hat{x}(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\, \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t - nT_s}{T_s}\right)
$$

> **Result — Whittaker–Shannon Interpolation Formula:**
> $$
> x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\, \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t - nT_s}{T_s}\right)
> $$
> This states that the original bandlimited signal is the **weighted sum of shifted sinc functions**, where each sinc is centered at a sample point and weighted by the sample's amplitude.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Time-Domain vs Frequency-Domain Properties

| Property | Time Domain $h(t)$ | Frequency Domain $H_R(f)$ |
|---|---|---|
| Functional Form | $\text{sinc}(t/T_s)$ | $T_s \cdot \text{rect}(f / 2f_m)$ |
| Support | Infinite (extends from $-\infty$ to $+\infty$) | Finite ($[-f_m, f_m]$) |
| Causal? | **No** (non-causal) | — |
| Stable? | **Yes** (absolutely integrable) | — |
| Realizable? | **No** (physically impossible) | **No** (zero transition band) |
| Peak Value | $h(0) = 1$ | $H_R(0) = T_s$ |
| Zero Crossings | $t = nT_s,\; n \neq 0$ | — |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of Sampling and Reconstruction System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Analog Input Signal x(t)"] --> B["Sampler Impulse Train p(t)"]
    B --> C["Sampled Signal xs(t) Equal to Sum of x nTs delta t minus nTs"]
    C --> D["Ideal Reconstruction Filter h(t) Equal to sinc t by Ts"]
    D --> E["Reconstructed Signal x hat t Equal to x(t)"]
    F["Sampling Frequency fs Greater than or Equal 2fm"] -.-> B
    G["Cutoff Frequency fc Equal to fs by 2 Equal to fm"] -.-> D
    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#009933,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#cc9900,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#cc0033,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Frequency-Domain Operation Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Original Spectrum X of f Bandlimited to fm"] --> B["After Sampling Xs of f Periodic with Period fs Scaled by 1 by Ts"]
    B --> C["Apply Ideal LPF with Cutoff fm and Gain Ts"]
    C --> D["Extract Baseband Replica X of f"]
    D --> E["Output is Original Signal x of t"]
    F["Replicas at k=1, k=-1, k=2, etc."] -.-> B
    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#cc9900,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#cc0033,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#009933,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#009933,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Sinc Interpolation Mechanism — Step-by-Step Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Sample x at t = 0"] --> Q1["Generate sinc centered at t=0, weighted by x of 0Ts"]
    P2["Sample x at t = Ts"] --> Q2["Generate sinc centered at t=Ts, weighted by x of 1Ts"]
    P3["Sample x at t = 2Ts"] --> Q3["Generate sinc centered at t=2Ts, weighted by x of 2Ts"]
    P4["Sample x at t = 3Ts"] --> Q4["Generate sinc centered at t=3Ts, weighted by x of 3Ts"]
    Q1 --> R["Sum All Sinc Functions"]
    Q2 --> R
    Q3 --> R
    Q4 --> R
    R --> S["Reconstructed Continuous Signal x of t"]
    style S fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#009933,stroke-width:2px
    style R fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#cc9900,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.4 Frequency Response of the Ideal Reconstruction Filter (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Frequency Axis f in Hz"] --> B["Region 1: Negative Frequencies"]
    A --> C["Region 2: Passband -fm to +fm"]
    A --> D["Region 3: Positive Frequencies"]
    C --> E["Gain = Ts in Passband, Flat Top"]
    B --> F["Gain = 0, Complete Attenuation"]
    D --> F
    E --> G["Brick-Wall LPF Response"]
    F --> G
    style C fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#009933,stroke-width:3px
    style E fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#009933,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#cc0033,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the Ideal Reconstruction Filter and state its frequency-domain expression.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Ideal Reconstruction Filter** is a theoretical low-pass filter used to perfectly reconstruct a bandlimited analog signal from its discrete samples, as required by the **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem**.

Its frequency response is a **brick-wall (rectangular)** function:

$$
H_R(f) = \begin{cases} T_s, & \vert f \vert \leq f_m \\ 0, & \vert f \vert > f_m \end{cases}
$$

where $f_m$ is the maximum frequency in the original signal and $T_s$ is the sampling period. The passband gain is $T_s$ (not unity), the cutoff frequency is $f_c = f_m = f_s/2$, and the transition band is **infinitely sharp** (zero width).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Why is the Ideal Reconstruction Filter non-realizable? Mention two reasons.**

**Model Answer:**

The Ideal Reconstruction Filter is **physically non-realizable** for the following reasons:

1. **Non-Causality:** The impulse response $h(t) = \text{sinc}(t/T_s)$ extends from $t = -\infty$ to $t = +\infty$, meaning the filter must respond *before* the input is applied — a physical impossibility for any real-time system.

2. **Zero Transition Band (Infinite Steepness):** The brick-wall frequency response has an infinitely sharp roll-off, which requires an **infinite-order filter** with infinite delay — impossible to construct in practice.

For these reasons, practical systems use **approximations** such as Butterworth, Chebyshev, or Elliptic filters, often combined with **oversampling** to ease the transition-band requirements.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Starting from the frequency-domain expression of the ideal reconstruction filter, **derive its impulse response** $h(t)$ and show that it is a sinc function. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Using the Whittaker–Shannon interpolation formula, **explain how the ideal reconstruction filter reconstructs the original signal from its samples**. Assume a signal $x(t)$ bandlimited to $f_m = 100$ Hz is sampled at $f_s = 250$ Hz. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Derivation of $h(t)$:**

We start with the brick-wall frequency response:

$$
H_R(f) = \begin{cases} T_s, & \vert f \vert \leq f_m \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

Taking the inverse Fourier transform:

$$
h(t) = \int_{-f_m}^{f_m} T_s\, e^{j2\pi f t}\, df
$$

$$
h(t) = T_s \cdot \left[\frac{e^{j2\pi f t}}{j2\pi t}\right]_{-f_m}^{f_m} = T_s \cdot \frac{e^{j2\pi f_m t} - e^{-j2\pi f_m t}}{j2\pi t}
$$

Using Euler's identity $\sin(x) = (e^{jx} - e^{-jx})/(2j)$:

$$
h(t) = T_s \cdot \frac{2j\sin(2\pi f_m t)}{j2\pi t} = T_s \cdot \frac{\sin(2\pi f_m t)}{\pi t}
$$

Substituting $f_m = 1/(2T_s)$:

$$
h(t) = T_s \cdot \frac{\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi t}{T_s}\right)}{\pi t} = \frac{\sin(\pi t / T_s)}{\pi t / T_s}
$$

$$
\boxed{h(t) = \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t}{T_s}\right)}
$$

The impulse response is a **sinc function** with peak value 1 at $t = 0$ and zero crossings at $t = nT_s$ for all integers $n \neq 0$.

**Part (b) — Reconstruction via Whittaker–Shannon Formula:**

Given: $f_m = 100$ Hz, $f_s = 250$ Hz.

First, check the Nyquist criterion: $f_s = 250$ Hz $> 2f_m = 200$ Hz. ✓ Sampling is valid (no aliasing).

The sampling period is $T_s = 1/f_s = 1/250 = 4$ ms.

The reconstructed signal is given by:

$$
x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\, \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t - nT_s}{T_s}\right)
$$

**Interpretation:**
- Each sample $x(nT_s)$ generates a sinc pulse centered at $t = nT_s$.
- The sinc pulses are weighted by their respective sample amplitudes.
- The **superposition** of all these sinc pulses exactly reconstructs the original bandlimited signal $x(t)$.

**Step-by-step reconstruction process:**
1. The impulse-sampled signal $x_s(t) = \sum_n x(nT_s)\,\delta(t - nT_s)$ is fed to the reconstruction filter.
2. The filter convolves this impulse train with its sinc impulse response: $\hat{x}(t) = x_s(t) * h(t)$.
3. By the **sifting property** of the impulse function, each sample produces a shifted, scaled sinc pulse.
4. The sum of all sinc pulses equals the original signal: $\hat{x}(t) = x(t)$.

**Conclusion:** Since the Nyquist criterion is satisfied ($f_s > 2f_m$), perfect reconstruction is achieved — the output of the ideal reconstruction filter is identical to the original input signal.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** **Explain the role of the ideal reconstruction filter in the sampling theorem.** Discuss what happens when the sampling frequency is **less than** the Nyquist rate. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A bandlimited signal $x(t)$ has spectrum $X(f) = \text{rect}(f/200)$ (i.e., bandwidth 100 Hz). The signal is sampled at $f_s = 300$ Hz and passed through an ideal reconstruction filter. **Determine** the filter's cutoff frequency, passband gain, impulse response, and verify whether aliasing occurs. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Role of the Ideal Reconstruction Filter and Aliasing:**

**Role:**
The ideal reconstruction filter is the **final stage** in any digital communication or signal processing system. Its role is to:
1. Extract the **baseband spectral replica** from the periodic spectrum of the sampled signal.
2. Restore the **original continuous-time waveform** from the discrete samples without any loss of information.
3. Eliminate all **high-frequency spectral images** (replicas at $\pm f_s, \pm 2f_s, \ldots$) generated by the sampling process.

**When $f_s < 2f_m$ (Aliasing):**
If the sampling rate falls below the Nyquist rate, the spectral replicas **overlap** in the frequency domain. This phenomenon is called **aliasing**. Specifically:
- Higher-frequency components of $X(f)$ "fold back" into the baseband.
- The baseband replica becomes **corrupted** and **indistinguishable** from the original.
- No filter (ideal or otherwise) can perfectly separate the overlapping spectra.
- The reconstructed signal suffers from **irreversible distortion**.

**Conclusion:** The ideal reconstruction filter can only guarantee perfect reconstruction when the **Nyquist criterion** $f_s \geq 2f_m$ is satisfied. Violating this condition causes aliasing, which makes faithful reconstruction impossible.

**Part (b) — Filter Parameter Determination:**

**Given:** $X(f) = \text{rect}(f/200)$, which means the signal bandwidth extends from $-100$ Hz to $+100$ Hz. So $f_m = 100$ Hz. Sampling frequency $f_s = 300$ Hz.

**Step 1 — Check for Aliasing:**
Nyquist rate = $2f_m = 200$ Hz. Since $f_s = 300$ Hz $> 200$ Hz, **no aliasing occurs**. ✓

**Step 2 — Cutoff Frequency:**
$$
f_c = f_m = \frac{f_s}{2} = \frac{300}{2} = 150 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 3 — Passband Gain:**
$$
\text{Gain} = T_s = \frac{1}{f_s} = \frac{1}{300} \text{ s} \approx 3.33 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Frequency Response:**
$$
H_R(f) = \begin{cases} 1/300, & \vert f \vert \leq 100 \text{ Hz} \\ 0, & \vert f \vert > 100 \text{ Hz} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 5 — Impulse Response:**
$$
h(t) = \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t}{T_s}\right) = \text{sinc}(300t) = \frac{\sin(300\pi t)}{300\pi t}
$$

**Step 6 — Reconstruction Equation:**
$$
x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x\!\left(\frac{n}{300}\right) \text{sinc}\!\left(300t - n\right)
$$

**Conclusion:** The ideal reconstruction filter with cutoff $f_c = 150$ Hz and gain $T_s = 1/300$ s will perfectly reconstruct the signal since the Nyquist criterion is satisfied.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Writing the passband gain as 1 instead of $T_s$:** The ideal reconstruction filter has a gain of $T_s$ in the passband, not unity. This is to compensate for the $1/T_s$ scaling introduced by impulse sampling. Examiners specifically test this — writing "gain = 1" costs full marks for the filter definition question.
>
> 2. **Confusing the sinc normalization:** Always use the **normalized form** $\text{sinc}(x) = \sin(\pi x)/(\pi x)$ as preferred in KTU 2024 Scheme. Mixing forms without stating the convention is a common error.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to verify the Nyquist condition:** When given a problem with numerical values, *always* check whether $f_s \geq 2f_m$ first. Jumping straight to the reconstruction formula without this check is a classic mistake.
>
> 4. **Stating that the ideal filter is realizable:** It is **NOT realizable** due to non-causality and infinite roll-off sharpness. Examiners often ask this in 3-mark questions.
>
> 5. **Confusing the cutoff frequency:** The cutoff is $f_c = f_s/2 = f_m$, **NOT** $f_s$. Many students write $f_c = f_s$ by mistake.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Ideal Reconstruction Filter:**

- ✅ The **Ideal Reconstruction Filter** is a **brick-wall low-pass filter** used to perfectly recover a bandlimited signal from its samples.

- ✅ **Frequency Response:** $H_R(f) = T_s \cdot \text{rect}(f / 2f_m)$ — rectangular shape, passband gain $= T_s$.

- ✅ **Impulse Response:** $h(t) = \text{sinc}(t/T_s)$ — symmetric, peak at $t = 0$, zero crossings at $t = nT_s$ ($n \neq 0$).

- ✅ **Cutoff Frequency:** $f_c = f_m = f_s/2$ (the **Nyquist frequency**).

- ✅ **Reconstruction Formula (Whittaker–Shannon):** $x(t) = \sum_{n} x(nT_s)\,\text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t - nT_s}{T_s}\right)$.

- ✅ **Nyquist Criterion:** $f_s \geq 2f_m$ must hold for perfect reconstruction. If violated → **aliasing** occurs and reconstruction is **impossible**.

- ✅ **Non-Causal:** $h(t)$ extends from $-\infty$ to $+\infty$ → the filter **cannot be realized** in real-time.

- ✅ **Non-Realizable:** Brick-wall response requires infinite-order filter → **physically impossible**.

- ✅ **Practical Substitutes:** Butterworth, Chebyshev, Elliptic filters; often combined with **oversampling** to relax transition-band requirements.

- ✅ **Passband Gain = $T_s$** (not 1!) — this is a frequent exam trap.

- ✅ **Zero Group Delay:** The filter has **zero phase distortion**, meaning all frequency components are delayed by the same amount.

- ✅ **Applications:** DACs in CD players, SDR systems, medical imaging, PCM telephony, audio reconstruction.
