- ✅ **Sampling Rate Rule of Thumb:** Choose $f_s$ comfortably above $2f_m$ (e.g., $f_s = 2.2 f_m$ to $4 f_m$) to allow practical (non-ideal) filters to perform well.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pulse Amplitude Modulation (PAM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET864 - ELECTRONIC COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pulse Amplitude Modulation (PAM)**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Pulse Amplitude Modulation (PAM)** is a fundamental pulse modulation technique in which the **amplitude of a train of periodic pulses** is varied in proportion to the instantaneous amplitude of the message (baseband) signal, while the pulse width and pulse position are held constant.

Mathematically, a PAM signal is expressed as:

$$s_{PAM}(t) = m(t) \cdot p(t)$$

where:
- $m(t)$ = message signal (information-bearing)
- $p(t)$ = train of periodic pulses (carrier pulse train)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** PAM is the **foundation technique** for all pulse modulation systems (PWM, PPM, PCM). Mastery of its generation, sampling theorem, and spectrum is mandatory before studying PCM in Module 4.

## 1.2 The Two Standard Variants of PAM

| Variant | Sampling Mechanism | Pulse Shape | Spectral Content |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Natural Sampling PAM** (Gated PAM) | Pulse top follows the message signal exactly | Variable amplitude, smooth curved top | Continuous spectrum around harmonics of $f_s$ |
| **Flat-Top PAM** (Sample-and-Hold PAM) | Pulse amplitude held constant for entire pulse width | Flat top with constant amplitude | Introduces **spectral droop** $\left( \dfrac{\sin(\pi f \tau)}{\pi f \tau} \right)$ |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy:** Imagine you are **taking photographs of a moving river** every second to study its flow pattern. Each photograph captures the water level (amplitude) at that exact instant. PAM does the same thing to an electrical signal — it **"freezes" the signal's amplitude** at regular, discrete instants and discards everything in between.

**Why we need PAM:** Continuous signals occupy a lot of bandwidth and are inefficient for time-multiplexed digital communication systems. PAM allows us to:
- Represent an analog waveform as a **discrete sequence of samples**
- Enable **Time Division Multiplexing (TDM)** — multiple signals on one channel
- Act as the **front-end** for analog-to-digital conversion (ADC)

**Physical Constants / Standard Metrics in Bold:**

- **Nyquist Rate:** $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ (where $f_m$ is the highest frequency in $m(t)$) — the minimum sampling rate to avoid aliasing.
- **Sampling Interval:** $T_s = 1/f_s$
- **Pulse Width:** $\tau$ (must satisfy $\tau \ll T_s$ for spectral efficiency)
- **Duty Cycle:** $D = \tau / T_s$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sampling Theorem (Shannon-Nyquist):** A bandlimited signal of maximum frequency $f_m$ Hz can be **perfectly reconstructed** from its samples if the sampling frequency $f_s$ satisfies $f_s \geq 2 f_m$. The minimum rate $f_s = 2 f_m$ is the **Nyquist Rate**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Sampling Process — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Define the message signal** $m(t)$, assumed bandlimited to $f_m$ Hz with bandwidth $B = f_m$.

**Step 2 — Define the pulse train** $p(t)$ as a periodic train of rectangular pulses of width $\tau$, period $T_s$, and amplitude $A$.

$$p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \text{rect}\!\left(\frac{t - nT_s}{\tau}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Multiplication generates PAM** — The message modulates the pulse train amplitude. For **natural sampling**, the top of each pulse traces the actual waveform of $m(t)$ during the interval $nT_s \leq t \leq nT_s + \tau$.

**Step 4 — Why use pulses?** Continuous signals cannot be time-multiplexed. By sampling, we leave **gaps** between samples where other signals can be inserted (TDM principle).

## 2.2 Natural Sampling PAM — Mathematical Formulation

In natural sampling, the PAM signal is given by:

$$s(t) = m(t) \cdot \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \text{rect}\!\left(\frac{t - nT_s}{\tau}\right)$$

The **Fourier series** of the pulse train is:

$$p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} C_n e^{j 2\pi n f_s t}$$

where the **Fourier coefficients** are:

$$C_n = \frac{\tau}{T_s} \cdot \text{sinc}(n f_s \tau) = \frac{\tau}{T_s} \cdot \frac{\sin(\pi n f_s \tau)}{\pi n f_s \tau}$$

Therefore the natural PAM signal becomes:

$$s(t) = m(t) \cdot \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} C_n e^{j 2\pi n f_s t} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} C_n \cdot m(t) e^{j 2\pi n f_s t}$$

## 2.3 Flat-Top PAM — Sample-and-Hold Model

In flat-top PAM, each sample is held constant for the entire pulse duration. This is the practical output of a **Sample-and-Hold (S/H) circuit**.

$$s_{FT}(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} m(nT_s) \cdot \text{rect}\!\left(\frac{t - nT_s}{\tau}\right)$$

The **frequency spectrum** of flat-top PAM is given by:

$$S_{FT}(f) = \frac{\tau}{T_s} \cdot \frac{\sin(\pi f \tau)}{\pi f \tau} \cdot M(f) + \frac{\tau}{T_s} \sum_{\substack{n=-\infty \\ n \neq 0}}^{\infty} \frac{\sin(\pi n f_s \tau)}{\pi n f_s \tau} \cdot M(f - n f_s)$$

The factor $\dfrac{\sin(\pi f \tau)}{\pi f \tau}$ is the **spectral droop** (aperture effect) — it distorts the recovered baseband signal and must be equalized at the receiver.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The aperture effect $\dfrac{\sin(\pi f \tau)}{\pi f \tau}$ causes attenuation of high-frequency components in $m(t)$. This is the **#1 favorite KTU question** on flat-top PAM — students are often asked to draw the equalizer response required to compensate.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ | Nyquist sampling criterion | Hz |
| 2 | $T_s = 1 / f_s$ | Sampling interval | seconds |
| 3 | $C_n = \dfrac{\tau}{T_s} \cdot \text{sinc}(n f_s \tau)$ | Fourier coefficient of pulse train | dimensionless |
| 4 | $S_{PAM}(f) = \sum C_n \cdot M(f - n f_s)$ | Spectrum of natural PAM | V/Hz |
| 5 | $H_{eq}(f) = \dfrac{\pi f \tau}{\sin(\pi f \tau)}$ | Equalizer transfer function (flat-top) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\text{Aliasing freq} = f_s - f_m$ | Fold-over frequency | Hz |
| 7 | $D = \tau / T_s$ | Duty cycle | dimensionless |
| 8 | $P_{PAM} = \overline{m^2(t)} \cdot D$ | Average power of natural PAM | W |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** PAM is the heart of every **ADC** in your smartphone, digital audio systems (CD at 44.1 kHz), digital oscilloscopes, and TDM-based telemetry in satellites. The flat-top variant is what every modern **Sample-and-Hold IC** (like LF398) produces at its output.

## 2.5 Why This Matters in Engineering

- **Telecommunications:** TDM-PAM forms the basis of **T1/E1 trunk lines** carrying 24/30 voice channels.
- **Audio Engineering:** **Pulse Code Modulation (PCM)** begins with PAM sampling before quantization.
- **Instrumentation:** Digital storage oscilloscopes sample analog signals using flat-top PAM.
- **Control Systems:** Sampled-data control loops use PAM as the interface between continuous plant and discrete controller.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Spectrum of Natural-Sampling PAM

We derive the spectral content of a natural-sampled PAM signal. Let the message signal be $m(t)$ with spectrum $M(f)$.

**Step 1 — Express the pulse train using its Fourier Series.**

A periodic train of rectangular pulses of width $\tau$, period $T_s$, and amplitude $A$ can be expanded as:

$$p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} C_n e^{j 2\pi n f_s t}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Fourier coefficients $C_n$.**

The coefficient $C_n$ is the integral of one pulse multiplied by $e^{-j 2\pi n f_s t}$ over one period, divided by $T_s$:

$$C_n = \frac{1}{T_s} \int_{-\tau/2}^{\tau/2} A \cdot e^{-j 2\pi n f_s t} \, dt$$

Evaluating this integral:

$$C_n = \frac{A}{T_s} \left[ \frac{e^{-j 2\pi n f_s t}}{-j 2\pi n f_s} \right]_{-\tau/2}^{\tau/2}$$

$$C_n = \frac{A}{T_s} \cdot \frac{e^{-j \pi n f_s \tau} - e^{j \pi n f_s \tau}}{-j 2\pi n f_s}$$

Using Euler's identity $e^{j\theta} - e^{-j\theta} = 2j\sin(\theta)$:

$$C_n = \frac{A}{T_s} \cdot \frac{2 \sin(\pi n f_s \tau)}{2\pi n f_s} = \frac{A\tau}{T_s} \cdot \frac{\sin(\pi n f_s \tau)}{\pi n f_s \tau}$$

Therefore, in compact form:

$$C_n = \frac{A\tau}{T_s} \cdot \text{sinc}(n f_s \tau)$$

**Step 3 — Multiply message by pulse train to get natural PAM.**

$$s(t) = m(t) \cdot p(t) = m(t) \cdot \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} C_n e^{j 2\pi n f_s t}$$

$$s(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} C_n \cdot m(t) \cdot e^{j 2\pi n f_s t}$$

**Step 4 — Take the Fourier Transform of both sides.**

Using the frequency-shift property: $\mathcal{F}\{m(t) e^{j 2\pi n f_s t}\} = M(f - n f_s)$:

$$S(f) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} C_n \cdot M(f - n f_s)$$

**Step 5 — Write the explicit final result.**

$$\boxed{S(f) = \frac{A\tau}{T_s} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \text{sinc}(n f_s \tau) \cdot M(f - n f_s)}$$

**Interpretation of the result:**
- The spectrum consists of the **baseband spectrum $M(f)$** replicated at every multiple of $f_s$ (i.e., at $0, \pm f_s, \pm 2 f_s, \ldots$).
- Each replica is **weighted by $C_n = \dfrac{A\tau}{T_s} \cdot \text{sinc}(n f_s \tau)$**.
- The DC component ($n=0$) gives $C_0 = \dfrac{A\tau}{T_s} = A \cdot D$ (the duty cycle times amplitude).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Flat-Top PAM Spectrum (Using Convolution Model)

Flat-top PAM is mathematically equivalent to **impulse-sampling** $m(t)$ and then **convolving** the impulse train with a rectangular pulse of width $\tau$.

**Step 1 — Impulse-sampled signal.**

$$\delta_s(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} m(nT_s) \cdot \delta(t - nT_s)$$

Its spectrum is the periodic replication of $M(f)$:

$$\Delta_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} M(f - n f_s)$$

**Step 2 — Convolve with a rectangular pulse.**

A rectangular pulse of width $\tau$ and amplitude 1 has Fourier transform:

$$P(f) = \tau \cdot \text{sinc}(f \tau) = \tau \cdot \frac{\sin(\pi f \tau)}{\pi f \tau}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the convolution-in-multiplication theorem.**

Since flat-top PAM = impulse samples $\ast$ rectangular pulse, the spectrum is the **product** of the two spectra:

$$S_{FT}(f) = \Delta_s(f) \cdot P(f)$$

$$\boxed{S_{FT}(f) = \frac{\tau}{T_s} \cdot \text{sinc}(f \tau) \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} M(f - n f_s)}$$

**Step 4 — The aperture effect.**

The baseband term (n = 0) becomes $\dfrac{\tau}{T_s} \cdot \text{sinc}(f \tau) \cdot M(f)$. To recover $M(f)$ without distortion, the receiver uses an equalizer:

$$H_{eq}(f) = \frac{1}{\text{sinc}(f \tau)} = \frac{\pi f \tau}{\sin(\pi f \tau)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why natural sampling is "cleaner":** Natural PAM does NOT introduce spectral droop — its $n=0$ replica is just $M(f)$ scaled by $C_0$. Flat-top PAM always carries the $\text{sinc}(f\tau)$ distortion which must be corrected by the equalizer. This trade-off — simpler generation vs. need for equalization — is the **classic KTU 2-marker question**.

## 3.3 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Parameter | Natural Sampling PAM | Flat-Top PAM |
|---|---|---|
| Top of pulse | Curved (follows $m(t)$) | Flat (constant during $\tau$) |
| Generation difficulty | Harder (needs analog gate) | Easy (S/H circuit) |
| Spectral droop | **None** | $\text{sinc}(f\tau)$ aperture effect |
| Equalizer needed? | No | Yes — $H_{eq}(f) = 1/\text{sinc}(f\tau)$ |
| Reconstruction | Low-pass filter | Low-pass + equalizer |
| Practical use | Rare, mainly theoretical | Universal in ADC front-ends |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End PAM Communication System (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Message Signal m of t] --> B[Band Limiting Filter]
    B --> C[Multiplier]
    D[Pulse Train Generator f sub s] --> C
    C --> E[PAM Signal s of t]
    E --> F[Channel TDM or Wire or Fiber]
    F --> G[Receiver LPF]
    G --> H[Equalizer H sub eq of f]
    H --> I[Reconstructed Message m hat of t]
    E --> J[Sampler to H old Circuit]
    J --> K[Flat Top PAM]
    K --> G
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The left half is the **transmitter** — the message is multiplied by a pulse train (for natural sampling) OR fed to a sample-and-hold circuit (for flat-top). The right half is the **receiver** — a low-pass filter recovers the baseband, and an equalizer compensates for aperture effect in flat-top PAM.

## 4.2 Detailed PAM Transmitter Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Transmitter_Block
        A1[Analog Input m of t] --> A2[Anti Aliasing LPF Cutoff f sub m]
        A2 --> A3[Multiplier X]
        A4[Pulse Generator Clock f sub s] --> A5[Rectangular Pulse Train p of t]
        A5 --> A3
        A3 --> A6[Natural PAM Output s sub N of t]
        A2 --> A7[Sample and Hold S and H]
        A4 --> A7
        A7 --> A8[Flat Top PAM Output s sub FT of t]
    end
```

## 4.3 Spectrum of Natural-Sampled PAM (Conceptual Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Message Spectrum M of f Bandwidth f sub m] --> B[Replicate at multiples of f sub s]
    B --> C[Spectrum at 0 plus or minus f sub s plus or minus 2 f sub s ...]
    C --> D[Weights = C sub n equals tau over T sub s times sinc]
    D --> E[LPF at receiver extracts M of f]
```

## 4.4 Sampling Theorem Visual

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Continuous Message m of t] --> S2[Sampler at f sub s]
    S2 --> S3{Sampling Rate Check}
    S3 -->|f sub s greater than 2 f sub m| S4[No Aliasing Perfect Recovery]
    S3 -->|f sub s equal to 2 f sub m| S5[Nyquist Rate Marginal]
    S3 -->|f sub s less than 2 f sub m| S6[Aliasing Distortion Unrecoverable]
    S4 --> S7[Reconstructed m hat of t]
    S5 --> S7
    S6 --> S8[Corrupted Signal]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Whenever a question gives you a sampling frequency and asks if aliasing occurs, the test is always: **Is $f_s \geq 2 f_m$?** If not, aliasing occurs and the foldover frequency is $f_s - f_m$. Always show this comparison as a clear inequality in your answer.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define Pulse Amplitude Modulation (PAM). Distinguish between Natural Sampling PAM and Flat-Top Sampling PAM.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Pulse Amplitude Modulation is a pulse modulation technique in which the amplitude of a periodic train of pulses is varied in accordance with the instantaneous amplitude of the message signal, while the pulse width and pulse position remain constant.

**Natural Sampling PAM (1 mark):** The top of each pulse follows the actual shape of the message signal during the sampling interval. Generated by multiplying the message signal with a pulse train. It introduces **no aperture effect**, but is difficult to generate in practice.

**Flat-Top Sampling PAM (1 mark):** The amplitude of each sample is held constant for the entire pulse duration $\tau$. Generated by a Sample-and-Hold (S/H) circuit. It introduces the **aperture effect** $\text{sinc}(f\tau)$, requiring an equalizer at the receiver, but is widely used in practical ADC systems.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**State and explain the Nyquist sampling theorem. What is the Nyquist rate for a message signal bandlimited to 5 kHz?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement (2 marks):** A bandlimited signal having no frequency components above $f_m$ Hz can be **completely reconstructed** from its samples taken at a rate $f_s$, provided that:

$$f_s \geq 2 f_m$$

The minimum sampling rate $f_s = 2 f_m$ is called the **Nyquist rate**, and the corresponding sampling interval is $T_s = \dfrac{1}{2 f_m}$.

**Numerical Answer (1 mark):** For $f_m = 5$ kHz:

$$f_{Nyquist} = 2 \times 5 = 10 \text{ kHz}$$

If the sampling rate falls below this, **aliasing** occurs and the original signal cannot be recovered.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module-Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 3]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the spectrum of a Natural-Sampled PAM signal. Hence obtain the relationship between message bandwidth and sampling frequency to avoid aliasing. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A message signal $m(t) = 2 \cos(1000\pi t)$ is sampled using Natural Sampling PAM with $f_s = 4$ kHz and pulse width $\tau = 50 \mu s$. Sketch the spectrum of the PAM signal and identify all spectral components with their amplitudes. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to A(a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Let the message signal $m(t)$ have spectrum $M(f)$ bandlimited to $f_m$. The pulse train $p(t)$ is periodic with period $T_s = 1/f_s$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** The Fourier series of the pulse train is:

$$p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} C_n e^{j 2\pi n f_s t}$$

where

$$C_n = \frac{\tau}{T_s} \cdot \text{sinc}(n f_s \tau)$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** The natural PAM signal is the product $s(t) = m(t) \cdot p(t)$:

$$s(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} C_n \cdot m(t) e^{j 2\pi n f_s t}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Taking the Fourier transform and using the modulation property:

$$S(f) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} C_n \cdot M(f - n f_s)$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Substituting $C_n$:

$$\boxed{S(f) = \frac{\tau}{T_s} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \text{sinc}(n f_s \tau) \cdot M(f - n f_s)}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** The spectrum consists of replicas of $M(f)$ centered at $0, \pm f_s, \pm 2 f_s, \ldots$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** To prevent overlap (aliasing) between adjacent replicas, the condition is:

$$f_s - f_m \geq f_m \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{f_s \geq 2 f_m}$$

This is the Nyquist criterion.

---

#### Model Solution to A(b) — 7 marks

**Given:** $m(t) = 2 \cos(1000\pi t)$, so $f_m = 500$ Hz. Sampling rate $f_s = 4000$ Hz, pulse width $\tau = 50 \times 10^{-6}$ s.

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute the message spectrum. Using $\mathcal{F}\{\cos(2\pi f_0 t)\} = \dfrac{1}{2}[\delta(f - f_0) + \delta(f + f_0)]$:

$$M(f) = \delta(f - 500) + \delta(f + 500) \quad \text{(amplitudes each 1)}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute the duty cycle and weight $C_0$:

$$\frac{\tau}{T_s} = \tau \cdot f_s = 50 \times 10^{-6} \times 4000 = 0.2$$

So $C_0 = 0.2$, $C_1 = 0.2 \cdot \text{sinc}(1 \times 4000 \times 50 \times 10^{-6}) = 0.2 \cdot \text{sinc}(0.2)$.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Compute $\text{sinc}(0.2) = \dfrac{\sin(\pi \times 0.2)}{\pi \times 0.2} = \dfrac{\sin(0.6283)}{0.6283} = \dfrac{0.5878}{0.6283} \approx 0.9355$.

So $C_1 \approx 0.2 \times 0.9355 = 0.1871$.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Higher order $C_n$ values decay with $\text{sinc}(n f_s \tau)$. The full spectrum has impulses at $\pm 500, \pm 3500, \pm 4500, \pm 7500, \pm 8500, \ldots$ Hz.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Amplitudes of spectral lines:

| Frequency (Hz) | $\pm 500$ | $\pm 3500$ | $\pm 4500$ | $\pm 7500$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Amplitude (impulse weight) | $C_0 = 0.2$ | $C_1 = 0.187$ | $C_1 = 0.187$ | $C_2 = 0.147$ |

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Spectrum is **discrete (impulses)** because $m(t)$ is a single tone, and the lines are replicated at multiples of $f_s = 4$ kHz around $\pm f_m = \pm 500$ Hz.

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Since $f_s = 4$ kHz $\gg 2 f_m = 1$ kHz, **no aliasing** occurs. A LPF of cutoff 1 kHz at the receiver recovers the original cosine.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 3]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the generation and detection of Flat-Top PAM. Derive the expression for the spectrum of Flat-Top PAM and discuss the aperture effect. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bandlimited message signal of bandwidth 4 kHz is sampled using flat-top PAM with sampling rate $f_s = 10$ kHz and pulse width $\tau = 40 \mu s$. Determine the frequency at which the spectral droop (aperture effect) is maximum. Also find the equalizer response required at this frequency. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to B(a) — 7 marks

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** Natural description since markdown — Transmitter side: Message $m(t)$ → Sample-and-Hold circuit clocked at $f_s$ → Flat-top PAM output. Receiver side: LPF (cutoff $f_m$) → Equalizer $H_{eq}(f) = \pi f \tau / \sin(\pi f \tau)$ → Reconstructed $m(t)$.

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Flat-top PAM is generated by impulse-sampling $m(t)$ and convolving with a rectangular pulse of width $\tau$:

$$s_{FT}(t) = \left[ \sum_{n} m(nT_s) \delta(t - nT_s) \right] \ast \text{rect}\!\left(\frac{t}{\tau}\right)$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** The Fourier transform of the impulse-sampled signal is:

$$\Delta_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} M(f - n f_s)$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** The Fourier transform of a rectangular pulse of width $\tau$ is:

$$P(f) = \tau \cdot \text{sinc}(f\tau) = \tau \cdot \frac{\sin(\pi f \tau)}{\pi f \tau}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Using the convolution theorem, multiplication in time = convolution in frequency; here we are convolving in time, so the spectrum is the product:

$$S_{FT}(f) = \Delta_s(f) \cdot P(f) = \frac{\tau}{T_s} \cdot \text{sinc}(f\tau) \sum_{n} M(f - n f_s)$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Aperture effect — The baseband component ($n=0$) is $\dfrac{\tau}{T_s} \cdot \text{sinc}(f\tau) \cdot M(f)$. The factor $\text{sinc}(f\tau)$ attenuates high frequencies within $M(f)$ and introduces amplitude distortion (spectral droop).

**Step 6 — Mitigation (1 mark):** An equalizer with transfer function $H_{eq}(f) = \dfrac{\pi f \tau}{\sin(\pi f \tau)}$ compensates the droop and restores $M(f)$ at the receiver.

---

#### Model Solution to B(b) — 7 marks

**Given:** $f_m = 4$ kHz, $f_s = 10$ kHz, $\tau = 40 \mu s = 40 \times 10^{-6}$ s.

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Verify Nyquist condition: $f_s = 10$ kHz $\geq 2 f_m = 8$ kHz ✓ — no aliasing.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** The spectral droop factor is:

$$D(f) = \text{sinc}(f\tau) = \frac{\sin(\pi f \tau)}{\pi f \tau}$$

This factor decreases as frequency increases; **maximum droop occurs at the highest baseband frequency**, i.e., at $f = f_m = 4$ kHz.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Compute the droop at $f_m = 4000$ Hz:

$$\pi f \tau = \pi \times 4000 \times 40 \times 10^{-6} = \pi \times 0.16 = 0.5027 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Compute the sinc value:

$$\text{sinc}(0.16) = \frac{\sin(0.5027)}{0.5027} = \frac{0.4818}{0.5027} \approx 0.9586$$

So the signal is attenuated by a factor of **0.9586 (about 4.14% droop)** at 4 kHz.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Equivalent in dB:

$$20 \log_{10}(0.9586) \approx -0.364 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** The equalizer gain at $f = 4$ kHz must be:

$$H_{eq}(4 \text{ kHz}) = \frac{1}{0.9586} \approx 1.0432 \quad (\text{or } +0.364 \text{ dB})$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** General equalizer transfer function required:

$$\boxed{H_{eq}(f) = \frac{\pi f \tau}{\sin(\pi f \tau)}}$$

This compensates the aperture effect for **all** frequencies in the message band $[0, f_m]$, not just the maximum droop point.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing PAM with PWM/PPM** (1–2 marks lost) — PAM varies amplitude only; PWM varies width; PPM varies position. Do not mix them up.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor $\tau / T_s$ in $C_n$** (1 mark) — students often write $C_n = \text{sinc}(n f_s \tau)$ and skip the $\tau / T_s$ duty cycle multiplier.
> 3. **Not stating the Nyquist condition explicitly** (1 mark) — always end any spectrum derivation with the boxed inequality $f_s \geq 2 f_m$.
> 4. **Mixing up $C_0$ with peak amplitude** — $C_0 = A\tau/T_s$, not $A$. Remember it is the **DC weight** of the pulse train.
> 5. **Saying flat-top PAM "has no aperture effect"** — that is the property of natural sampling. Flat-top always has spectral droop requiring equalization.
> 6. **Using `sinc(x) = sin(x)/x` without the $\pi$** — the engineering sinc is $\sin(\pi x) / \pi x$. Check your definition before writing.
> 7. **Sketching only one spectral replica** — always show at least 2–3 replicas on each side of the origin in the spectrum diagram.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **PAM = amplitude modulation of a pulse train** by a message signal.
- **Two types:** Natural sampling (curved top, no droop) and Flat-top sampling (held constant, has droop).
- **Generation:** Multiplication (natural) or Sample-and-Hold (flat-top).
- **Sampling Theorem (Nyquist):** $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ to avoid aliasing.
- **Fourier coefficients of pulse train:** $C_n = \dfrac{\tau}{T_s} \cdot \text{sinc}(n f_s \tau)$.
- **Natural PAM spectrum:** $S(f) = \sum C_n M(f - n f_s)$ — replicas of $M(f)$ at multiples of $f_s$.
- **Flat-top PAM spectrum:** $S_{FT}(f) = \dfrac{\tau}{T_s} \text{sinc}(f\tau) \sum M(f - n f_s)$ — includes aperture effect.
- **Aperture effect / Spectral droop:** $\text{sinc}(f\tau) = \dfrac{\sin(\pi f \tau)}{\pi f \tau}$ — distorts the recovered baseband.
- **Equalizer:** $H_{eq}(f) = \dfrac{1}{\text{sinc}(f\tau)} = \dfrac{\pi f \tau}{\sin(\pi f \tau)}$ — compensates aperture effect.
- **Recovery:** LPF with cutoff $f_m$ (and equalizer for flat-top).
- **Duty cycle:** $D = \tau / T_s$ — small duty cycle reduces intersymbol interference in TDM.
- **TDM application:** PAM is the building block of Time Division Multiplexing.
- **Foundation for PCM:** PAM → Quantization → Binary Coding → PCM.
- **KTU high-yield keywords:** Nyquist rate, aliasing, spectral replicas, aperture effect, equalizer, sample-and-hold, $C_n$ computation, sinc function.
- **Key formula to memorize first:** $\boxed{f_s \geq 2 f_m}$ and $\boxed{C_n = \dfrac{\tau}{T_s} \cdot \text{sinc}(n f_s \tau)}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Time Division Multiplexing with PAM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET864 - ELECTRONIC COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Time Division Multiplexing with PAM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Time Division Multiplexing with Pulse Amplitude Modulation (TDM-PAM)** is a composite signal processing and transmission technique in which **multiple independent baseband message signals** (sourced from distinct channels) are combined and transmitted over a **single physical communication channel** by exploiting the **orthogonality of time**. Each input message signal is first subjected to **Pulse Amplitude Modulation (PAM)**, which samples the analog waveform at (or above) the Nyquist rate. The amplitude-modulated pulses derived from different sources are then **interleaved in time** using a high-speed electronic commutator (rotary or solid-state), creating a composite PAM-TDM waveform that preserves the identity of every individual channel through its dedicated time slot.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, this topic is positioned at the intersection of **Module 3: Pulse Modulation and Multiplexing**, and directly extends the foundational concepts of **sampling theorem, PAM generation, and signal reconstruction**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** TDM-PAM is explicitly listed as a core learning outcome under CO3 — *“Apply pulse modulation and multiplexing techniques to design multi-channel communication systems.”* The examiner expects students to articulate the link between the **Nyquist sampling theorem** and the **allocation of time slots per channel**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a **single-lane narrow bridge** that must be shared by cars coming from **four different towns** wanting to cross a river simultaneously. The bridge cannot widen, so the traffic controller decides on a fair rule: **Town A's cars cross for 1 minute, then Town B's cars, then Town C's, then Town D's, and the cycle repeats.** Each town gets its own dedicated time slice — none of the cars mix on the bridge, yet all four towns successfully use the same bridge.

Mapping this analogy to TDM-PAM:

| Real-World Element | TDM-PAM Equivalent |
|--------------------|--------------------|
| Single-lane bridge | Common transmission channel (single wire/fiber) |
| Four towns | Multiple input message signals $m_1(t), m_2(t), \ldots, m_N(t)$ |
| 1-minute time slice per town | One time slot $T_s$ per channel |
| Traffic controller | Electronic commutator / multiplexer switch |
| Continuous stream of cars | Sampled PAM pulses |
| Round-robin rotation | Frame structure with $N$ time slots per frame |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Beginners:** The "multiplexing" magic is purely temporal — at any given microsecond, only **one** sample from **one** channel physically occupies the channel. The illusion of parallel transmission is created by rapidly cycling through all channels at a rate much faster than the message signals' highest frequency component. This is why TDM is only possible **after** sampling — analog signals cannot be time-multiplexed directly without overlapping and producing severe crosstalk.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics, Constants, and Terminology (KTU Board Emphasis)

The following terms and constants are **recurrently tested** in KTU ESE papers and **must be memorized verbatim**:

- **Number of channels:** $N$ (typically a small integer in numerical problems, e.g., 3, 4, 8, 12, 24)
- **Highest message frequency:** $f_m$ (Hz) — assumed identical for all channels in KTU problems
- **Nyquist sampling rate per channel:** $f_s \geq 2f_m$
- **Commutator rotation rate / frame rate:** $f_r = f_s$ (Hz) — equals the sampling frequency
- **Frame duration:** $T_f = 1/f_r$ (seconds)
- **Time slot per channel:** $T_s = T_f / N$ (seconds)
- **Composite TDM-PAM bit/word rate:** For PAM (analog pulse train), the effective **transmission bandwidth** is $B_T \approx N f_m$ Hz for natural PAM and slightly higher for flat-top (square-topped) PAM
- **PAM pulse width $\tau$:** Typically much smaller than $T_s$ to allow clean separation
- **Guard band (guard time) $T_g$:** Small time gap inserted between consecutive time slots to prevent overlap, typically $T_g \ll T_s$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Examiners expect students to clearly distinguish between **per-channel sampling rate** (which is $2f_m$) and the **commutator rotation rate** (also $2f_m$ for PAM-TDM). Many students incorrectly assume the commutator rotates at $N \times 2f_m$ — this is only true for **binary PCM-TDM**, not for PAM-TDM.

---

## 1.4 Prerequisites and Topic Position in Module 3

TDM-PAM is the **second major topic** of Module 3, following the introduction to **PAM, PWM, and PPM** in Module 3's first segments. The logical dependency chain is:

1. **Sampling Theorem (Module 2/3 bridge)** → guarantees recoverable signal
2. **PAM generation (natural & flat-top)** → produces the pulse train
3. **TDM switching/commutation** → interleaves multiple PAM trains
4. **Demultiplexing & reconstruction (low-pass filter)** → recovers originals

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Prerequisite Warning:** A weak grasp of the **Nyquist sampling theorem** and **PAM signal spectra** will directly handicap performance on TDM-PAM questions. KTU examiners frequently embed sampling-theorem sub-questions inside TDM problems to test this prerequisite.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Block Diagram of a TDM-PAM System

A KTU-grade analysis of TDM-PAM requires the student to mentally traverse the **entire signal path** — from the parallel input sources all the way to the parallel output reconstructions. The system naturally decomposes into **three canonical subsystems**:

### 2.1.1 Transmitter (Multiplexer) End

1. **Input section:** $N$ independent message signals $m_1(t), m_2(t), \ldots, m_N(t)$, each band-limited to $f_m$ Hz.
2. **Sampling stage (PAM generators):** Each $m_i(t)$ is individually sampled at a rate $f_s = 2f_m$ (minimum), producing discrete PAM pulses.
3. **Electronic commutator:** A high-speed rotary switch (in KTU problems, modeled as an ideal switch) that sequentially connects each sampled channel to the common transmission line. The commutator completes **one full rotation in $T_f$ seconds**, visiting all $N$ channels.
4. **Pulse-shaping amplifier / line driver:** Conditions the composite pulse train (with proper amplitude and possibly flat-top shaping) for transmission over the channel.

### 2.1.2 Channel

A **single shared physical medium** — copper pair, coaxial cable, or RF link. The composite waveform $s_{TDM}(t)$ propagates with assumed **additive white Gaussian noise (AWGN)** being neglected in KTU 2024 syllabus-level questions.

### 2.1.3 Receiver (Demultiplexer) End

1. **Synchronization recovery:** A critical sub-system that extracts the **frame clock** and **slot clock** from the received waveform using a pilot tone or framing bit. *KTU frequently tests this — synchronization loss equals complete system failure.*
2. **De-commutator (distribution switch):** Synchronized to the transmitter commutator, it routes each time slot to the correct output low-pass filter.
3. **Low-pass reconstruction filters:** One per channel, each with cutoff $f_m$ Hz, recovering the analog message $m_i(t)$ from the demultiplexed PAM samples.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is synchronization so critical?** If the receiver commutator drifts by even a fraction of a slot width, samples will be assigned to wrong channels — producing severe **inter-channel crosstalk** that no filter can remove. This is why real TDM systems dedicate a **framing pulse** at the end of every frame.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Operational Walk-Through (Step-by-Step Logic)

Let $N = 3$ channels and each $m_i(t)$ band-limited to $f_m = 4$ kHz (audio band). Then:

- **Sampling rate per channel:** $f_s = 2f_m = 8$ kHz
- **Frame period:** $T_f = 1/f_s = 125\ \mu s$
- **Slot width per channel:** $T_s = T_f / N = 125/3 \approx 41.67\ \mu s$

**Step 1 (Sampling):** At $t = 0$, channel 1 is sampled → $m_1(0)$ placed on the line.
**Step 2 (Commutation delay):** The switch waits for $T_s$ and at $t = T_s$, channel 2 is sampled → $m_2(T_s)$ placed.
**Step 3:** At $t = 2T_s$, channel 3 is sampled → $m_3(2T_s)$ placed.
**Step 4 (Frame end):** At $t = 3T_s = T_f$, the cycle restarts: $m_1(T_f)$ is sampled and the process repeats.

The **composite waveform** $s_{TDM}(t)$ on the line is therefore a sparse sequence where, in every interval of $125\ \mu s$, exactly **one** pulse from **one** channel appears.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hidden Detail Students Miss:** The KTU board frequently asks — *"What is the effective sampling rate of each individual channel as seen by the receiver?"* The answer is **NOT** $3 \times 8$ kHz. It is **still 8 kHz**, because each channel is sampled once per frame. The commutator's rotation rate is what increases, not the per-channel sampling rate.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield, Board-Exam Essentials)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | Per-channel sampling rate | $f_s = 2f_m$ | Hz — minimum by Nyquist theorem |
| 2 | Frame period | $T_f = 1/f_s$ | seconds — time for one complete rotation |
| 3 | Slot duration | $T_s = T_f / N$ | seconds — per-channel time allocation |
| 4 | Commutator rotation rate | $f_r = f_s = 2f_m$ | Hz — **not** $N f_s$ for PAM-TDM |
| 5 | Total samples per second on line | $R_s = N f_s = 2N f_m$ | samples/sec (PAM pulses) |
| 6 | Transmission BW — natural PAM | $B_T = N f_m$ | Hz — narrowest spectrum |
| 7 | Transmission BW — flat-top PAM | $B_T \approx N f_m$ to $1.5\,N f_m$ | Hz — wider due to sinc roll-off |
| 8 | Pulse width constraint | $\tau \leq T_s$ | seconds — must fit in slot |
| 9 | Guard time | $T_g \ll T_s$ | prevents inter-channel overlap |
| 10 | Frame efficiency | $\eta_f = (N \tau)/(N T_s + T_g)$ | dimensionless, 0 to 1 |
| 11 | Reconstruction LPF cutoff | $f_c = f_m$ | Hz — same as original message BW |
| 12 | Sinc interpolation kernel | $m_i(t) = \sum_k m_i(kT_s)\,\text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t - kT_s}{T_s}\right)$ | ideal low-pass recovery |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Table-Formatting Pitfall:** When using $\vert x \vert$ (absolute value) inside markdown tables, ALWAYS use the LaTeX command $\vert$ or $\mid$ to avoid breaking the table cell. Example: $\vert H(f) \vert$, not $\vert H(f) \vert$.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility and Engineering Significance

TDM-PAM is **not just an academic construct** — it forms the foundation of several commercial systems:

1. **T1 Digital Carrier System (USA, 1960s):** Multiplexes **24 voice channels** (8 kHz sampled, 8-bit PCM) over two twisted pairs. Although now using PCM, the historical precursor was TDM-PAM.
2. **Public Switched Telephone Network (PSTN) trunk lines:** Long-haul telephony historically used TDM stages before full digital conversion.
3. **Telemetry and SCADA systems:** Industrial sensor arrays (temperature, pressure, flow) are routinely TDM-PAM multiplexed onto a single RF or wired link in oil/gas, power-grid, and aerospace applications.
4. **Military and avionics data buses:** MIL-STD-1553 and ARINC 429 use time-division principles.
5. **Modern digital communication:** The conceptual leap from TDM-PAM to TDM-PCM is direct — students should mentally **replace "amplitude of pulse"** with "binary codeword" to see the bridge.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Connection for KTU Placements:** Interviewers at companies like **Bharat Electronics (BEL), ISRO, and DRDO** frequently ask: *"Why do we sample at 8 kHz for voice telephony?"* The answer threads back directly to TDM-PAM fundamentals — 4 kHz voice bandwidth × 2 (Nyquist) = 8 kHz sampling, which defines the slot width in subsequent multiplexing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Per-Channel Sampling Rate and Frame Period

**Problem Statement:** A TDM-PAM system carries $N$ voice channels, each band-limited to $f_m = 3.4$ kHz. Determine the **minimum per-channel sampling rate**, the **frame period**, the **slot duration**, and the **commutator rotation rate** in RPM (revolutions per minute), assuming the system uses the **Nyquist criterion with zero margin**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Apply the Nyquist Sampling Theorem**

The Nyquist sampling theorem states that for a band-limited signal of maximum frequency $f_m$, the **minimum sampling frequency** required for unambiguous reconstruction is:

$$
f_s = 2 f_m
$$

This is the absolute theoretical minimum. In practical systems a guard-band factor $\alpha$ (typically 10–25%) is added, yielding $f_s = 2 f_m (1 + \alpha)$, but for the present problem we set $\alpha = 0$.

Substituting $f_m = 3.4$ kHz:

$$
f_s = 2 \times 3.4\ \text{kHz} = 6.8\ \text{kHz}
$$

This is the **rate at which each individual channel is sampled** — note carefully that this does **not** depend on $N$.

**Step 2 — Frame Period**

One **frame** is defined as the time taken for the commutator to complete one full cycle, sampling each of the $N$ channels exactly once. Since the commutator advances from one channel to the next every $1/f_s$ seconds, the frame period is:

$$
T_f = \frac{1}{f_s}
$$

Substituting $f_s = 6.8$ kHz:

$$
T_f = \frac{1}{6.8 \times 10^3}\ \text{seconds} = 1.4706 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{seconds} = 147.06\ \mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Slot Duration**

Within one frame, the time allocated to a single channel is:

$$
T_s = \frac{T_f}{N}
$$

For $N = 4$ (typical KTU problem value):

$$
T_s = \frac{147.06\ \mu\text{s}}{4} = 36.76\ \mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Commutator Rotation Rate in RPM**

The commutator completes one revolution per frame period. Therefore, the rotation frequency is $f_r = 1/T_f = f_s = 6.8$ kHz. To convert to RPM:

$$
f_{r,\text{RPM}} = f_r \times 60 = 6.8 \times 10^3 \times 60 = 4.08 \times 10^5\ \text{RPM}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_{r,\text{RPM}} = 408{,}000\ \text{RPM}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this is unrealistically high:** 408,000 RPM is impossible for any mechanical rotary switch. This is exactly why **modern TDM systems use solid-state electronic commutators** (CMOS analog switches, MOSFET arrays). The KTU 2024 syllabus uses this calculation to motivate the transition to **electronic switching**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Transmission Bandwidth of a TDM-PAM Composite Signal

**Problem Statement:** $N$ PAM signals, each sampled at $f_s = 2f_m$ with **natural (sinc-shaped) sampling pulses**, are TDM-multiplexed. Derive the **minimum transmission bandwidth** required on the channel.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Spectrum of a Single Natural-Sampled PAM Signal**

For a single message $m(t)$ band-limited to $f_m$, natural sampling produces a PAM signal whose spectrum $M_s(f)$ is a **periodic replication** of the baseband spectrum $M(f)$ at integer multiples of $f_s$:

$$
M_s(f) = f_s \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} M(f - n f_s) \cdot \tau \cdot \text{sinc}(n f_s \tau)
$$

For an ideal narrow pulse ($\tau \to 0$), this simplifies to:

$$
M_s(f) \approx f_s \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} M(f - n f_s)
$$

**Step 2 — Effect of Time-Division Multiplexing**

When $N$ such PAM signals are interleaved with **mutually exclusive time slots**, the **time-domain multiplication** is replaced by a **time-shifted summation**:

$$
s_{TDM}(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} m_k(t) \cdot p(t - k T_s)
$$

where $p(t)$ is the sampling pulse train. In the **frequency domain**, this is a **linear superposition** — the spectrum of $s_{TDM}(t)$ is the **sum of the individual PAM spectra**, weighted by phase factors $e^{-j 2\pi f k T_s}$:

$$
S_{TDM}(f) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} M_{s,k}(f) \cdot e^{-j 2\pi f k T_s}
$$

Since all $M_{s,k}(f)$ have **identical magnitudes** (differing only in the message $m_k(t)$), the **envelope** of $|S_{TDM}(f)|$ is $N$ times the envelope of any single $|M_{s,k}(f)|$ — but the **bandwidth** of the support is **unchanged**, because the spectral replicas are still centered at multiples of $f_s$.

**Step 3 — Identify the Baseband Replica Spacing**

The replicas of $M(f)$ appear at $\ldots, -2f_s, -f_s, 0, f_s, 2f_s, \ldots$ For unambiguous recovery of the baseband ($n=0$ replica), the replica centered at $f_s$ must not overlap the one at $0$:

$$
f_s \geq 2 f_m
$$

This recovers the Nyquist condition. The **lowest non-zero replica** is centered at $f_s = 2f_m$, so the **first null** of the baseband sinc envelope occurs at $f_m$. Therefore, the **minimum bandwidth** to pass the baseband and the first replica cleanly is:

$$
B_{T,\min} = f_s - f_m = 2 f_m - f_m = f_m
$$

This is the bandwidth required for **one channel**. For $N$ channels, the total composite bandwidth sums to:

$$
B_T = N \cdot f_m
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{B_T = N f_m\ \text{Hz}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Practical Note:** This is the **ideal** minimum. In practice, with **flat-top sampling** (square pulses of width $\tau$), each spectral replica is multiplied by a $\text{sinc}(f\tau)$ envelope, which has **slow roll-off**. To accommodate this, engineers use a **bandwidth expansion factor** of 1.2 to 1.5, giving:

$$
B_T^{\text{flat-top}} \approx (1.2 \text{ to } 1.5) \cdot N f_m
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Signal Reconstruction via Low-Pass Filtering (Demultiplexed Channel)

**Problem Statement:** After demultiplexing a TDM-PAM signal, channel 1's samples are passed through an ideal low-pass filter of cutoff $f_c$. Determine $f_c$ and write the interpolation formula that recovers $m_1(t)$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the Sampled Signal for Channel 1**

Channel 1's contribution to the composite TDM signal is:

$$
s_1(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} m_1(kT_s) \cdot p(t - kT_s)
$$

where $T_s = 1/f_s$ and $p(t)$ is the sampling pulse (sinc-shaped for natural PAM).

**Step 2 — Identify the Spectrum of $s_1(t)$**

Taking the Fourier transform (assuming ideal impulses for natural sampling):

$$
S_1(f) = f_s \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} M_1(f - n f_s)
$$

**Step 3 — Choose the LPF Cutoff**

To recover $M_1(f)$ (the original baseband spectrum) and reject all replicas ($n \neq 0$), the LPF must pass the band $\vert f \vert \leq f_m$ and reject $\vert f \vert \geq f_s - f_m = f_m$. Therefore:

$$
\boxed{f_c = f_m}
$$

**Step 4 — Reconstruction Formula (Whittaker–Shannon Interpolation)**

In the time domain, ideal LPF reconstruction is mathematically equivalent to convolution with the sinc kernel:

$$
m_1(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} m_1(kT_s) \cdot \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t - kT_s}{T_s}\right)
$$

where $\text{sinc}(x) = \sin(\pi x)/(\pi x)$ is the **normalized sinc function**. This is the famous **Whittaker–Shannon interpolation theorem**, and KTU examiners occasionally test it as a 3-mark conceptual question.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Natural vs. Flat-Top PAM in TDM

| Parameter | Natural-Sampled PAM-TDM | Flat-Top (Sample-and-Hold) PAM-TDM |
|-----------|--------------------------|--------------------------------------|
| Pulse shape | Sinc-shaped, $\tau \to 0$ | Rectangular, $\tau = T_s/N$ |
| Spectral envelope | Sharp sinc decay | Slower sinc decay with $\text{sinc}(f\tau)$ roll-off |
| Transmission bandwidth | $B_T = N f_m$ | $B_T \approx 1.5 N f_m$ (typical) |
| Aperture effect (gain droop at $f_m$) | Negligible | Significant — needs equalizer |
| Implementation complexity | Difficult (impractical pulse shape) | Easy (S/H circuit) |
| TDM suitability | Theoretical reference | **Preferred in practice** |
| KTU exam frequency | High (theory questions) | High (numerical problems) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Aperture Effect — Often Ignored:** When flat-top PAM is low-pass filtered for reconstruction, the output amplitude is **attenuated by a factor** $\text{sinc}(f T_s)$ compared to the original. At the highest frequency $f_m$, the attenuation is $\text{sinc}(f_m T_s) = \text{sinc}(1/2) = 2/\pi \approx 0.637$. This **36% droop** is unacceptable in high-fidelity systems, motivating the use of **equalizing amplifiers** in real receivers. KTU 2024 questions on TDM-PAM sometimes include this as a 3-mark sub-part.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Complete TDM-PAM System Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src1[Source m1 t] -->|Bandlimited| pam1[PAM Sampler 1]
    src2[Source m2 t] -->|Bandlimited| pam2[PAM Sampler 2]
    src3[Source m3 t] -->|Bandlimited| pam3[PAM Sampler 3]
    srcN[Source mN t] -->|Bandlimited| pamN[PAM Sampler N]

    pam1 --> sw1[Commutator Switch Position 1]
    pam2 --> sw2[Commutator Switch Position 2]
    pam3 --> sw3[Commutator Switch Position 3]
    pamN --> swN[Commutator Switch Position N]

    sw1 --> mux[High Speed Multiplexer Bus]
    sw2 --> mux
    sw3 --> mux
    swN --> mux

    mux -->|Composite PAM| chan[Transmission Channel Single Medium]

    chan --> rx[Receiver Front End Amplifier]
    rx -->|Waveform| sync[Synchronization and Frame Clock Recovery]
    sync -->|Slot Clock| desm[De commutator Distribution Switch]

    desm -->|Slot 1 out| lpf1[LPF Cutoff fm Channel 1]
    desm -->|Slot 2 out| lpf2[LPF Cutoff fm Channel 2]
    desm -->|Slot 3 out| lpf3[LPF Cutoff fm Channel 3]
    desm -->|Slot N out| lpfN[LPF Cutoff fm Channel N]

    lpf1 --> out1[Recovered m1 t]
    lpf2 --> out2[Recovered m2 t]
    lpf3 --> out3[Recovered m3 t]
    lpfN --> outN[Recovered mN t]

    style chan fill:#ffd966,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:3px
    style sync fill:#c5e0b4,stroke:#385723,stroke-width:2px
    style mux fill:#9bc2e6,stroke:#1f4e79,stroke-width:2px
    style desm fill:#9bc2e6,stroke:#1f4e79,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.2 Frame Structure and Time-Slot Allocation Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph frame1[ONE COMPLETE FRAME T sub f]
        slot1[Slot 1 Channel 1 m1 t sampled at t=0]
        slot2[Slot 2 Channel 2 m2 t sampled at t=Ts]
        slot3[Slot 3 Channel 3 m3 t sampled at t=2Ts]
        slot4[Slot N Channel N mN t sampled at t=NTs minus Ts]
        guard[Guard Time T sub g small gap]
    end

    slot1 --> slot2 --> slot3 --> slot4 --> guard

    note1[Ts equals T sub f divided by N] -.-> slot1
    note2[Frame repeats every T sub f equals 1 over f sub s] -.-> frame1

    style slot1 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656
    style slot2 fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#e69138
    style slot3 fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#6aa84f
    style slot4 fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#3c78d8
    style guard fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#cc0000
    style note1 fill:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-dasharray:5 5
    style note2 fill:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-dasharray:5 5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Frame Diagram:** Reading left-to-right along the timeline of one frame, the **electronic commutator** samples channel 1 first, then channel 2, then channel 3, ..., then channel $N$. The small red **guard time** at the end absorbs any timing jitter. The next frame begins immediately, sampling channel 1 again (now at $t = T_f$).

---

## 4.3 PAM-TDM Composite Waveform Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph inputs[INPUT SOURCES]
        A[Message m1 t sine wave]
        B[Message m2 t cosine wave]
        C[Message m3 t ramp wave]
    end

    subgraph samples[SAMPLED PULSES]
        A1[Pulse 1 from m1 amplitude A]
        B1[Pulse 2 from m2 amplitude B]
        C1[Pulse 3 from m3 amplitude C]
        A2[Pulse 4 from m1 next sample]
        B2[Pulse 5 from m2 next sample]
        C2[Pulse 6 from m3 next sample]
    end

    subgraph composite[COMPOSITE TDM WAVEFORM ON CHANNEL]
        S[Interleaved pulse stream A B C A B C ...]
    end

    A --> A1 --> S
    A --> A2 --> S
    B --> B1 --> S
    B --> B2 --> S
    C --> C1 --> S
    C --> C2 --> S

    style S fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,stroke-width:3px
    style A fill:#d9ead3
    style B fill:#cfe2f3
    style C fill:#f4cccc
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway from the Waveform Diagram:** The composite TDM waveform has **variable amplitudes** — each pulse height is determined by the *current* sample of whichever channel was just commutated. Although the channel "carries" $N$ separate messages, the line voltage at any instant reflects only **one channel's sample**. This is what makes the receiver demultiplexer possible: a synchronized switch can re-separate the pulses by time slot.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (Conceptual, CO3, Bloom Level: Remember/Understand)
**`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**
**Q: Define Time Division Multiplexing (TDM). State two advantages of TDM over Frequency Division Multiplexing (FDM).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Time Division Multiplexing (TDM) is a technique in which multiple independent signals are transmitted over a single communication channel by allocating **distinct, non-overlapping time slots** to each signal in a **cyclically repeating frame**. The transmission medium is shared in the time domain.

**Advantages over FDM (2 marks, 1 each):**
1. **No cross-talk due to spectral overlap:** Since TDM signals occupy the same bandwidth but at different times, there is no intermodulation distortion from nonlinear amplifiers — a major issue in FDM.
2. **No need for complex band-pass filters:** TDM demultiplexing requires only simple **synchronized electronic switches** and **low-pass filters**, whereas FDM needs banks of precisely tuned BPFs.
3. *(Bonus optional third point)* Easier to integrate with digital systems — a natural bridge to PCM-TDM.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Part A:** Students often confuse **FDM advantages** (e.g., "no synchronization needed") with **TDM advantages**. Stick strictly to TDM's benefits in your answer.

---

### Question 2 (Numerical/Definition, CO3, Bloom Level: Apply)
**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**
**Q: A TDM-PAM system multiplexes 4 signals, each band-limited to 5 kHz. Calculate the commutator rotation rate and the time slot per channel. State the Nyquist sampling rate used.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Nyquist sampling rate per channel:
$$
f_s = 2 f_m = 2 \times 5\ \text{kHz} = 10\ \text{kHz}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Commutator rotation rate (= sampling rate for PAM-TDM):
$$
f_r = f_s = 10\ \text{kHz}
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Time slot per channel:
$$
T_s = \frac{T_f}{N} = \frac{1}{N f_s} = \frac{1}{4 \times 10{,}000} = 25\ \mu\text{s}
$$

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Design Problem

**`[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Adapted to 2024 Scheme]`**
**Q: A 4-channel TDM-PAM system carries voice signals each band-limited to 3.4 kHz. The system uses natural sampling with $\tau = 5\ \mu\text{s}$ pulse width and includes a 1 µs guard time between slots.**

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Draw the complete block diagram of the TDM-PAM transmitter and receiver. Determine the per-channel sampling rate, the frame period, the slot duration, and the minimum transmission bandwidth.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** Explain with suitable diagrams (i) the function of the commutator, (ii) the necessity of synchronization, and (iii) the role of the low-pass filter at the receiver. State one limitation of TDM-PAM.

---

### MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** [Draw the standard TDM-PAM system: 4 message sources → 4 PAM samplers → electronic commutator → channel → receiver amplifier → de-commutator → 4 LPFs → 4 outputs. Label all blocks clearly.]

**Calculations (5 marks):**

**Step 1 — Per-channel sampling rate (Nyquist):**
$$
f_s = 2 f_m = 2 \times 3.4 = 6.8\ \text{kHz}
$$

**Step 2 — Frame period:**
$$
T_f = \frac{1}{f_s} = \frac{1}{6800} = 1.4706 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{s} = 147.06\ \mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Slot duration (with guard time):**

The frame must accommodate 4 pulses of width $\tau$ and 4 guard times of width $T_g$ (one per slot, or equivalently distributed):
$$
T_f = N \tau + N T_g
$$

$$
T_f = 4 \times 5\ \mu\text{s} + 4 \times 1\ \mu\text{s} = 20 + 4 = 24\ \mu\text{s}
$$

But the **true frame period** is set by the sampling rate: $T_f = 147.06\ \mu\text{s}$. The "effective" busy time per frame is only 24 µs; the rest is "idle" time (no pulse present, just zero amplitude).

The **time slot per channel** is:
$$
T_s = \frac{T_f}{N} = \frac{147.06\ \mu\text{s}}{4} = 36.76\ \mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Minimum transmission bandwidth (natural PAM):**
$$
B_T = N f_m = 4 \times 3.4\ \text{kHz} = 13.6\ \text{kHz}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** The pulse width $\tau = 5\ \mu\text{s}$ and $T_g = 1\ \mu\text{s}$ together occupy only $\tau + T_g = 6\ \mu\text{s}$ per slot, which is well within the $36.76\ \mu\text{s}$ slot — providing a large safety margin. Students who calculate the bandwidth using $\tau$ directly (e.g., $B_T = 1/2\tau$) make a common error.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**(i) Function of the commutator (2 marks):**
The electronic commutator is a high-speed sequential switch that **rotates at the sampling rate $f_s$**, connecting each input channel to the common bus for a brief interval $T_s$. It performs the **time-interleaving** operation that defines TDM. In modern systems, it is implemented using **MOSFET analog switches** triggered by a ring counter.

**(ii) Necessity of synchronization (2 marks):**
The receiver de-commutator must rotate **in lockstep** with the transmitter commutator — same frequency, same phase. If synchronization is lost, samples from channel 1 may be routed to channel 2's filter, producing **inter-channel crosstalk**. Synchronization is achieved by transmitting a **framing pulse** (a distinctive marker) at the end of every frame, which the receiver PLL locks onto.

**(iii) Role of the LPF (2 marks):**
Each demultiplexed PAM channel contains **replicas of the original baseband message** at multiples of $f_s$. The LPF with cutoff $f_m$ passes the baseband replica (0 to $f_m$) and rejects all higher-frequency replicas, recovering the original analog message via Whittaker-Shannon interpolation.

**Limitation of TDM-PAM (1 mark):**
TDM-PAM is highly sensitive to **timing jitter** and **synchronization errors**. A small drift in the commutator can cause severe inter-channel interference. Additionally, PAM is **vulnerable to channel noise** (no noise immunity like FM or PCM) — this is why most real systems use **TDM-PCM** instead.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Adapted to 2024 Scheme]`**
**Q: An 8-channel TDM system uses PAM with each message signal band-limited to 4 kHz. The system employs flat-top sampling with pulse width $\tau$ equal to half the slot duration.**

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Calculate the Nyquist sampling rate per channel, the commutator rotation rate, the frame duration, the slot duration, and the pulse width. Also determine the minimum transmission bandwidth assuming a 30% bandwidth expansion factor for flat-top sampling.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** Compare natural sampling and flat-top sampling in the context of TDM. With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the operation of the receiver section, and state the cutoff frequency of the reconstruction filter with justification.

---

### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Nyquist sampling rate per channel:**
$$
f_s = 2 f_m = 2 \times 4 = 8\ \text{kHz}
$$

**Step 2 — Commutator rotation rate:**
$$
f_r = f_s = 8\ \text{kHz} = 8{,}000\ \text{revolutions/second}
$$

In RPM:
$$
f_{r,\text{RPM}} = 8{,}000 \times 60 = 480{,}000\ \text{RPM}
$$

**Step 3 — Frame duration:**
$$
T_f = \frac{1}{f_s} = \frac{1}{8000} = 1.25 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{s} = 125\ \mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Slot duration:**
$$
T_s = \frac{T_f}{N} = \frac{125\ \mu\text{s}}{8} = 15.625\ \mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 5 — Pulse width (flat-top, $\tau = T_s/2$):**
$$
\tau = \frac{T_s}{2} = \frac{15.625\ \mu\text{s}}{2} = 7.8125\ \mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 6 — Minimum transmission bandwidth (with 30% expansion):**

Ideal (natural) bandwidth:
$$
B_{T,\text{ideal}} = N f_m = 8 \times 4\ \text{kHz} = 32\ \text{kHz}
$$

With flat-top expansion factor $\beta = 1.3$:
$$
B_T = \beta \cdot N f_m = 1.3 \times 32\ \text{kHz} = 41.6\ \text{kHz}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Mistake:** Students sometimes compute $B_T$ from $\tau$ alone (e.g., $B_T = 1/2\tau$). This is **incorrect** for TDM-PAM — the bandwidth is set by the **message content and sampling rate**, not the pulse width. Pulse width affects only the **spectral envelope roll-off**, not the fundamental bandwidth.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Comparison table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Natural Sampling | Flat-Top Sampling |
|-----------|------------------|--------------------|
| Pulse shape | Tapered (sinc-like) | Rectangular, constant amplitude |
| Practical implementability | Difficult | Easy (S/H amplifier) |
| Aperture effect | None | Yes — $\text{sinc}(f\tau)$ droop |
| Required equalizer | Not needed | Often required |
| Bandwidth efficiency | Higher | Slightly lower |
| Use in TDM | Reference model | **Industry standard** |

**Receiver block diagram (2 marks):**
Channel → Synchronization recovery (PLL) → De-commutator (synchronized switch) → 8 parallel LPFs → 8 reconstructed message outputs.

**Cutoff frequency of reconstruction filter (2 marks):**

The cutoff must equal the **highest message frequency**:
$$
f_c = f_m = 4\ \text{kHz}
$$

**Justification:** The baseband replica of $M(f)$ occupies $\vert f \vert \leq f_m$. The next spectral replica is centered at $f_s = 8$ kHz. An ideal LPF with cutoff $f_c = f_m$ will pass the baseband replica and reject all higher replicas, achieving perfect reconstruction by the Whittaker-Shannon theorem.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **Do not** confuse commutator rotation rate ($2f_m$) with the total pulse rate ($2Nf_m$) — this is a **3-mark deduction** in KTU papers.
> 2. **Do not** omit the units (Hz, kHz, µs) in numerical answers — KTU board examiners **strictly deduct** 0.5 marks per missing unit.
> 3. **Always** state the Nyquist theorem explicitly before calculating $f_s$ — students who jump straight to $f_s = 2f_m$ without naming the theorem lose 1 mark.
> 4. In Part B answers, ensure the **block diagram is fully labeled** — an unlabeled block is treated as missing content and attracts a 2-mark penalty.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Revision — 5-Minute Summary**

- ✅ **Core Formula Triad:** $f_s = 2f_m$, $T_f = 1/f_s$, $T_s = T_f/N$
- ✅ **Commutator rotates at $f_s$**, NOT at $N f_s$ — common error trap
- ✅ **Total samples per second on the line:** $R = 2N f_m$ (PAM pulses/sec)
- ✅ **Minimum transmission bandwidth (natural PAM):** $B_T = N f_m$
- ✅ **Flat-top PAM bandwidth:** multiply by 1.2 to 1.5 (aperture effect)
- ✅ **Reconstruction LPF cutoff:** $f_c = f_m$ (NOT $f_s$)
- ✅ **Frame efficiency:** $\eta_f = N\tau / (N T_s + T_g) < 1$
- ✅ **Synchronization is critical** — a single framing pulse per frame suffices in practice
- ✅ **Two filters at receiver per channel:** synchronization recovery + reconstruction LPF
- ✅ **TDM-PAM is a stepping stone to TDM-PCM** — replace amplitude with codeword
- ✅ **Aperture effect droop at $f_m$ for flat-top:** $2/\pi \approx 0.637$ (36% loss) — needs equalizer
- ✅ **Real-world examples:** T1 carrier (24 channels), telemetry/SCADA, avionics buses
- ✅ **Limitation of TDM-PAM:** sensitive to noise, timing jitter, and synchronization loss
- ✅ **Guard time $T_g$** must be **much smaller** than $T_s$ to keep frame efficiency high
- ✅ **Whittaker-Shannon interpolation** is the mathematical basis of ideal reconstruction — know the sinc kernel

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
