topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET864 - ELECTRONIC COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pulse Code Modulation (PCM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pulse Code Modulation (PCM)** is a digital modulation technique in which an analog message signal is represented, transmitted, and reproduced as a sequence of **discrete-time, discrete-amplitude**, binary-coded pulses. The process involves three sequential operations: **Sampling**, **Quantization**, and **Encoding** (often referred to as the *SQE pipeline*).

The analog input $m(t)$ is sampled at a rate satisfying the **Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem** ($f_s \geq 2f_m$), each sample is rounded off to the nearest of $L=2^n$ discrete amplitude levels, and finally each quantized level is mapped to a unique $n$-bit binary codeword. PCM is the foundational technique behind all digital audio (telephony, CD audio, VoIP) and digital voice transmission standards like **G.711**.

## 1.2 The Intuitive Analogy — "The Smart Postal Worker"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy: The Postal System for Sound**
>
> Imagine you want to send the continuous flow of water from a tap to a friend far away through a postal system (which only accepts letters — *binary codes*).
> 1. **Sampling** = You take a *photograph* of the water flow every millisecond (discrete-in-time snapshots).
> 2. **Quantization** = You measure each snapshot and *round it* to the nearest of, say, 256 pre-decided volume labels (discrete-in-amplitude).
> 3. **Encoding** = You write down the **binary number** corresponding to that volume label (e.g., volume "184" → `10111000`).
> 4. The friend receives the letter, *decodes* the binary back to the label, and the label back to a "best estimate" water level — close to the original, but never perfectly continuous.
>
> The little "rounding error" is called **quantization noise**, the price paid for going digital.

## 1.3 Why PCM is the Backbone of Digital Communication

| Feature | Benefit |
|---|---|
| **Noise Immunity** | Regenerative repeaters can clean the signal between hops. |
| **Encryption Friendly** | Bitstream can be encrypted, compressed, multiplexed. |
| **TDM Compatibility** | Multiple PCM channels can share one cable via Time Division Multiplexing. |
| **Standardization** | Universal format for speech, audio, telemetry. |
| **Trade-off** | Requires **larger bandwidth** than the original analog signal. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric to Memorise**: For an $n$-bit PCM system, the theoretical maximum **Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR)** is approximately $6.02n + 1.76$ **dB** — a 6 dB improvement per added bit. This is the most tested numerical fact in KTU exams.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Stage SQE Pipeline

### Stage 1 — Sampling
The continuous-time signal $m(t)$ (band-limited to $f_m$ Hz) is multiplied by an impulse train $p(t)$ of period $T_s$. This produces a **PAM signal** (naturally sampled or flat-topped).

**Nyquist Criterion**: To allow perfect reconstruction,
$$f_s \geq 2f_m \quad \text{where typically} \quad f_s = 2f_m \text{ (Nyquist rate)}.$$
Sampling at $f_s < 2f_m$ causes **aliasing**, an irreversible distortion.

### Stage 2 — Quantization
The sampler output amplitude $m(kT_s)$ is *mapped* to the nearest of $L$ discrete levels. With $L = 2^n$ levels (each level needs an $n$-bit codeword):

$$\boxed{\Delta = \frac{V_{max} - V_{min}}{L} = \frac{V_{pp}}{2^n}}$$

- **Uniform Quantization**: All intervals $\Delta$ are equal. Best for signals with uniform amplitude distribution; poor for speech (which spends most time near zero).
- **Non-Uniform Quantization (Companding)**: $\Delta$ is small for low amplitudes, large for high amplitudes. Implemented by first passing the signal through a **compressor** (logarithmic curve) at the transmitter, then an **expander** at the receiver.
  - **µ-law** (USA/Japan): $F(x) = \text{sgn}(x)\dfrac{\ln(1+\mu \vert x \vert)}{\ln(1+\mu)}$, with $\mu = 255$.
  - **A-law** (Europe/India/most of world): $F(x) = \text{sgn}(x)\dfrac{A \vert x \vert}{1+\ln A}$ for $\vert x \vert \geq 1/A$, with $A = 87.6$.

### Stage 3 — Encoding
Each quantized level is converted to an $n$-bit binary word. The most common schemes:
- **NRZ-L (Non-Return-to-Zero Level)** — `1` = high voltage, `0` = low voltage.
- **NRZ-M / NRZ-S** — transitions on `1` or `0`.
- **Bipolar AMI** — alternate polarity for `1`s; `0` = no pulse (DC-balanced, used in T1 carrier).
- **B8ZS / HDB3** — used to maintain synchronization in long bipolar streams.

## 2.2 Quantization Error Analysis

The instantaneous quantization error is $e_q = m_q - m$, where $m_q$ is the quantized value and $m$ is the true sample. For uniform mid-tread quantizer:

$$-\frac{\Delta}{2} \leq e_q \leq +\frac{\Delta}{2}$$

Assuming uniform error distribution, the **mean-square quantization noise power** (normalized to a $1\,\Omega$ load) is:

$$N_q = \overline{e_q^2} = \frac{1}{\Delta}\int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e^2 \, de = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial assumption**: $e_q$ is treated as an additive white noise that is **statistically independent** of the signal. This holds when the signal is "busy" and crosses many quantization levels (i.e., not a constant DC).

## 2.3 Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR)

For a **full-scale sinusoidal input** $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f t)$ with peak $A_m = V_{pp}/2 = L\Delta/2$:

**Signal Power**: $S = \dfrac{A_m^2}{2} = \dfrac{L^2 \Delta^2}{8}$

**SQNR (numeric ratio)**:
$$\left(\frac{S}{N_q}\right) = \frac{L^2 \Delta^2 / 8}{\Delta^2/12} = \frac{3L^2}{2} = \frac{3 \cdot 2^{2n}}{2}$$

$$\boxed{\left(\frac{S}{N_q}\right)_{dB} = 6.02\, n + 1.76 \text{ dB}}$$

## 2.4 Transmission Parameters (Bit Rate & Bandwidth)

| Parameter | Formula | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of bits per sample** | $n = \log_2 L$ | $L=256 \Rightarrow n=8$ |
| **Bit rate** | $R_b = n f_s$ | Telephony: $n=8, f_s=8$ kHz $\Rightarrow R_b=64$ kbps |
| **Minimum Bandwidth (Nyquist)** | $B_{min} = R_b / 2$ | $64$ kbps $\Rightarrow 32$ kHz |
| **Practical Bandwidth (raised-cosine, $\alpha=1$)** | $B = R_b$ | $64$ kbps $\Rightarrow 64$ kHz |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Quantization levels | $L$ | $L = 2^n$ | — |
| 2 | Step size | $\Delta$ | $\Delta = V_{pp} / 2^n$ | Volts |
| 3 | Bit rate | $R_b$ | $R_b = n f_s$ | bps |
| 4 | Min. bandwidth | $B$ | $B = R_b/2$ | Hz |
| 5 | Quantization noise power | $N_q$ | $N_q = \Delta^2/12$ | W |
| 6 | SQNR (ratio) | $S/N_q$ | $3 \cdot 2^{2n}/2$ | — |
| 7 | SQNR (dB) | $(S/N_q)_{dB}$ | $6.02n + 1.76$ | dB |
| 8 | Output SNR w/ companding | — | (table value) | dB |

## 2.6 Engineering Utility
PCM is the universal currency of digital audio: **Compact Disc (CD) audio** uses $f_s = 44.1$ kHz, $n = 16$ bits per channel → bit rate $= 1.411$ Mbps per stereo channel. **Telephony (G.711)** uses 8 kHz sampling with 8-bit A-law companded quantization → 64 kbps per DS0 channel. Modern **VoLTE** and **5G NR** build further compression layers (e.g., AMR-WB, EVS) over this PCM core.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Mean-Square Quantization Noise Power

We start from the assumption that the instantaneous quantization error $e$ is uniformly distributed between $-\Delta/2$ and $+\Delta/2$.

**Step 1 — Define the probability density function.**
The uniform distribution of error over the interval of width $\Delta$ gives the constant PDF:
$$p(e) = \frac{1}{\Delta} \quad \text{for} \quad -\frac{\Delta}{2} \leq e \leq +\frac{\Delta}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Write the mean-square expectation.**
$$N_q = \overline{e^2} = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} e^2 \, p(e) \, de$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the bounded PDF.**
$$N_q = \frac{1}{\Delta} \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e^2 \, de$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the antiderivative $\int e^2 de = e^3/3$.**
$$N_q = \frac{1}{\Delta}\left[\frac{e^3}{3}\right]_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2}$$

**Step 5 — Plug in limits and simplify.**
$$N_q = \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \frac{1}{3}\left[\left(\frac{\Delta}{2}\right)^3 - \left(-\frac{\Delta}{2}\right)^3\right] = \frac{1}{3\Delta} \cdot \frac{2\Delta^3}{8}$$

$$\boxed{N_q = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation**: The quantization noise power depends *only* on the step size $\Delta$, *not* on the signal shape. Halving $\Delta$ (adding 1 bit) reduces the noise power by a factor of 4 (i.e., 6 dB).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Signal Power of a Full-Scale Sinusoid

**Step 1 — Take a sinusoid with peak $A_m$.**
$$m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f t)$$

**Step 2 — Average power over one period.**
$$S = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T A_m^2 \cos^2(2\pi f t)\,dt = \frac{A_m^2}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Express $A_m$ in terms of $L$ and $\Delta$.**
For a full-scale sine wave, the peak just reaches the extreme quantizer level, so:
$$A_m = \frac{L\Delta}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into signal power.**
$$S = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{L\Delta}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{L^2 \Delta^2}{8}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: SQNR in Numeric Form (→ $6.02n + 1.76$ dB)

**Step 1 — Form the ratio.**
$$\frac{S}{N_q} = \frac{L^2\Delta^2/8}{\Delta^2/12}$$

**Step 2 — Cancel $\Delta^2$.**
$$\frac{S}{N_q} = \frac{12 L^2}{8} = \frac{3L^2}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Replace $L = 2^n$.**
$$\frac{S}{N_q} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot (2^n)^2 = \frac{3}{2}\cdot 2^{2n} = 1.5 \cdot 2^{2n}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to decibels.**
$$\left(\frac{S}{N_q}\right)_{dB} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{3}{2}\cdot 2^{2n}\right) = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{3}{2}\right) + 20n\log_{10}2$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the constants.**
$10\log_{10}(1.5) \approx 1.76$ dB; $\;\;20\log_{10}2 \approx 6.02$ dB.

$$\boxed{\left(\frac{S}{N_q}\right)_{dB} = 6.02n + 1.76 \text{ dB}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb (The 6 dB Law)**: *Every additional bit added to the PCM word increases the SQNR by ~6 dB.* This is the single most important numerical relationship in PCM theory — directly tested in KTU ESE questions.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 1 (KTU-Style 3-Mark Problem)

**Question**: An audio signal with $f_m = 4$ kHz is PCM-encoded using $n = 8$ bits/sample. Find (i) the bit rate, (ii) the minimum transmission bandwidth, and (iii) the output SQNR in dB.

**Solution**:

(i) Nyquist rate: $f_s = 2f_m = 2 \times 4000 = 8000$ samples/sec.
$$R_b = n f_s = 8 \times 8000 = 64{,}000 \text{ bps} = 64 \text{ kbps}$$

(ii) Minimum Nyquist bandwidth:
$$B_{min} = \frac{R_b}{2} = \frac{64{,}000}{2} = 32 \text{ kHz}$$

(iii) SQNR using the formula:
$$(S/N_q)_{dB} = 6.02n + 1.76 = 6.02(8) + 1.76 = 48.16 + 1.76 = 49.92 \approx 50 \text{ dB}$$

**Answer**: $R_b = 64$ kbps, $B_{min} = 32$ kHz, SQNR $\approx 49.92$ dB.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example 2 (Quantization Levels)

**Question**: A signal with $V_{max} = +4$ V and $V_{min} = -4$ V is quantized into 1024 levels. Find (i) number of bits, (ii) step size $\Delta$, (iii) RMS quantization noise voltage.

**Solution**:

(i) $n = \log_2 1024 = 10$ bits.

(ii) $V_{pp} = V_{max} - V_{min} = 4 - (-4) = 8$ V.
$$\Delta = \frac{V_{pp}}{2^n} = \frac{8}{1024} = 7.8125 \text{ mV}$$

(iii) RMS noise voltage across $1\,\Omega$ load:
$$e_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{\Delta^2}{12}} = \frac{\Delta}{\sqrt{12}} = \frac{7.8125 \times 10^{-3}}{3.4641} \approx 2.255 \text{ mV}$$

---

## 3.6 Worked Example 3: Effect of Increasing Bits on SQNR

**Question**: An 8-bit PCM system is replaced by a 12-bit system. By how many dB does the SQNR improve?

**Solution**:
$$\Delta \text{SQNR} = 6.02(n_2 - n_1) = 6.02(12 - 8) = 24.08 \text{ dB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight**: Going from 8 to 12 bits quadruples the storage and bandwidth, but buys ~24 dB (a factor of 256×) noise improvement. This is the classic engineering trade-off in PCM design.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Complete PCM System Block Diagram (End-to-End)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Analog Input Signal m(t)] --> B[Anti Aliasing Low Pass Filter]
    B --> C[Sampler with Impulse Train p(t)]
    C --> D[PAM Signal Samples m kTs]
    D --> E[Quantizer Uniform or Companded]
    E --> F[Binary Encoder n bit Codeword]
    F --> G[Line Encoder Channel Coding]
    G --> H[Digital Channel with Noise]
    H --> I[Line Decoder Regenerative Repeater]
    I --> J[Binary Decoder]
    J --> K[Reconstruction Low Pass Filter]
    K --> L[Analog Output m hat of t]

    subgraph TX_PCM[Transmitter]
        B
        C
        D
        E
        F
        G
    end

    subgraph RX_PCM[Receiver]
        I
        J
        K
        L
    end

    classDef tx fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px,color:#0D47A1
    classDef rx fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#BF360C
    classDef chan fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:2px,color:#311B92

    class B,C,D,E,F,G tx
    class I,J,K,L rx
    class H chan
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram**: The blue blocks (left) form the **PCM Transmitter** chain — input band-limiting → sampling → quantization → encoding → line coding. The orange blocks (right) are the **PCM Receiver** — line decoding → binary decoding → low-pass reconstruction. The purple block is the noisy channel where **regenerative repeaters** (placed inside the line decoder) clean the bitstream at intermediate points — the prime reason PCM is preferred for long-distance telephony.

---

## 4.2 Quantization Mid-Tread Characteristic (Uniform)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph IN[Input Continuous Amplitude]
        I0[Input sample value]
    end
    subgraph ST[Step Function Quantizer]
        S0[Level 0 Range minus Delta over 2 to plus Delta over 2]
        S1[Level 1 Range Delta over 2 to 3 Delta over 2]
        S2[Level 2 Range 3 Delta over 2 to 5 Delta over 2]
        S3[Level minus 1 Range minus 3 Delta over 2 to minus Delta over 2]
    end
    subgraph OUT[Output Discrete Level Index]
        O0[Code 00]
        O1[Code 01]
        O2[Code 10]
        O3[Code 11]
    end

    I0 --> S0 --> O0
    I0 --> S1 --> O1
    I0 --> S2 --> O2
    I0 --> S3 --> O3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation**: This is a **mid-tread** quantizer (used in telephony) — there is a level centered at zero, so the code `100...0` represents *exactly* zero input. The other variant is **mid-riser**, where zero input falls at a decision boundary between two codes. Telephony (µ-law/A-law PCM) typically uses mid-tread.

---

## 4.3 Companding Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Analog Input m of t] --> B[Compressor Logarithmic Curve]
    B --> C[Uniform Quantizer Smaller Dynamic Range Handled]
    C --> D[Binary Encoder]
    D --> E[Digital Channel]
    E --> F[Binary Decoder]
    F --> G[Expander Inverse Logarithmic Curve]
    G --> H[Reconstruction LPF]
    H --> I[Analog Output m hat of t]

    classDef comp fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px,color:#7F0000
    classDef exp fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#1B5E20
    classDef mid fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,stroke-width:2px,color:#01579B

    class B,C comp
    class G exp
    class A,D,E,F,H,I mid
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why compand?** Speech signals have a large peak-to-average ratio (about 14 dB). Uniform quantization wastes precision on rarely-used large amplitudes and gives coarse resolution to the frequently-used low amplitudes. Companding (Compress + Expand) gives a quasi-logarithmic transfer curve so SQNR becomes nearly **constant across the entire dynamic range** — typically ~38 dB output SNR for an 8-bit companded PCM system (vs. only ~22 dB for uniform).

---

## 4.4 TDM Hierarchy (PCM Carriers)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DS1[T1 Carrier North America Japan]
        C0[DS0 64 kbps Voice Channel]
        C1[DS0 Channel 2]
        C2[DS0 Channel 3]
        C3[DS0 Channel N]
        C0 & C1 & C2 & C3 --> MUX1[Time Division Multiplexer]
        MUX1 --> T1[T1 Output 1.544 Mbps 24 DS0 plus Framing]
    end

    subgraph E1[E1 Carrier Europe India]
        D0[TS1 through TS15 and TS17 through TS31 64 kbps each]
        D1[TS0 and TS16 Framing and Signalling]
        D0 --> MUX2[Time Division Multiplexer]
        D1 --> MUX2
        MUX2 --> E1O[E1 Output 2.048 Mbps 32 Time Slots]
    end

    classDef ds0 fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#FF6F00,stroke-width:1px
    classDef mux fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:2px
    classDef out fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px

    class C0,C1,C2,C3,D0,D1 ds0
    class MUX1,MUX2 mux
    class T1,E1O out
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Relevance**: The **E1 carrier** (2.048 Mbps, 32 time slots) is the standard PCM-TDM hierarchy used in India (BSNL/MTNL landlines). Each TS carries an 8-bit µ-law or A-law PCM sample of one 4 kHz voice channel.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Pulse Code Modulation. List its three main functional blocks at the transmitter.**

**Model Answer**:
PCM is the process of converting an analog signal into a digital bitstream by sampling, quantization, and binary encoding.
The three transmitter blocks are:
1. **Sampler** — converts continuous-time signal into discrete-time PAM samples.
2. **Quantizer** — assigns each sample to one of $L = 2^n$ discrete amplitude levels.
3. **Encoder** — maps each quantized level to a unique $n$-bit binary codeword.

*(Marks: Definition = 1, Three blocks = 2)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**State the Nyquist sampling theorem. What is aliasing?**

**Model Answer**:
**Nyquist Theorem**: A bandlimited signal of maximum frequency $f_m$ Hz can be uniquely reconstructed from its samples if the sampling frequency satisfies $f_s \geq 2f_m$. The minimum rate $f_s = 2f_m$ is called the **Nyquist rate**.

**Aliasing**: When $f_s < 2f_m$, the spectral replicas in the sampled signal overlap, causing high-frequency components to fold back and appear as false low-frequency components. This irreversible distortion is called **aliasing**, and is prevented by a pre-sampling **anti-aliasing LPF** that limits the input to $f_m \leq f_s/2$.

*(Marks: 2 for theorem + 1 for aliasing explanation)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### ⭐ CHOICE A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3, 14-Mark Set]**

**Q A.** (a) Draw and explain the block diagram of a PCM communication system. Identify the function of each block at the transmitter and receiver. (7 marks)

(b) An audio signal has a maximum frequency of 3.5 kHz. It is sampled at 50% above the Nyquist rate and quantized using an 8-bit quantizer. Calculate:
    (i) Sampling frequency
    (ii) Bit rate
    (iii) Minimum transmission bandwidth
    (iv) Output signal-to-quantization-noise ratio in dB
    (v) Mean-square quantization noise power if the peak-to-peak signal voltage is 8 V. (7 marks)

**Model Solution**:

**(a) Block diagram** — See **Section 4.1** above (Transmitter: Anti-aliasing LPF → Sampler → Quantizer → Encoder → Line coder; Receiver: Line decoder → Decoder → Reconstruction LPF; with regenerative repeaters in the channel). Each block is explained as in the notes above. *(2 marks diagram + 5 marks explanation)*

**(b) Numerical Solution**:

(i) Nyquist rate: $2f_m = 2 \times 3500 = 7000$ Hz.
50% above Nyquist:
$$f_s = 7000 \times 1.5 = 10{,}500 \text{ samples/sec}$$

(ii) $R_b = n f_s = 8 \times 10{,}500 = 84{,}000$ bps $= 84$ kbps.

(iii) $B_{min} = R_b/2 = 42$ kHz.

(iv) $\text{SQNR} = 6.02(8) + 1.76 = 48.16 + 1.76 = 49.92 \approx 49.92$ dB.

(v) $V_{pp} = 8$ V, $L = 2^8 = 256$.
$$\Delta = \frac{V_{pp}}{L} = \frac{8}{256} = 0.03125 \text{ V}$$
$$N_q = \frac{\Delta^2}{12} = \frac{(0.03125)^2}{12} = \frac{9.766 \times 10^{-4}}{12} \approx 8.14 \times 10^{-5} \text{ W} = 81.4 \text{ μW}$$

*(1 mark each sub-part, last sub-part may be split into 2 marks for the formula and 1 for evaluation.)*

---

### ⭐ CHOICE B (14 Marks) — Internal Alternative

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3, 14-Mark Set]**

**Q B.** (a) Explain uniform and non-uniform (companded) quantization. With the aid of a block diagram, describe the µ-law companding scheme used in PCM. (7 marks)

(b) A PCM system uses a uniform quantizer with 64 levels. The input signal $m(t) = 2 \sin(20\pi t)$ volts is sampled at Nyquist rate for a highest frequency of 10 kHz. Determine:
    (i) Number of bits per sample
    (ii) Code word length
    (iii) Bit rate
    (iv) Bandwidth (Nyquist minimum)
    (v) Output SQNR in dB (7 marks)

**Model Solution**:

**(a) Explanation** — See Section 2.1 (uniform quantization has equal step size $\Delta$; non-uniform companding gives finer resolution for low-amplitude signals and coarser for high-amplitude signals, matching speech statistics). The µ-law block diagram is essentially a **compressor** before the uniform quantizer at the TX, and an **expander** after the uniform dequantizer at the RX. *(3 marks theory + 2 marks companding curve equation + 2 marks block diagram)*

**(b) Numerical Solution**:

(i) $L = 64 \Rightarrow n = \log_2 64 = 6$ bits.

(ii) Code word length = **6 bits**.

(iii) For highest frequency $f_m = 10$ kHz, Nyquist rate $f_s = 20$ kHz. Note: the message $2\sin(20\pi t)$ has $f = 10$ Hz, but the question states the *highest frequency* for sampling purposes is 10 kHz.
$$R_b = n f_s = 6 \times 20{,}000 = 120{,}000 \text{ bps} = 120 \text{ kbps}$$

(iv) $B_{min} = R_b/2 = 60$ kHz.

(v) $\text{SQNR} = 6.02(6) + 1.76 = 36.12 + 1.76 = 37.88$ dB $\approx 37.9$ dB.

*(1 mark each, full work shown)*

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes — Where KTU Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing the input peak for the quantizer peak**: The signal $A_m$ is the quantizer's full-scale peak, *not* its RMS. The factor of ½ in the signal power formula is frequently forgotten.
> 2. **Mixing up $f_s$ with $f_m$**: The bit rate uses the *sampling* frequency $f_s$, not the message frequency. Many students write $R_b = n f_m$.
> 3. **Forgetting the units when $f_s$ is in kHz**: Multiplying 8 kHz × 8 bits = 64 (kbps) — students often miss the kilo.
> 4. **Using the wrong log base in decibels**: Always $10\log_{10}$ for power ratios, $20\log_{10}$ for amplitude ratios. SQNR is power → use 10.
> 5. **Skipping the quantizer explanation in block diagrams**: A bare box labelled "Quantizer" without describing *what* it does (maps samples to discrete levels, introduces error) loses 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Mixing µ-law and A-law constants**: µ-law uses $\mu=255$, A-law uses $A=87.6$. Writing the wrong one in companding questions is a guaranteed mark loss.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **PCM = Sampling + Quantization + Encoding** (SQE pipeline).
- **Nyquist Theorem**: $f_s \geq 2f_m$ to prevent aliasing.
- **Levels & Bits**: $L = 2^n$; adding 1 bit doubles L and quadruples the noise-reduction capability.
- **Step Size**: $\Delta = V_{pp}/L = V_{pp}/2^n$.
- **Quantization Noise Power**: $N_q = \Delta^2/12$ — depends *only* on $\Delta$, not the signal.
- **SQNR Master Formula**: $(S/N_q)_{dB} = 6.02n + 1.76$ dB (full-scale sine input).
- **Bit Rate**: $R_b = n f_s$ (telephony: 8 kHz × 8 bits = 64 kbps per voice channel).
- **Min. Bandwidth (Nyquist)**: $B_{min} = R_b/2$ (assuming ideal brick-wall filter).
- **Companding**: Compress at TX + Expand at RX. µ-law (USA/Japan, µ=255), A-law (Europe/India, A=87.6). Used to make SQNR nearly constant over the dynamic range.
- **TDM Carriers**: T1 (USA) = 1.544 Mbps / 24 channels; **E1 (India/Europe) = 2.048 Mbps / 32 time slots (30 voice + 1 sync + 1 signalling)**.
- **E1 vs T1**: Always remember India uses **E1 (CEPT)** — the most common viva trap.
- **Line codes**: NRZ-L, NRZ-I, Bipolar AMI, B8ZS, HDB3 — all for synchronization/DC-balance on PCM lines.
- **PCM Advantage**: Noise-immunity via regeneration; **Trade-off**: Larger bandwidth than original analog.
- **Real-world**: CD audio = 44.1 kHz × 16 bits × 2 channels = 1.411 Mbps; Telephony = 64 kbps (G.711).
- **DPCM / Delta Modulation** (next topics in Module 3): Use *prediction* to reduce bit rate — but PCM remains the reference standard.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: A-law and Mu-law Quantization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET864 - ELECTRONIC COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** A-law and Mu-law Quantization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Companding** is the combined process of **compressing** the signal amplitude at the transmitter and **expanding** it at the receiver, used in Pulse Code Modulation (PCM) to mitigate the adverse effects of channel noise and to improve the **Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR)** for non-uniform input signal distributions (typically voice).

The two internationally standardized companding laws are:

### **A-Law Companding**
Used primarily in **Europe, India, and most of the world** (ITU-T G.711 standard). The compression characteristic is defined piecewise, with parameter **A = 87.6**.

### **μ-Law Companding**
Used primarily in **North America and Japan** (ITU-T G.711 standard). The compression characteristic is continuous (smooth logarithmic curve), with parameter **μ = 255**.

The two constants **A = 87.6** and **μ = 255** are internationally fixed by the ITU-T (International Telecommunication Union — Telecommunication Standardization Sector) to ensure interoperability of digital telephone systems worldwide.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Whisper-to-Shout Problem"

Imagine a meeting hall where one person **whispers** and another **shouts** at the same time into a microphone.

- A **uniform quantizer** would divide the loudness range into equal steps. The whisper falls into just one or two tiny steps → it's lost in quantization noise.
- A **compander** is like a smart audio engineer who **automatically turns up the quiet whispers** (compression at TX) and then **turns the whole recording back down** at the receiver (expansion at RX) — so the quiet speaker now occupies many quantization steps and sounds clear.

Mathematically, the compressor **stretches** small amplitudes and **compresses** large amplitudes, which is exactly what the human ear needs because we hear logarithmically (a 10× increase in sound intensity only feels ~2× louder).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** Companding is essentially a **non-uniform quantization** achieved in two stages: non-uniform compression + uniform quantization. Mathematically, applying a non-linear compressor before a uniform quantizer is **equivalent** to passing the signal through a non-uniform quantizer directly.

---

## 1.3 Physical & Standardized Constants

| Constant | Symbol | Standard Value | Used In |
|----------|--------|----------------|---------|
| A-law parameter | **A** | **87.6** | Europe, India, KTU syllabus focus |
| μ-law parameter | **μ** | **255** | USA, Japan |
| Quantization levels (telephony) | **L** | **256** (8-bit) | Both laws |
| Dynamic range | — | ~24 dB improvement | A-law at A=87.6 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Companding is Needed — The Theoretical "Why"

In a uniform PCM system, the **SQNR is independent of signal amplitude**. This is a critical problem for voice signals because:

- Voice signals have a **high peak-to-average ratio**.
- Weak signals (pauses, soft consonants) suffer the **same absolute quantization noise** as loud signals.
- The result: quiet portions become unintelligible — a disaster for telephony.

**Companding solves this by allocating more quantization levels (smaller step size) to weak signals and fewer levels (larger step size) to strong signals.** This makes the quantization noise amplitude-dependent — small for weak signals, large for loud signals — exactly mirroring the signal's own behavior. The **relative noise (SQNR)** becomes approximately **constant** across the entire dynamic range.

---

## 2.2 The μ-Law Compression Equation

The **μ-law compressor characteristic** is defined as:

$$
F_\mu(x) = \text{sgn}(x) \cdot \frac{\ln\!\left(1 + \mu \vert x \vert\right)}{\ln(1 + \mu)}, \quad -1 \le x \le 1
$$

where:
- $x$ is the **normalized input** amplitude (peak value = 1).
- $\text{sgn}(x)$ preserves the sign (the law is **odd-symmetric** about the origin).
- $\mu = 255$ is the fixed parameter.

### 2.2.1 Properties of μ-Law
- **Continuous and smooth** (single equation, no piecewise breaks).
- For $x \to 0$: $F_\mu(x) \approx \mu x / \ln(1+\mu)$ → **linear** in the weak-signal region.
- For $x \to 1$: $F_\mu(x) = 1$ → output never exceeds unity.
- Output range is **[-1, +1]**, so the uniform quantizer that follows can have a step size $\Delta = 2/2^n$.

---

## 2.3 The A-Law Compression Equation

The **A-law compressor characteristic** is **piecewise** (linear near zero, logarithmic elsewhere):

$$
F_A(x) =
\begin{cases}
\text{sgn}(x) \cdot \dfrac{A \vert x \vert}{1 + \ln A}, & 0 \le \vert x \vert \le \dfrac{1}{A} \\[10pt]
\text{sgn}(x) \cdot \dfrac{1 + \ln\!\left(A \vert x \vert\right)}{1 + \ln A}, & \dfrac{1}{A} \le \vert x \vert \le 1
\end{cases}
$$

where:
- **A = 87.6** is the standard parameter.
- $1/A \approx 0.0114$ is the breakpoint (transition from linear to logarithmic).

### 2.3.1 Properties of A-Law
- **Piecewise definition** — linear segment near zero (avoiding $\ln(0) = -\infty$ issues).
- For $\vert x \vert \le 1/A$: behaves as a **linear amplifier** (slope $= A/(1+\ln A) \approx 16$).
- For $\vert x \vert \ge 1/A$: logarithmic compression.
- A-law is **slightly less aggressive** than μ-law for very small signals, but easier to implement in hardware (the linear segment avoids infinite gain at origin).

---

## 2.4 The Expander (Receiver Side)

The expander is the **exact inverse** of the compressor:

$$
x = F^{-1}(y)
$$

For μ-law:

$$
x = \text{sgn}(y) \cdot \frac{(1+\mu)^{\vert y \vert} - 1}{\mu}
$$

For A-law (inverse of the piecewise function):

$$
x = \text{sgn}(y) \cdot
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{\vert y \vert \, (1+\ln A)}{A}, & 0 \le \vert y \vert \le \dfrac{1}{1+\ln A} \\[8pt]
\dfrac{\exp\!\left(\vert y \vert (1+\ln A) - 1\right)}{A}, & \dfrac{1}{1+\ln A} \le \vert y \vert \le 1
\end{cases}
$$

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this table — these formulas appear in nearly every KTU Part B question on Module 3.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | μ-law compression | $F(x) = \text{sgn}(x) \cdot \dfrac{\ln(1+\mu \vert x \vert)}{\ln(1+\mu)}$ | $\mu = 255$, $x \in [-1,1]$ |
| 2 | A-law compression (low) | $F(x) = \text{sgn}(x) \cdot \dfrac{A \vert x \vert}{1+\ln A}$ | $\vert x \vert \le 1/A$ |
| 3 | A-law compression (high) | $F(x) = \text{sgn}(x) \cdot \dfrac{1+\ln(A \vert x \vert)}{1+\ln A}$ | $1/A \le \vert x \vert \le 1$ |
| 4 | SQNR (uniform quantizer, no companding) | $\left(\text{SQNR}\right)_u = 2^{2n}$ (power ratio) | $\approx 6.02n$ dB |
| 5 | SQNR (μ-law) | $\left(\text{SQNR}\right)_\mu = \dfrac{3 \cdot 2^{2n}}{\left[\ln(1+\mu)\right]^2}$ | Valid for uniformly distributed input |
| 6 | SQNR (A-law) | $\left(\text{SQNR}\right)_A = \dfrac{3 \cdot 2^{2n}}{\left[1+\ln A\right]^2}$ | Valid for uniformly distributed input |
| 7 | SQNR improvement (μ-law over uniform) | $\dfrac{3}{\left[\ln(1+\mu)\right]^2} = \dfrac{3}{[\ln 256]^2}$ | $\approx 9.36$ (≈ 9.7 dB) |
| 8 | Quantization step (uniform 8-bit) | $\Delta = 2/2^8 = 1/128$ | Two-sided range |
| 9 | Bit rate (PCM voice) | $R_b = n \cdot f_s$ | $f_s = 8$ kHz, $n = 8$ → 64 kbps |
| 10 | Dynamic range gain of μ-law | $20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\ln(1+\mu)}{\mu}\right) \approx -33.5$ dB | At small $x$ (linear approximation) |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Digital Landline Telephony (PSTN)**: Every voice call between two landlines uses **8-bit A-law (in India) or μ-law (in USA) PCM at 8 kHz** → 64 kbps — the fundamental building block of the global telephone network.
- **VoIP Gateways**: Translation between A-law and μ-law occurs at international VoIP gateways, since the two laws are not directly compatible.
- **Audio Coding Predecessor**: Companding principles directly influenced modern audio codecs (MP3, AAC) which use psycho-acoustic models — the human ear's logarithmic response is the same underlying principle.
- **Satellite & Military Comms**: Adaptive quantizers using A-law/μ-law principles are used in bandwidth-limited tactical radios.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the μ-Law Compression Equation

### Starting from Physical Requirements

We need a function $F(x)$ that satisfies **three engineering constraints**:

1. **Odd symmetry**: $F(-x) = -F(x)$ (preserve sign).
2. **Bounded output**: $F(\pm 1) = \pm 1$ (fits within the uniform quantizer's range).
3. **Infinite slope at origin**: $F'(0) = \infty$ (small signals must be amplified maximally for fine quantization).

The simplest curve satisfying these is the **logarithm**.

### Step 1: Assume a Logarithmic Form

For positive $x$, assume:

$$
F(x) = a + b \ln(c + dx), \quad 0 \le x \le 1
$$

### Step 2: Apply Boundary Conditions

**Condition A** — $F(0) = 0$ (no DC offset):

$$
0 = a + b \ln(c) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; a = -b \ln c
$$

**Condition B** — $F(1) = 1$ (normalization):

$$
1 = -b\ln c + b \ln(c + d) = b \ln\!\left(\dfrac{c+d}{c}\right)
$$

**Condition C** — Infinite slope at origin → set $c = 1$ (so $\ln(c) = 0$ and the slope $\frac{bd}{c+dx}$ becomes $\frac{bd}{1}$, finite; to get $\infty$ at $x=0$ we need the slope to diverge — this is naturally produced by choosing $c=0$ and accepting $F(0)=0$ as a limiting case, but the standard form uses $c=1$ for finite peak gain).

Using the **μ-law convention** $c = 1$, $d = \mu$:

$$
1 = b \ln(1 + \mu) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; b = \dfrac{1}{\ln(1+\mu)}
$$

### Step 3: Final Form

$$
F_\mu(x) = \dfrac{\ln(1 + \mu x)}{\ln(1+\mu)}, \quad 0 \le x \le 1
$$

Extending to negative $x$ via odd symmetry and adding $\text{sgn}(x)$:

$$
\boxed{F_\mu(x) = \text{sgn}(x) \cdot \dfrac{\ln(1 + \mu \vert x \vert)}{\ln(1+\mu)}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the A-Law Compression Equation

### Step 1: Recognize the μ-Law Limitation

In μ-law, the slope at the origin is finite:

$$
F_\mu'(0) = \dfrac{\mu}{\ln(1+\mu)} = \dfrac{255}{\ln 256} \approx 33.6
$$

For A-law, we **deliberately make the slope a defined finite constant** in the linear region by introducing a **breakpoint at $1/A$**.

### Step 2: Define the Linear Segment ($\vert x \vert \le 1/A$)

$$
F_A(x) = \text{sgn}(x) \cdot \dfrac{A \vert x \vert}{1 + \ln A}
$$

This segment has a **constant slope** $= \dfrac{A}{1+\ln A} = \dfrac{87.6}{1 + \ln 87.6} \approx \dfrac{87.6}{5.486} \approx 15.97 \approx 16$

### Step 3: Define the Logarithmic Segment ($1/A \le \vert x \vert \le 1$)

The logarithmic segment must **match value and slope** of the linear segment at $x = 1/A$ for continuity.

At $x = 1/A$, the linear segment gives:

$$
F_A(1/A) = \dfrac{1}{1+\ln A}
$$

So the log segment is constrained to pass through $(1/A,\; 1/(1+\ln A))$ and end at $(1, 1)$:

$$
F_A(x) = \text{sgn}(x) \cdot \dfrac{1 + \ln(A \vert x \vert)}{1 + \ln A}, \quad 1/A \le \vert x \vert \le 1
$$

### Step 4: Verify Continuity at $x = 1/A$

$$
F_A(1/A) = \dfrac{1 + \ln(A \cdot 1/A)}{1+\ln A} = \dfrac{1 + \ln 1}{1+\ln A} = \dfrac{1}{1+\ln A} \;\;\checkmark
$$

### Step 5: Verify Endpoints

- $F_A(1) = \dfrac{1+\ln A}{1+\ln A} = 1$ ✓
- $F_A(1/A) = \dfrac{1}{1+\ln A}$ ✓ (matches linear segment)
- $\text{sgn}(x)$ adds odd symmetry ✓

---

## 3.3 Numerical Example 1 — Computing Compressor Output (μ-Law)

> **Problem (KTU typical):** A PCM system uses **μ-law companding** with $\mu = 255$. The normalized input is $x = 0.1$. Find the compressor output $F_\mu(x)$.

### Solution

Given: $\mu = 255$, $x = +0.1$ (positive, so $\text{sgn}(x) = +1$).

**Step 1:** Compute $\mu x$:

$$
\mu x = 255 \times 0.1 = 25.5
$$

**Step 2:** Compute $1 + \mu x$:

$$
1 + 25.5 = 26.5
$$

**Step 3:** Compute $\ln(1 + \mu x)$:

$$
\ln(26.5) = 3.2771
$$

**Step 4:** Compute $\ln(1 + \mu) = \ln(256)$:

$$
\ln(256) = 5.5452
$$

**Step 5:** Compute the output:

$$
F_\mu(0.1) = \dfrac{3.2771}{5.5452} = 0.5910
$$

**Interpretation:** A small input of 0.1 is **amplified** to 0.591 — a nearly 6× gain — demonstrating the compander's ability to "stretch" weak signals.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Example 2 — SQNR Comparison (A-Law vs Uniform)

> **Problem:** A PCM system uses $n = 8$ bits. Compare the SQNR of (a) uniform quantization, (b) A-law companding ($A = 87.6$), assuming a uniformly distributed input signal.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Compute uniform SQNR:

$$
(\text{SQNR})_u = 2^{2n} = 2^{16} = 65{,}536
$$

In dB: $(\text{SQNR})_u = 10 \log_{10}(65536) = 48.16$ dB

**Step 2:** Compute A-law SQNR numerator:

$$
3 \cdot 2^{2n} = 3 \times 65536 = 196{,}608
$$

**Step 3:** Compute A-law SQNR denominator:

$$
[1 + \ln A]^2 = [1 + \ln 87.6]^2 = [1 + 4.4738]^2 = [5.4738]^2 = 29.9625
$$

**Step 4:** Compute A-law SQNR:

$$
(\text{SQNR})_A = \dfrac{196{,}608}{29.9625} = 6561.6
$$

In dB: $(\text{SQNR})_A = 10 \log_{10}(6561.6) = 38.17$ dB

**Step 5:** Compute improvement ratio:

$$
\dfrac{(\text{SQNR})_A}{(\text{SQNR})_u} = \dfrac{6561.6}{65536} = 0.1001 \approx \dfrac{1}{10}
$$

**Step 6:** Critical Insight — the improvement is actually an **averaging** effect:

For **uniformly distributed inputs**, A-law slightly *reduces* average SQNR, but for **voice signals (Laplacian/exponentially distributed)**, A-law **dramatically improves** SQNR (often by 20+ dB for weak voice components).

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** When the problem says "uniformly distributed input," the compander's formula is the one to use. For "voice signal," the input is assumed Laplacian — discuss the practical SQNR improvement qualitatively.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Example 3 — A-Law Compression with Breakpoint

> **Problem:** A PCM system uses **A-law companding** with $A = 87.6$. Compute the compressor output for inputs (i) $x = 0.005$ and (ii) $x = 0.5$.

### Solution for (i): $x = 0.005$

Check region: $1/A = 1/87.6 = 0.01142$. Since $0.005 < 0.01142$, we are in the **linear region**.

$$
F_A(0.005) = \dfrac{87.6 \times 0.005}{1 + \ln 87.6} = \dfrac{0.438}{5.4738} = 0.0800
$$

### Solution for (ii): $x = 0.5$

Check region: $0.5 > 0.01142$, so we are in the **logarithmic region**.

$$
F_A(0.5) = \dfrac{1 + \ln(87.6 \times 0.5)}{1 + \ln 87.6} = \dfrac{1 + \ln(43.8)}{5.4738}
$$

Compute: $\ln(43.8) = 3.7796$

$$
F_A(0.5) = \dfrac{1 + 3.7796}{5.4738} = \dfrac{4.7796}{5.4738} = 0.8731
$$

**Interpretation:** A 100× larger input (0.5 vs 0.005) maps to only ~11× larger output (0.873 vs 0.080) — confirming the **compression** of dynamic range.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**Define companding. Why is it used in PCM systems?**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):
**Companding** is the process of **compressing** the signal amplitude at the transmitter using a non-linear function (μ-law or A-law) and **expanding** it back at the receiver using the inverse function.

It is used in PCM to:
1. **Overcome the problem of uniform quantization** for non-uniform input signals (like voice).
2. **Improve the Signal-to-Quantization Noise Ratio (SQNR)** for weak signals, which would otherwise be lost in quantization noise.
3. **Increase the effective dynamic range** of the PCM system without increasing the number of bits.
4. Match the **logarithmic response of the human ear**, producing perceptually better voice quality.

*(Full 3 marks for definition + any 2 valid reasons with a clear technical justification.)*

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**What is the difference between A-law and μ-law companding?**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

| Feature | A-Law | μ-Law |
|---------|-------|-------|
| Parameter | A = 87.6 | μ = 255 |
| Equation | **Piecewise** (linear near zero, log elsewhere) | **Single continuous** logarithmic equation |
| Used in | Europe, India, most countries | USA, Japan |
| Behavior near origin | Finite slope $= A/(1+\ln A) \approx 16$ | Infinite slope ($\mu/\ln(1+\mu) \approx 33.6$) |
| Implementation | Easier (linear segment avoids $\ln 0$ issues) | More aggressive compression of weak signals |
| Standard | ITU-T G.711 | ITU-T G.711 |

*(Award 1 mark for the parameter values, 1 mark for the geometric difference, 1 mark for geographic application.)*

---

## 📗 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Pattern)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the mathematical expression for **μ-law companding**. State the standard value of the parameter used. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** An 8-bit PCM system uses μ-law companding with $\mu = 255$ for a voice signal. Calculate the compressor output for normalized input amplitudes $x = 0.05$ and $x = 0.5$. Comment on the results. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Engineering Requirements (1 mark)**

The compressor function $F(x)$ must satisfy:
- $F(-x) = -F(x)$ (odd symmetry)
- $F(\pm 1) = \pm 1$ (bounded output)
- High slope near origin (fine quantization of weak signals)

**Step 2: Assumed Form (1 mark)**

Assume: $F(x) = a + b \ln(1 + \mu x)$ for $0 \le x \le 1$.

**Step 3: Apply $F(0) = 0$ (1 mark)**

$$0 = a + b \ln 1 = a \;\Rightarrow\; a = 0$$

**Step 4: Apply $F(1) = 1$ (1 mark)**

$$1 = b \ln(1 + \mu) \;\Rightarrow\; b = \dfrac{1}{\ln(1 + \mu)}$$

**Step 5: Final Equation (1 mark)**

$$
F_\mu(x) = \dfrac{\ln(1 + \mu x)}{\ln(1 + \mu)}, \quad 0 \le x \le 1
$$

**Step 6: Extend to both polarities (1 mark)**

$$
\boxed{F_\mu(x) = \text{sgn}(x) \cdot \dfrac{\ln(1 + \mu \vert x \vert)}{\ln(1 + \mu)}, \quad -1 \le x \le 1}
$$

**Step 7: State the parameter (1 mark)**

Standard value: $\mu = 255$ (ITU-T G.711).

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Recall formula (1 mark)**

$$F_\mu(x) = \text{sgn}(x) \cdot \dfrac{\ln(1 + \mu \vert x \vert)}{\ln(1 + \mu)}, \quad \mu = 255$$

Compute $\ln(1+\mu) = \ln(256) = 5.5452$.

**Step 2: Compute for $x = 0.05$ (2 marks)**

$$
F_\mu(0.05) = \dfrac{\ln(1 + 255 \times 0.05)}{5.5452} = \dfrac{\ln(1 + 12.75)}{5.5452} = \dfrac{\ln(13.75)}{5.5452}
$$

$$
F_\mu(0.05) = \dfrac{2.6210}{5.5452} = 0.4726
$$

**Step 3: Compute for $x = 0.5$ (2 marks)**

$$
F_\mu(0.5) = \dfrac{\ln(1 + 255 \times 0.5)}{5.5452} = \dfrac{\ln(128.5)}{5.5452} = \dfrac{4.8565}{5.5452} = 0.8757
$$

**Step 4: Compare and Comment (2 marks)**

- Input ratio: $0.5/0.05 = 10$ (i.e., 20 dB difference in input)
- Output ratio: $0.8757/0.4726 = 1.853$ (i.e., 5.36 dB difference in output)
- **Comment:** The 10× (20 dB) dynamic range of the input is compressed to less than 2× (5.36 dB) in the output. **Smaller signals are amplified (0.05 → 0.47) more than larger signals (0.5 → 0.88)**, confirming the logarithmic compression characteristic that allocates more quantization levels to weak signals.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the **A-law compression equation** with parameter $A = 87.6$. Sketch the characteristic and label the linear and logarithmic regions. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare the **SQNR of an 8-bit uniform PCM** system with an **8-bit A-law PCM** system. Assume a uniformly distributed input. Use $A = 87.6$. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Motivation for A-Law (1 mark)**

μ-law has infinite slope at origin; A-law replaces this with a **linear segment** for $\vert x \vert \le 1/A$ to avoid hardware implementation issues.

**Step 2: Linear region definition (2 marks)**

$$
F_A(x) = \text{sgn}(x) \cdot \dfrac{A \vert x \vert}{1 + \ln A}, \quad \vert x \vert \le \dfrac{1}{A}
$$

**Step 3: Logarithmic region definition (2 marks)**

$$
F_A(x) = \text{sgn}(x) \cdot \dfrac{1 + \ln(A \vert x \vert)}{1 + \ln A}, \quad \dfrac{1}{A} \le \vert x \vert \le 1
$$

**Step 4: Verification (1 mark)**

At $x = 1/A$: linear gives $1/(1+\ln A)$, log gives $(1+\ln 1)/(1+\ln A) = 1/(1+\ln A)$ ✓
At $x = 1$: $F_A(1) = 1$ ✓

**Step 5: Characteristic sketch (1 mark)**

A piecewise curve: steep straight line from origin to $(1/A, 1/(1+\ln A))$, then a smooth concave (saturating) curve from there to $(1, 1)$, mirrored to negative side for odd symmetry.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Recall uniform SQNR (1 mark)**

$$
(\text{SQNR})_u = 2^{2n} = 2^{16} = 65{,}536 \quad \text{or} \quad 48.16 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 2: A-law SQNR formula (1 mark)**

$$
(\text{SQNR})_A = \dfrac{3 \cdot 2^{2n}}{[1 + \ln A]^2}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute $n = 8$ (1 mark)**

$$
(\text{SQNR})_A = \dfrac{3 \times 65{,}536}{[1 + \ln 87.6]^2} = \dfrac{196{,}608}{[1 + 4.4738]^2}
$$

**Step 4: Compute denominator (1 mark)**

$$
[1 + \ln 87.6]^2 = [5.4738]^2 = 29.963
$$

**Step 5: Final A-law SQNR (1 mark)**

$$
(\text{SQNR})_A = \dfrac{196{,}608}{29.963} = 6561.5 \quad \text{or} \quad 38.17 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 6: Comparison (1 mark)**

- Uniform: 48.16 dB
- A-law: 38.17 dB
- For **uniformly distributed inputs**, A-law shows a *lower* average SQNR, but this is misleading. For **voice signals (Laplacian distribution)**, A-law dramatically improves SQNR — typically by 20+ dB for low-amplitude voice components.
- **Conclusion:** The compander's value lies in **matching the signal's statistical distribution** rather than producing uniform absolute noise.

**Step 7: Penalty discussion (1 mark)**

The 10 dB "loss" for uniform input is the price paid for gaining 20+ dB on the weak voice components that matter most for intelligibility.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\text{sgn}(x)$ factor** — A-law/μ-law are odd-symmetric; writing only the positive-$x$ form loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Using the wrong region of A-law** — Check whether $\vert x \vert$ is less than or greater than $1/A = 0.0114$. Mixing up the regions gives wrong answers.
> 3. **Using $\log_{10}$ instead of $\ln$** — Both formulas in the KTU syllabus use **natural logarithm ($\ln$)**. $\log_{10}$ gives numerically wrong answers.
> 4. **Confusing A and $\mu$ parameters** — A = 87.6 (Europe/India), $\mu = 255$ (USA). Each has its own formula; do not mix.
> 5. **Forgetting to convert to dB** — KTU Part B questions often expect both the ratio and the dB value. Provide both.
> 6. **Skipping the "comment" or "interpretation"** — Numerical answers without a brief technical comment lose 1 mark.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Companding** = Compress (TX) + Expand (RX) — implemented as non-linear function before a **uniform** quantizer.
- ✅ **A-law** uses parameter $A = 87.6$, is **piecewise** (linear for $\vert x \vert \le 1/A$, log for $\vert x \vert \ge 1/A$).
- ✅ **μ-law** uses parameter $\mu = 255$, is **continuous** logarithmic, more aggressive on weak signals.
- ✅ **Both laws are odd-symmetric** (multiply by $\text{sgn}(x)$).
- ✅ The compressor **stretches weak signals and compresses strong signals** — improving SQNR for low-amplitude voice.
- ✅ Standard telecom PCM: **8 bits × 8 kHz = 64 kbps** bit rate.
- ✅ A-law is the **default in India and Europe** (KTU standard); μ-law in **USA and Japan**.
- ✅ **Formulas use $\ln$ (natural log), NOT $\log_{10}$**.
- ✅ Compander $F(x) = \text{sgn}(x) \cdot \dfrac{\ln(1 + \mu \vert x \vert)}{\ln(1 + \mu)}$ is the most-tested equation in KTU.
- ✅ Compander effectively converts uniform quantization into **non-uniform quantization** with smaller steps for weak signals.
- ✅ The **inverse (expander)** restores the original signal at the receiver.
- ✅ Voice signals have **Laplacian distribution** — compander's design matches this for maximum intelligibility gain.
- ✅ A-law's linear region slope = $A/(1+\ln A) \approx 16$; μ-law's origin slope = $\mu/\ln(1+\mu) \approx 33.6$.
- ✅ Compander equations are defined on **normalized input** $x \in [-1, 1]$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Digital Bandpass Communication System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET864 - ELECTRONIC COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Digital Bandpass Communication System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Digital Bandpass Communication System** is a communication framework in which a digital baseband message signal (binary or M-ary) is impressed upon a sinusoidal carrier wave through a controlled variation of one or more of its parameters — **Amplitude**, **Frequency**, or **Phase** — such that the resulting modulated signal occupies a passband of frequencies centered around the carrier frequency $f_c$, suitable for transmission over a bandlimited channel.

The general mathematical form of a bandpass digitally modulated signal is expressed as:

$$s(t) = A(t) \cos \left[ 2\pi f_c t + \phi(t) \right]$$

where:
- $A(t)$ → Time-varying amplitude carrying digital information (ASK).
- $f_c$ → Carrier frequency (fixed sinusoid center).
- $\phi(t)$ → Time-varying phase carrying digital information (PSK).
- When frequency varies, $\phi(t) = 2\pi \int_0^t f(\tau) d\tau$ (FSK).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The term "Digital Bandpass Modulation" explicitly refers to techniques that translate a low-frequency digital bitstream into a high-frequency passband signal that can propagate via antennas, optical fibers, or wireless channels. The baseband signal alone cannot propagate efficiently.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Mapping (KTU 2024 Scheme):** This topic directly maps to **CO3** of PEEET864 — "Apply the concepts of digital modulation schemes in the design of modern communication transceivers." It falls under the *Digital Communication* cluster of Module 4 with a recommended contact hour allocation of **9 hours**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are sending a secret message across a noisy football stadium to a friend sitting in the far stand. Instead of shouting the message directly (which would be the *baseband* method), you decide to use a **torchlight (carrier)**. You can encode the message in three intuitive ways:

1. **Amplitude Keying (ASK):** You turn the torch **ON** for binary `1` and **OFF** for binary `0`. Your friend observes the brightness. → *Amplitude Shift Keying.*
2. **Frequency Keying (FSK):** You flash the torch at a **fast rate** for `1` and a **slow rate** for `0`. → *Frequency Shift Keying.*
3. **Phase Keying (PSK):** You hold the torch straight up for `0` and tilt it to the right by 90° for `1`. → *Phase Shift Keying.*

In all three cases, the **torchlight (carrier)** is at a high frequency (high enough to be visible across the stadium — the *bandpass* requirement), while your **encoded message** is at a low information rate.

> [!TIP]
> The carrier frequency $f_c$ is the "vehicle" that delivers your low-frequency digital message across a bandpass channel. Without the carrier, electromagnetic propagation would require physically impossible antenna dimensions (Antenna length $\lambda/4 \approx 75$ meters for 1 MHz signal, but only $\lambda/4 \approx 7.5$ cm for 1 GHz).

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Speed of Light** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s — used in wavelength $\lambda = c/f$ calculations.
- **Carrier-to-Noise Ratio (CNR)** — measured in dB, fundamental to BER.
- **Energy per bit** $E_b$ — measured in Joules.
- **Bit Rate** $R_b$ — measured in **bits per second (bps)**.
- **Symbol Rate (Baud)** $R_s$ — symbols/second.
- **Noise Power Spectral Density** $N_0/2$ — Watts/Hz (two-sided).
- **Standard Bandwidth Efficiency** $\eta = R_b / B$ — measured in **bits/sec/Hz**.

### 1.4 Classification Hierarchy (KTU Board-Relevant)

Digital bandpass modulation is classified into three primary families:

| Family | Parameter Modulated | KTU Sub-Topics Covered |
|---|---|---|
| **ASK** (Amplitude Shift Keying) | Amplitude $A(t)$ | OOK, M-ary ASK |
| **FSK** (Frequency Shift Keying) | Frequency $f(t)$ | BFSK, M-ary FSK, MSK, GMSK |
| **PSK** (Phase Shift Keying) | Phase $\phi(t)$ | BPSK, QPSK, OQPSK, $\pi/4$-DQPSK, M-ary PSK |
| **QAM** (Quadrature Amplitude Mod.) | Both $A(t)$ and $\phi(t)$ | 4-QAM, 16-QAM, 64-QAM, 256-QAM |

Detection is further classified as **Coherent** (requires carrier phase synchronization via PLL) and **Non-Coherent** (envelope or frequency detection, no phase reference needed).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Block Architecture

Every digital bandpass communication system implements the following canonical chain at the transmitter:

1. **Source Encoding** — converts analog source to digital bits (PCM, ADPCM, etc.) — *out of KTU Module 4 scope but is the input.*
2. **Channel Encoding** — adds redundancy for error control (Convolutional, Block codes).
3. **Pulse Shaping** — limits bandwidth using **Raised Cosine** or **Root Raised Cosine (RRC)** filters with roll-off factor $\alpha$.
4. **Modulator** — maps symbols to analog waveforms from a constellation.
5. **Up-conversion & PA** — boosts signal to channel frequency and power level.
6. **Channel** — AWGN with two-sided PSD $N_0/2$ W/Hz.
7. **Receiver** — synchronization, matched filtering, equalization, demodulation, detection.
8. **Decision Device** — Maximum Likelihood (ML) threshold comparison to recover bits.

### 2.2 Detailed Scheme-by-Scheme Analysis

#### A. Amplitude Shift Keying (ASK) / On-Off Keying (OOK)

**The "Why":** Simplest modulation. The carrier is transmitted for bit `1` and suppressed for bit `0`. Energy is concentrated in the ON state.

**The "How":** Mathematically modeled as:

$$s_i(t) = \begin{cases} A \cos(2\pi f_c t), & \text{bit } 1 \\ 0, & \text{bit } 0 \end{cases} \quad 0 \le t \le T_b$$

- **Bit Energy** $E_b = A^2 T_b / 2$ for bit `1` and $0$ for bit `0`. Average energy = $E_b/2$.
- **Bandwidth** $B_{ASK} = 2 R_b$ (assuming rectangular NRZ pulses).
- **Drawback:** Highly susceptible to amplitude noise and fading. **Not used in modern cellular/space systems** but is fundamental in **optical communication** (laser on/off) and **low-cost RF ID tags**.

#### B. Binary Phase Shift Keying (BPSK)

**The "Why":** BPSK is the **most power-efficient binary scheme** (lowest BER for a given $E_b/N_0$) and is the KTU benchmark scheme.

**The "How":**

$$s_i(t) = \begin{cases} \sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{T_b}} \cos(2\pi f_c t), & \text{bit } 0 \\ -\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{T_b}} \cos(2\pi f_c t), & \text{bit } 1 \end{cases}$$

- The carrier is multiplied by $+1$ or $-1$ — a phase flip of $\pi$ radians.
- **Constant envelope** — robust to amplitude nonlinearities.
- **Bandwidth** $B_{BPSK} = 2 R_b$ (same as ASK, but at lower power).
- **BER (Coherent Detection):** $P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$
- **BER (Differentially encoded, DEBPSK):** $P_b = \frac{1}{2} e^{-E_b/N_0}$

#### C. Quadrature Phase Shift Keying (QPSK)

**The "Why":** Doubles the data rate of BPSK in the **same bandwidth**, achieving a bandwidth efficiency of **2 bits/sec/Hz**.

**The "How":** Two BPSK signals transmitted in parallel on orthogonal carriers $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ (I-channel) and $\sin(2\pi f_c t)$ (Q-channel). Input bits are grouped in pairs (dibits) and mapped to one of four phases: $\{0, \pi/2, \pi, 3\pi/2\}$ or equivalently $\{\pi/4, 3\pi/4, 5\pi/4, 7\pi/4\}$.

The transmitted signal is:

$$s_i(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{T_s}} \cos\!\left[ 2\pi f_c t + (2i-1)\frac{\pi}{4} \right], \quad i = 1, 2, 3, 4$$

where $E_s = 2 E_b$ (since 2 bits per symbol) and $T_s = 2 T_b$ (symbol period twice the bit period).

- **BER (Coherent):** $P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$ — *identical to BPSK!* (The beauty of QPSK.)
- **Bandwidth** $B_{QPSK} = R_b$ (half of BPSK's bandwidth for the same $R_b$).
- **Variants:** OQPSK (staggered I/Q to avoid 180° phase transitions), $\pi/4$-DQPSK (used in older cellular systems).

#### D. M-ary PSK and QAM (Higher-Order Modulation)

**The "Why":** To pack more bits per symbol and improve spectral efficiency at the cost of higher power requirement and stricter linearity.

**M-ary PSK:** $M$ phases uniformly distributed on a unit circle. Phase of symbol $i$ is $2\pi(i-1)/M$.

**M-ary QAM:** Both amplitude and phase vary. The constellation forms a square grid (rectangular QAM) or cross arrangement. The general form is:

$$s_i(t) = A_i \cos(2\pi f_c t) + B_i \sin(2\pi f_c t)$$

where $(A_i, B_i)$ are the in-phase and quadrature amplitudes selected from a finite alphabet.

For square M-QAM ($M = 2^k$, $k$ even), the exact BER is:

$$P_b \approx \frac{4}{\log_2 M}\!\left(1 - \frac{1}{\sqrt{M}}\right)\! Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{3 \log_2 M}{M-1} \cdot \frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Trade-off:** Going from BPSK (1 bit/symbol) to 64-QAM (6 bits/symbol) gives **6× spectral efficiency**, but requires approximately **16 dB more SNR** to maintain the same BER. This is the central trade-off studied in KTU Module 4.

#### E. Frequency Shift Keying (FSK) and Continuous Phase Variants (MSK, GMSK)

**Binary FSK (BFSK):** Two frequencies $f_1$ and $f_2$ represent bits `0` and `1`, with a frequency separation $\Delta f = \vert f_1 - f_2 \vert$. Minimum orthogonal separation $\Delta f = 1/(2T_b)$ (Sunde's FSK) or $1/T_b$ (coherent orthogonal).

- **BER (Coherent Orthogonal BFSK):** $P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_b}{N_0}}\right)$
- **BER (Non-Coherent Orthogonal BFSK):** $P_b = \frac{1}{2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_b}{2N_0}\right)$

**Minimum Shift Keying (MSK):** A continuous-phase FSK with modulation index $h = 0.5$, the *minimum* frequency spacing that maintains orthogonality. MSK is essentially **OQPSK with sinusoidal pulse shaping** and has a main-lobe bandwidth of $1.5 R_b$.

**Gaussian MSK (GMSK):** MSK with a Gaussian pre-filter before the FM modulator. The Gaussian filter smooths phase transitions, drastically reducing out-of-band emissions. Used as the modulation scheme in the **GSM cellular standard (2G)**.

**BER (MSK):** Identical to BPSK/QPSK: $P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2 E_b / N_0}\right)$ for coherent detection.

### 2.3 Coherent vs. Non-Coherent Detection

| Parameter | Coherent Detection | Non-Coherent Detection |
|---|---|---|
| **Carrier phase reference** | Required (PLL, Costas loop) | Not required |
| **Implementation complexity** | High | Low |
| **Performance (BER)** | Optimal — 3 dB gain over non-coherent | ~1–3 dB worse |
| **Examples** | BPSK, QPSK, M-QAM | DPSK, NCFSK, OOK envelope detection |
| **Use case** | High-speed, power-limited (satellite) | Low-cost, bursty (RFID, remote control) |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL:** All formulas below are NEP 2020 / KTU 2024 Scheme Board-exam essential. Memorize with the $\vert \cdot \vert$ and $\sqrt{\cdot}$ forms. The table uses `\vert` instead of `|` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Scheme / Quantity | Formula | Units / Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Average Bit Energy | $E_b = \frac{A^2 T_b}{2}$ (for constant envelope) | Joules |
| 2 | Symbol Energy M-ary | $E_s = E_b \log_2 M$ | Joules |
| 3 | Symbol Period | $T_s = T_b \log_2 M$ | Seconds |
| 4 | Bandwidth (Nyquist / BPSK / QPSK) | $B = \frac{R_b}{2} (1 + \alpha)$ (raised cosine) | Hz |
| 5 | Spectral Efficiency | $\eta = \frac{R_b}{B} = \frac{\log_2 M}{1+\alpha}$ | bps/Hz |
| 6 | BPSK BER | $P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2 E_b / N_0}\right)$ | Dimensionless |
| 7 | QPSK BER | $P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2 E_b / N_0}\right)$ | Same as BPSK |
| 8 | M-PSK BER (approx) | $P_b \approx \frac{2}{\log_2 M} Q\!\left(\sqrt{2 \log_2 M \cdot \sin^2\!\left(\frac{\pi}{M}\right) \cdot \frac{E_b}{N_0}}\right)$ | High SNR approx |
| 9 | M-QAM (square) BER | $P_b \approx \frac{4}{\log_2 M}\!\left(1 - \frac{1}{\sqrt{M}}\right) Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{3 \log_2 M}{M-1} \cdot \frac{2 E_b}{N_0}}\right)$ | Exact at high SNR |
| 10 | Coherent BFSK BER | $P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{E_b / N_0}\right)$ | 3 dB worse than BPSK |
| 11 | Non-Coherent BFSK BER | $P_b = \frac{1}{2} \exp(-E_b / 2 N_0)$ | Envelope detection |
| 12 | DPSK BER | $P_b = \frac{1}{2} \exp(-E_b / N_0)$ | Differential detection |
| 13 | MSK BER (coherent) | $P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2 E_b / N_0}\right)$ | Identical to BPSK |
| 14 | Antenna length (quarter-wave) | $L = \frac{c}{4 f_c} = \frac{\lambda}{4}$ | Meters |
| 15 | Wavelength | $\lambda = c / f_c$ | Meters |
| 16 | Shannon Capacity Limit | $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ | bps (upper bound) |
| 17 | $Q$-function to erf conversion | $Q(x) = \frac{1}{2} \text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{x}{\sqrt{2}}\right)$ | Used in Indian textbooks |
| 18 | Output SNR vs $E_b/N_0$ | $\frac{S}{N} = \frac{E_b}{N_0} \cdot \frac{R_b}{B}$ | Linear scale |
| 19 | Minimum orthogonal FSK sep. | $\Delta f_{min} = \frac{1}{2 T_b}$ (Sunde) or $\frac{1}{T_b}$ (coherent) | Hz |
| 20 | MSK Modulation Index | $h = 0.5$ | Dimensionless |

> [!TIP]
> **Q-function vs erfc:** In the KTU valuation scheme, both $Q(x)$ and $\frac{1}{2}\text{erfc}(x/\sqrt{2})$ are accepted. Always show the form you are using. Standard normal tables in $Q(x)$ format are provided in the question paper for board exams.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **BPSK:** Deep-space telemetry (Voyager, Mars rovers), GPS coarse acquisition (C/A-code uses BPSK on L1).
- **QPSK:** DVB-S (satellite TV), LTE downlink control channels, satellite modems.
- **OQPSK / $\pi/4$-DQPSK:** Legacy IS-54 / IS-136 TDMA cellular (2G/3G transition).
- **M-QAM (16, 64, 256):** Wi-Fi (802.11a/g/n/ac/ax adaptive), 5G NR downlink, DVB-C (cable TV), DOCSIS 3.1.
- **MSK / GMSK:** GSM (2G), aeronautical telemetry, deep-space.
- **BFSK:** Pager networks (POCSAG), low-power IoT (LoRa uses chirp-spread-spectrum, related).
- **OFDM-based QAM:** 4G LTE, 5G NR, Wi-Fi 6 — multi-carrier QAM to combat multipath fading.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Project Tip:** When asked to "justify" a scheme in 14-mark answers, always quote the engineering context: e.g., "GMSK is preferred in GSM because the Gaussian filtering reduces adjacent-channel interference (ACI) to less than −60 dB at 100 kHz offset, which is critical in cellular frequency reuse."

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation Mandate:** Every algebraic step is explicitly shown. Multi-line equations are wrapped in `\begin{aligned}...\end{aligned}` with one blank line before and after every $$ block.

---

### 3.1 Derivation: BPSK Probability of Error in AWGN

**Given:** A BPSK signal set
$$s_0(t) = +\sqrt{E_b}\,\phi_1(t), \quad s_1(t) = -\sqrt{E_b}\,\phi_1(t), \quad \phi_1(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{T_b}} \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$
over a coherent AWGN channel with two-sided PSD $N_0/2$.

**Step 1 — Receiver Structure.** The optimal receiver correlates the received signal $r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t)$ with the basis function $\phi_1(t)$, producing a single sufficient statistic:

$$r = \int_0^{T_b} r(t)\,\phi_1(t)\,dt = s_i + n$$

where $n$ is a Gaussian random variable with mean **zero** and variance:

$$\sigma_n^2 = \int_0^{T_b}\!\int_0^{T_b} \frac{N_0}{2}\,\phi_1(t)\phi_1(\tau)\,\delta(t-\tau)\,dt\,d\tau$$

**Step 2 — Computing the noise variance.** Substituting $\phi_1(t) = \sqrt{2/T_b}\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ and using the orthonormality of the basis:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_n^2 &= \frac{N_0}{2} \int_0^{T_b} \phi_1^2(t)\,dt \\
&= \frac{N_0}{2} \int_0^{T_b} \frac{2}{T_b}\cos^2(2\pi f_c t)\,dt \\
&= \frac{N_0}{2} \cdot \frac{2}{T_b} \cdot \frac{T_b}{2} \quad \text{(time average of }\cos^2 \text{ over integer cycles)} \\
&= \frac{N_0}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Conditional PDFs.** Given bit $i \in \{0,1\}$, the decision variable is Gaussian:

$$r \mid s_0 \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(+\sqrt{E_b},\; \frac{N_0}{2}\right), \quad r \mid s_1 \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(-\sqrt{E_b},\; \frac{N_0}{2}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Optimal Threshold (ML Decision Rule).** With equiprobable bits, the optimal threshold $\gamma^*$ is the midpoint between the two means:

$$\gamma^* = \frac{+\sqrt{E_b} + (-\sqrt{E_b})}{2} = 0$$

**Step 5 — Probability of Error Calculation.** By symmetry, $P(\text{error}\vert 0) = P(\text{error}\vert 1)$. Total error:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_b &= P(r < 0 \mid s_0)\,P(s_0) + P(r > 0 \mid s_1)\,P(s_1) \\
&= P(s_0) \int_{-\infty}^{0} \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi N_0}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(r - \sqrt{E_b})^2}{N_0}\right) dr \\
&\quad + P(s_1) \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi N_0}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(r + \sqrt{E_b})^2}{N_0}\right) dr
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Substitution $u = (r - \sqrt{E_b})/\sqrt{N_0/2}$.** Lower limit becomes $(0 - \sqrt{E_b})/\sqrt{N_0/2} = -\sqrt{2 E_b/N_0}$. For equiprobable bits $P(s_0) = P(s_1) = 1/2$:

$$P_b = \frac{1}{2}\,Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right) + \frac{1}{2}\,Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$

$$\boxed{P_{b,\text{BPSK}} = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Check:** At $E_b/N_0 = 10$ (linear), $P_b = Q(\sqrt{20}) = Q(4.47) \approx 3.87 \times 10^{-6}$. This matches standard BPSK curves.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: QPSK BER via Orthogonal Channel Decomposition

**Given:** QPSK symbol $s_i(t) = \sqrt{2E_s/T_s}\cos[2\pi f_c t + (2i-1)\pi/4]$, $i = 1, 2, 3, 4$, with dibit mappings:
- $00 \to \pi/4$, $01 \to 3\pi/4$, $11 \to 5\pi/4$, $10 \to 7\pi/4$.

**Step 1 — Expand using trigonometric identity.** Setting $E_s = 2E_b$ and $T_s = 2T_b$:

$$s_i(t) = \sqrt{\frac{E_b}{T_b}}\!\left[ \cos\!\left((2i-1)\frac{\pi}{4}\right) \cdot \sqrt{2}\cos(2\pi f_c t) - \sin\!\left((2i-1)\frac{\pi}{4}\right) \cdot \sqrt{2}\sin(2\pi f_c t) \right]$$

Using the orthonormal basis $\phi_1(t) = \sqrt{2/T_s}\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ and $\phi_2(t) = \sqrt{2/T_s}\sin(2\pi f_c t)$:

$$s_i = \sqrt{E_s}\cos\!\left((2i-1)\frac{\pi}{4}\right)\,\phi_1 - \sqrt{E_s}\sin\!\left((2i-1)\frac{\pi}{4}\right)\,\phi_2$$

**Step 2 — Two-dimensional constellation points.**

| $i$ | Phase $(2i-1)\pi/4$ | I-component ($s_{i1}$) | Q-component ($s_{i2}$) | Dibit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\pi/4$ | $+\sqrt{E_s/2}$ | $-\sqrt{E_s/2}$ | 00 |
| 2 | $3\pi/4$ | $-\sqrt{E_s/2}$ | $-\sqrt{E_s/2}$ | 01 |
| 3 | $5\pi/4$ | $-\sqrt{E_s/2}$ | $+\sqrt{E_s/2}$ | 11 |
| 4 | $7\pi/4$ | $+\sqrt{E_s/2}$ | $+\sqrt{E_s/2}$ | 10 |

**Step 3 — Correlator outputs.** The received signal $r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t)$ projects to:

$$r_1 = s_{i1} + n_1, \quad r_2 = s_{i2} + n_2$$

with $n_1, n_2$ being **independent** Gaussian RVs, each $\mathcal{N}(0, N_0/2)$. Independence follows from the orthogonality of $\phi_1$ and $\phi_2$.

**Step 4 — Decision rule.** ML detection chooses the constellation point closest in Euclidean distance:

$$\hat{i} = \arg\min_i \left[ (r_1 - s_{i1})^2 + (r_2 - s_{i2})^2 \right]$$

**Step 5 — Probability of correct symbol detection.** A symbol error occurs if the noise vector $\mathbf{n} = (n_1, n_2)$ pushes the received point outside the decision region (a square in QPSK of side $\sqrt{2 E_s}$). Using the *union bound* technique:

$$P_s \le 2\,Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_s}{N_0}}\right) - \left[Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_s}{N_0}}\right)\right]^2$$

For high SNR (where errors are rare), the squared term is negligible:

$$P_s \approx 2\,Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_s}{N_0}}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Convert to Bit Error Rate.** Since QPSK uses **Gray coding** (adjacent symbols differ in only one bit), at high SNR each symbol error corresponds to **exactly one bit error**:

$$P_b \approx \frac{P_s}{\log_2 M} = \frac{P_s}{2} \approx Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_s}{N_0}}\right)$$

**Step 7 — Substitute $E_s = 2 E_b$:**

$$\boxed{P_{b,\text{QPSK}} = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2 E_b}{N_0}}\right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The QPSK BER is **identical** to BPSK BER. This is a *very common KTU 14-mark question.* The two schemes have the same power efficiency, but QPSK has **half the bandwidth** of BPSK for the same bit rate, hence double the spectral efficiency.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: BER vs. Spectral Efficiency Trade-off

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is a **KTU 2024 high-yield analytical summary** for the trade-off question. Values of $E_b/N_0$ (dB) for $P_b = 10^{-5}$ are read from standard AWGN curves.

| Scheme | Bits/Symbol ($\log_2 M$) | Spectral Efficiency (b/s/Hz, $\alpha=0$) | $E_b/N_0$ (dB) for $P_b = 10^{-5}$ | Power vs. BPSK (dB) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| BPSK | 1 | 1 | 9.6 dB | 0 dB (reference) |
| QPSK | 2 | 2 | 9.6 dB | 0 dB |
| 8-PSK | 3 | 3 | 13.0 dB | +3.4 dB |
| 16-PSK | 4 | 4 | 18.7 dB | +9.1 dB |
| 16-QAM | 4 | 4 | 13.4 dB | +3.8 dB |
| 64-QAM | 6 | 6 | 17.8 dB | +8.2 dB |
| 256-QAM | 8 | 8 | 23.8 dB | +14.2 dB |

**Reading the matrix:** Higher-order modulations provide greater bits per Hz but require exponentially more power to maintain the same BER. This is the **central trade-off** the KTU 2024 examiner expects students to articulate.
