## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below are mapped to the **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern**: Part A (3 marks each, no choice, answer in 1–2 pages) and Part B (14 marks each, internal choice, two sub-parts of 7 marks each).

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`** *(3 Marks)*

**Q: Distinguish between coherent and non-coherent detection in digital communication. Give one example scheme for each.**

**Model Answer:**

Coherent detection is a method in which the receiver uses a **locally generated carrier** that is **phase-locked** to the incoming modulated carrier via a synchronization circuit (e.g., Phase-Locked Loop, Costas loop). The receiver multiplies the incoming signal by this synchronized local oscillator before integrating. This achieves the **optimal (lowest possible) BER** at the cost of synchronization hardware. **Example:** BPSK, QPSK, M-QAM.

Non-coherent detection **does not require** knowledge of the carrier phase. The receiver uses either an envelope detector (for ASK) or a frequency discriminator / differential comparator (for FSK/DPSK). It is simpler and cheaper, but is **3 dB worse in SNR** at low BER. **Example:** OOK with envelope detection, DPSK, NCFSK.

**Examiner Keywords (for 3 marks):** Carrier sync (1 mark), example coherent (1 mark), example non-coherent (1 mark).

---

#### **Question 2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`** *(3 Marks)*

**Q: Why is QPSK preferred over BPSK in bandwidth-limited systems, even though both have the same BER?**

**Model Answer:**

BPSK and QPSK have **identical bit error probability** $P_b = Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ in AWGN under coherent detection. However, QPSK transmits **2 bits per symbol** while BPSK transmits **1 bit per symbol**. Consequently, for the **same bit rate $R_b$**, the QPSK symbol duration $T_s = 2 T_b$ is twice that of BPSK, which means the **required transmission bandwidth is halved** ($B_{QPSK} = R_b/2$ vs $B_{BPSK} = R_b$ for $\alpha=0$ raised-cosine shaping). This makes QPSK **twice as bandwidth-efficient** ($\eta = 2$ bps/Hz vs $1$ bps/Hz), which is critical in **band-limited channels** like satellite transponders and cellular downlink.

**Examiner Keywords (for 3 marks):** Same BER formula (1 mark), $T_s = 2T_b$ for QPSK (1 mark), bandwidth halved / $\eta = 2$ bps/Hz (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE regulations**, every Part B question provides an internal choice. Question A and Question B are completely independent — students answer EITHER the full Question A OR the full Question B.

---

#### **Part B — Question A: `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`** *(14 Marks)*

**Q: (a)** Draw the block diagram of a QPSK transmitter and explain the function of each block. Show that the QPSK signal can be represented as two orthogonal BPSK signals. *(7 Marks)*

**Q: (b)** For a QPSK system operating over an AWGN channel with $E_b/N_0 = 10$ dB, compute the probability of bit error. Compare it with BPSK and coherent BFSK at the same SNR. *(7 Marks)*

---

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Block Diagram.** The QPSK transmitter consists of:
- **Serial-to-Parallel Converter:** Splits incoming bit stream $b_k$ into two parallel streams (odd-indexed bits → I-channel, even-indexed bits → Q-channel), forming **dibits** (pairs of bits).
- **NRZ Bipolar Encoder:** Maps bit `0` → $+1$ V and bit `1` → $-1$ V (or vice versa). This produces the in-phase and quadrature baseband signals $d_I(t)$ and $d_Q(t)$.
- **Pulse Shaping Filters $P(t)$:** Typically **Root Raised Cosine (RRC)** filters with roll-off factor $\alpha$ to limit bandwidth and prevent ISI.
- **Mixers (Balanced Modulators):** Multiply $d_I(t)$ by $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ and $d_Q(t)$ by $\sin(2\pi f_c t)$.
- **Local Oscillator & 90° Phase Shifter:** Generates the carrier and a quadrature version.
- **Linear Summer:** Adds the I- and Q-channel outputs to form the composite QPSK signal $s(t)$.

**Step 2 — Mathematical Representation.** Let the dibit at time $k$ be $(I_k, Q_k)$ where $I_k, Q_k \in \{+1, -1\}$. The transmitted QPSK waveform over symbol interval $kT_s \le t \le (k+1)T_s$ is:

$$s_k(t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\,d_I(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) - \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\,d_Q(t)\sin(2\pi f_c t)$$

Setting $A = 1/\sqrt{2}$ and applying the identity $\cos(\alpha)\cos(\beta) - \sin(\alpha)\sin(\beta) = \cos(\alpha + \beta)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
s_k(t) &= A\left[ d_I(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) - d_Q(t)\sin(2\pi f_c t) \right] \\
&= A\,\sqrt{d_I^2 + d_Q^2}\,\cos\!\left[2\pi f_c t + \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{d_Q}{d_I}\right)\right]
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Two Orthogonal BPSK Signals.** Define:
$$s_{I}(t) = d_I(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t), \quad s_{Q}(t) = d_Q(t)\sin(2\pi f_c t)$$

The functions $\phi_1(t) = \sqrt{2/T_s}\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ and $\phi_2(t) = \sqrt{2/T_s}\sin(2\pi f_c t)$ are **orthonormal**:

$$\int_0^{T_s} \phi_1(t)\phi_2(t)\,dt = \int_0^{T_s} \frac{2}{T_s}\cos(2\pi f_c t)\sin(2\pi f_c t)\,dt = 0$$

using the identity $2\cos\theta\sin\theta = \sin(2\theta)$, which integrates to zero over an integer number of carrier cycles. Therefore $s_I(t)$ and $s_Q(t)$ are **two independent BPSK modulations on orthogonal carriers**, and the QPSK signal is their sum. **(7 marks satisfied)**

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Convert $E_b/N_0$ from dB to linear.**

$$\frac{E_b}{N_0}\bigg|_{\text{dB}} = 10 \text{ dB} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{E_b}{N_0} = 10^{10/10} = 10$$

**Step 2 — QPSK BER.**

$$P_{b,\text{QPSK}} = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2 E_b}{N_0}}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2 \times 10}\right) = Q(\sqrt{20}) = Q(4.472)$$

From standard normal $Q$-tables: $Q(4.47) \approx 3.87 \times 10^{-6}$.

**Step 3 — BPSK BER.**

$$P_{b,\text{BPSK}} = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2 E_b}{N_0}}\right) = Q(\sqrt{20}) = 3.87 \times 10^{-6} \quad \text{(identical to QPSK)}$$

**Step 4 — Coherent BFSK BER.**

$$P_{b,\text{BFSK}} = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_b}{N_0}}\right) = Q(\sqrt{10}) = Q(3.162)$$

From tables: $Q(3.16) \approx 7.91 \times 10^{-4}$.

**Step 5 — Comparison Table.**

| Scheme | BER at $E_b/N_0 = 10$ dB | Performance vs BPSK |
|---|---|---|
| BPSK | $3.87 \times 10^{-6}$ | Reference |
| QPSK | $3.87 \times 10^{-6}$ | Identical |
| Coherent BFSK | $7.91 \times 10^{-4}$ | ~204× worse (≈2.3 dB) |

**Conclusion:** QPSK matches BPSK's power efficiency while using half the bandwidth; BFSK is 3 dB worse in power efficiency. **(7 marks satisfied)**

---

#### **Part B — Question B: `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`** *(14 Marks)*

**Q: (a)** Explain the generation and detection of coherent Binary Phase Shift Keying (BPSK). Derive the expression for the probability of bit error in AWGN. *(7 Marks)*

**Q: (b)** With a clear block diagram, explain the generation of MSK. Show that MSK is a special case of continuous-phase FSK (CPFSK) with modulation index $h = 0.5$. Compare MSK with QPSK in terms of bandwidth, envelope, and out-of-band emission. *(7 Marks)*

---

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Step 1 — BPSK Generation.** The BPSK modulator multiplies the NRZ-encoded baseband signal $d(t) \in \{+1, -1\}$ with a carrier $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$:

$$s(t) = d(t)\,\sqrt{\frac{2 E_b}{T_b}}\cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

For bit `0`: $s(t) = +\sqrt{2E_b/T_b}\cos(2\pi f_c t)$.
For bit `1`: $s(t) = -\sqrt{2E_b/T_b}\cos(2\pi f_c t)$.

A typical implementation uses a **double-balanced mixer** with the carrier applied to the LO port and the NRZ data to the IF port.

**Step 2 — BPSK Coherent Detection.** The received signal $r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t)$ (where $n(t)$ is AWGN with PSD $N_0/2$) is multiplied by a **locally generated carrier** that is phase-locked to the transmitter's carrier:

$$y(t) = r(t) \cdot 2\cos(2\pi f_c t) = 2 s_i(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) + 2 n(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

The $2s_i\cos^2$ term yields a DC component plus a high-frequency term at $2 f_c$, which is removed by an **integrate-and-dump** filter (or matched filter) over one bit period $T_b$.

**Step 3 — Decision Variable.** The output of the integrator is the sufficient statistic:

$$r = \int_0^{T_b} y(t)\,dt = \pm\sqrt{E_b} + n$$

where $n$ is Gaussian with mean 0 and variance $N_0/2$.

**Step 4 — Threshold Detection.** Optimal ML threshold is $\gamma^* = 0$ (midpoint of $\pm\sqrt{E_b}$).

$$\hat{b} = \begin{cases} 0, & r > 0 \\ 1, & r < 0 \end{cases}$$

**Step 5 — BER Derivation.** Compute $P(\hat{b} = 1 \mid b = 0) = P(r < 0 \mid s_0)$:

$$P(\text{error}\vert s_0) = \int_{-\infty}^{0} \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi N_0}} \exp\!\left( -\frac{(r - \sqrt{E_b})^2}{N_0} \right) dr$$

Substitute $u = (r - \sqrt{E_b})/\sqrt{N_0/2}$:

$$P(\text{error}\vert s_0) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{-\infty}^{-\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{u^2}{2}\right) du = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$

By symmetry, total $P_b = P(\text{error}\vert s_0)\,P(b=0) + P(\text{error}\vert s_1)\,P(b=1)$. With $P(b=0) = P(b=1) = 0.5$:

$$\boxed{P_{b,\text{BPSK}} = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)} \quad \textbf{(7 marks satisfied)}$$

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Step 1 — MSK Generation Block Diagram.** MSK modulator consists of:
- **Serial-to-Parallel Converter:** Splits bit stream into odd and even bits → I and Q channels.
- **NRZ Encoders** with differential encoding: $d_I(k) = b(k) \oplus b(k-1)$ (odd bits) and $d_Q(k) = b(k+1)$ (even bits).
- **Pulse Shapers:** Use **half-sinusoidal** weighting $P(t) = \sin(\pi t / T_b)$ for $-T_b \le t \le T_b$ instead of rectangular NRZ.
- **Mixers & Quadrature Carrier Oscillator:** Multiplies the I and Q streams with $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ and $\sin(2\pi f_c t)$ respectively.
- **Summer:** Adds the two orthogonal components.

**Step 2 — Mathematical Form of MSK.** The MSK signal can be written as:

$$s(t) = d_I(t)\cos\!\left(\frac{\pi t}{2 T_b}\right)\cos(2\pi f_c t) - d_Q(t)\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi t}{2 T_b}\right)\sin(2\pi f_c t)$$

The half-sinusoidal pulse weighting causes the **frequency** to be:

$$f(t) = f_c \pm \frac{1}{4 T_b}$$

with the $+$ sign for bit `1` and $-$ sign for bit `0`. Thus **frequency deviation** $\Delta f = 1/(4 T_b)$.

**Step 3 — Modulation Index Derivation.** The frequency spacing is $\Delta f = 1/(4T_b)$. The bit rate is $R_b = 1/T_b$. By definition, the modulation index of an FSK signal is:

$$h = \frac{2 \Delta f}{R_b} = \frac{2 \cdot (1/(4 T_b))}{1/T_b} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5$$

This is the **minimum** $h$ that keeps the two FSK tones **phase-coherent and orthogonal** over $T_b$, hence the name **Minimum Shift Keying**. Furthermore, since the I/Q data streams are weighted by half-sinusoids and the I/Q components are offset by $T_b$ (Offset QPSK structure), the resulting phase trajectory is **continuous** — no abrupt $\pi$ phase jumps occur, making MSK a true **CPFSK**. **(7 marks satisfied, partial)**

**Step 4 — Comparison Table MSK vs QPSK.**

| Parameter | MSK | QPSK |
|---|---|---|
| Envelope | **Constant** (true constant envelope) | Approximately constant (180° jumps cause envelope dips after BPF) |
| Main-lobe bandwidth | $1.5 R_b$ | $1.0 R_b$ (raised cosine, $\alpha = 0$) |
| Out-of-band emission | Lower (continuous phase) | Higher (discrete phase jumps) |
| Spectral efficiency | $0.67$ b/s/Hz | $1.0$ b/s/Hz |
| Power efficiency (BER) | $Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ | $Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ — **identical** |
| Use case | GSM (as GMSK), aerospace telemetry | Satellite TV, LTE control, generic |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 ways KTU students lose marks on this topic (Module 4 — Digital Bandpass Communication):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert dB to linear in BER problems.** If the question gives $E_b/N_0 = 10$ dB, you MUST convert: $E_b/N_0 = 10^{10/10} = 10$. Plugging `10 dB` directly into $Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ yields a wrong answer. **Loss: 2 marks per instance.**
>
> 2. **Mixing up the BER formulas for BPSK and coherent BFSK.** BPSK is $Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$; coherent orthogonal BFSK is $Q(\sqrt{E_b/N_0})$. The 3 dB difference is a common trap question.
>
> 3. **Confusing spectral efficiency with symbol rate.** Spectral efficiency $\eta$ is in **bps/Hz**, not baud. QPSK gives 2 bps/Hz, not 2 baud.
>
> 4. **Missing Gray coding justification in QPSK BER derivation.** Examiners explicitly check for the statement *"Since Gray coding maps adjacent symbols to dibits differing in one bit, each symbol error corresponds to exactly one bit error, hence $P_b \approx P_s / 2$."* **Loss: 1–2 marks.**
>
> 5. **Omitting block diagram labels in 7-mark "draw and explain" questions.** A block diagram without **labeled inputs and outputs** gets only 2–3 marks out of 7. Always label: $b(t)$, $d_I(t)$, $d_Q(t)$, $f_c$, $s(t)$, $r(t)$, $y(t)$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE (Module 4):**

- **Definition:** Digital bandpass modulation maps a digital bitstream onto a sinusoidal carrier's amplitude (ASK), frequency (FSK), or phase (PSK), enabling propagation across bandlimited channels.
- **Carrier's Role:** High-frequency carrier $f_c$ allows small antenna dimensions ($\lambda/4 = c/(4 f_c)$) and frequency multiplexing (FDM).
- **Three Pillars of Comparison:** Every modulation scheme is evaluated on **(i) Power Efficiency (BER vs $E_b/N_0$)**, **(ii) Bandwidth Efficiency (b/s/Hz)**, **(iii) Implementation Complexity (coherent vs non-coherent)**.
- **BPSK BER:** $P_b = Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ — the benchmark binary scheme.
- **QPSK BER:** $P_b = Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ — **identical to BPSK** but at **half the bandwidth** (Gray coded).
- **BFSK BER (coherent):** $P_b = Q(\sqrt{E_b/N_0})$ — 3 dB worse than BPSK.
- **BFSK BER (non-coherent):** $P_b = \frac{1}{2} \exp(-E_b/2N_0)$.
- **DPSK BER:** $P_b = \frac{1}{2} \exp(-E_b/N_0)$.
- **MSK Modulation Index:** $h = 0.5$ — minimum for orthogonal CPFSK. **GMSK** is MSK + Gaussian prefilter.
- **M-QAM trade-off:** From 4-QAM to 64-QAM, spectral efficiency increases 3× but $E_b/N_0$ requirement rises by ~8 dB for $P_b = 10^{-5}$.
- **Receiver Building Blocks:** LNA → Down-converter → Carrier Sync (PLL) → Matched Filter → Sampler → ML Detector → Decoder.
- **Coherent Detection:** Requires PLL/Costas loop; ~3 dB better than non-coherent at low BER.
- **Modern Use:** Wi-Fi/5G = adaptive QAM-OFDM, GPS = BPSK on L1/L2, GSM = GMSK, Satellite TV = QPSK/8-PSK.
- **Q-function in Indian textbooks:** $Q(x) = \frac{1}{2}\text{erfc}(x/\sqrt{2})$ — both forms accepted in KTU answer scripts.
- **Signal Space Foundation:** QPSK has 2-dimensional orthogonal basis $\{\cos(2\pi f_c t), \sin(2\pi f_c t)\}$. BPSK uses 1-dimensional basis. M-PSK has 2 dimensions. M-QAM is also 2-dimensional but uses both axes for amplitude variation.
- **Nyquist Bandwidth:** $B = R_s (1 + \alpha) = \frac{R_b}{\log_2 M} (1+\alpha)$ with raised-cosine roll-off $\alpha$.
- **Key Standard-Form Equation:** $s(t) = \text{Re}\left\{ \tilde{s}(t) e^{j 2\pi f_c t} \right\}$ — equivalent complex baseband representation used in modern software-defined radio (SDR) implementations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bandpass Modulation Techniques: Amplitude Shift Keying
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET864 - ELECTRONIC COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bandpass Modulation Techniques: Amplitude Shift Keying

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Amplitude Shift Keying (ASK)** is a fundamental **bandpass digital modulation technique** in which the amplitude of a high-frequency sinusoidal carrier signal is switched between two (or more) discrete levels in accordance with the digital baseband (message) signal, while the **frequency** and the **phase** of the carrier are held constant. In its most common binary form — **Binary Amplitude Shift Keying (BASK)**, also known as **On–Off Keying (OOK)** — the carrier is transmitted when the binary input is a logic `1` and is suppressed (amplitude = 0) when the input is a logic `0`.

The mathematically rigorous KTU 2024 definition is:

$$s_{\text{ASK}}(t) \;=\; A_c \, b(t) \, \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

where:
- $A_c$ is the peak carrier amplitude (**volts**),
- $f_c$ is the carrier frequency (**Hz**),
- $b(t) \in \lbrace 0,\;1 \rbrace$ is the binary NRZ data waveform,
- $t$ is continuous time in **seconds**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 4):** ASK is treated as the *first* and *simplest* member of the **M-ary bandpass modulation family** (which also includes PSK, FSK, and QAM). The examiner expects you to (a) write the time-domain equation, (b) sketch the PSD, (c) compute bandwidth, and (d) compare it with PSK/FSK in later parts.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Lighthouse Flash

Imagine a **lighthouse keeper** at the top of a coastal tower. The lighthouse lamp is always ready to flash (the carrier wave is always generated), but the keeper has a simple rule:
- When a ship is approaching → the lamp is **ON** (transmits a burst of light, i.e., logic `1`).
- When no ship is sighted → the lamp is **OFF** (no light, i.e., logic `0`).

The **frequency of the flashing pattern** (analogous to carrier frequency $f_c$) and the **colour/tint** (analogous to phase) never change — only the **brightness (amplitude)** is being switched. A distant observer, with a photodetector, can decode the "on/off" sequence back into the original message. This is *exactly* how ASK works in radio: the **carrier is the lamp, the binary data is the ship/no-ship signal, and the photodetector is the demodulator**.

## 1.3 Why ASK Is "Bandpass"

The term **bandpass modulation** is crucial: ASK translates a **low-frequency baseband spectrum** (centred at DC) up to a high-frequency band **centred at $f_c$**, so that the resulting signal can be radiated efficiently by an antenna of practical size (Wiener–Hertz antenna theory: efficient radiation requires $L \ge \lambda/10$). The KTU syllabus classifies ASK under "bandpass" precisely because the message is impressed on a high-frequency sinusoidal carrier.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constants / Metrics to Memorise**
> - Carrier frequency range for typical ASK: **535 kHz – 1700 kHz** (AM broadcast legacy), but laboratory ASK uses **100 kHz – 10 MHz**.
> - Standard bit rate range: **300 bps – 10 kbps** (low-speed digital links, optical communication, RFID).
> - Modulation index $m$ for ASK: **0 ≤ m ≤ 1** (with $m=1$ being **100% modulation**).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Construction — Step-by-Step Logic

The ASK signal is built by performing a direct **multiplication** of the binary NRZ waveform $b(t)$ with the continuous-time carrier $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$. This product operation (in the time domain) translates to a **convolution of spectra** in the frequency domain — shifting the baseband lobes to $\pm f_c$. The logical chain is:

1. **Generate the carrier:** $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ — a pure sinusoid whose single spectrum line lives at $f = f_c$.
2. **Generate the baseband data:** $b(t)$ is a unipolar NRZ pulse train with amplitude levels `0` and `1`, with bit duration $T_b$.
3. **Multiply:** $s(t) = b(t) \cdot c(t)$. Wherever $b(t) = 0$, the product is zero (carrier suppressed). Wherever $b(t) = 1$, the full carrier is transmitted.
4. **Result:** The output $s(t)$ is a *gated* sinusoid — present for `1` bits, absent for `0` bits.

## 2.2 Why "On–Off" Specifically?

When $b(t)$ is purely binary ($\lbrace 0,1 \rbrace$), the transmitted signal during bit-interval $kT_b \le t < (k+1)T_b$ becomes:

$$s_k(t) = \begin{cases} A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t), & \text{if } b_k = 1 \\ 0, & \text{if } b_k = 0 \end{cases}$$

This **binary suppression of the carrier** is the defining feature of OOK. The "off" state is essentially *no transmission*, which leads to a non-constant envelope — a critical property that KTU examiners frequently ask about.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Envelope Property:** The ASK waveform has a **non-constant envelope** (it goes to zero for `0` bits). Therefore ASK is **highly sensitive to non-linear amplifiers and amplitude fading**, which is why it is rarely used in mobile radio — but very common in low-cost, short-range, line-of-sight links (e.g., IR remote controls, optical fibre links, RFID tags).

## 2.3 Modulation Index for ASK

The **modulation index** $m$ controls the depth of amplitude variation:

$$m \;=\; \frac{A_{\max} - A_{\min}}{A_{\max} + A_{\min}}$$

For OOK, $A_{\min} = 0$, so $m = 1$ always (100% modulation). For general M-ary ASK, $m$ can be tuned between `0` and `1`.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks / Boundary Condition |
|---|----------|---------|-------|------------------------------|
| 1 | ASK time-domain signal | $s(t) = A_c \, b(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | Volts | $b(t) \in \lbrace 0,1 \rbrace$ for BASK |
| 2 | Carrier signal | $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | Volts | Pure sinusoid, single spectral line at $f_c$ |
| 3 | Transmitted power (OOK) | $P_t = \dfrac{A_c^{2}}{2} \cdot P(1)$ | Watts | $P(1)$ is probability of bit `1` (usually 0.5) |
| 4 | Average power (equiprobable) | $P_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{A_c^{2}}{4}$ | Watts | When $P(0)=P(1)=0.5$ |
| 5 | Transmission bandwidth | $B_T = 2B$ | Hz | Twice the baseband bandwidth $B$ |
| 6 | Null-to-null bandwidth | $B_{\text{nn}} = \dfrac{2}{T_b}$ | Hz | For rectangular NRZ pulses |
| 7 | Bit energy | $E_b = P_{\text{avg}} \cdot T_b$ | Joules | Average energy per transmitted bit |
| 8 | Modulation index | $m = \dfrac{A_{\max} - A_{\min}}{A_{\max} + A_{\min}}$ | dimensionless | $0 \le m \le 1$; OOK always has $m=1$ |
| 9 | BER — Coherent OOK | $P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{E_b}{N_0}}\right)$ | dimensionless | $Q(\cdot)$ is Gaussian Q-function |
| 10 | BER — Non-coherent OOK | $P_e = \dfrac{1}{2} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_b}{2N_0}\right)$ | dimensionless | Envelope detection |
| 11 | Spectral efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{R_b}{B_T} = \dfrac{1}{2}$ | bits/s/Hz | Lowest among binary schemes |
| 12 | Spectral density (OOK) | $S_s(f) = \dfrac{A_c^{2}}{4}\left[\delta(f-f_c) + \delta(f+f_c)\right] + \dfrac{A_c^{2}}{4}\left[P_b(f-f_c) + P_b(f+f_c)\right]$ | W/Hz | $P_b(\cdot)$ is baseband PSD |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** ASK/OOK is the workhorse of (i) **optical fibre links** (LED on/off), (ii) **RFID tags** (passive backscatter), (iii) **IR remote controls** (TV/AC remotes using 38 kHz carrier), (iv) **low-cost telemetry**, and (v) the historical **Aloha-net** packet radio. Its simplicity makes it the cheapest to implement, but the worst in noise immunity — a perfect KTU contrast point to discuss with PSK and FSK.

## 2.5 Power Spectral Density (PSD) — Spectral Interpretation

The PSD of OOK consists of:
- **Two discrete impulses** at $\pm f_c$ (the carrier residue when the data has a DC component).
- **Two continuous lobes** centred at $\pm f_c$, each shaped like $\text{sinc}^{2}(fT_b)$, representing the data spectrum translated up to the carrier.

This is the **most-sketched diagram in the KTU exam** for this topic — practice drawing both the time-domain and the PSD sketch side-by-side.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Time-Domain Expression for BASK

We begin with the binary data waveform modelled as a sum of rectangular pulses:

$$b(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} a_k \, p(t - kT_b)$$

where $a_k \in \lbrace 0, 1 \rbrace$ and $p(t)$ is a unit-amplitude rectangular pulse of duration $T_b$:

$$p(t) = \begin{cases} 1, & 0 \le t < T_b \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 1 — Write the carrier:**

$$c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Step 2 — Perform the multiplication (modulation):**

$$s(t) = b(t) \cdot c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} a_k \, p(t - kT_b)$$

**Step 3 — Interchange the product and sum (linearity of multiplication):**

$$s(t) = A_c \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} a_k \, p(t - kT_b) \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Step 4 — Recognise that within each bit interval $[kT_b, (k+1)T_b)$, the term $a_k$ is constant. Hence the signal is a gated sinusoid:**

$$s(t) = \begin{cases} A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t), & \text{if } a_k = 1 \quad \text{(bit interval } k) \\ 0, & \text{if } a_k = 0 \quad \text{(bit interval } k) \end{cases}$$

This completes the time-domain derivation. ✅

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Average Transmitted Power for Equiprobable BASK

The instantaneous power of the carrier is $p(t) = \dfrac{s^{2}(t)}{R}$, where $R = 1\,\Omega$ is normalised. With $R = 1\,\Omega$ and $\cos^{2}(\cdot)$ averaged over a full carrier period:

**Step 1 — Power during a `1` bit:**

$$P_1 = \left\langle A_c^{2} \cos^{2}(2\pi f_c t) \right\rangle = \frac{A_c^{2}}{2}$$

(The angled brackets denote time-averaging; $\langle \cos^{2}\rangle = 1/2$.)

**Step 2 — Power during a `0` bit:**

$$P_0 = 0$$

**Step 3 — Average power assuming equiprobable bits** ($P(a_k=1) = P(a_k=0) = 0.5$):

$$P_{\text{avg}} = P(a_k=1) \cdot P_1 + P(a_k=0) \cdot P_0 = (0.5)\cdot \frac{A_c^{2}}{2} + (0.5)\cdot 0$$

$$\boxed{\,P_{\text{avg}} = \frac{A_c^{2}}{4}\,} \quad \text{(watts, for equiprobable OOK)}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bit Error Probability for Coherent BASK

Consider an AWGN channel with two-sided PSD $N_0/2$. The received signal in the $k$-th bit interval is:

$$r(t) = s_k(t) + n(t)$$

The **optimum detector** correlates $r(t)$ with the basis function $\phi_1(t) = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{T_b}} \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ over one bit duration, then compares with a threshold $\gamma$.

**Step 1 — Define the correlator output:**

$$z = \int_{kT_b}^{(k+1)T_b} r(t) \, \phi_1(t) \, dt = s + n$$

where $s = \pm \sqrt{E_b}$ (signal component) and $n \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, N_0/2)$ is Gaussian noise.

**Step 2 — For OOK, hypothesis H0 (`0` sent) gives $s = 0$; hypothesis H1 (`1` sent) gives $s = \sqrt{E_b}$.**

**Step 3 — Decision rule:** choose H1 if $z > \gamma$, else H0. With equiprobable bits, optimum threshold is $\gamma = \sqrt{E_b}/2$.

**Step 4 — Probability of error under each hypothesis:**

$$P(\text{error} \mid H_0) = P(z > \gamma \mid H_0) = Q\!\left(\frac{\gamma}{\sqrt{N_0/2}}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_b}{2N_0}}\right)$$

$$P(\text{error} \mid H_1) = P(z < \gamma \mid H_1) = Q\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{E_b} - \gamma}{\sqrt{N_0/2}}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_b}{2N_0}}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Total bit error probability (equally likely):**

$$P_e = P(H_0)\,P(e\mid H_0) + P(H_1)\,P(e\mid H_1) = 0.5\cdot Q + 0.5\cdot Q$$

$$\boxed{\,P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_b}{2N_0}}\right)\,} \quad \text{(Coherent OOK detection)}$$

This is **3 dB worse** than coherent BPSK (which has $P_e = Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$), a frequently-tested KTU comparison.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Bandwidth of BASK

The baseband NRZ pulse $p(t)$ has Fourier transform $P(f) = T_b \, \text{sinc}(fT_b)$, whose main lobe width is $2/T_b$. Multiplication by $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ shifts the spectrum by $\pm f_c$, but does **not change its width**.

$$\text{Baseband null-to-null bandwidth} = \frac{2}{T_b}$$

$$\text{Passband null-to-null bandwidth} = \frac{2}{T_b}$$

$$\boxed{\,B_T = 2R_b\,} \quad \text{(in Hz, where } R_b = 1/T_b \text{ is the bit rate)}$$

This confirms the **spectral efficiency** of BASK as $\eta = R_b / B_T = 1/2$ bit/s/Hz.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — ASK vs. PSK vs. FSK

| Property | **BASK (OOK)** | **BPSK** | **BFSK (coherent)** |
|----------|----------------|----------|---------------------|
| Parameter varied | Amplitude | Phase | Frequency |
| Bandwidth | $2R_b$ | $2R_b$ | $\vert f_1 - f_2 \vert + 2R_b$ |
| BER (coherent) | $Q(\sqrt{E_b/2N_0})$ | $Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ | $Q(\sqrt{E_b/N_0})$ |
| Noise immunity | Worst | Best | Intermediate |
| Power efficiency | Lowest | Highest | Intermediate |
| Envelope | Non-constant | Constant | Constant |
| Complexity | Simplest | Moderate | Moderate |
| Application | IR remote, RFID | Satellite, deep-space | Modems, paging |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Heuristic:** Whenever a comparison question appears, present this matrix in **tabular form** with the **bold** worst/best annotations. Examiners reward systematic tabular comparison highly.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — BASK Modulator (Product Modulator)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[dataSRC[/Binary Data b of t/]:::src] --> B[NRZEncoder[/NRZ Encoder Unipolar/]:::blk]
    B --> C[mulBlock((Multiplier X)):::mod]
    D[oscOSC[/Local Oscillator A sub c cos 2 pi f sub c t/]:::src] --> C
    C --> E[bandPA[/Bandpass Filter and Power Amplifier/]:::blk]
    E --> F[antTX(((Antenna TX))):::ant]

    classDef src fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#0C4A6E
    classDef blk fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#78350F
    classDef mod fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#15803D,stroke-width:3px,color:#14532D
    classDef ant fill:#FCE7F3,stroke:#9D174D,stroke-width:2px,color:#831843
```

**Block-Level Operation:**
- The **Binary Data Source** generates a stream of `0`s and `1`s at rate $R_b = 1/T_b$.
- The **NRZ Encoder** converts this into a unipolar voltage waveform $b(t) \in \lbrace 0, 1 \rbrace$.
- The **Multiplier** (a balanced modulator or an analog switch) gates the carrier.
- The **Local Oscillator** generates the pure sinusoid $A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$.
- The **Bandpass Filter + PA** removes out-of-band harmonics and boosts power.
- The **Antenna** radiates the ASK signal into the channel.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Coherent BASK Demodulator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G[antRX(((Antenna RX))):::ant] --> H[bpLNA[/Bandpass LNA/]:::blk]
    H --> I[mulRX((Multiplier X)):::mod]
    J[loRX[/Local Oscillator Phase Locked/]:::src] --> I
    I --> K[intLPF[/Integrate over T sub b and Lowpass Filter/]:::blk]
    K --> L[decTh[{Threshold Comparator}]:::blk]
    L --> M[dataOUT[/Recovered Binary Data/]:::src]

    classDef src fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#0C4A6E
    classDef blk fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#78350F
    classDef mod fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#15803D,stroke-width:3px,color:#14532D
    classDef ant fill:#FCE7F3,stroke:#9D174D,stroke-width:2px,color:#831843
```

**Block-Level Operation:**
- The **Antenna** captures the noisy ASK signal.
- The **LNA** amplifies while adding minimal noise.
- The **Multiplier** mixes the received signal with a locally generated carrier that is **phase-locked** to the transmitter's carrier — this is what makes the detection *coherent*.
- The **Integrate-and-Dump + LPF** computes the correlation $\int_0^{T_b} r(t)\phi_1(t)\,dt$.
- The **Threshold Comparator** decides whether the integrated value corresponds to a `1` (above $\gamma = \sqrt{E_b}/2$) or a `0` (below $\gamma$).
- The **Output** is the recovered binary stream.

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Non-Coherent Envelope Detector

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N[antRX2(((Antenna RX))):::ant] --> O[bpFilter[/Bandpass Filter f sub c/]:::blk]
    O --> P[envDet[/Envelope Detector Diode plus RC/]:::blk]
    P --> Q[decTh2[{Threshold Comparator}]:::blk]
    Q --> R[dataOUT2[/Recovered Binary Data/]:::src]

    classDef src fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#0C4A6E
    classDef blk fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#78350F
    classDef ant fill:#FCE7F3,stroke:#9D174D,stroke-width:2px,color:#831843
```

**Block-Level Operation:**
- This is the **simplest, cheapest** ASK receiver — no carrier synchronisation needed.
- The **Bandpass Filter** selects the ASK channel.
- The **Diode + RC** rectifies and smooths to extract the envelope.
- The **Comparator** checks whether the envelope exceeds the threshold.

> [!NOTE]
> **Trade-off:** Non-coherent detection is **simpler and cheaper** but suffers a **3 dB SNR penalty** (BER = $\tfrac{1}{2}\exp(-E_b/2N_0)$) compared to coherent detection. KTU questions often ask for this trade-off.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define Amplitude Shift Keying (ASK). Write the expression for the BASK signal and draw its waveform for the data sequence `1011001`.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Amplitude Shift Keying (ASK) is a digital modulation scheme in which the amplitude of a high-frequency carrier is varied in accordance with the digital baseband signal, while the frequency and phase remain constant. The binary version is called BASK or On–Off Keying (OOK).

**Expression (1 mark):**

$$s(t) = A_c \, b(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t), \qquad b(t) \in \lbrace 0,1 \rbrace$$

**Waveform (1 mark):** For data `1011001`, the ASK waveform consists of carrier bursts during `1` bits and zero amplitude during `0` bits, with each bit occupying duration $T_b$.

```
Bit:       1     0     1     1     0     0     1
Amplitude:  A_c   0     A_c   A_c   0     0     A_c
Carrier:   ON    OFF   ON    ON    OFF   OFF   ON
```

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Compare BASK with BPSK in terms of bandwidth, BER performance, and envelope property.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per row):**

| Parameter | BASK (OOK) | BPSK |
|-----------|------------|------|
| **Bandwidth** | $2R_b$ (same as BPSK) | $2R_b$ |
| **BER (coherent)** | $Q(\sqrt{E_b/2N_0})$ — **worse by 3 dB** | $Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ — **best** |
| **Envelope** | **Non-constant** (goes to zero for `0` bits) | **Constant** (suitable for non-linear amps) |

**Conclusion (additional credit):** BPSK is preferred for power-limited, noisy links (e.g., satellite, deep-space), while BASK is preferred for ultra-low-cost, short-range applications (e.g., IR remotes, RFID).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as **7 + 7** sub-parts. **Internal choice is mandatory** — you must attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the time-domain expression for the Binary Amplitude Shift Keying (BASK) signal. Sketch its power spectral density (PSD) and label the spectral lines and continuous lobes clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A BASK system transmits a binary sequence at a bit rate of $R_b = 10$ kbps using a carrier of $f_c = 1$ MHz and peak amplitude $A_c = 10$ V into a $50\,\Omega$ antenna. Calculate the **(i)** null-to-null transmission bandwidth, **(ii)** average transmitted power (assuming equiprobable bits), and **(iii)** bit energy $E_b$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.A (a) [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Define baseband binary data (1 mark):**

$$b(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} a_k \, p(t - kT_b), \quad a_k \in \lbrace 0, 1 \rbrace$$

where $p(t)$ is a unit rectangular pulse of width $T_b$.

**Step 2 — Define the carrier (1 mark):**

$$c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Step 3 — Perform multiplication to obtain BASK signal (2 marks):**

$$s(t) = b(t) \cdot c(t) = A_c \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} a_k \, p(t - kT_b) \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Step 4 — Sketch the PSD (3 marks):**

The PSD is obtained by shifting the baseband PSD $P_b(f) = T_b \, \text{sinc}^{2}(fT_b)$ (for random binary data) to $\pm f_c$, plus two delta functions at $\pm f_c$ due to the DC component in $b(t)$:

$$S_s(f) = \frac{A_c^{2}}{4}\Big[\delta(f - f_c) + \delta(f + f_c)\Big] + \frac{A_c^{2}}{4}\Big[P_b(f - f_c) + P_b(f + f_c)\Big]$$

```
           S_s(f)
             |
             |   *  (delta at -f_c)
        _____|________              _____
       /     |        \            /     \
      /      |         \          /       \
─────/───────|──────────\────────/─────────\────►  f
   -f_c      |           \      /           +f_c
             |            *  (delta at +f_c)
```

**Mark allocation:** Time-domain expression (3 marks) + PSD with labels (4 marks).

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.A (b) [7 marks]**

**Given:** $R_b = 10$ kbps, $f_c = 1$ MHz, $A_c = 10$ V, $R = 50\,\Omega$, equiprobable bits.

**(i) Null-to-null bandwidth (2 marks):**

$$B_T = 2R_b = 2 \times 10 \times 10^{3} = 20 \text{ kHz}$$

**(ii) Average transmitted power (3 marks):**

$$P_{\text{avg}} = \frac{A_c^{2}}{4R} = \frac{(10)^{2}}{4 \times 50} = \frac{100}{200} = 0.5 \text{ W}$$

**(iii) Bit energy (2 marks):**

$$T_b = \frac{1}{R_b} = \frac{1}{10 \times 10^{3}} = 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 100 \,\mu\text{s}$$

$$E_b = P_{\text{avg}} \cdot T_b = 0.5 \times 10^{-4} = 5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ J} = 50 \,\mu\text{J}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the generation of BASK using a **product modulator** with a neat block diagram. State two advantages and two disadvantages of BASK. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a block schematic, explain the operation of a **coherent BASK detector**. Derive the expression for the bit error probability in an AWGN channel and show that the BER is $P_e = Q(\sqrt{E_b/2N_0})$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.B (a) [7 marks]**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** *(Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the product-modulator block diagram. Describe the role of each block as shown there.)*

The product modulator consists of:
1. A **local oscillator** generating the carrier $A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$.
2. A **balanced multiplier** (or an analog switch such as a MOSFET switch) that multiplies the carrier by the unipolar NRZ data $b(t)$.
3. A **bandpass filter** to suppress switching harmonics and a **PA** to raise power.

**Mathematical output:**

$$s(t) = b(t) \cdot A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Advantages (2 marks — any two):**
1. **Simplest modulation scheme** to implement — requires only a switch and an oscillator; no complex DSP.
2. **Lowest bandwidth** among binary schemes when used with NRZ ($B_T = 2R_b$).
3. **Low cost** — ideal for RFID tags, IR remotes, optical fibre links.

**Disadvantages (2 marks — any two):**
1. **Poor noise immunity** — high BER ($Q(\sqrt{E_b/2N_0})$) compared to BPSK.
2. **Non-constant envelope** — cannot use efficient non-linear (Class C) amplifiers; very susceptible to amplitude fading.
3. **Wasps bandwidth efficiency** — spectral efficiency is only 0.5 bit/s/Hz.

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.B (b) [7 marks]**

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** *(Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes. The coherent detector uses a phase-locked local oscillator, multiplier, integrator over $T_b$, and a threshold comparator.)*

**Derivation (5 marks):**

**Step 1 — Received signal in bit interval $k$ (1 mark):**

$$r(t) = s_k(t) + n(t) = \begin{cases} A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + n(t), & a_k = 1 \\ n(t), & a_k = 0 \end{cases}$$

where $n(t)$ is AWGN with PSD $N_0/2$.

**Step 2 — Correlator output (1 mark):**

$$z = \int_{kT_b}^{(k+1)T_b} r(t) \cdot \phi_1(t) \, dt, \quad \phi_1(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{T_b}} \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Step 3 — Decompose $z = s + n$ (1 mark):**

- If `1` sent: $s = \sqrt{E_b}$, $n \sim \mathcal{N}(0, N_0/2)$.
- If `0` sent: $s = 0$, $n \sim \mathcal{N}(0, N_0/2)$.

**Step 4 — Decision rule and error probability (2 marks):**

The optimum threshold is $\gamma = \sqrt{E_b}/2$ (for equiprobable bits). The probability of error under either hypothesis is:

$$P(e \mid 1) = P(e \mid 0) = Q\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{E_b} - 0}{\sqrt{2 \cdot (N_0/2)}}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_b}{2N_0}}\right)$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\,P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_b}{2N_0}}\right)\,}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 inside the Q-argument** — students often incorrectly write $P_e = Q(\sqrt{E_b/N_0})$ which is the BPSK result. ASK is **3 dB worse** than BPSK because half the time no energy is transmitted.
> 2. **Confusing $E_b$ with $E_s$** — in OOK, $E_b$ is the **average** energy per bit, not the energy during the `1` bit. During `1` bits the energy is $2E_b$ (for equiprobable bits).
> 3. **Forgetting to normalise to $1\,\Omega$** in power calculations — always state "assume $R = 1\,\Omega$" or include $R$ explicitly.
> 4. **Skipping the PSD sketch** — it carries 3–4 marks by itself. Always draw both **discrete lines at $\pm f_c$** and the **continuous $\text{sinc}^{2}$ lobes**.
> 5. **Not stating the bandpass property** — explicitly write "ASK is a bandpass modulation technique" because the examiner awards marks for the classification.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **ASK** = **A**mplitude **S**hift **K**eying — the **amplitude of the carrier is varied** in step with the binary data; **frequency and phase are constant**.
- [x] **Binary ASK (BASK / OOK)** is the most common form: $s(t) = A_c \, b(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)$, with $b(t) \in \lbrace 0, 1 \rbrace$.
- [x] **Time-domain waveform** of OOK is a **gated sinusoid** — carrier ON for `1`, OFF for `0`.
- [x] **Bandwidth:** $B_T = 2R_b$ Hz (twice the bit rate for NRZ pulses).
- [x] **Average power (equiprobable bits):** $P_{\text{avg}} = A_c^{2} / 4$ W (assuming $R = 1\,\Omega$).
- [x] **Bit energy:** $E_b = P_{\text{avg}} \cdot T_b$ Joules.
- [x] **BER (coherent OOK):** $P_e = Q(\sqrt{E_b / 2N_0})$ — **3 dB worse than BPSK**.
- [x] **BER (non-coherent OOK):** $P_e = \tfrac{1}{2} \exp(-E_b/2N_0)$ — envelope detection.
- [x] **Modulation index:** $m = 1$ always for OOK ($A_{\min} = 0$).
- [x] **PSD** consists of **two delta functions at $\pm f_c$** plus **two $\text{sinc}^{2}$ lobes** of width $2/T_b$ centred at $\pm f_c$.
- [x] **Spectral efficiency:** $\eta = R_b / B_T = 1/2$ bit/s/Hz (lowest among binary schemes).
- [x] **Generation methods:** Product modulator (multiplier + LO) and **on–off keying switch** (MOSFET/relay gating the carrier).
- [x] **Demodulation methods:** **Coherent** (synchronous detector with PLL) and **non-coherent** (envelope detector with diode + RC).
- [x] **Envelope property:** OOK has a **non-constant envelope** (zero during `0` bits) — **cannot use Class C amplifiers**.
- [x] **Key applications:** **IR remote controls** (38 kHz carrier), **RFID tags** (passive backscatter), **optical fibre digital links** (LED on/off), and **Aloha-net** packet radio.
- [x] **Comparison mantra:** ASK = **simple but noisy**; BPSK = **robust but complex**; FSK = **intermediate trade-off**.
- [x] **Always state three things in any KTU answer:** (1) the **time-domain equation**, (2) the **bandwidth formula**, and (3) the **BER formula** — these are the examiner's three checkpoints.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Phase Shift Keying
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET864 - ELECTRONIC COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Phase Shift Keying

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 PHASE SHIFT KEYING (PSK) — KTU 2024 SCHEME

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Phase Shift Keying (PSK)** is a **digital modulation technique** in which the **phase** of a constant-amplitude, constant-frequency carrier signal is varied in discrete steps in accordance with the digital baseband message signal, while keeping the **amplitude** and **frequency** of the carrier strictly constant.

Mathematically, a general PSK signal is expressed as:

$$s(t) = A_c \cos\bigl[2\pi f_c t + \phi_m(t)\bigr]$$

where:
- $A_c$ = Carrier amplitude (constant) — typically **A = √(2E_b/T_b)**
- $f_c$ = Carrier frequency (constant)
- $\phi_m(t)$ = Discrete phase term that carries the information, taking one of **M finite values** (M = 2 for BPSK, M = 4 for QPSK, M = 8 for 8-PSK, etc.)

For an M-ary PSK system, the discrete phase is:

$$\phi_m = \frac{2\pi (m-1)}{M}, \quad m = 1, 2, 3, \dots, M$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🧠

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of PSK like a clock hand on a wall clock.**
> - The **clock hand** is the carrier wave.
> - Instead of moving smoothly, the hand **jumps only to specific marked positions** (12 o'clock, 3 o'clock, 6 o'clock, 9 o'clock) — these are the **discrete phase states**.
> - Each marked position represents a unique **symbol** (a group of bits).
> - The observer (receiver) only needs to identify **which position the hand jumped to**, NOT how fast it moved. So the **information is encoded in the angle/position**, not the magnitude or the speed.

For example, in **BPSK (Binary PSK)**:
- Bit **'0'** → Carrier phase = **0°**
- Bit **'1'** → Carrier phase = **180°** (π radians)

The hand either points **up** (bit 0) or **down** (bit 1). Two clear positions — no confusion.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!TIP]
> **Standard KTU-Reported Metrics for PSK:**
> - **Bit Energy:** $E_b$ (Joules)
> - **Symbol Energy:** $E_s = E_b \cdot \log_2(M)$ (Joules)
> - **Bit Rate:** $R_b$ (bits/second)
> - **Symbol Rate:** $R_s = R_b / \log_2(M)$ (symbols/second or **Baud**)
> - **Bit Duration:** $T_b = 1/R_b$ (seconds)
> - **Symbol Duration:** $T_s = 1/R_s$ (seconds)
> - **Noise Power Spectral Density:** $N_0/2$ (Watts/Hz, two-sided)

### 1.4 Why PSK is Preferred Over ASK/FSK

| Property | ASK | FSK | PSK |
|----------|-----|-----|-----|
| Noise Immunity | **Low** | Medium | **High** |
| Bandwidth Efficiency | Medium | **Low** | **High** |
| Power Efficiency | Low | Medium | **High** |
| Constant Envelope | No | Yes | Yes |

> [!NOTE]
> Because PSK maintains a **constant envelope**, it is highly tolerant to **non-linear amplifiers** used in satellite and mobile transmitters — a key reason for its dominance in real-world digital communication systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How PSK Works

### Step-by-Step Logic Flow

1. **Baseband Input:** A binary (or M-ary) data stream enters the modulator.
2. **Bit-to-Phase Mapping:** Each group of $k = \log_2(M)$ bits is mapped to a discrete carrier phase $\phi_m$ from a predefined constellation.
3. **Carrier Phase Modulation:** The local oscillator generates $A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ and the phase-shifting network multiplies/alters the phase according to $\phi_m$.
4. **Transmission:** The modulated signal $s(t)$ is transmitted over the AWGN channel.
5. **Coherent Detection at Receiver:** The receiver uses a **phase-coherent local oscillator** (synchronized to the carrier) and computes the **correlation** of the received signal with reference sinusoids $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ and $\sin(2\pi f_c t)$.
6. **Decision Device:** The recovered phase is compared against the constellation; the nearest constellation point is selected, and the corresponding bit group is output.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Phase Coherence is the Achilles Heel of PSK.** Any phase ambiguity or synchronization error at the receiver directly causes **bit errors**. This is why **DPSK (Differential PSK)** is used in systems where carrier recovery is difficult.

## 2.2 Classification of PSK Schemes (KTU High-Yield)

| Scheme | M (Levels) | Bits/Symbol | Phase States | Key Use Case |
|--------|------------|-------------|--------------|-------------|
| **BPSK** | 2 | 1 | $0, \pi$ | Deep-space, low-data-rate telemetry |
| **QPSK** | 4 | 2 | $\pi/4, 3\pi/4, 5\pi/4, 7\pi/4$ | Satellite (DVB-S), CDMA, 4G |
| **OQPSK** | 4 | 2 | Same as QPSK (offset timing) | Mobile, satellite with non-linear amps |
| **π/4 QPSK** | 4 | 2 | 8 possible (rotated π/4 each symbol) | Cellular, Bluetooth |
| **8-PSK** | 8 | 3 | 8 phases at 45° spacing | High-bandwidth digital TV |
| **M-PSK** | M | $\log_2 M$ | $2\pi/M$ spacing | Bandwidth-limited links |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ⭐

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the EXACT formula cluster the KTU Board Examiner expects you to reproduce from memory for ESE questions on PSK.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | General M-PSK signal | $s_m(t) = A_c \cos\bigl[2\pi f_c t + \frac{2\pi(m-1)}{M}\bigr]$ | $m = 1, 2, \dots, M$ |
| 2 | BPSK signal | $s_m(t) = \pm \sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{T_b}} \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | $0 \le t \le T_b$ |
| 3 | QPSK in-phase component | $s_I(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{T_s}} \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | Orthogonal basis 1 |
| 4 | QPSK quadrature component | $s_Q(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{T_s}} \sin(2\pi f_c t)$ | Orthogonal basis 2 |
| 5 | Symbol energy relation | $E_s = E_b \cdot \log_2 M$ | Joules |
| 6 | BPSK BER (coherent) | $P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$ | Exact closed form |
| 7 | QPSK BER (coherent) | $P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$ | Same as BPSK (per-bit) |
| 8 | M-PSK symbol error | $P_s \approx 2\,Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{N_0}}\,\sin\!\frac{\pi}{M}\right)$ | Union bound for large SNR |
| 9 | M-PSK bit error (approx) | $P_b \approx \frac{2}{\log_2 M}\,Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{N_0}}\,\sin\!\frac{\pi}{M}\right)$ | Valid for M ≥ 4, Gray coding |
| 10 | BPSK bandwidth (main lobe) | $B = 2R_b$ | Hz (raised cosine roll-off) |
| 11 | M-PSK bandwidth | $B = \frac{2R_b}{\log_2 M} = 2R_s$ | Hz |
| 12 | Bandwidth efficiency | $\eta = \frac{R_b}{B} = \frac{\log_2 M}{2}$ | bits/sec/Hz |
| 13 | DPSK BER | $P_e = \frac{1}{2}\exp\!\left(-\frac{E_b}{N_0}\right)$ | Non-coherent detection |
| 14 | Minimum distance BPSK | $d_{\min} = 2\sqrt{E_b}$ | Euclidean distance between signals |
| 15 | Minimum distance M-PSK | $d_{\min} = 2\sqrt{E_s}\,\sin\!\frac{\pi}{M}$ | Decreases as M grows |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Satellite Communication (DVB-S2 / VSAT):** QPSK and 8-PSK dominate because of high power efficiency and robustness against satellite transponder non-linearities.
- **4G LTE & 5G NR:** QPSK, 16-QAM, 64-QAM, 256-QAM are used adaptively based on channel quality (Adaptive Modulation and Coding — AMC).
- **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11):** BPSK, QPSK at low SNR for reliable connection establishment.
- **Bluetooth (LE):** GFSK (close to PSK family) for low-power short-range links.
- **Deep Space Network (NASA):** BPSK remains the gold standard due to extreme noise immunity and unambiguous phase reference.

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb:** As **M increases** in M-PSK, the constellation points come **closer together** in phase space → $d_{\min}$ shrinks → **more errors** at the same SNR. So you trade **bandwidth efficiency** for **power efficiency**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: BPSK Signal Representation

**Starting Point:** The general M-PSK signal is:

$$s_m(t) = A_c \cos\!\left[2\pi f_c t + \frac{2\pi(m-1)}{M}\right], \quad 0 \le t \le T_s$$

**For BPSK (M = 2):** The two possible phases are $\phi_1 = 0$ and $\phi_2 = \pi$. So the two signals are:

$$s_1(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t), \quad 0 \le t \le T_b$$
$$s_2(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t + \pi) = -A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t), \quad 0 \le t \le T_b$$

**We can combine these using a binary antipodal signal** $b(t) \in \{+1, -1\}$ representing the data bit:

$$s(t) = b(t)\,A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t), \quad 0 \le t \le T_b$$

**To normalize the energy per bit to $E_b$**, we need:

$$E_b = \int_0^{T_b} s^2(t)\, dt = A_c^2 \int_0^{T_b} \cos^2(2\pi f_c t)\, dt$$

Assuming $f_c T_b \gg 1$ (many carrier cycles per bit), $\cos^2$ averages to $1/2$:

$$E_b = A_c^2 \cdot \frac{T_b}{2} \;\Rightarrow\; A_c = \sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{T_b}}$$

**Substituting back:**

$$s(t) = b(t)\,\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{T_b}}\,\cos(2\pi f_c t), \quad b(t) \in \{-1, +1\}$$

**Equivalent vector (in signal space) representation:** Using orthonormal basis $\phi_1(t) = \sqrt{2/T_b}\cos(2\pi f_c t)$:

$$\vec{s_1} = +\sqrt{E_b}, \quad \vec{s_2} = -\sqrt{E_b}$$

The Euclidean distance between the two constellation points is:

$$d_{\min} = \left\vert \sqrt{E_b} - (-\sqrt{E_b}) \right\vert = 2\sqrt{E_b}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation II: BPSK Bit Error Probability (BER)

**Received signal over AWGN channel:**

$$r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t), \quad i = 1, 2$$

where $n(t)$ is **AWGN** with two-sided PSD $N_0/2$.

**Optimal detector (Matched Filter / Correlator):** Project $r(t)$ onto $\phi_1(t)$:

$$r = \int_0^{T_b} r(t)\,\phi_1(t)\, dt = \pm\sqrt{E_b} + n$$

where $n$ is Gaussian with mean 0 and variance:

$$\sigma^2 = \mathrm{Var}(n) = \frac{N_0}{2}$$

(The matched filter output noise variance for a signal of unit energy over a channel with two-sided PSD $N_0/2$ is $N_0/2$.)

**Decision rule:** Choose $s_1$ if $r > 0$, else choose $s_2$. (Threshold = 0, exactly midway between the two antipodal points.)

**Probability of error** (assume $s_1$ transmitted — by symmetry the result is identical for $s_2$):

$$P_e = P(r < 0 \mid s_1 \text{ sent}) = P\!\left(\sqrt{E_b} + n < 0\right) = P\!\left(n < -\sqrt{E_b}\right)$$

Since $n \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, N_0/2)$:

$$P_e = \int_{-\infty}^{-\sqrt{E_b}} \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi N_0}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2}{N_0}\right) dx$$

**Substitute** $u = x/\sqrt{N_0/2}$:

$$P_e = \int_{-\infty}^{-\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{u^2}{2}\right) du = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final BPSK BER result:**
> $$P_{e,\text{BPSK}} = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$
> This is the **most important equation in the entire PSK module** for KTU exams.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III: QPSK Modulator Output (I/Q Decomposition)

**QPSK bit stream** is split into two bit streams: **In-phase bits** $b_I(t)$ and **Quadrature bits** $b_Q(t)$, each at half the original bit rate $R_b/2$.

The QPSK modulator multiplies each stream by an orthogonal carrier and sums them:

$$s_{\text{QPSK}}(t) = b_I(t)\,\sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{T_s}}\,\cos(2\pi f_c t) - b_Q(t)\,\sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{T_s}}\,\sin(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Compact form:**

$$s_{\text{QPSK}}(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{T_s}}\,\cos\!\left[2\pi f_c t + \phi_k\right]$$

where the four possible phases are:

$$\phi_k = (2k+1)\frac{\pi}{4}, \quad k = 0, 1, 2, 3$$

giving phases of $\pi/4,\, 3\pi/4,\, 5\pi/4,\, 7\pi/4$ (i.e., 45°, 135°, 225°, 315°).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why subtract the sine term?** The minus sign ensures that the constellation rotates in the correct mathematical sense (counter-clockwise as bits increase), aligning with the standard I-Q plane convention used in KTU textbooks.

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV: M-PSK Symbol Error Probability (Union Bound)

**Distance between adjacent constellation points** (for equally spaced phases on a circle of radius $\sqrt{E_s}$):

$$d_{\min} = 2\sqrt{E_s}\,\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi}{M}\right)$$

**Decision regions:** Voronoi regions around each constellation point occupy an angle of $\pm \pi/M$ around each phase. A symbol error occurs when the noise phasor pushes the received vector outside this wedge.

**For high SNR**, the dominant error mechanism is confusion between **adjacent** points (not across the entire circle). Using the standard Q-function result for a noise phasor:

$$P_s \approx 2\,Q\!\left(\frac{d_{\min}/2}{\sigma}\right) = 2\,Q\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{E_s}\,\sin(\pi/M)}{\sqrt{N_0/2}}\right)$$

**Simplifying:**

$$P_{s,\text{M-PSK}} \approx 2\,Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{N_0}}\,\sin\!\frac{\pi}{M}\right)$$

**Converting to bit error rate** (assuming Gray coding, where a symbol error corresponds to exactly **one** bit error):

$$P_{b,\text{M-PSK}} \approx \frac{2}{\log_2 M}\,Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{N_0}}\,\sin\!\frac{\pi}{M}\right)$$

> [!TIP]
> **Verify the BPSK limit:** Set $M=2$, so $E_s = E_b$, $\sin(\pi/2) = 1$, and $\log_2 2 = 1$:
> $$P_b = 2 \cdot Q\!\left(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}\right) \div 1$$
> But the BPSK formula we derived was $Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ — the factor of 2 difference arises because for M = 2 there is only **one** adjacent point (the other side), while the M-PSK formula over-counts. This is a classic KTU exam trap!

---

## 3.5 Derivation V: Power Spectral Density of BPSK

The PSD of a baseband NRZ random binary signal is:

$$S_{\text{NRZ}}(f) = T_b \,\mathrm{sinc}^2(fT_b) = T_b \left(\frac{\sin \pi f T_b}{\pi f T_b}\right)^2$$

When this modulates a carrier at $f_c$, the spectrum is shifted to $\pm f_c$ with amplitude scaling:

$$S_{\text{BPSK}}(f) = \frac{E_b}{2}\left[\mathrm{sinc}^2\bigl((f - f_c)T_b\bigr) + \mathrm{sinc}^2\bigl((f + f_c)T_b\bigr)\right]$$

**Main-lobe bandwidth** (null-to-null):

$$B_{\text{null-null}} = 2R_b$$

**99% power bandwidth** (raised cosine reference):

$$B_{99\%} \approx 1.6\,R_b$$

---

## 3.6 Numerical Example (Typical KTU Problem)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A BPSK system operates at a bit rate of $R_b = 1$ Mbps over an AWGN channel with $N_0 = 10^{-9}$ W/Hz. The received signal energy per bit is $E_b = 4 \times 10^{-6}$ J. Compute the BER.

**Solution:**

$$\frac{E_b}{N_0} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-6}}{10^{-9}} = 4000 \; (\text{linear}) = 36.02 \text{ dB}$$

$$\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}} = \sqrt{2 \times 4000} = \sqrt{8000} \approx 89.44$$

$$P_e = Q(89.44) \approx 0 \;(\text{effectively zero errors})$$

> [!TIP]
> **At $E_b/N_0 = 10$ dB, BPSK BER ≈ 3.87 × 10⁻⁶** — this is the **standard benchmark** KTU examiners use.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 BPSK Modulator — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Data Bits b(t)"] --> B["Level Shifter +-1"]
    B --> C["Multiplier X with Carrier"]
    D["Local Oscillator cos 2 pi fc t"] --> C
    C --> E["BPSK Output s t = b t A cos 2 pi fc t"]
    E --> F["AWGN Channel adds n t"]
    F --> G["BPSK Receiver Input r t"]

    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00
    style D fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4
    style E fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22
    style F fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C
```

## 4.2 BPSK Coherent Demodulator (Correlation Receiver)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R["Received r t"] --> C1["Multiplier with cos 2 pi fc t"]
    LO["Local Oscillator cos 2 pi fc t"] --> C1
    C1 --> INT1["Integrator over 0 to Tb"]
    INT1 --> S1["Sampler at t = Tb"]
    S1 --> D1["Decision Device threshold = 0"]
    D1 --> OUT1["Recovered Bit"]

    R2["Received r t"] --> C2["Multiplier with sin 2 pi fc t"]
    LO2["Local Osc sin 2 pi fc t"] --> C2
    C2 --> INT2["Integrator over 0 to Tb"]
    INT2 --> S2["Sampler at t = Tb"]
    S2 --> D2["Decision Q-channel"]

    style R fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C
    style LO fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4
    style D1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22
```

## 4.3 QPSK Modulator — I/Q Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN["Serial Data Rb bps"] --> SP["Serial to Parallel Converter"]
    SP --> I["Even Bits bI at Rb/2"]
    SP --> Q["Odd Bits bQ at Rb/2"]
    I --> NRZI["NRZ Level Shifter"]
    Q --> NRZQ["NRZ Level Shifter"]
    NRZI --> MI["Multiplier I-channel"]
    NRZQ --> MQ["Multiplier Q-channel"]
    OSC["LO cos 2 pi fc t"] --> MI
    OSC90["LO sin 2 pi fc t"] --> MQ
    MI --> SUM["Summing Junction"]
    MQ --> SUM
    SUM --> OUT["QPSK s t"]

    style IN fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00
    style OSC fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4
    style OSC90 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4
    style OUT fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22
    style SUM fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B
```

## 4.4 QPSK Constellation Diagram (Mermaid Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph QPSK_Constellation
        direction LR
        A1["Bit 00 phase pi/4 +45 deg"] --- ORIGIN1((Origin))
        A2["Bit 01 phase 3pi/4 +135 deg"] --- ORIGIN1
        A3["Bit 11 phase 5pi/4 -135 deg"] --- ORIGIN1
        A4["Bit 10 phase 7pi/4 -45 deg"] --- ORIGIN1
    end

    style A1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#FF6347
    style A2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#FF6347
    style A3 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#FF6347
    style A4 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#FF6347
    style ORIGIN1 fill:#000000,stroke:#000000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Bookwork:** QPSK constellation points are at 45°, 135°, 225°, 315° when Gray coding is used. Memorize the phase-to-bit mapping: $00 = 45°$, $01 = 135°$, $11 = 225°$, $10 = 315°$.

## 4.5 M-PSK Signal Constellation (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Binary Input Rb"] --> B["Group bits k = log2 M"]
    B --> C["Map to Phase phi m = 2 pi m minus 1 over M"]
    C --> D["Phase Shifter Network"]
    D --> E["Multiply with Carrier A cos 2 pi fc t"]
    E --> F["M-PSK Output s t"]

    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00
    style C fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4
    style F fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22
```

## 4.6 OQPSK vs QPSK — Block-Level Offset Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q["QPSK Architecture"] --> OQ["OQPSK Architecture"]
    Q -.Both have.-> I["In-phase Stream I"]
    Q -.Both have.-> QCH["Quadrature Stream Q"]
    OQ --> DELAY["Delay Q by Tb/2 offset"]
    DELAY --> ICH["Same Modulator Path"]
    ICH --> OUT["Output s t no 180 deg transitions"]

    style Q fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C
    style OQ fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4
    style DELAY fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B
    style OUT fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why OQPSK?** In standard QPSK, when the I and Q channels both transition simultaneously, the carrier phase can flip through **0°** (a 180° jump). This causes large **AM envelope fluctuation**, which is harmful with non-linear RF power amplifiers. **OQPSK** (Offset QPSK) delays the Q channel by half a symbol period, **eliminating simultaneous transitions** and limiting phase changes to **±90°**, keeping the envelope much more constant.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define Phase Shift Keying. List the main types of PSK and state one application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** PSK is a digital modulation scheme in which the phase of a constant-amplitude carrier is discretely varied according to the digital modulating signal, while amplitude and frequency remain fixed.
>
> **Types and applications (1.5 marks):**
> - **BPSK (Binary PSK):** Used in deep-space telemetry (e.g., NASA DSN) and low-SNR robust links.
> - **QPSK (Quadrature PSK):** Used in satellite TV (DVB-S), 4G LTE, and CDMA cellular systems.
> - **8-PSK:** Used in band-limited digital microwave links and DVB-S2.

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**Compare BPSK and QPSK in terms of bandwidth efficiency, bit error rate, and bandwidth requirement for the same bit rate $R_b$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | BPSK | QPSK |
|-----------|------|------|
| Bits per symbol | 1 | 2 |
| Bandwidth (Hz) | $2R_b$ | $R_b$ |
| Bandwidth efficiency | 0.5 bit/s/Hz | **1 bit/s/Hz** |
| BER expression | $Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ | $Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ — identical |
| Constellation points | 2 (antipodal) | 4 (quadrature) |

> [!TIP]
> **Conclusion (1 mark):** QPSK achieves **twice the bandwidth efficiency** of BPSK for the **same BER performance**, making it preferred for bandwidth-limited applications.

---

## 📝 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### 🔷 **Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]**

> **(a)** Derive an expression for the **bit error probability** of a coherent BPSK system operating over an AWGN channel. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** A QPSK system transmits at a bit rate of $R_b = 2$ Mbps over an AWGN channel with $E_b/N_0 = 10$ dB. Compute the **symbol energy, bandwidth, and bit error rate**. Compare QPSK and BPSK in terms of bandwidth efficiency. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Define BPSK signals (1 mark):**

$$s_1(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{T_b}}\,\cos(2\pi f_c t), \quad 0 \le t \le T_b \quad \text{(bit 0)}$$

$$s_2(t) = -\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{T_b}}\,\cos(2\pi f_c t), \quad 0 \le t \le T_b \quad \text{(bit 1)}$$

**Step 2 — Received signal model (1 mark):**

$$r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t), \quad i = 1, 2$$

where $n(t)$ is AWGN with two-sided PSD $N_0/2$.

**Step 3 — Correlation receiver output (1.5 marks):**

Using matched filter with basis $\phi(t) = \sqrt{2/T_b}\cos(2\pi f_c t)$:

$$r = \int_0^{T_b} r(t)\,\phi(t)\, dt = \pm\sqrt{E_b} + n_0$$

where $n_0 \sim \mathcal{N}(0, N_0/2)$.

**Step 4 — Decision rule and error probability (2 marks):**

Threshold = 0. Probability of error (assume $s_1$ sent):

$$P_e = P(r < 0 \mid s_1) = P\!\left(\sqrt{E_b} + n_0 < 0\right) = P\!\left(n_0 < -\sqrt{E_b}\right)$$

$$P_e = \int_{-\infty}^{-\sqrt{E_b}} \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi N_0}}\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2}{N_0}\right) dx$$

**Step 5 — Standard form (1.5 marks):**

Using the Q-function definition $Q(x) = \int_x^\infty \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-u^2/2} du$:

$$\boxed{P_{e,\text{BPSK}} = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)}$$

---

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Convert $E_b/N_0$ from dB to linear (1 mark):**

$$\frac{E_b}{N_0}\bigg|_{\text{linear}} = 10^{10/10} = 10$$

**Step 2 — Symbol energy (1 mark):**

For QPSK, $M = 4$, $\log_2 4 = 2$ bits/symbol:

$$E_s = E_b \cdot \log_2 M = 2 E_b$$

If $E_b/N_0 = 10$ (linear), then $E_s/N_0 = 2 \times 10 = 20$ (linear) = **13.01 dB**.

**Step 3 — Bandwidth (1 mark):**

$$R_s = R_b / \log_2 M = 2 \times 10^6 / 2 = 10^6 \text{ symbols/s}$$

$$B = 2 R_s = 2 \times 10^6 = \mathbf{2 \; \text{MHz}}$$

**Step 4 — Bit error rate (2 marks):**

$$P_{e,\text{QPSK}} = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{20}\right) = Q(4.472)$$

$$P_e \approx \mathbf{3.87 \times 10^{-6}}$$

**Step 5 — Bandwidth efficiency comparison (2 marks):**

| Scheme | Bit Rate | Bandwidth | Efficiency |
|--------|----------|-----------|------------|
| BPSK | 2 Mbps | 4 MHz | 0.5 bit/s/Hz |
| QPSK | 2 Mbps | 2 MHz | **1.0 bit/s/Hz** |

**Conclusion:** For the same bit rate, QPSK uses **half the bandwidth** of BPSK while achieving the **same BER**, doubling the bandwidth efficiency.

---

### 🔷 **Question B: [KTU University Exam - June 2023, Module 4]** (Internal Choice)

> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **generation and detection of QPSK** signals. Sketch the QPSK constellation diagram. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Explain **DPSK** with its modulator block diagram. Derive an expression for the BER of DPSK and compare it with coherent BPSK. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — QPSK Generation (3 marks):**

The serial binary stream of rate $R_b$ is split by a **Serial-to-Parallel converter** into two streams $b_I$ (even bits) and $b_Q$ (odd bits), each at rate $R_b/2$. The streams are level-shifted to $\pm 1$ and multiplied by orthogonal carriers $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ and $\sin(2\pi f_c t)$ respectively. The two products are summed to produce:

$$s_{\text{QPSK}}(t) = b_I(t)\sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{T_s}}\cos(2\pi f_c t) - b_Q(t)\sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{T_s}}\sin(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Step 2 — QPSK Detection (2.5 marks):**

The received QPSK signal is correlated with both $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ and $\sin(2\pi f_c t)$ over a symbol period $T_s$. The correlator outputs are sampled at $t = T_s$, and decision devices (threshold = 0) recover $b_I$ and $b_Q$. A **parallel-to-serial** converter reconstructs the original stream. **Carrier synchronization** is required for coherent detection.

**Step 3 — Constellation (1.5 marks):**

Four points on a circle of radius $\sqrt{E_s}$ at angles $45°, 135°, 225°, 315°$ (Gray-coded as $00, 01, 11, 10$).

---

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — DPSK Concept (1.5 marks):**

In DPSK, the information is conveyed by the **phase difference between successive symbols**, NOT the absolute phase. This eliminates the need for a coherent reference at the receiver.

**Step 2 — DPSK Modulator (1.5 marks):**

- Incoming bit $b_k$ is XORed with the **delayed** previous bit $b_{k-1}$ to form the differentially encoded bit $d_k = b_k \oplus b_{k-1}$.
- The encoded bit $d_k$ modulates a BPSK carrier, producing phase transitions of $0$ or $\pi$ as required.

**Step 3 — DPSK Demodulator (1 mark):**

The received signal in the current bit interval is multiplied by the **delayed received signal** from the previous bit interval. The product is integrated, and the polarity of the integrator output determines the recovered bit. This is **non-coherent** detection — no carrier reference needed.

**Step 4 — BER of DPSK (2 marks):**

For DPSK over AWGN:

$$\boxed{P_{e,\text{DPSK}} = \frac{1}{2}\exp\!\left(-\frac{E_b}{N_0}\right)}$$

**Step 5 — Comparison with BPSK (1 mark):**

- **BPSK (coherent):** $P_e = Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ — requires carrier synchronization
- **DPSK (non-coherent):** $P_e = \tfrac{1}{2}\exp(-E_b/N_0)$ — slightly worse BER at high SNR but **3 dB simpler** receiver

At $P_e = 10^{-5}$: BPSK needs $E_b/N_0 \approx 9.6$ dB, while DPSK needs $E_b/N_0 \approx 10.3$ dB — a **~0.7 dB penalty** for the simpler receiver.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in PSK Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $E_b$ and $E_s$:** Many students write $P_e = Q(\sqrt{2E_s/N_0})$ for BPSK instead of $Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$. For BPSK, $E_s = E_b$, so this may coincidentally give a correct numerical answer — but **the formula must be stated in terms of $E_b$** because KTU uses $E_b$ as the standard reference. **(Lose 1 mark)**
>
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in M-PSK symbol error:** The general M-PSK formula has a factor of 2 from "two adjacent points." For $M=2$ (BPSK), the union bound is loose and gives $2Q(\cdot)$ instead of $Q(\cdot)$. **(Lose 0.5–1 mark)**
>
> 3. **Sign error in QPSK I/Q modulation:** Many students write $+$ instead of $-$ before the $b_Q(t)\sin$ term, giving a mirrored (clockwise vs counter-clockwise) constellation. **(Lose 0.5 mark)**
>
> 4. **Confusing OQPSK and $\pi/4$-QPSK:** OQPSK uses the **same phases as QPSK** (45°, 135°, 225°, 315°) but with **time offset**. $\pi/4$-QPSK **rotates the constellation by 45° every symbol** so transitions never pass through 0°. **(Lose 1 mark)**
>
> 5. **DPSK formula must be written with $\exp(-E_b/N_0)$, NOT $E_b/2N_0$:** This is a single-energy-bit (not half) non-coherent reference. **(Lose 1 mark)**
>
> 6. **Not specifying the channel:** KTU expects "**over an AWGN channel**" in every BER derivation. A bare formula without the channel context loses **0.5 mark**.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — PSK Module**
>
> ✅ **BPSK** has **2** antipodal constellation points; phase shift = **180°**; BER = $Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$
>
> ✅ **QPSK** has **4** points at 45° spacing; **2 bits/symbol**; bandwidth = $R_b/2$ for given $R_b$; same per-bit BER as BPSK
>
> ✅ **Bandwidth efficiency** doubles from BPSK (0.5) → QPSK (1.0) → 8-PSK (1.5) bits/s/Hz
>
> ✅ **M-PSK symbol error** ≈ $2Q\!\left(\sqrt{2E_s/N_0}\,\sin(\pi/M)\right)$
>
> ✅ **DPSK** is **non-coherent**; BER = $\tfrac{1}{2}\exp(-E_b/N_0)$; uses phase **differences**; ~0.7 dB worse than BPSK at $P_e = 10^{-5}$
>
> ✅ **OQPSK** delays Q-channel by $T_b/2$; eliminates 180° phase jumps; envelope variation reduced
>
> ✅ **$\pi/4$-QPSK** rotates constellation by $\pi/4$ each symbol; useful in **differential detection** mobile systems (USDC, IS-54)
>
> ✅ **Minimum Euclidean distance** for M-PSK: $d_{\min} = 2\sqrt{E_s}\sin(\pi/M)$ — **decreases** with M
>
> ✅ **Carrier recovery** is mandatory for **coherent** detection; DPSK is the answer when this is impractical
>
> ✅ **Gray coding** is the standard bit-to-symbol mapping convention — adjacent constellation points differ by **only 1 bit**
>
> ✅ **Constant envelope** of PSK is its **biggest advantage** for satellite and mobile non-linear amplifier systems
>
> ✅ **KTU favorite exam topic:** "**Derive BPSK BER over AWGN**" — appears in almost every End-Semester exam paper
>
> ✅ **Standard numerical benchmark:** At $E_b/N_0 = 10$ dB, BPSK/QPSK BER ≈ $3.87 \times 10^{-6}$

---
