topics.title: Frequency Shift Keying
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET864 - ELECTRONIC COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Frequency Shift Keying

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Frequency Shift Keying (FSK)** is a digital frequency modulation technique in which the frequency of a sinusoidal carrier wave is discretely varied (shifted) between two or more predefined frequencies in accordance with the binary (or M-ary) digital baseband message signal, while the **amplitude** and **phase** of the carrier are held constant.

In its most common binary form (BFSK), the digital information bit stream $m(t) \in \{0, 1\}$ selects one of two carrier frequencies:

- **Mark frequency ($f_1$ or $f_H$):** transmitted for binary '1' (logic HIGH).
- **Space frequency ($f_0$ or $f_L$):** transmitted for binary '0' (logic LOW).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** FSK is classified under *Angle Modulation* (a sub-category of digital passband modulation), since only the **angle (frequency)** of the carrier varies — *not* the amplitude. This is a frequent two-mark short-answer question in ESE Module-end assessments.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "Two Walkie-Talkies on the Same Channel"**
> 
> Imagine you and your friend are communicating using a walkie-talkie. You agree that whenever you want to say "YES", you press button A and transmit on **Channel 1 (say 150 MHz)**. Whenever you want to say "NO", you press button B and transmit on **Channel 2 (say 154 MHz)**. The receiver simply watches which channel is active and decodes the message.
> 
> This is *exactly* how FSK works — the **information** (YES/NO, 1/0) is encoded in the **frequency** of the transmitted carrier, not in its amplitude or phase. As long as noise doesn't push the frequency by a huge amount, the receiver can cleanly separate the two states.

### 1.3 Why Use FSK? (Engineering Motivation)

| Property | Engineering Benefit |
|----------|--------------------|
| **Constant envelope** | Highly resilient to amplitude noise and non-linear amplifiers (used in legacy modems, RF, satellite) |
| **Simple receiver design** | Non-coherent envelope detection possible (cheaper hardware) |
| **Robust to channel fading** | Frequency diversity exploits frequency-selective fading |
| **Easy generation** | Only a VCO or two oscillators needed (historically simpler than PSK) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trade-off:** FSK requires **larger bandwidth** than PSK for the same bit rate — this is a classic KTU 2-mark conceptual question.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mathematical Structure of BFSK Signal

The BFSK signal $s(t)$ over one bit interval $0 \le t \le T_b$ is expressed as:

$$
s_i(t) = \begin{cases} s_1(t) = \sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{T_b}} \cos(2\pi f_1 t + \phi_1), & \text{for bit } '1' \text{ (i.e., } i=1) \\[8pt] s_2(t) = \sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{T_b}} \cos(2\pi f_2 t + \phi_2), & \text{for bit } '0' \text{ (i.e., } i=2) \end{cases}
$$

where:
- $E_b$ = Energy per bit (Joules)
- $T_b$ = Bit duration (seconds)
- $f_1, f_2$ = Two distinct carrier frequencies
- $\phi_1, \phi_2$ = Carrier phases (often set to 0 for simplicity)

We can re-express in a **symmetric form** using a center (nominal) carrier frequency $f_c$ and a frequency deviation $\Delta f$:

$$
f_1 = f_c + \Delta f, \qquad f_2 = f_c - \Delta f
$$

The frequency separation is therefore $\vert f_1 - f_2 \vert = 2\Delta f$.

### 2.2 Condition for Orthogonality (CRITICAL for KTU)

Two FSK signals are **orthogonal** over a bit interval $T_b$ if:

$$
\int_0^{T_b} s_1(t)\, s_2(t)\, dt = 0
$$

This gives the **minimum frequency separation**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Coherent (phase-aligned) FSK:} \quad & \vert f_1 - f_2 \vert = \dfrac{n}{T_b}, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots \\[4pt]
\text{Non-coherent (random phase) FSK:} \quad & \vert f_1 - f_2 \vert = \dfrac{n}{2T_b}, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap:** Students often write $\vert f_1 - f_2 \vert = 1/T_b$ for BFSK without specifying **coherent** vs **non-coherent**. The examiner deducts 1 mark for this omission. Always qualify the answer.

### 2.3 Bandwidth of BFSK (Carson's Rule)

Applying **Carson's rule** to FSK (treating it as analog FM with peak deviation $\Delta f$ and message bandwidth $f_m = 1/T_b$):

$$
\text{BW} = 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2\Delta f + \dfrac{1}{T_b}
$$

For the **minimum-bandwidth orthogonal FSK** (coherent, $n=1$):

$$
\boxed{\text{BW}_{\text{min}} = \dfrac{3}{T_b} = 3 R_b \quad \text{(where } R_b = 1/T_b \text{ is the bit rate)}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This explains why BFSK needs *more* bandwidth than BPSK ($\approx 2R_b$) — the frequency spectrum has two lobes.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-survival cheat sheet** for all FSK numerical problems in KTU ESE.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | BFSK Signal (bit '1') | $s_1(t) = \sqrt{2E_b/T_b}\cos(2\pi f_1 t)$ | Volts | Logic HIGH |
| 2 | BFSK Signal (bit '0') | $s_2(t) = \sqrt{2E_b/T_b}\cos(2\pi f_2 t)$ | Volts | Logic LOW |
| 3 | Nominal Carrier Freq. | $f_c = (f_1 + f_2)/2$ | Hz | Center of spectrum |
| 4 | Frequency Deviation | $\Delta f = \vert f_1 - f_2 \vert / 2$ | Hz | Half separation |
| 5 | Modulation Index (FSK) | $h = \vert f_1 - f_2 \vert \cdot T_b = 2\Delta f \cdot T_b$ | Dimensionless | Sunde's FSK index |
| 6 | Min. Sep. (Coherent) | $\vert f_1 - f_2 \vert = 1/T_b$ | Hz | Orthogonal |
| 7 | Min. Sep. (Non-coherent) | $\vert f_1 - f_2 \vert = 1/(2T_b)$ | Hz | Orthogonal |
| 8 | BFSK Bandwidth (Carson) | $\text{BW} = 2(\Delta f + 1/T_b)$ | Hz | Carson's Rule |
| 9 | BFSK Min. Bandwidth | $\text{BW}_{\text{min}} = 3/T_b$ | Hz | For min-sep. coherent |
| 10 | BER — Coherent BFSK | $P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{E_b}{N_0}}\right) = \dfrac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{E_b}{2N_0}}\right)$ | — | AWGN channel |
| 11 | BER — Non-coherent BFSK | $P_e = \dfrac{1}{2}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_b}{2N_0}\right)$ | — | AWGN channel |
| 12 | M-ary FSK Bandwidth | $\text{BW} = \left(\dfrac{M-1}{2} + 1\right) \cdot \dfrac{1}{T_s}$ | Hz | Approximate |
| 13 | M-ary FSK Symbol Energy | $E_s = E_b \log_2 M$ | Joules | Bit energy relation |

### 2.5 Engineering Utility of FSK

- **Legacy data modems** (Bell 202 modem: 1200 bps FSK on telephone lines).
- **Caller ID (CID)** signalling in PSTN.
- **Amateur radio packet** (AFSK — Audio FSK on SSB).
- **Wireless telemetry** in industrial IoT (e.g., simple RF modules like 433 MHz TX/RX).
- **GSM signalling (GMSK is a refined FSK variant).**
- **DTMF** — although technically two-tone, conceptually related to multi-frequency keying.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Orthogonality Condition for Coherent BFSK

**Goal:** Show that for minimum bandwidth, the frequency separation must be $\vert f_1 - f_2 \vert = 1/T_b$.

**Step 1.** Write the inner product over the bit interval $T_b$:

$$
I = \int_0^{T_b} s_1(t)\, s_2(t)\, dt
$$

**Step 2.** Substitute the BFSK signals (assuming $\phi_1 = \phi_2 = 0$ for coherent case):

$$
I = \int_0^{T_b} \sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{T_b}}\cos(2\pi f_1 t) \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{T_b}}\cos(2\pi f_2 t)\, dt
$$

**Step 3.** Pull out the constant factor:

$$
I = \dfrac{2E_b}{T_b}\int_0^{T_b} \cos(2\pi f_1 t)\cos(2\pi f_2 t)\, dt
$$

**Step 4.** Apply the trigonometric product-to-sum identity:

$$
\cos A \cos B = \dfrac{1}{2}\bigl[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)\bigr]
$$

**Step 5.** Substitute $A = 2\pi f_1 t$ and $B = 2\pi f_2 t$:

$$
I = \dfrac{E_b}{T_b}\int_0^{T_b} \Bigl[\cos\bigl(2\pi(f_1-f_2)t\bigr) + \cos\bigl(2\pi(f_1+f_2)t\bigr)\Bigr]\, dt
$$

**Step 6.** Integrate each term separately:

$$
I = \dfrac{E_b}{T_b}\left[\dfrac{\sin(2\pi(f_1-f_2)t)}{2\pi(f_1-f_2)} + \dfrac{\sin(2\pi(f_1+f_2)t)}{2\pi(f_1+f_2)}\right]_0^{T_b}
$$

**Step 7.** Evaluate the upper limit $t = T_b$:

$$
I = \dfrac{E_b}{T_b}\left[\dfrac{\sin(2\pi(f_1-f_2)T_b)}{2\pi(f_1-f_2)} + \dfrac{\sin(2\pi(f_1+f_2)T_b)}{2\pi(f_1+f_2)}\right]
$$

**Step 8.** Impose orthogonality $I = 0$. Since $f_1 + f_2$ is typically very large, the second term averages to zero rapidly. The dominant condition becomes:

$$
\sin\bigl(2\pi(f_1-f_2)T_b\bigr) = 0
$$

**Step 9.** The sine vanishes when its argument is an integer multiple of $\pi$:

$$
2\pi(f_1-f_2)T_b = n\pi \;\Longrightarrow\; \boxed{\vert f_1 - f_2 \vert = \dfrac{n}{T_b}, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots}
$$

For minimum bandwidth, choose $n=1$. **Result derived.** $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: BFSK Transmitted Power

The average transmitted power over a long bit stream (assuming equal probability of 0 and 1) is:

$$
P_t = \dfrac{1}{T_b}\int_0^{T_b} s_i^2(t)\, dt = \dfrac{1}{T_b}\int_0^{T_b} \dfrac{2E_b}{T_b}\cos^2(2\pi f_i t)\, dt
$$

Using $\cos^2\theta = (1 + \cos 2\theta)/2$:

$$
P_t = \dfrac{1}{T_b} \cdot \dfrac{2E_b}{T_b} \cdot \dfrac{T_b}{2} = \dfrac{E_b}{T_b}
$$

Since $R_b = 1/T_b$, we have the **fundamental relation**:

$$
\boxed{E_b = P_t \cdot T_b = \dfrac{P_t}{R_b}}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Bandwidth via Carson's Rule

For FSK, the modulation index is $h = 2\Delta f \cdot T_b$. The instantaneous frequency swings between $f_c - \Delta f$ and $f_c + \Delta f$, i.e., a total swing of $2\Delta f$. By Carson's rule for an FM signal:

$$
\text{BW} = 2(\Delta f + f_m)
$$

where $f_m$ is the highest baseband frequency. For a rectangular NRZ bit stream, the dominant spectral component is at $f_m = 1/(2T_b)$ for a single tone, but for the worst-case **two-tone** interpretation of FSK (one of two sinusoids switching on/off), the effective modulating frequency is $f_m = 1/T_b$. Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\text{BW}_{\text{BFSK}} = 2\Delta f + \dfrac{1}{T_b}}
$$

For minimum-separation coherent FSK ($2\Delta f = 1/T_b$):

$$
\text{BW}_{\text{min}} = \dfrac{1}{T_b} + \dfrac{1}{T_b} = \dfrac{2}{T_b} \; \text{(spectrum of each tone)}
$$

Adding the spacing, the **occupied bandwidth** with both tones is:

$$
\boxed{\text{BW}_{\text{occupied}} = \dfrac{3}{T_b} = 3R_b}
$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4: BER of Coherent BFSK (Sketch)

In AWGN with two-sided PSD $N_0/2$, the matched filter outputs for the two hypotheses $H_1$ (bit 1 sent) and $H_0$ (bit 0 sent) are Gaussian with means $\pm\sqrt{E_b}$ and variance $N_0/2$. The probability of error is:

$$
P_e = P(1)\,P(D_0 \mid 1) + P(0)\,P(D_1 \mid 0)
$$

With $P(1) = P(0) = 0.5$ and symmetric decision threshold:

$$
P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right) \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} + \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{P_e^{\text{coh BFSK}} = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{E_b}{N_0}}\right)}
$$

where the standard Q-function is:

$$
Q(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\int_x^{\infty} e^{-u^2/2}\, du
$$

### 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **This comparison table is a favourite 7-mark KTU question.**

| Parameter | BPSK | QPSK | BFSK (Coherent) | BFSK (Non-coherent) |
|-----------|------|------|-----------------|---------------------|
| BER ($P_e$) | $Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ | $Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ | $Q(\sqrt{E_b/N_0})$ | $\tfrac{1}{2}e^{-E_b/2N_0}$ |
| Min. Bandwidth | $2R_b$ | $R_b$ | $3R_b$ | $3R_b$ |
| Power Efficiency | **Best** (3 dB better) | Same as BPSK | 3 dB worse than BPSK | Worst |
| Bandwidth Efficiency | Moderate | **Best** | Worst | Worst |
| Receiver Complexity | Coherent only | Coherent only | Coherent | **Simple (envelope)** |
| Constant Envelope | No | No | **Yes** | **Yes** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 BFSK Transmitter Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeBit["Binary Bit Stream m of t"] --> nodeEnc["NRZ Encoder"]
    nodeEnc --> nodeSwitch["Frequency Selection Switch"]
    nodeOsc1["Oscillator f1 Mark"] --> nodeSwitch
    nodeOsc2["Oscillator f2 Space"] --> nodeSwitch
    nodeSwitch --> nodePA["Power Amplifier"]
    nodePA --> nodeAnt["Antenna"]
    nodeAnt --> nodeChan["AWGN Channel"]
    nodeChan --> nodeRx["Receiver Front End"]

    style nodeBit fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#333
    style nodeEnc fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#333
    style nodeSwitch fill:#d4edda,stroke:#333
    style nodeOsc1 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#333
    style nodeOsc2 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#333
    style nodePA fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#333
    style nodeAnt fill:#e2d4f0,stroke:#333
    style nodeChan fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#333
    style nodeRx fill:#f5c6cb,stroke:#333
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The binary bit stream is NRZ-encoded and routed via an electronic switch that toggles between two crystal-controlled oscillators (one at $f_1$, the other at $f_2$). The selected tone is amplified and radiated.

### 4.2 BFSK Coherent Receiver Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeRxIn["Received r of t"] --> nodeBPF1["BPF Centered at f1"]
    nodeRxIn --> nodeBPF2["BPF Centered at f2"]
    nodeBPF1 --> nodeMix1["Mixer with Local Osc f1"]
    nodeBPF2 --> nodeMix2["Mixer with Local Osc f2"]
    nodeMix1 --> nodeInt1["Integrator 0 to Tb"]
    nodeMix2 --> nodeInt2["Integrator 0 to Tb"]
    nodeInt1 --> nodeDec["Decision Device Threshold 0"]
    nodeInt2 --> nodeDec
    nodeDec --> nodeOut["Recovered Bit Stream m hat of t"]

    style nodeRxIn fill:#f5c6cb,stroke:#333
    style nodeBPF1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#333
    style nodeBPF2 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#333
    style nodeMix1 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#333
    style nodeMix2 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#333
    style nodeInt1 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#333
    style nodeInt2 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#333
    style nodeDec fill:#e2d4f0,stroke:#333
    style nodeOut fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#333
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Implementation Note:** Two bandpass filters (centered at $f_1$ and $f_2$) isolate the two possible tones. Each branch is coherently demodulated by mixing with a locally generated reference, then integrated over $T_b$. A comparator decides '1' if the $f_1$-branch output exceeds the $f_2$-branch output, else '0'.

### 4.3 BFSK Non-Coherent Receiver (Envelope Detection)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeIn2["Received r of t"] --> nodeBPF1a["BPF at f1"]
    nodeIn2 --> nodeBPF2a["BPF at f2"]
    nodeBPF1a --> nodeEnv1["Envelope Detector"]
    nodeBPF2a --> nodeEnv2["Envelope Detector"]
    nodeEnv1 --> nodeSamp1["Sample at t equals Tb"]
    nodeEnv2 --> nodeSamp2["Sample at t equals Tb"]
    nodeSamp1 --> nodeComp2["Comparator Threshold"]
    nodeSamp2 --> nodeComp2
    nodeComp2 --> nodeOut2["Recovered Bits"]

    style nodeIn2 fill:#f5c6cb,stroke:#333
    style nodeBPF1a fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#333
    style nodeBPF2a fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#333
    style nodeEnv1 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#333
    style nodeEnv2 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#333
    style nodeSamp1 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#333
    style nodeSamp2 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#333
    style nodeComp2 fill:#e2d4f0,stroke:#333
    style nodeOut2 fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#333
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why non-coherent?** No need for a phase-locked loop or local oscillator — envelope detection (diode + RC + sample-and-hold) is sufficient. This is the receiver of choice for low-cost FSK systems (pagers, RFID, simple RF links).

### 4.4 Time-Domain Waveform Visualization (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Binary Input 1 0 1 1 0"] --> nodeB["BFSK Output High Freq for 1 Low Freq for 0"]
    nodeA2["0 1 0 1 0 1"] --> nodeB2["Continuous Phase FSK CPFSK Smoother Transitions"]

    style nodeA fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#333
    style nodeA2 fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#333
    style nodeB fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#333
    style nodeB2 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#333
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Discontinuous-Phase FSK vs CPFSK:** The basic "switch between two oscillators" FSK has abrupt phase discontinuities at bit boundaries, creating high out-of-band sidelobes. **Continuous-Phase FSK (CPFSK)** and its special case **MSK (Minimum Shift Keying)** — where $h = 0.5$ — eliminate this, making them spectrally efficient. KTU Module 4 may extend to MSK in subsequent topics.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model Question]`

**Define Frequency Shift Keying (FSK). What are the two parameters of the carrier that remain constant in FSK?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Frequency Shift Keying (FSK) is a digital modulation scheme in which the frequency of the sinusoidal carrier is shifted between two discrete values, $f_1$ and $f_2$, in accordance with the binary information bits '1' and '0' respectively.

In FSK, the two parameters of the carrier that **remain constant** are:

1. **Amplitude** — held at a fixed value $\sqrt{2E_b/T_b}$.
2. **Phase** — held constant (typically at 0) for the duration of each bit (in basic BFSK).

Only the instantaneous **frequency** is varied. (3 marks)

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Question]`

**State and explain the minimum frequency separation condition for orthogonal BFSK in the coherent case.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For two BFSK signals to be orthogonal (i.e., to give zero cross-correlation at the receiver), the minimum frequency separation between $f_1$ and $f_2$ for the **coherent** case must be:

$$
\vert f_1 - f_2 \vert = \dfrac{1}{T_b}
$$

where $T_b$ is the bit duration.

**Derivation (sketch):** Setting the cross-correlation integral
$\int_0^{T_b} \cos(2\pi f_1 t)\cos(2\pi f_2 t)\,dt = 0$
yields $\sin(2\pi(f_1-f_2)T_b) = 0$, which gives $(f_1-f_2) = n/T_b$. Choosing the minimum $n=1$ produces the above result. This condition is essential for matched filter / correlator-based detection to achieve minimum error probability. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### ⭐ **Question A (14 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper Pattern]`

**A.** Derive the mathematical expression for a Binary Frequency Shift Keying (BFSK) signal. Also, derive the minimum frequency separation required for orthogonality in coherent BFSK. **(7 marks)**

**B.** A BFSK system transmits data at $R_b = 10$ kbps over an AWGN channel with $E_b/N_0 = 10$ dB. Calculate:
- (i) the minimum bandwidth of the BFSK signal.
- (ii) the probability of bit error for **coherent** detection.
- (iii) the probability of bit error for **non-coherent** detection.

Use $Q(\sqrt{10}) \approx 7.83 \times 10^{-4}$ and $e^{-5} \approx 6.74 \times 10^{-3}$. **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ **Model Solution — Question A**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** The BFSK signal in a bit interval $0 \le t \le T_b$ is:

$$
s_i(t) = \begin{cases} s_1(t) = \sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{T_b}}\cos(2\pi f_1 t), & \text{for bit '1'} \\[6pt] s_2(t) = \sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{T_b}}\cos(2\pi f_2 t), & \text{for bit '0'} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Express the frequencies in terms of a nominal carrier $f_c$ and deviation $\Delta f$:

$$
f_1 = f_c + \Delta f, \qquad f_2 = f_c - \Delta f
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Orthogonality condition — the cross-correlation between $s_1(t)$ and $s_2(t)$ over $[0, T_b]$ must be zero:

$$
\rho_{12} = \int_0^{T_b} s_1(t)\, s_2(t)\, dt = 0
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Substituting and applying the product-to-sum identity:

$$
\rho_{12} = \dfrac{E_b}{T_b}\int_0^{T_b}\left[\cos\bigl(2\pi(f_1-f_2)t\bigr) + \cos\bigl(2\pi(f_1+f_2)t\bigr)\right]dt
$$

Evaluating gives:

$$
\rho_{12} = \dfrac{E_b}{2\pi T_b}\left[\dfrac{\sin(2\pi(f_1-f_2)T_b)}{f_1-f_2} + \dfrac{\sin(2\pi(f_1+f_2)T_b)}{f_1+f_2}\right]
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** The high-frequency term averages to zero. Setting the first term to zero:

$$
\sin\bigl(2\pi(f_1-f_2)T_b\bigr) = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; \boxed{\vert f_1 - f_2 \vert = \dfrac{n}{T_b}, \quad n \in \mathbb{Z}^+}
$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Minimum bandwidth case: $n=1$ gives the **minimum frequency separation**:

$$
\boxed{\vert f_1 - f_2 \vert_{\min} = \dfrac{1}{T_b}}
$$

This is the minimum coherent orthogonal separation.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:** $R_b = 10\,\text{kbps} = 10^4$ bps, so $T_b = 1/R_b = 10^{-4}$ s. $E_b/N_0 = 10$ dB $= 10$ (linear).

**(i) Minimum bandwidth (2 marks):**

$$
\text{BW}_{\min} = \dfrac{3}{T_b} = 3 R_b = 3 \times 10^4\,\text{Hz} = \boxed{30\,\text{kHz}}
$$

**(ii) Coherent BER (2.5 marks):**

$$
P_e^{\text{coh}} = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{E_b}{N_0}}\right) = Q(\sqrt{10}) \approx \boxed{7.83 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

**(iii) Non-coherent BER (2.5 marks):**

$$
P_e^{\text{non-coh}} = \dfrac{1}{2}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_b}{2N_0}\right) = \dfrac{1}{2}\,e^{-5} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 6.74 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
P_e^{\text{non-coh}} \approx \boxed{3.37 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

**Observation (bonus understanding):** The non-coherent detector is $\sim 4.3\times$ worse in BER at this $E_b/N_0$, justifying the $\sim 0.5$–1 dB SNR penalty of envelope detection.

---

### ⭐ **Question B (14 Marks)** [Alternative Choice]

`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]`

**A.** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the generation and **coherent detection** of a BFSK signal. Derive the bit error probability for coherent BFSK in AWGN. **(7 marks)**

**B.** Compare BFSK with BPSK in terms of bandwidth, BER performance, and receiver complexity. A system uses BFSK with $f_1 = 100$ kHz and $f_2 = 104$ kHz. The bit rate is 2 kbps. Determine:
- (i) the modulation index $h$.
- (ii) the nominal carrier frequency $f_c$.
- (iii) the frequency deviation $\Delta f$.
- (iv) whether the signals are orthogonal. **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ **Model Solution — Question B**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Generation (2 marks):** A BFSK transmitter consists of two oscillators (at $f_1$ and $f_2$), an electronic switch controlled by the binary data, a summing amplifier, and a power amplifier feeding the antenna. (See transmitter block diagram in SECTION 4.1.)

**Coherent Detection (3 marks):** The received signal is split into two branches. Each branch contains:
1. A **bandpass filter** centered at $f_1$ (or $f_2$) with bandwidth $\sim 1/T_b$.
2. A **coherent demodulator** (multiplier) using a locally generated reference at the same frequency.
3. An **integrator** (or matched filter) over $[0, T_b]$.
4. The integrator outputs are compared by a **decision device**; the larger value determines the bit. (See coherent receiver diagram in SECTION 4.2.)

**BER Derivation (2 marks):** Under AWGN, the matched filter output for hypothesis $H_i$ is Gaussian with mean $\pm\sqrt{E_b}$ (variance $N_0/2$). For equiprobable bits, the error probability is:

$$
P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{E_b}{N_0}}\right) = \dfrac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{E_b}{2N_0}}\right)
$$

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Comparison table (2 marks):** Already presented in the analytical matrix in SECTION 3.5.

**Numerical problem (5 marks):**

Given: $f_1 = 100$ kHz, $f_2 = 104$ kHz, $R_b = 2$ kbps $\Rightarrow T_b = 1/2000 = 0.5$ ms.

**(i) Modulation index (1.5 marks):**

$$
h = \vert f_1 - f_2 \vert \cdot T_b = 4000 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3} = \boxed{2}
$$

**(ii) Nominal carrier (1 mark):**

$$
f_c = \dfrac{f_1 + f_2}{2} = \dfrac{100 + 104}{2} = \boxed{102\,\text{kHz}}
$$

**(iii) Frequency deviation (1.5 marks):**

$$
\Delta f = \dfrac{\vert f_1 - f_2 \vert}{2} = \dfrac{4\,\text{kHz}}{2} = \boxed{2\,\text{kHz}}
$$

**(iv) Orthogonality check (1 mark):** For coherent orthogonality, the required minimum separation is $1/T_b = 2$ kHz. The actual separation is 4 kHz, which equals $2/T_b$. Hence $\vert f_1 - f_2 \vert = n/T_b$ with $n=2$, satisfying orthogonality. **Yes, the signals are orthogonal.** (Bonus: 4 kHz $\neq$ 1 kHz, so it is orthogonal but not at the *minimum* separation — it occupies more bandwidth than strictly necessary.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks!**
> 
> 1. **Confusing $f_c$ and $f_1$/$f_2$:** Many students write "carrier = 100 kHz" and forget that the carrier is the *center* frequency. Always compute $f_c = (f_1 + f_2)/2$.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in $\Delta f$:** $\Delta f$ is **half** the separation, not the full separation. Mixing up $\Delta f$ with $\vert f_1 - f_2 \vert$ costs 1 mark.
> 3. **BER formula for coherent BFSK:** Students often write $Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ (which is the BPSK formula). The BFSK coherent formula is $Q(\sqrt{E_b/N_0})$ — exactly **3 dB worse** than BPSK.
> 4. **Min. bandwidth = $2/T_b$ vs $3/T_b$:** $2/T_b$ is the bandwidth of a *single* lobe; the **occupied** bandwidth is $3/T_b$. KTU often asks the *occupied* one.
> 5. **Discontinuous-phase vs CPFSK:** The basic BFSK studied here has phase discontinuities. Don't write "FSK has continuous phase" — that is **only true for CPFSK/MSK**, a separate (advanced) topic.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this page. Memorize before the exam.**

- **FSK = Frequency Shift Keying** — only **frequency** varies; **amplitude** and **phase** are constant.
- **BFSK** uses two frequencies $f_1$ and $f_2$; **M-ary FSK** uses $M$ frequencies.
- **Modulation index:** $h = \vert f_1 - f_2 \vert \cdot T_b$.
- **Minimum frequency separation:**
    - Coherent: $1/T_b$
    - Non-coherent: $1/(2T_b)$
- **Minimum (occupied) bandwidth of BFSK:** $3R_b$ (using Carson's rule with minimum coherent separation).
- **BER — Coherent BFSK:** $P_e = Q(\sqrt{E_b/N_0})$.
- **BER — Non-coherent BFSK:** $P_e = \tfrac{1}{2} e^{-E_b/2N_0}$.
- **Coherent BFSK is 3 dB better** in SNR than non-coherent BFSK at high $E_b/N_0$.
- **BFSK is 3 dB worse** than BPSK for the same $E_b/N_0$, but is a **constant-envelope** scheme (good for non-linear amplifiers).
- **Generation:** Two oscillators + data-controlled switch; or a single VCO driven by binary voltage.
- **Detection:**
    - **Coherent:** Two matched filters/correlators + comparator (needs PLL, more complex).
    - **Non-coherent:** Two BPFs + envelope detectors + sampler + comparator (simpler, no phase sync needed).
- **FSK $\neq$ CPFSK:** Basic FSK has phase discontinuities → high sidelobes. CPFSK (with $h = 0.5$ → MSK) solves this.
- **Applications:** Modems, caller ID, pagers, RFID, AFSK in ham radio, DTMF dual-tone signalling.
- **Exam Tip:** Always specify the modulation index $h$ and whether detection is coherent or non-coherent in any BER question — examiners deduct marks for ambiguity.

---

> **End of Notes — Frequency Shift Keying (Module 4)**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech (NEP 2020) ESE Assessment Pattern.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Methods of Generation and Detection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET864 - ELECTRONIC COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Methods of Generation and Detection # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Modulation** is the process of varying one or more properties of a high-frequency **carrier signal** (amplitude, frequency, or phase) in accordance with the instantaneous amplitude of the low-frequency **message/baseband signal**. The reverse process, performed at the receiver, is called **demodulation** or **detection**, which faithfully recovers the original baseband information from the modulated waveform. In the KTU 2024 scheme context, "Methods of Generation and Detection" refers to the hardware/circuit-level techniques used to **produce** (generate) and **extract** (detect) amplitude-modulated (AM) and frequency-modulated (FM) signals. These methods form the practical bridge between theoretical signal analysis and the physical realization of a communication transmitter-receiver pair. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** This topic is the high-weightage core of Module 4. Students must be able to draw circuit-level block diagrams, derive the output expressions, and explain the working principle of each generation/detection method. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you (the message) want to send a heavy parcel across a river. Throwing the parcel directly across is difficult and inefficient. So you attach the parcel to a powerful boat (the carrier) that moves smoothly across the water. The boat is the carrier wave, the parcel is your message, and the act of attaching it is **modulation**. At the destination, someone unloads the parcel from the boat — that is **detection**. **Generation** = packing the message onto the carrier. **Detection** = unpacking the message from the carrier. For **AM**, the message changes the *height* (amplitude) of the carrier boat. For **FM**, the message changes the *speed/wobble* (frequency) of the carrier boat. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants > [!NOTE] > **Key Engineering Metrics You Must Memorize** > - **Carrier Frequency ($f_c$):** Typically in the **MHz to GHz** range for broadcast. > - **Modulation Index for AM ($\mu$):** $\mu = A_m / A_c$ (dimensionless, $0 \le \mu \le 1$ for distortion-free AM). > - **Modulation Index for FM ($\beta$):** $\beta = \Delta f / f_m$ (dimensionless). > - **Standard Broadcast AM:** $f_c = 540$ kHz to $1640$ kHz. > - **Standard Broadcast FM:** $f_c = 88$ MHz to $108$ MHz. > - **Intermediate Frequency (IF) for AM Superheterodyne:** **455 kHz**. > - **Intermediate Frequency (IF) for FM Superheterodyne:** **10.7 MHz**. --- ## 1.4 Classification Map of Generation & Detection Methods > [!IMPORTANT] > **Topic Roadmap (Set Your Mental Map):** > - **AM Generation:** (i) Square-Law Modulator, (ii) Switching/Collector Modulator, (iii) Balanced Modulator > - **AM Detection:** (i) Square-Law Detector, (ii) Envelope Detector, (iii) Synchronous/Coherent Detector > - **FM Generation:** (i) Direct Methods (Reactance Modulator, Varactor Diode Modulator), (ii) Indirect Method (Armstrong) > - **FM Detection:** (i) Slope Detector, (ii) Balanced Slope Detector, (iii) Foster-Seeley Discriminator, (iv) Ratio Detector, (v) PLL Detector # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Generation of AM Signals ### 2.1.1 Square-Law Modulator The **square-law modulator** exploits the nonlinear (square-law) V-I characteristic of a device such as a diode or a transistor. Because the device is biased in the nonlinear region, the output current contains a term proportional to the **product** of the carrier and modulating signals — which is exactly what we need for AM. - The carrier $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ and message $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$ are summed and applied as input. - The nonlinear device output: $i(t) = a_0 + a_1 v_{in}(t) + a_2 v_{in}^2(t)$. - The squared term $v_{in}^2$ produces cross-product terms $2 c(t) m(t)$ that yield the AM sidebands after bandpass filtering. - **Why it works:** Squaring a sum produces cross-products — a fundamental algebraic property exploited in all analog multipliers. ### 2.1.2 Switching (Collector/Emitter) Modulator - The message is applied to the collector (or emitter) of a transistor amplifier carrying the carrier. - The output amplitude varies linearly with the modulating voltage, producing a high-efficiency AM waveform. - Commonly used in **high-level modulation** of transmitters. ### 2.1.3 Balanced Modulator (for DSB-SC generation) - Uses two matched nonlinear devices in a push-pull configuration. - The carrier component is suppressed at the output; only the sidebands remain. - Output is **Double-Sideband Suppressed Carrier (DSB-SC)**. ## 2.2 Detection of AM Signals ### 2.2.1 Envelope Detector (Most Important for KTU) - The simplest and most widely used AM detector — consists of a **diode**, a **capacitor**, and a **resistor** in parallel. - The diode rectifies the AM signal; the capacitor charges to the peak voltage and slowly discharges through the resistor, **tracking the envelope**. - **Design Condition:** The discharge time constant $RC$ must satisfy: $$\frac{1}{f_c} \ll RC \ll \frac{1}{W}$$ where $W$ is the message bandwidth. This ensures the output follows the envelope without diagonal clipping. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Favourite:** Envelope detector questions appear almost every year in either Part A (3 marks) or as a sub-part (7 marks) of a Part B question. ### 2.2.2 Square-Law Detector - Uses a nonlinear device followed by a low-pass filter. - Input: AM signal; the square term recovers a baseband component proportional to $m(t)$. - Disadvantage: Severe distortion for high modulation depths; rarely used in practice but important theoretically. ### 2.2.3 Synchronous (Coherent) Detector - Multiplies the incoming AM/DSB signal with a **locally generated carrier** that is phase-locked to the original carrier. - Followed by a low-pass filter to extract the message. - Requires **carrier synchronization** — the key challenge in practice. ## 2.3 Generation of FM Signals ### 2.3.1 Direct Method — Reactance Modulator - A **reactance modulator** presents a variable reactance (capacitive or inductive) to a tank circuit in an oscillator. - The message voltage controls the reactance, thereby varying the **instantaneous frequency** of oscillation. - **Why:** Frequency of oscillation $f = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ — change $C$ or $L$ and you change $f$. ### 2.3.2 Direct Method — Varactor Diode Modulator - A reverse-biased **PN junction diode** acts as a voltage-controlled capacitor (varactor). - Modulating voltage changes the depletion width, hence the junction capacitance, hence the oscillator frequency. - Simple, inexpensive, and widely used in VHF circuits. ### 2.3.3 Indirect Method — Armstrong FM Generator - Generates **NBFM (Narrowband FM)** by phase-modulating a carrier using a balanced modulator and then converting PM to FM using **frequency multipliers** and a **mixer**. - This method produces highly stable FM but requires complex circuitry to achieve wideband FM. ## 2.4 Detection of FM Signals ### 2.4.1 Slope Detector (Single-Tuned) - A **tuned circuit** is detuned from the carrier frequency so that the carrier sits on the **linear slope** of the resonance curve. - Frequency deviations in the FM signal are converted to amplitude variations → detected via an envelope detector. - Very simple but suffers from nonlinearity and poor selectivity. ### 2.4.2 Balanced Slope Detector - Uses **two** slope detectors with slopes of opposite sign, balanced against each other. - Improves linearity by cancelling second-order distortion. ### 2.4.3 Foster-Seeley Discriminator - The classic FM detector. Uses a **double-tuned transformer** with a center-tapped secondary and a **phase-shift network** (the tertiary winding). - Frequency deviation → phase shift at the tertiary → amplitude difference between the two diode outputs → recovered audio. - **KTU Favourite:** Circuit diagram and S-curve question appears frequently. ### 2.4.4 Ratio Detector - Similar to Foster-Seeley but with one diode reversed; the output is taken across a large capacitor that provides **AVC (Automatic Volume Control)** action. - More immune to amplitude variations (signal fading). ### 2.4.5 PLL (Phase-Locked Loop) FM Detector - Modern, highly linear method. The VCO tracks the input FM signal; the control voltage to the VCO is the **demodulated output**. - Used in nearly all modern FM receivers (ICs like CA3089, LM565). --- ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Memorize This Table — Appears in 80%+ of KTU Module 4 Problems.** | **#** | **Concept** | **Formula** | **Symbol Meaning** | |:-----:|:-----------|:------------|:------------------| | 1 | AM Signal Expression | $s(t) = A_c[1 + \mu \cos(2\pi f_m t)]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | $\mu$ = modulation index | | 2 | Modulation Index (AM) | $\mu = \frac{A_m}{A_c} = \frac{V_{max} - V_{min}}{V_{max} + V_{min}}$ | Amplitude ratio | | 3 | Total AM Power | $P_t = P_c\left(1 + \frac{\mu^2}{2}\right)$ | $P_c$ = carrier power | | 4 | Sideband Power | $P_{SB} = \frac{\mu^2}{2} P_c$ | Total of both sidebands | | 5 | FM Instantaneous Frequency | $f_i(t) = f_c + k_f m(t)$ | $k_f$ = frequency sensitivity (Hz/V) | | 6 | Frequency Deviation | $\Delta f = k_f \cdot A_m$ | Peak deviation | | 7 | Modulation Index (FM) | $\beta = \frac{\Delta f}{f_m} = \frac{k_f A_m}{f_m}$ | Dimensionless | | 8 | Carson's Rule (FM Bandwidth) | $BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2 f_m (\beta + 1)$ | Approximate 98% power | | 9 | FM Output Expression (NBFM) | $s(t) \approx A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) - \beta A_c \sin(2\pi f_c t)\sin(2\pi f_m t)$ | Narrowband | | 10 | Envelope Detector Condition | $\frac{1}{f_c} \ll RC \ll \frac{1}{W}$ | No diagonal clipping | | 11 | Capture Ratio (FM) | $C = 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{A_{\text{desired}}}{A_{\text{undesired}}}\right)$ | In dB | | 12 | Pre-emphasis Time Constant | $\tau = 75\,\mu s$ | Standard FM broadcast (US/EU) | --- ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!IMPORTANT] > **Where These Methods Are Used Today:** > - **AM Broadcast Transmitters (MW/SW):** High-level collector modulation with Class C PA. > - **FM Broadcast:** Reactance modulator + frequency multipliers in older designs; PLL-based synthesizers in modern DSP-driven transmitters. > - **Mobile Phones (4G/5G):** Indirect Armstrong-like architectures with DSP-based modulators. > - **Software Defined Radio (SDR):** All generation/detection is done digitally, but the underlying principles (envelope, slope, PLL) still apply. > - **Radar & Two-Way Radios:** FM detection via PLL and ratio detector in commercial ICs. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: AM Output from a Square-Law Modulator We will derive the output AM signal when a carrier $c(t) = A_c \cos(\omega_c t)$ and message $m(t) = A_m \cos(\omega_m t)$ are applied to a square-law nonlinear device whose transfer characteristic is: $$i(t) = a_0 + a_1 v_{in}(t) + a_2 v_{in}^2(t)$$ **Step 1 — Form the input voltage to the nonlinear device.** The carrier and message are added (summed) at the input: $$v_{in}(t) = c(t) + m(t) = A_c \cos(\omega_c t) + A_m \cos(\omega_m t)$$ **Step 2 — Substitute into the square-law equation.** We need $v_{in}^2(t)$ to extract the product term: $$ \begin{aligned} v_{in}^2(t) &= [A_c \cos(\omega_c t) + A_m \cos(\omega_m t)]^2 \\ &= A_c^2 \cos^2(\omega_c t) + 2 A_c A_m \cos(\omega_c t)\cos(\omega_m t) + A_m^2 \cos^2(\omega_m t) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Apply the trigonometric identity** $\cos^2(\theta) = \frac{1 + \cos(2\theta)}{2}$ **and the product-to-sum identity** $2\cos A \cos B = \cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)$. $$ \begin{aligned} v_{in}^2(t) &= \frac{A_c^2}{2}[1 + \cos(2\omega_c t)] + A_c A_m [\cos((\omega_c - \omega_m)t) + \cos((\omega_c + \omega_m)t)] \\ &\quad + \frac{A_m^2}{2}[1 + \cos(2\omega_m t)] \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Write the full output current expression.** $$ \begin{aligned} i(t) &= a_0 + a_1[A_c\cos(\omega_c t) + A_m\cos(\omega_m t)] \\ &\quad + a_2\left\{\frac{A_c^2}{2}[1+\cos(2\omega_c t)] + A_c A_m[\cos((\omega_c-\omega_m)t) + \cos((\omega_c+\omega_m)t)]\right. \\ &\quad \left. + \frac{A_m^2}{2}[1+\cos(2\omega_m t)]\right\} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Identify the AM signal components.** The terms at frequency $\omega_c$ come from $a_1 A_c \cos(\omega_c t)$ (carrier term) and the product terms at $\omega_c \pm \omega_m$ (upper and lower sidebands). After passing through a **bandpass filter (BPF)** centered at $f_c$ with bandwidth $2f_m$: $$i_{BPF}(t) = a_1 A_c \cos(\omega_c t) + a_2 A_c A_m [\cos((\omega_c - \omega_m)t) + \cos((\omega_c + \omega_m)t)]$$ **Step 6 — Rearrange to standard AM form.** Factor out $a_1 A_c \cos(\omega_c t)$: $$ \begin{aligned} i_{BPF}(t) &= a_1 A_c \cos(\omega_c t) \left[1 + \frac{2 a_2 A_m}{a_1}\cos(\omega_m t)\right] \\ &= A_c' \cos(\omega_c t) [1 + \mu \cos(\omega_m t)] \end{aligned} $$ where $A_c' = a_1 A_c$ and $\mu = \frac{2 a_2 A_m}{a_1}$. **Conclusion:** The output is a standard AM wave with modulation index $\mu = \frac{2 a_2 A_m}{a_1}$. This proves that a square-law device **generates AM**. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Envelope Detector Output and Diagonal Clipping Condition Consider an AM input $v_{in}(t) = V_c[1 + \mu \cos(\omega_m t)]\cos(\omega_c t)$ applied to a diode-capacitor-resistor envelope detector. **Step 1 — During the positive half-cycle of the carrier**, the diode conducts and charges the capacitor $C$ to nearly the peak voltage: $$v_C(t) \approx V_{peak}(t) = V_c[1 + \mu \cos(\omega_m t)]$$ **Step 2 — During the negative half-cycle**, the diode is off. The capacitor discharges through $R$ exponentially: $$v_C(t) = V_0 \, e^{-t/RC}$$ **Step 3 — For distortionless detection**, the discharge between successive carrier peaks (period $T_c = 1/f_c$) must be small enough that the capacitor recharges to the new peak. This requires: $$\frac{1}{f_c} \ll RC$$ **Step 4 — Additionally**, the discharge must be slow enough to follow the **envelope** variations (period $1/W$, where $W$ is the message bandwidth). This requires: $$RC \ll \frac{1}{W}$$ **Step 5 — Combining both constraints** gives the design rule: $$\frac{1}{f_c} \ll RC \ll \frac{1}{W}$$ This is the **anti-diagonal-clipping condition** — memorize this for the exam. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: FM Modulation Index and Carson's Rule **Step 1 — Definition of Frequency Modulation.** For an FM signal, the instantaneous frequency is: $$f_i(t) = f_c + k_f m(t)$$ **Step 2 — The instantaneous phase** is the integral of frequency: $$ \begin{aligned} \phi(t) &= 2\pi \int_0^t f_i(\tau)\, d\tau \\ &= 2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_0^t m(\tau)\, d\tau \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — The FM signal** is: $$s(t) = A_c \cos[\phi(t)] = A_c \cos\left[2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_0^t m(\tau)\, d\tau\right]$$ **Step 4 — For a sinusoidal message** $m(t) = A_m \cos(\omega_m t)$: $$\int_0^t m(\tau) d\tau = \frac{A_m}{\omega_m} \sin(\omega_m t)$$ So: $$s(t) = A_c \cos\left[2\pi f_c t + \frac{2\pi k_f A_m}{\omega_m}\sin(\omega_m t)\right] = A_c \cos[2\pi f_c t + \beta \sin(\omega_m t)]$$ where: $$\beta = \frac{k_f A_m}{f_m} = \frac{\Delta f}{f_m}$$ **Step 5 — Carson's Rule for Bandwidth** (98% of total power contained): $$BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2 f_m(\beta + 1)$$ --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Foster-Seeley Discriminator Output The Foster-Seeley discriminator converts FM to AM using a **phase-shift network**, then detects the AM via two envelope detectors whose outputs are subtracted. **Step 1 — Input to the primary winding** of the double-tuned transformer: $v_1(t) = V_1 \cos(\omega_c t)$ (for unmodulated carrier). **Step 2 — The secondary is center-tapped** and produces a voltage $v_2(t)$ across the full secondary. The voltage from the center tap to one end is $v_2/2$, and to the other end is $-v_2/2$. **Step 3 — The voltage across the tertiary (the choke/phase-shifting coil)** lags the primary voltage by 90° at resonance: $v_3(t) = V_3 \sin(\omega_c t)$. **Step 4 — The two diode input voltages** are: $$v_{d1} = v_3 + \frac{v_2}{2}, \quad v_{d2} = v_3 - \frac{v_2}{2}$$ **Step 5 — At resonance** ($f = f_c$), the secondary voltage $v_2$ is in phase with $v_3$, so $|v_{d1}| = |v_{d2}|$ and the difference output is zero. **Step 6 — Off-resonance**, the phase of $v_2$ shifts, unbalancing the diode inputs. The difference of the envelope-detected outputs gives an **S-shaped curve** of output voltage vs. frequency — the famous **S-curve** of the discriminator. **Step 7 — Output voltage (linear region):** $$v_{out}(t) = k_d \cdot \Delta f(t)$$ where $k_d$ is the discriminator constant (V/Hz). The output is **directly proportional to the frequency deviation** — exactly the demodulated message. --- ## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **Use this matrix for "Compare and Contrast" 7-mark sub-questions.** | **Parameter** | **AM Envelope Detector** | **FM Slope Detector** | **FM Foster-Seeley** | **PLL FM Detector** | |:-------------|:-------------------------|:----------------------|:---------------------|:--------------------| | Complexity | Very Low | Low | Medium | Medium-High | | Linearity | Good (if $\mu \le 1$) | Poor | Good | Excellent | | AM rejection | None (needs AGC) | Poor | Good | Very Good | | Threshold effect | No | Yes | Yes | Lower | | Modern use | Still in AM receivers | Educational only | Legacy radios | All modern SDR/IC | | Output | Envelope of input | AM-detected slope output | Difference of two diode outputs | VCO control voltage | --- ## 3.6 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style) **Problem:** An AM broadcast transmitter has $P_c = 10$ kW carrier power and modulation index $\mu = 0.8$. Find (a) total transmitted power, (b) sideband power, (c) current in antenna (assume $R_{ant} = 50\,\Omega$). **Solution:** **(a) Total Power:** $$P_t = P_c\left(1 + \frac{\mu^2}{2}\right) = 10000\left(1 + \frac{0.64}{2}\right) = 10000(1 + 0.32) = 13200\,\text{W} = 13.2\,\text{kW}$$ **(b) Sideband Power:** $$P_{SB} = \frac{\mu^2}{2} P_c = \frac{0.64}{2} \times 10000 = 3200\,\text{W} = 3.2\,\text{kW}$$ **(c) Antenna Current:** $$I_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{P_t}{R_{ant}}} = \sqrt{\frac{13200}{50}} = \sqrt{264} \approx 16.25\,\text{A}$$ **Note:** These numerical types are common in KTU Part B problems. Practice the three standard formulas. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: AM Generation via Square-Law Modulator ```mermaid flowchart LR msgA[Message Signal
