KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]** ### **Q1. Define modulation index for an AM signal. What is its significance?** *(3 Marks — CO2, Remember/Understand Level)* **Model Answer:** The modulation index $\mu$ of an AM signal is defined as the ratio of the peak amplitude of the modulating signal $A_m$ to the peak amplitude of the carrier $A_c$: $$\mu = \frac{A_m}{A_c}$$ It can also be expressed using the maximum and minimum envelope amplitudes as: $$\mu = \frac{V_{max} - V_{min}}{V_{max} + V_{min}}$$ **Significance:** - It indicates the **extent** to which the carrier amplitude is varied by the message. - For distortion-free envelope detection, **$\mu$ must lie between 0 and 1** ($\mu \le 1$). If $\mu > 1$, **overmodulation** occurs, causing envelope distortion and "splatter" of sidebands into adjacent channels. - It determines the **sideband power** relative to the carrier power: $P_{SB}/P_c = \mu^2/2$. --- > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** ### **Q2. List any three advantages of FM over AM. State Carson's rule for FM bandwidth.** *(3 Marks — CO2, Understand Level)* **Model Answer:** **Three advantages of FM over AM:** 1. **Noise immunity:** FM is much more resistant to amplitude noise because the information is in frequency, not amplitude. Most noise is amplitude-type, so FM rejects it via the limiter stage. 2. **Capture effect:** An FM receiver naturally suppresses weaker interfering signals, giving excellent co-channel rejection. 3. **Better fidelity:** FM can support much wider bandwidths (200 kHz for broadcast FM vs. 10 kHz for AM), allowing high-fidelity audio transmission. **Carson's Rule:** $$BW_{FM} = 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2 f_m(\beta + 1)$$ This rule states that approximately **98% of the total FM signal power** is contained within a bandwidth equal to twice the sum of the peak frequency deviation $\Delta f$ and the highest modulating frequency $f_m$. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read This Before Answering:** > - Always **draw a neat block diagram** with labeled signals at every stage. No diagram = up to **3 marks deducted**. > - In FM derivations, students commonly **mix up $\Delta f$ and $f_m$** in the modulation index formula. $\beta = \Delta f / f_m$ — write it correctly. > - For envelope detector questions, students often **forget the RC design condition** $\frac{1}{f_c} \ll RC \ll \frac{1}{W}$. This is a 2-mark item by itself. > - For Foster-Seeley or ratio detector, examiners expect an **S-curve sketch** with proper axis labels. Skipping the curve = 2 marks lost. > - State the **unit** of every quantity you derive. Numerical answers without units = 0 in some valuation rubrics. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** **Q.A (a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the **square-law method of AM generation**. Derive the expression for the AM signal at the output of the bandpass filter. *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** **Block Diagram:** (Refer Figure 4.1 in Section 4.1 above — same architecture.) **Working Principle:** The message signal $m(t) = A_m \cos(\omega_m t)$ and carrier signal $c(t) = A_c \cos(\omega_c t)$ are first summed using a linear adder to form: $$v_{in}(t) = m(t) + c(t) = A_m \cos(\omega_m t) + A_c \cos(\omega_c t)$$ This sum is applied to a **nonlinear device** (such as a diode biased in the square-law region or a transistor in its nonlinear region). The V-I characteristic of such a device can be approximated as: $$i(t) = a_0 + a_1 v_{in}(t) + a_2 v_{in}^2(t)$$ where $a_0, a_1, a_2$ are constants. **Derivation:** Substituting $v_{in}(t) = m(t) + c(t)$: $$i(t) = a_0 + a_1[m(t) + c(t)] + a_2[m(t) + c(t)]^2$$ Expanding the square: $$ \begin{aligned} i(t) &= a_0 + a_1 m(t) + a_1 c(t) + a_2 m^2(t) + 2 a_2 m(t)c(t) + a_2 c^2(t) \end{aligned} $$ We identify the terms by their frequency content: - $a_0$ : DC term - $a_1 m(t)$ : low-frequency message - $a_1 c(t) = a_1 A_c \cos(\omega_c t)$ : carrier - $a_2 m^2(t)$ : low-frequency (around $2\omega_m$) - $\mathbf{2 a_2 m(t)c(t)}$ : product term — **contains sidebands** at $\omega_c \pm \omega_m$ - $a_2 c^2(t)$ : high-frequency at $2\omega_c$ The **bandpass filter** centered at $f_c$ with bandwidth $2f_m$ rejects the DC, the low-frequency baseband, and the $2\omega_c$ term, passing only the carrier and sideband terms: $$i_{BPF}(t) = a_1 A_c \cos(\omega_c t) + 2 a_2 A_c A_m \cos(\omega_c t)\cos(\omega_m t)$$ Factoring out $a_1 A_c \cos(\omega_c t)$: $$i_{BPF}(t) = a_1 A_c \cos(\omega_c t) \left[1 + \frac{2 a_2 A_m}{a_1}\cos(\omega_m t)\right]$$ This is the standard AM signal with: $$s_{AM}(t) = A_c' [1 + \mu \cos(\omega_m t)] \cos(\omega_c t)$$ where $A_c' = a_1 A_c$ and $\mu = \frac{2 a_2 A_m}{a_1}$ is the modulation index. **Conclusion:** The square-law method produces a standard AM wave. (7 marks: 2 for diagram, 3 for derivation, 2 for identifying AM form.) --- **Q.A (b)** Explain the working of an **envelope detector** for AM demodulation. Derive the condition for distortionless detection. *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** **Circuit:** A diode D in series with a parallel RC network, with output taken across the capacitor. **Block Diagram:** (Refer Figure 4.2 in Section 4.2 above.) **Working:** The AM signal $v_{AM}(t) = V_c[1 + \mu\cos(\omega_m t)]\cos(\omega_c t)$ is applied to the envelope detector. 1. **During the positive half-cycle** of each carrier cycle, the diode is forward-biased and conducts. The capacitor C charges rapidly (through the small diode forward resistance) to nearly the peak instantaneous voltage of the input AM signal. 2. **During the negative half-cycle**, the diode is reverse-biased and acts as an open switch. The capacitor now discharges slowly through the load resistor R. 3. Between successive carrier peaks (spaced by $T_c = 1/f_c$), the capacitor voltage decays exponentially, but only slightly, so it closely tracks the next carrier peak. 4. The resulting voltage across the capacitor is an **approximate replica of the envelope** of the AM signal — i.e., $V_c[1 + \mu\cos(\omega_m t)]$. 5. A **DC blocking capacitor** (not shown in the basic detector) removes the DC component $V_c$, leaving the recovered message $A_m \cos(\omega_m t)$. **Condition for Distortionless Detection:** Two requirements must be met: - **Requirement 1:** The capacitor must discharge slowly enough that it can follow the envelope variations of the message. The envelope varies at a rate determined by the highest message frequency $W$ (or $f_m$ max). Therefore: $$RC \ll \frac{1}{W}$$ - **Requirement 2:** The capacitor must charge fast enough to follow the carrier peaks and discharge slowly enough between carrier cycles. The discharge interval is $T_c = 1/f_c$, so: $$\frac{1}{f_c} \ll RC$$ **Combined Condition:** $$\frac{1}{f_c} \ll RC \ll \frac{1}{W}$$ **Diagonal Clipping:** If $RC$ is too large, the discharge curve of one cycle cuts across (clips) the next cycle's envelope, producing a distortion called **diagonal clipping**. The clipped envelope no longer faithfully represents the message. **Conclusion:** With proper choice of RC, the envelope detector provides simple, efficient, and nearly distortionless demodulation of AM signals. (7 marks: 2 for diagram, 3 for working explanation, 2 for the condition and clipping discussion.) --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** **Q.B (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Armstrong method of FM generation**. State its advantages. *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** **Block Diagram:** (Refer Figure 4.3 in Section 4.3 above.) **Need for Indirect Method:** Direct FM generation (reactance modulator, varactor) suffers from **poor frequency stability** because the carrier center frequency drifts with temperature, supply voltage, and component aging. Armstrong's **indirect method** uses a **crystal-controlled oscillator** for the carrier, giving excellent frequency stability at the cost of increased circuit complexity. **Working Principle:** **Step 1 — Integration:** The message signal $m(t)$ is passed through an **integrator** to produce $\int m(t)\,dt$. This is necessary because we will perform **phase modulation (PM)** first and convert it to **frequency modulation (FM)** mathematically. **Step 2 — Phase Modulation:** The integrated message modulates the phase of a crystal-derived carrier using a **balanced modulator**. A balanced modulator is used because: - It suppresses the carrier, leaving only the phase-modulated sidebands. - This is essentially **NBFM (Narrowband FM)** with carrier suppressed. The output is: $$s_{NBFM}(t) \approx A_c\cos(\omega_c t) - \beta A_c \sin(\omega_c t)\sin(\omega_m t)$$ **Step 3 — Frequency Multiplication:** The NBFM output is passed through **frequency multipliers** (doublers and triplers in cascade) to increase both the carrier frequency and the frequency deviation by a factor of $n = n_1 \times n_2 \times \ldots$ After multiplication by factor $n$: - New carrier frequency: $f_c' = n f_c$ - New frequency deviation: $\Delta f' = n \Delta f$ **Step 4 — Frequency Translation (Optional Mixer):** If the final desired carrier frequency is not exactly $n f_c$, a **mixer** shifts the frequency by combining it with a local oscillator $f_{LO}$. **Step 5 — Final Output:** Wideband FM at the desired carrier frequency, with crystal-grade stability. **Advantages of Armstrong Method:** 1. **Excellent frequency stability** due to crystal control of the master oscillator. 2. **Low distortion** because no nonlinear reactive element is used in the modulating path. 3. **Predictable modulation index** — easy to design and analyze. 4. **Standardized architecture** — used in many commercial FM transmitters. **Disadvantages:** 1. Complex and expensive circuitry. 2. Bandwidth limitations of the multipliers and mixers. **Conclusion:** The Armstrong method is the standard approach for high-quality, stable FM generation. (7 marks: 2 for diagram, 3 for working steps, 2 for advantages.) --- **Q.B (b)** Explain the **Foster-Seeley discriminator** for FM detection with a block diagram and the S-curve. Derive the relationship between the output voltage and frequency deviation. *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** **Block Diagram:** (Refer Figure 4.4 in Section 4.4 above.) **Need:** Frequency-modulated signals cannot be detected by a simple envelope detector because the amplitude is constant. We need a circuit that converts frequency variations into amplitude variations, then envelope-detects them. **Construction:** The Foster-Seeley discriminator consists of: - A **double-tuned RF transformer** with primary, secondary, and tertiary windings. - The **secondary is center-tapped**. - Two diodes $D_1$ and $D_2$ are connected with their anodes to opposite ends of the secondary and their cathodes joined. - A **large capacitor** $C$ at the output provides a stable reference voltage. - A choke (RFC) at the tertiary provides the 90° phase-shifted reference. **Working:** 1. The primary winding receives the FM input. It is magnetically coupled to the secondary. 2. The voltage across the secondary $v_2$ is **in phase** with the primary at resonance but shifts phase as the input frequency deviates from $f_c$. 3. The tertiary (choke) voltage $v_3$ **always lags the primary by 90°** (independent of frequency, due to the choke). 4. The two diode input voltages are: $$v_{d1} = v_3 + \frac{v_2}{2}, \quad v_{d2} = v_3 - \frac{v_2}{2}$$ 5. At resonance ($f_i = f_c$): $|v_{d1}| = |v_{d2}|$ → output is **zero**. 6. Above resonance ($f_i > f_c$): one diode gets a larger voltage → **positive output**. 7. Below resonance ($f_i < f_c$): the other diode dominates → **negative output**. **S-Curve:** The output voltage vs. frequency plot is an **S-shaped curve** crossing zero at $f_c$, positive for $f > f_c$ and negative for $f < f_c$. The linear central region of the S-curve is the operating range. **Relationship Between Output and Deviation:** In the linear region: $$v_{out}(t) = k_d \cdot \Delta f(t)$$ where $k_d$ is the **discriminator constant** (in V/Hz). Since $\Delta f(t) = k_f A_m \cos(\omega_m t)$ for a sinusoidal message: $$v_{out}(t) = k_d \cdot k_f \cdot A_m \cos(\omega_m t)$$ This is the recovered message — proportional to the original modulating signal. **Conclusion:** The Foster-Seeley discriminator is a classic FM detector that converts frequency variations into proportional voltage variations via the S-curve characteristic. (7 marks: 2 for diagram + S-curve, 3 for working, 2 for the output-vs-deviation relationship.) --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Day-Before-Exam Quick-Recall Bullets:** ### **AM Generation** - **Square-law modulator** uses nonlinear device + BPF; output is $A_c[1 + \mu\cos\omega_m t]\cos\omega_c t$. - **Switching modulator** uses collector modulation; high efficiency. - **Balanced modulator** gives DSB-SC; carrier suppressed. ### **AM Detection** - **Envelope detector** = diode + RC. Condition: $\frac{1}{f_c} \ll RC \ll \frac{1}{W}$. Output is envelope $\propto m(t)$. - **Square-law detector** = nonlinear device + LPF. Suffers from distortion at high $\mu$. - **Synchronous detector** = multiplier + LPF. Needs carrier sync. ### **FM Generation** - **Reactance modulator** = voltage-controlled reactance in oscillator tank. - **Varactor modulator** = reverse-biased diode as VCC. Simple and cheap. - **Armstrong indirect method** = integrate → PM via balanced modulator → frequency multiply → mixer. High stability. ### **FM Detection** - **Slope detector** = detuned tuned circuit + envelope detector. Simple but nonlinear. - **Balanced slope** = two slope detectors back-to-back. Improved linearity. - **Foster-Seeley** = double-tuned transformer + two diodes. S-curve output. - **Ratio detector** = similar to Foster-Seeley but with reversed diode. Built-in AVC. - **PLL detector** = phase detector + LF + VCO. Modern, linear, robust. Used in ICs (LM565, CA3089). ### **Key Numerical Formulas to Memorize** - $P_t = P_c(1 + \mu^2/2)$ - $P_{SB} = \mu^2 P_c / 2$ - $\beta = \Delta f / f_m$ - $BW_{Carsons} = 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2 f_m(\beta + 1)$ - Envelope detector RC design: $\frac{1}{f_c} \ll RC \ll \frac{1}{W}$ ### **Common KTU Pitfalls to Avoid** - Forgetting to draw the block diagram (-2 to -3 marks). - Confusing $\Delta f$ with $f_m$ in $\beta$ formula. - Forgetting the diagonal-clipping condition. - Skipping the S-curve sketch in Foster-Seeley questions. - Writing $\mu$ in percentage instead of decimal (incorrect dimension). - Forgetting units in final numerical answers. ### **Exam-Winning Tip** > [!TIP] > In 14-mark questions, always structure your answer as: **(1) Diagram, (2) Working Principle, (3) Mathematical Derivation, (4) Conclusion/Advantages.** Examiners reward structured answers with full marks.

topics.title: Signal Constellations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET864 - ELECTRONIC COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Signal Constellations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Signal Constellation** is a two-dimensional geometric representation of the discrete set of allowable modulated symbols $s_i(t)$ plotted on an **In-phase / Quadrature (I–Q) plane**, where the horizontal axis represents the In-phase component ($I = \sqrt{E_s}\cos\phi$) and the vertical axis represents the Quadrature component ($Q = \sqrt{E_s}\sin\phi$). Each point on the constellation corresponds to one unique digital symbol (or group of bits) that the transmitter can send over the channel.

Formally, for an M-ary modulation scheme, the constellation is the set:

$$\mathcal{S} = \{s_i \in \mathbb{C} \mid i = 1, 2, \dots, M\}$$

where each complex symbol is expressed as:

$$s_i = I_i + jQ_i = A_i e^{j\phi_i}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A constellation diagram simultaneously conveys THREE physical attributes of a digital signal — **Amplitude ($A_i$)**, **Phase ($\phi_i$)**, and **the decision boundaries** used by the receiver to distinguish symbols in the presence of noise.

## 1.2 Why Do We Need Constellation Diagrams?

A digital communication engineer must be able to:
1. **Visualize the "alphabet"** of the modulation scheme (like a language's letter set).
2. **Measure the minimum Euclidean distance** between any two symbols — this directly controls the **Bit Error Rate (BER)**.
3. **Compute average symbol energy** $E_s$, **spectral efficiency** $\eta = \log_2 M$ bits/s/Hz, and **noise immunity**.
4. **Diagnose channel impairments** like phase rotation, amplitude fading, and ISI by observing how received points scatter from their ideal positions (a process called **constellation observation** on a vector signal analyzer).

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Constellation as a City Map"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Constellation = Apartment Buildings in a City**
> 
> Imagine a city where each building represents one possible transmitted symbol. The **horizontal street (I-axis)** and **vertical street (Q-axis)** form a grid:
> - **BPSK** = 2 buildings on opposite sides of a single street (left and right) — only 2 choices.
> - **QPSK** = 4 buildings at the 4 corners of a square — 4 equally spaced choices.
> - **16-QAM** = 16 buildings arranged in a 4×4 grid — dense city with 16 choices packed closer together.
> - **AWGN noise** = a fog rolling into the city. If buildings are too close (low $d_{min}$), the fog causes confusion (errors). Wider spacing = more robust communication.
> - **Fading** = a tornado that knocks buildings sideways (phase rotation) or shrinks them (amplitude scaling).

## 1.4 Standard Constellation Families

| Constellation Family | Varying Attribute | Typical Applications |
|---|---|---|
| **M-ASK** (Amplitude Shift Keying) | Amplitude only | Low-cost optical links, legacy modems |
| **M-PSK** (Phase Shift Keying) | Phase only | Satellite, deep-space (BPSK, QPSK) |
| **M-QAM** (Quadrature Amplitude) | Both amplitude & phase | Wi-Fi (64/256-QAM), 4G/5G (up to 1024-QAM) |
| **M-FSK** (Frequency Shift Keying) | Frequency (orthogonal in N-D space) | Power-line comms, Rician fading channels |

## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Carrier frequency** $f_c$ — does **not** appear in the constellation (it is the modulation reference).
- **Symbol duration** $T_s$ — the time the constellation "stays" at one point.
- **Bit rate** $R_b = \log_2 M / T_s$.
- **Noise spectral density** $N_0$ (Watts/Hz) — used for SNR calculations.
- **Es/No** (symbol SNR) and **Eb/No** (bit SNR) — the two standard figures-of-merit.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Geometric Signal Space — Orthonormal Basis

Any passband digital signal $s_i(t)$ over one symbol duration $T_s$ can be expanded on an orthonormal basis $\{\phi_1(t), \phi_2(t)\}$ where:

$$\phi_1(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{T_s}}\cos(2\pi f_c t), \quad \phi_2(t) = -\sqrt{\frac{2}{T_s}}\sin(2\pi f_c t)$$

The vector representation is:

$$\mathbf{s}_i = (s_{i1}, s_{i2}) = \left(\int_0^{T_s} s_i(t)\phi_1(t)\,dt,\; \int_0^{T_s} s_i(t)\phi_2(t)\,dt\right)$$

The **Euclidean distance** between any two constellation points $\mathbf{s}_i$ and $\mathbf{s}_j$ is:

$$d_{ij} = \sqrt{(s_{i1} - s_{j1})^2 + (s_{i2} - s_{j2})^2} = \vert \mathbf{s}_i - \mathbf{s}_j \vert$$

## 2.2 Minimum Distance & Energy Relations

The **minimum Euclidean distance** $d_{min}$ is the smallest separation among all pairs:

$$d_{min} = \min_{i \neq j} \vert \mathbf{s}_i - \mathbf{s}_j \vert$$

For an M-ary constellation with **average symbol energy** $E_s$ and **$k = \log_2 M$ bits per symbol**, the average **bit energy** is:

$$E_b = \frac{E_s}{\log_2 M} = \frac{E_s}{k}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Complex symbol | $s_i = I_i + jQ_i = A_i e^{j\phi_i}$ | Dimensionless (normalized) |
| 2 | Passband signal | $s_i(t) = I_i\phi_1(t) + Q_i\phi_2(t)$ | Volts |
| 3 | Symbol energy | $E_s = \frac{1}{M}\sum_{i=1}^{M}(I_i^2 + Q_i^2)$ | Joules |
| 4 | Bit energy | $E_b = E_s / \log_2 M$ | Joules |
| 5 | Spectral efficiency | $\eta = \log_2 M$ | bits/s/Hz |
| 6 | BPSK / QPSK $d_{min}$ | $d_{min} = 2\sqrt{E_s}$ | (QPSK) |
| 7 | M-PSK $d_{min}$ | $d_{min} = 2\sqrt{E_s}\sin(\pi/M)$ | General M-PSK |
| 8 | Square M-QAM $d_{min}$ | $d_{min} = 2\sqrt{\dfrac{3E_s}{2(M-1)}}$ | $M = 2^{2k}$ |
| 9 | M-QAM $E_s$ in terms of $E_b$ | $E_s = E_b \log_2 M$ with $E_s = \dfrac{2(M-1)}{3}\,d^2$ (avg) | — |
| 10 | M-PSK BER approx. (AWGN) | $P_b \approx \dfrac{2}{\log_2 M}Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{2E_s}{N_0}}\sin\!\dfrac{\pi}{M}\right)$ | Coherent detection |
| 11 | M-QAM SER approx. (AWGN) | $P_s \approx 4Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{3E_s}{(M-1)N_0}}\right)\!\left[1-Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{3E_s}{(M-1)N_0}}\right)\right]\!/\log_2 M$ | Square QAM |
| 12 | Q-function | $Q(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\int_x^{\infty} e^{-t^2/2}\,dt$ | Monotonic, $Q(0)=0.5$ |
| 13 | Bit-to-symbol mapping | $k = \log_2 M$ bits $\to$ 1 symbol | Gray coding preferred |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse $E_s$ and $E_b$. Remember: **$E_s = E_b \log_2 M$** — the *symbol* energy is always $\log_2 M$ times the *bit* energy because one symbol carries that many bits.

## 2.4 Classification of Constellations by Geometry

**A. Constant-Envelope Constellations (M-PSK):**
All points lie on a circle of radius $\sqrt{E_s}$. Only the phase $\phi_i$ changes. This gives excellent **non-linear amplifier (HPA) tolerance** because the envelope does not fluctuate — critical for satellite links.

**B. Rectangular / Square Constellations (M-QAM):**
Points lie on a rectangular grid. Both amplitude and phase vary. These achieve the **highest spectral efficiency** for a given $E_s/N_0$ and are the workhorse of 4G/5G/Wi-Fi.

**C. Cross Constellations ($\pi/4$-DQPSK, 8-QAM cross):**
A hybrid between PSK and QAM — partial constant envelope with multi-ring structure.

**D. Differential Constellations (DPSK, DQPSK):**
Information encoded in the *change* of phase between successive symbols. Robust to phase ambiguity (avoids need for carrier recovery), but ~3 dB worse than coherent.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **5G NR** uses 256-QAM and 1024-QAM, with **adaptive modulation** that drops to QPSK when channel SNR falls — constellations are dynamic.
- **GPS** uses BPSK because it must work in very low SNR ($E_b/N_0 \approx 14$ dB for acquisition).
- **Wi-Fi 6 (802.11ax)** uses up to 1024-QAM, packed tightly — a textbook example of trading distance for throughput.
- **Engineers use constellation "eyes"** to estimate SNR, equalizer performance, phase noise, and IQ imbalance in real-time on vector signal analyzers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — I-Q Representation of a Passband Symbol

**Problem:** Show that any passband M-ary symbol $s_i(t)$ for $0 \le t \le T_s$ can be written as an I-Q form using orthonormal basis functions.

**Step 1 — Start from the standard M-PSK form:**

$$s_i(t) = A\cos\!\big(2\pi f_c t + \phi_i\big), \quad 0 \le t \le T_s$$

**Step 2 — Expand the cosine using the angle-addition identity:**

$$s_i(t) = A\cos(2\pi f_c t)\cos\phi_i - A\sin(2\pi f_c t)\sin\phi_i$$

**Step 3 — Identify the in-phase and quadrature components:**

$$I_i = A\cos\phi_i, \qquad Q_i = A\sin\phi_i$$

**Step 4 — Use the orthonormal basis $\phi_1(t), \phi_2(t)$ with normalization $\sqrt{2/T_s}$:**

$$s_i(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{T_s}}\,I_i\,T_s\,\phi_1(t) - \sqrt{\frac{2}{T_s}}\,Q_i\,T_s\,\phi_2(t) \cdot (-1)$$

After algebra (assuming $A=\sqrt{2E_s/T_s}$ for energy normalization), the symbol vector is:

$$\mathbf{s}_i = (s_{i1}, s_{i2}) = \big(\sqrt{E_s}\cos\phi_i,\; \sqrt{E_s}\sin\phi_i\big)$$

**Step 5 — Express as a complex number (constellation point):**

$$s_i = s_{i1} + j\,s_{i2} = \sqrt{E_s}\,e^{j\phi_i}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the fundamental constellation point in the I-Q plane. For **BPSK**, $\phi_i \in \{0, \pi\}$ giving two points on the I-axis. For **QPSK**, $\phi_i \in \{\pi/4, 3\pi/4, 5\pi/4, 7\pi/4\}$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Minimum Distance for M-PSK

**Step 1 — Two adjacent M-PSK points lie on a circle of radius $\sqrt{E_s}$ separated by an angle of $2\pi/M$:**

$$\mathbf{s}_1 = \sqrt{E_s}e^{j0}, \quad \mathbf{s}_2 = \sqrt{E_s}e^{j2\pi/M}$$

**Step 2 — Use the chord-length formula on a circle:**

$$d_{min} = 2\sqrt{E_s}\sin\!\left(\frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{2\pi}{M}\right) = 2\sqrt{E_s}\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi}{M}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Verification for M = 2 (BPSK):**

$$d_{min} = 2\sqrt{E_s}\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right) = 2\sqrt{E_s}$$

This matches: BPSK points are at $+\sqrt{E_s}$ and $-\sqrt{E_s}$, separated by $2\sqrt{E_s}$. ✓

**Step 4 — For M = 4 (QPSK):**

$$d_{min} = 2\sqrt{E_s}\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right) = 2\sqrt{E_s}\cdot\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} = \sqrt{2E_s} \cdot \sqrt{2} = 2\sqrt{E_s}\cdot\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$$

Wait — corrected: $\sin(\pi/4) = 1/\sqrt{2}$, so $d_{min} = 2\sqrt{E_s}/\sqrt{2} = \sqrt{2E_s}$. ✓

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Average Symbol Energy for Square M-QAM

**Setup:** Square M-QAM with $M = 2^{2k}$ points arranged in a $\sqrt{M}\times\sqrt{M}$ grid. Let half the side length be $d$, so points are at $\{(2i+1-\sqrt{M})d,\,(2j+1-\sqrt{M})d\}$.

**Step 1 — Compute $E_s$ (sum of squares):**

$$E_s = \frac{2d^2}{M}(M-1)\cdot\frac{M}{3}\cdot 2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; E_s = \frac{2(M-1)d^2}{3}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $d$ in terms of $E_s$:**

$$d = \sqrt{\frac{3E_s}{2(M-1)}}$$

**Step 3 — Minimum distance is $2d$ (adjacent grid points):**

$$d_{min} = 2d = 2\sqrt{\frac{3E_s}{2(M-1)}} = \sqrt{\frac{6E_s}{M-1}}$$

**Step 4 — Verification for M = 4 (which is QPSK, a degenerate QAM):**

$$d_{min} = \sqrt{\frac{6E_s}{3}} = \sqrt{2E_s}$$

This matches the QPSK result above. ✓

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Bit Error Probability for BPSK in AWGN

**Setup:** Two equiprobable points at $\pm\sqrt{E_b}$ on the I-axis. Received signal:

$$r = s_i + n, \quad n \sim \mathcal{N}(0, N_0/2)$$

**Step 1 — Decision rule:** Choose $s_1$ if $r > 0$, else $s_0$.

**Step 2 — Error probability given $s_0 = +\sqrt{E_b}$ sent:**

$$P(e \mid s_0) = P(n < -\sqrt{E_b}) = Q\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{E_b}}{\sqrt{N_0/2}}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Total error probability (equiprobable):**

$$P_b = \tfrac{1}{2}P(e \mid s_0) + \tfrac{1}{2}P(e \mid s_1) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Symbol Error Probability for M-QAM (AWGN)

**Step 1 — For square M-QAM, the I and Q components are independent. Error occurs if either I or Q is in error.**

**Step 2 — For an inner point (PAM of $\sqrt{M}$ levels), the error probability is:**

$$P_{\sqrt{M}} = 2\left(1 - \frac{1}{\sqrt{M}}\right)Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{3E_s}{(M-1)N_0}}\right)$$

**Step 3 — For M-QAM, the SER is (using union bound on independent I, Q):**

$$P_s = 1 - (1 - P_{\sqrt{M}})^2 \approx 2P_{\sqrt{M}} - P_{\sqrt{M}}^2$$

**Step 4 — Exact union-bound approximation (high SNR):**

$$P_s \approx 4\!\left(1 - \frac{1}{\sqrt{M}}\right)Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{3E_s}{(M-1)N_0}}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Convert to BER via Gray coding (one bit error per symbol error, on average):**

$$P_b \approx \frac{P_s}{\log_2 M} = \frac{4}{\log_2 M}\left(1 - \frac{1}{\sqrt{M}}\right)Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{3E_s}{(M-1)N_0}}\right)$$

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — 16-QAM Energy and BER

**Given:** $M = 16$, $E_b/N_0 = 10$ dB (linear = 10), Gray coding.

**Step 1 — Spectral efficiency:**

$$k = \log_2 16 = 4 \text{ bits/symbol}, \quad \eta = 4 \text{ bits/s/Hz}$$

**Step 2 — Symbol SNR:**

$$\frac{E_s}{N_0} = k \cdot \frac{E_b}{N_0} = 4 \times 10 = 40$$

**Step 3 — Argument of Q-function:**

$$\gamma = \frac{3E_s}{(M-1)N_0} = \frac{3 \times 40}{15} = 8$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Q-function:**

$$Q(\sqrt{8}) = Q(2.828)$$

From standard Q-tables: $Q(2.83) \approx 2.34 \times 10^{-3}$

**Step 5 — Compute the BER:**

$$P_b \approx \frac{4}{4}\!\left(1 - \frac{1}{4}\right) \cdot 2.34 \times 10^{-3} = 0.75 \times 2.34 \times 10^{-3} \approx 1.76 \times 10^{-3}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** 16-QAM at $E_b/N_0 = 10$ dB has a BER of about $1.76 \times 10^{-3}$, which is acceptable for voice but marginal for data.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Digital Modulator with Constellation Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input Bit Stream b t"] --> B["Serial to Parallel Converter"]
    B --> C["Group k bits per symbol"]
    C --> D["Constellation Mapper LUT"]
    D --> E["I Coordinate I i"]
    D --> F["Q Coordinate Q i"]
    E --> G["DAC I branch"]
    F --> H["DAC Q branch"]
    G --> I["Pulse Shaping Filter I"]
    H --> J["Pulse Shaping Filter Q"]
    I --> K["Mixer cos 2 pi fc t"]
    J --> L["Mixer sin 2 pi fc t"]
    K --> M["Summer Adder"]
    L --> M
    M --> N["RF Output s i t"]
    D -.-> P["Constellation Display on VSA"]
    P -.-> Q["Measure d min and E s"]
    Q -.-> D
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The bit stream is grouped into $k$-bit blocks, mapped to $(I, Q)$ coordinates from a Look-Up Table (LUT), filtered for spectral shaping, and upconverted by I and Q mixers before summation. A Vector Signal Analyzer (VSA) tap provides feedback on the constellation integrity.

## 4.2 Constellation Geometry Map — PSK Family

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BPSK["BPSK: 2 points on I axis at + sqrt Es and - sqrt Es"] --> QPSK["QPSK: 4 points on circle radius sqrt Es at 45 135 225 315 deg"]
    QPSK --> PSK8["8 PSK: 8 points on circle spaced 45 deg apart"]
    PSK8 --> PSK16["16 PSK: 16 points on circle spaced 22.5 deg apart"]
    QPSK -.-> note["Constant envelope: amp = sqrt Es for all M. d min decreases as M increases."]
    PSK8 -.-> note
    PSK16 -.-> note
```

## 4.3 Constellation Geometry Map — QAM Family

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q4["4 QAM = QPSK: 2 by 2 grid"] --> Q16["16 QAM: 4 by 4 grid"]
    Q16 --> Q64["64 QAM: 8 by 8 grid"]
    Q64 --> Q256["256 QAM: 16 by 16 grid"]
    Q256 --> Q1024["1024 QAM: 32 by 32 grid"]
    Q16 -.-> note2["Rectangular grid: amplitude varies across points. d min = 2d. Average Es grows as M increases."]
    Q64 -.-> note2
    Q256 -.-> note2
```

## 4.4 Decision Region Diagram — QPSK with AWGN

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R["Received noisy point r = s i + n in I Q plane"] --> D1{"Is r in upper right quadrant?"}
    D1 -- yes --> S1["Decide symbol 00 at sqrt Es 2 j sqrt Es 2"]
    D1 -- no --> D2{"Is r in upper left quadrant?"}
    D2 -- yes --> S2["Decide symbol 01 at -sqrt Es 2 j sqrt Es 2"]
    D2 -- no --> D3{"Is r in lower left quadrant?"}
    D3 -- yes --> S3["Decide symbol 11 at -sqrt Es 2 -j sqrt Es 2"]
    D3 -- no --> S4["Decide symbol 10 at sqrt Es 2 -j sqrt Es 2"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** For **QPSK with Gray coding**, the axes (I = 0 and Q = 0) form the optimal **decision boundaries**. These are the lines that minimize the total probability of error because they are equidistant from the two nearest constellation points in each region.

## 4.5 Error Probability Intuition — Effect of $d_{min}$ on BER

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Higher M"] --> B["More bits per symbol"]
    B --> C["Higher spectral efficiency"]
    A --> D["Smaller d min for fixed Es"]
    D --> E["Noise clouds overlap more"]
    E --> F["Higher BER"]
    C --> G["Trade off: throughput vs reliability"]
    F --> G
    G --> H["Adaptive modulation: pick M based on SNR"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` What is a signal constellation diagram? Why is the minimum Euclidean distance $d_{min}$ important?

**Model Answer:**

A **signal constellation diagram** is a 2D plot of the discrete set of allowed M-ary symbols on the **I (in-phase) and Q (quadrature) axes**, where each point represents one symbol $s_i = I_i + jQ_i$ with specific amplitude $A_i$ and phase $\phi_i$.

The **minimum Euclidean distance** $d_{min}$ between any two constellation points determines the **noise immunity** of the modulation scheme. A larger $d_{min}$ means the AWGN noise cloud of radius $\sigma_n$ is less likely to push a received point into the decision region of a neighboring symbol, **lowering the Bit Error Rate (BER)**. Mathematically:

$$P_b \propto Q\!\left(\frac{d_{min}}{2\sigma_n}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{d_{min}^2}{2N_0}}\right)$$

**(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for $d_{min}$ concept, 1 for BER link)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` Compare M-PSK and M-QAM constellations in terms of envelope, spectral efficiency, and noise performance.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | M-PSK | M-QAM |
|---|---|---|
| Envelope | **Constant** (all points on circle) | **Variable** (outer points have higher amplitude) |
| Spectral efficiency | $\log_2 M$ bits/s/Hz | $\log_2 M$ bits/s/Hz (equal) |
| Noise performance (AWGN) | Better at low M; degrades as $M$ grows | Better at high M (e.g., 16, 64-QAM beats 16-PSK) |
| Amplifier requirement | Tolerant to non-linear HPA | Needs **linear HPA** (high PAPR issue) |
| $d_{min}$ formula | $2\sqrt{E_s}\sin(\pi/M)$ | $\sqrt{6E_s/(M-1)}$ |
| Application | Satellite, deep-space | Wi-Fi, 4G/5G, cable modems |

**(3 marks — 1 per major comparison row)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` — **14 Marks**

**a)** Draw the signal constellation diagrams of **BPSK** and **QPSK**. For each, derive the expression for the **minimum Euclidean distance** in terms of symbol energy $E_s$. **(7 marks)**

**b)** For **16-QAM**, derive the **average symbol energy** $E_s$ in terms of the half-side-length $d$, and compute the **BER approximation** for an AWGN channel at $E_b/N_0 = 12$ dB. Assume Gray coding. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q.A (a):**

**BPSK Constellation:** Two points on the I-axis at $(+\sqrt{E_s}, 0)$ and $(-\sqrt{E_s}, 0)$, representing binary 0 and 1.

**QPSK Constellation:** Four points on a circle of radius $\sqrt{E_s}$ at phase offsets $\pi/4, 3\pi/4, 5\pi/4, 7\pi/4$:

$$s_i = \sqrt{E_s}\,e^{j(2i+1)\pi/4}, \quad i = 0, 1, 2, 3$$

i.e., points at $(\frac{\sqrt{E_s}}{\sqrt 2}, \frac{\sqrt{E_s}}{\sqrt 2})$, $(-\frac{\sqrt{E_s}}{\sqrt 2}, \frac{\sqrt{E_s}}{\sqrt 2})$, etc.

**Derivation of $d_{min}$:**

For BPSK, the two points are at $\pm\sqrt{E_s}$ on the I-axis, so:

$$d_{min}^{BPSK} = \vert\sqrt{E_s} - (-\sqrt{E_s})\vert = 2\sqrt{E_s}$$

For QPSK, the two nearest neighbors are at angular separation $\pi/2$ on a circle of radius $\sqrt{E_s}$. Using chord length:

$$d_{min}^{QPSK} = 2\sqrt{E_s}\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi/2}{2}\right) = 2\sqrt{E_s}\sin(\pi/4) = 2\sqrt{E_s}\cdot\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = \sqrt{2E_s}$$

**Marking guide (7 marks):** 1 mark BPSK diagram, 1 mark QPSK diagram, 1 mark BPSK derivation, 2 marks QPSK derivation, 1 mark final formula, 1 mark comparison comment.

---

#### **Solution to Q.A (b):**

**Step 1 — Setup 16-QAM grid:** Points at $\{(\pm d, \pm d), (\pm 3d, \pm d), (\pm d, \pm 3d), (\pm 3d, \pm 3d)\}$ — a 4×4 grid.

**Step 2 — Compute sum of squared magnitudes:**

For each coordinate, the values are $\{-3d, -d, +d, +3d\}$ with mean 0 and mean-square value $\frac{1}{4}(2\cdot d^2 + 2\cdot 9d^2) = 5d^2$.

Average $I^2 + Q^2 = 2 \times 5d^2 = 10d^2$. So:

$$E_s = 10d^2 \quad\Rightarrow\quad d = \sqrt{E_s/10}$$

Alternative: $E_s = \frac{2(M-1)d^2}{3} = \frac{2 \times 15 \times d^2}{3} = 10d^2$. ✓

**Step 3 — Compute $E_s/N_0$ at $E_b/N_0 = 12$ dB:**

$$E_b/N_0 = 10^{1.2} = 15.849 \text{ (linear)}$$

$$E_s/N_0 = 4 \times 15.849 = 63.396$$

**Step 4 — BER formula (16-QAM, AWGN, Gray):**

$$P_b \approx \frac{4}{\log_2 M}\left(1 - \frac{1}{\sqrt{M}}\right)Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{3E_s}{(M-1)N_0}}\right)$$

With $M = 16$:

$$P_b \approx \frac{4}{4}\left(1 - \frac{1}{4}\right)Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{3 \times 63.396}{15}}\right) = 0.75 \cdot Q\!\left(\sqrt{12.679}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the argument and the Q-function:**

$$\sqrt{12.679} = 3.561$$

$$Q(3.561) \approx 1.85 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 6 — Final answer:**

$$P_b \approx 0.75 \times 1.85 \times 10^{-4} \approx 1.39 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Marking guide (7 marks):** 1 mark for $E_s$ formula, 1 mark for $d_{min}$ formula, 1 mark for converting dB to linear, 1 mark for $E_s/N_0$, 2 marks for BER formula substitution, 1 mark for numerical answer.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` — **14 Marks** *(Alternative Choice)*

**a)** Derive the **bit error probability** for **BPSK** in AWGN. Show that doubling the energy per bit improves the BER by a factor of approximately $\sqrt{2}$ in the argument of the Q-function. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Explain the concept of **adaptive modulation** with a constellation diagram. Why is **64-QAM preferred over 16-PSK** at high SNR? Show the $d_{min}$ comparison. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q.B (a):**

**Step 1 — Setup BPSK signals:**

$$s_0(t) = +\sqrt{E_b}\,\phi_1(t), \quad s_1(t) = -\sqrt{E_b}\,\phi_1(t), \quad 0 \le t \le T_b$$

with $\phi_1(t) = \sqrt{2/T_b}\cos(2\pi f_c t)$.

**Step 2 — Received signal in AWGN:**

$$r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t), \quad n(t) \text{ is white Gaussian with PSD } N_0/2$$

**Step 3 — Correlator output:**

$$r = \int_0^{T_b} r(t)\phi_1(t)\,dt = \pm\sqrt{E_b} + n, \quad n \sim \mathcal{N}(0, N_0/2)$$

**Step 4 — Decision rule:** Decide $s_0$ if $r > 0$, else $s_1$.

**Step 5 — Conditional error probability given $s_0$ sent:**

$$P(e \mid s_0) = P(n < -\sqrt{E_b}) = \int_{-\infty}^{-\sqrt{E_b}} \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi N_0}} e^{-n^2/N_0}\,dn = Q\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{E_b}}{\sqrt{N_0/2}}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Total BER (equiprobable symbols):**

$$P_b = \tfrac{1}{2}P(e \mid s_0) + \tfrac{1}{2}P(e \mid s_1) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$

**Step 7 — Doubling $E_b$ effect:** If $E_b' = 2E_b$, then:

$$\sqrt{2E_b'/N_0} = \sqrt{4E_b/N_0} = 2\sqrt{E_b/N_0} = \sqrt{2}\cdot\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}$$

So the argument of Q increases by a factor of $\sqrt{2}$, which (for $Q(x) \sim e^{-x^2/2}/\sqrt{2\pi x}$ for large $x$) reduces the BER by approximately $e^{x^2}$ — a dramatic improvement. ✓

**Marking guide (7 marks):** 1 mark signal setup, 1 mark received signal, 1 mark correlator output, 1 mark decision rule, 1 mark conditional error, 1 mark final $P_b$ expression, 1 mark doubling analysis.

---

#### **Solution to Q.B (b):**

**Adaptive Modulation Concept:**

In time-varying channels (mobile, fading), the SNR at the receiver changes. A **fixed** constellation cannot be optimal for all conditions. **Adaptive modulation** dynamically switches between different constellation sizes (BPSK → QPSK → 16-QAM → 64-QAM) based on instantaneous channel quality (e.g., feedback of CQI in 4G/5G).

- **Low SNR** → use **BPSK/QPSK** (wide $d_{min}$, low throughput).
- **High SNR** → use **64-QAM or 256-QAM** (narrow spacing, high throughput).

**64-QAM vs 16-PSK at high SNR:**

For both, $M = 16$ so $k = 4$ bits/symbol, equal spectral efficiency.

**$d_{min}$ of 16-PSK:**

$$d_{min}^{16\text{-PSK}} = 2\sqrt{E_s}\sin(\pi/16) = 2\sqrt{E_s}(0.1951) \approx 0.390\sqrt{E_s}$$

**$d_{min}$ of 16-QAM:**

$$d_{min}^{16\text{-QAM}} = \sqrt{\frac{6E_s}{15}} = \sqrt{0.4 E_s} \approx 0.632\sqrt{E_s}$$

**Comparison:**

$$\frac{d_{min}^{16\text{-QAM}}}{d_{min}^{16\text{-PSK}}} = \frac{0.632}{0.390} \approx 1.62$$

So 16-QAM has a **62% larger minimum distance** for the same $E_s$, meaning significantly lower BER. This is why **QAM dominates in high-SNR, linear-amplifier environments** (cellular, Wi-Fi).

**Marking guide (7 marks):** 2 marks adaptive modulation explanation, 2 marks $d_{min}$ derivations, 1 mark numerical comparison, 2 marks engineering conclusion.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing $E_s$ and $E_b$** — when a problem gives $E_b/N_0$, always convert to $E_s/N_0$ by multiplying by $\log_2 M$ before substituting into M-PSK/M-QAM formulas.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\log_2 M$ factor in BER** — many students write $P_b \approx Q(\sqrt{2E_s/N_0}\sin(\pi/M))$ for M-PSK without dividing by $\log_2 M$. This gives **symbol error rate** $P_s$, not **bit error rate** $P_b$.
> 3. **dB to linear conversion errors** — $E_b/N_0 \text{ (dB)} = 10\log_{10}(E_b/N_0)_{\text{linear}}$. Use $E_b/N_0 = 10^{(dB/10)}$.
> 4. **Missing the $1 - 1/\sqrt{M}$ factor** in QAM BER — the standard formula has this scaling for non-binary PAM levels.
> 5. **Not specifying Gray coding** — Gray coding makes the assumption "1 bit error per symbol error" valid; without it, BER is much worse.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- A **signal constellation** plots M-ary symbols in the **I-Q (complex) plane** as points $s_i = I_i + jQ_i = A_i e^{j\phi_i}$.
- The **I-axis** = In-phase component ($\cos$ carrier); **Q-axis** = Quadrature component ($-\sin$ carrier).
- **M-PSK** has **constant envelope** (radius $\sqrt{E_s}$); **M-QAM** has **variable envelope** (rectangular grid).
- **Spectral efficiency** $\eta = \log_2 M$ bits/s/Hz.
- **Energy relation:** $E_s = E_b \log_2 M$. Always convert between them.
- **$d_{min}$ formulas:**
  * M-PSK: $2\sqrt{E_s}\sin(\pi/M)$.
  * Square M-QAM: $\sqrt{6E_s/(M-1)}$.
  * BPSK/QPSK: $2\sqrt{E_s}$ and $\sqrt{2E_s}$ respectively.
- **BER approximations (AWGN, coherent, Gray coding):**
  * BPSK: $Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$.
  * QPSK: $Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ (same as BPSK!).
  * M-PSK: $\dfrac{2}{\log_2 M}Q\!\left(\sqrt{2E_s/N_0}\sin(\pi/M)\right)$.
  * M-QAM: $\dfrac{4}{\log_2 M}\!\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{M}}\right)Q\!\left(\sqrt{3E_s/[(M-1)N_0]}\right)$.
- **Decision regions** are formed by the perpendicular bisectors of lines joining adjacent constellation points — for PSK/QAM grids, these are vertical and horizontal lines.
- **Trade-off:** Higher $M$ → higher throughput but lower $d_{min}$ → higher BER. Solved by **adaptive modulation**.
- **Constant-envelope advantage:** PSK survives non-linear amplifiers (satellite); **QAM's high PAPR** requires linear HPA but achieves lower BER at high SNR.
- **Differential variants** (DPSK, DQPSK) avoid carrier recovery but are ~3 dB worse than coherent.
- **Practical constellations used:** BPSK (GPS), QPSK (satellite, 3G), 16/64-QAM (4G), 256/1024-QAM (5G, Wi-Fi 6).
- **Visual diagnostic tool:** A vector signal analyzer displays the live constellation; **spreading** indicates noise, **rotation** indicates phase noise, **I/Q asymmetry** indicates imbalance, **clusters shifting** indicate fading.
- **Key constants to memorize:** $\sin(\pi/4) = 1/\sqrt{2}$, $\sin(\pi/6) = 0.5$, $\sin(\pi/8) \approx 0.383$, $\sin(\pi/16) \approx 0.195$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: M-ary Digital Modulation Schemes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET864 - ELECTRONIC COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** M-ary Digital Modulation Schemes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4: M-ARY DIGITAL MODULATION SCHEMES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **M-ary Digital Modulation** is a class of digital modulation techniques in which **each transmitted symbol represents a group of $k = \log_2 M$ binary bits**, where $M$ denotes the alphabet size (the number of distinct symbols or signal states used to convey information over the channel). In binary modulation, $M = 2$ and each symbol carries 1 bit; in M-ary modulation, $M > 2$ and each symbol carries multiple bits, allowing a higher data rate within a constrained symbol rate (baud).

The four principal M-ary modulation families studied under KTU Module 4 are:

1. **M-ary Amplitude Shift Keying (MASK)** — varies amplitude across $M$ levels.
2. **M-ary Frequency Shift Keying (MFSK)** — varies frequency across $M$ tones.
3. **M-ary Phase Shift Keying (MPSK)** — varies phase across $M$ equi-spaced points.
4. **M-ary Quadrature Amplitude Modulation (MQAM)** — varies both amplitude **and** phase on a rectangular/circular grid.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Traffic Light vs. Highway Code" View

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine driving through a city:**
> - **Binary (2-ary) signaling** is like a traffic light that has only **two states**: *RED* or *GREEN*. Each signal conveys one piece of information (stop/go).
> - **M-ary signaling** is like a **highway code sign with $M$ distinct colored arrows** (e.g., 16 different combinations). One glance at the sign transfers **$\log_2 16 = 4$ bits** of information.
>
> The bigger the alphabet $M$, the more information you pack per symbol — but the harder it becomes to distinguish symbols in the presence of noise (e.g., fog, glare). This is the **fundamental bandwidth–power–error trade-off** that drives all M-ary design.

### 1.3 Why M-ary? — Motivation Metrics

- **Bandwidth efficiency** improves because the same bit rate $R_b$ can be transmitted at a lower symbol rate $R_s = R_b / \log_2 M$, reducing the Nyquist bandwidth requirement.
- **Power efficiency** generally *decreases* for a fixed $P_e$ as $M$ increases (signals become closer in signal space).
- **Spectral efficiency** $\eta = R_b / B$ (in **bits/s/Hz**) is the key KTU benchmark.

The bit rate and symbol rate are linked by:

$$
R_b = R_s \cdot \log_2 M \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad T_s = T_b \cdot \log_2 M
$$

where $T_b$ and $T_s$ are the bit and symbol durations respectively.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Generic Transmitted Signal Model

Every M-ary scheme maps a $k$-bit block $\mathbf{b} = (b_1, b_2, \dots, b_k)$ into one of $M = 2^k$ signals $s_i(t)$:

$$
s_i(t) = A_i \cos(2\pi f_c t + \phi_i) \quad \text{for } 0 \le t \le T_s, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, M
$$

- MASK varies $A_i$ only.
- MFSK varies $f_c$ (or $f_i$).
- MPSK varies $\phi_i = \frac{2\pi (i-1)}{M}$ with constant envelope.
- MQAM varies both $A_i$ and $\phi_i$ in a 2-D lattice.

### 2.2 M-ary Amplitude Shift Keying (MASK)

- Information is carried in $M$ discrete amplitude levels: $A_i = (2i - 1 - M) \cdot \Delta$, for $i = 1, \dots, M$.
- **Envelope detection** is sufficient — simple, but highly susceptible to amplitude noise.
- Bandwidth is the **same as binary ASK**: $BW = 2R_s = \frac{2R_b}{\log_2 M}$.
- Poor power efficiency; used mainly in low-cost optical and short-haul systems.

### 2.3 M-ary Frequency Shift Keying (MFSK)

- $M$ orthogonal carriers $f_1, f_2, \dots, f_M$, separated by $\Delta f \approx \tfrac{1}{2T_s}$ for orthogonality.
- **Non-coherent** detection is possible (simpler receiver).
- Bandwidth **grows linearly with $M$**: $BW \approx M \cdot \Delta f = \frac{M}{2T_s}$.
- Power-efficient for large $M$ but extremely **bandwidth-inefficient**.

### 2.4 M-ary Phase Shift Keying (MPSK)

- Constant envelope: $|s_i(t)| = A$ for all $i$, with $\phi_i = \frac{2\pi(i-1)}{M}$.
- **Coherent detection** mandatory — reference carrier required.
- Bandwidth: $BW = \frac{2}{T_s} = \frac{2R_b}{\log_2 M}$ — **bandwidth-efficient**.
- As $M$ increases, adjacent phase points become closer; the noise margin (Euclidean distance) shrinks.

### 2.5 M-ary Quadrature Amplitude Modulation (MQAM)

- A 2-D signal set: both $I$ and $Q$ amplitudes are varied.
- Most common: **Square M-QAM** with $M = 2^k$ where $k$ is even (e.g., 4, 16, 64, 256).
- Example: **16-QAM** uses 4 amplitude levels on each of the $I$ and $Q$ rails.
- **Highest spectral efficiency** of all the schemes — used in Wi-Fi, LTE, 5G NR, DVB.
- Requires linear amplifiers (PAPR issue).

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are examinable in KTU ESE. Master this table first.**

| Parameter / Metric | MASK | MFSK (Orthogonal) | MPSK | M-QAM (Square) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Bits per symbol $k$** | $\log_2 M$ | $\log_2 M$ | $\log_2 M$ | $\log_2 M$ |
| **Symbol energy $E_s$** | $E_b \log_2 M$ | $E_b \log_2 M$ | $E_b \log_2 M$ | $E_b \log_2 M$ |
| **Bandwidth $BW$ (Hz)** | $\dfrac{2R_b}{\log_2 M}$ | $\dfrac{(M+3)R_b}{2\log_2 M}$ | $\dfrac{2R_b}{\log_2 M}$ | $\dfrac{2R_b}{\log_2 M}$ |
| **Spectral Efficiency $\eta$** | $\dfrac{\log_2 M}{2}$ | $\dfrac{2\log_2 M}{M+3}$ | $\dfrac{\log_2 M}{2}$ | $\dfrac{\log_2 M}{2}$ |
| **Symbol Error $P_s$ (approx.)** | $\dfrac{2(M-1)}{M}\,Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{3\,E_{avg}}{(M^2-1)N_0}}\right)$ | $\le (M-1)\,Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{E_s}{N_0}}\right)$ | $\approx 2\,Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{2E_s}{N_0}}\,\sin\!\dfrac{\pi}{M}\right)$ | $\approx 4\!\left(1-\dfrac{1}{\sqrt M}\right)\!Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{3E_s}{(M-1)N_0}}\right)$ |
| **Bit Error $P_b$ (approx.)** | $\dfrac{P_s}{\log_2 M}$ | $\dfrac{P_s}{\log_2 M}$ | $\dfrac{P_s}{\log_2 M}$ (Gray) | $\dfrac{P_s}{\log_2 M}$ (Gray) |
| **Detection** | Coherent/Envelope | Coherent/Non-coherent | Coherent | Coherent |
| **Envelope** | Variable | Constant | Constant | Variable |

> **Universal relationships used in every problem:**
> $$R_b = R_s \log_2 M \quad ; \quad E_s = E_b \log_2 M \quad ; \quad T_s = T_b \log_2 M$$
> $$Q(x) = \tfrac{1}{2}\operatorname{erfc}\!\left(\tfrac{x}{\sqrt{2}}\right) \quad ; \quad \eta = R_b / BW \; \text{(bits/s/Hz)}$$

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **4-PSK = QPSK**: Used in satellite downlinks, CDMA, DVB-S.
- **8-PSK**: Used in GSM EDGE, deep-space telemetry (Voyager, Cassini).
- **16-QAM, 64-QAM, 256-QAM**: Wi-Fi (802.11ax), 4G LTE, 5G NR, DOCSIS cable modems, DVB-C.
- **MFSK (large $M$)**: Military HF links, acoustic underwater comms where bandwidth is cheap but power is precious.
- **MASK**: Optical communication (PAM-4 in 100G/400G Ethernet, 5G fronthaul).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: MPSK Symbol Error Probability

The received signal in AWGN is $r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t)$. In the 2-D signal space (I/Q basis), the MPSK constellation is a circle of radius $\sqrt{E_s}$. The minimum distance between two adjacent phase points is:

$$
d_{min} = 2\sqrt{E_s}\,\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi}{M}\right)
$$

A symbol error occurs if the noise vector pushes the received phasor across the decision boundary of the ideal point. The boundary is a chord of the circle; using the union bound and integrating the Gaussian noise density over the half-plane on the "wrong" side of the boundary gives:

$$
P_s \approx 2\,Q\!\left(\frac{d_{min}/2}{\sqrt{N_0/2}}\right)
$$

**Step 1:** Substitute the expression for $d_{min}$:

$$
\frac{d_{min}}{2} = \sqrt{E_s}\,\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi}{M}\right)
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the Q-argument with noise variance per dimension $= N_0/2$:

$$
P_s \approx 2\,Q\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{E_s}\,\sin(\pi/M)}{\sqrt{N_0/2}}\right)
$$

**Step 3:** Simplify the denominator:

$$
\frac{\sqrt{E_s}}{\sqrt{N_0/2}} = \sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{N_0}}
$$

**Step 4:** Final compact form:

$$
P_s \approx 2\,Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{N_0}}\;\sin\!\frac{\pi}{M}\right) \quad \text{for } M \ge 4
$$

> **Conversion logic:** This expression is exact in the limit of high SNR. It shows that as $M$ grows, $\sin(\pi/M) \approx \pi/M$ shrinks, increasing $P_s$ — i.e., **higher-order MPSK needs more power for the same error rate**.

**Verification (special cases):**
- For $M = 2$ (BPSK), the formula reduces to $P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}\right)$ — the well-known BPSK result.
- For $M = 4$ (QPSK), $P_s \approx 2Q\!\left(\sqrt{2E_s/N_0}\,\sin 45°\right) = 2Q\!\left(\sqrt{E_s/N_0}\right) = 2Q\!\left(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}\right)$, which matches QPSK.

### 3.2 Derivation: M-QAM Symbol Error Probability

For a square M-QAM constellation with $M = L^2$ points (L levels on each axis), the average symbol energy is:

$$
E_s = \frac{2(M-1)}{3}\,d^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad d^2 = \frac{3E_s}{2(M-1)}
$$

where $d$ is half the minimum distance between adjacent points on the I or Q rail. The decision region for an interior point is a square of side $2d$. The probability that a single dimension error occurs is:

$$
P_{I} = 2\,Q\!\left(\frac{d}{\sqrt{N_0/2}}\right) = 2\,Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2d^2}{N_0}}\right)
$$

For an interior point, both dimensions must be correct, but for a corner or edge, the analysis uses the **union bound**:

$$
P_s \approx 4\,Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2d^2}{N_0}}\right) \quad \text{(upper bound, valid for high SNR)}
$$

**Step 1:** Substitute $d^2 = \dfrac{3E_s}{2(M-1)}$ into the Q-argument:

$$
\sqrt{\frac{2d^2}{N_0}} = \sqrt{\frac{2}{N_0}\cdot\frac{3E_s}{2(M-1)}} = \sqrt{\frac{3E_s}{(M-1)N_0}}
$$

**Step 2:** Correct for the fact that $\frac{1}{\sqrt{M}}$ fraction of the symbols are *interior* and give a slightly different expression. The final high-SNR approximation is:

$$
P_s \approx 4\left(1 - \frac{1}{\sqrt{M}}\right)Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{3E_s}{(M-1)N_0}}\right)
$$

**Step 3:** Convert to bit error using **Gray coding** (where $P_b \approx P_s / \log_2 M$ at high SNR).

> **Conversion logic:** The factor $\left(1 - \frac{1}{\sqrt M}\right)$ accounts for the corner points, which have only one error direction instead of four. As $M \to \infty$, this factor $\to 1$ and $P_s \to 4Q(\cdot)$.

### 3.3 Derivation: Orthogonal MFSK Bandwidth

For $M$ orthogonal signals with frequency separation $\Delta f = \frac{1}{2T_s}$ (minimum orthogonality), the total spectrum spans from $f_1 - \tfrac{1}{2T_s}$ to $f_M + \tfrac{1}{2T_s}$:

$$
BW = f_M - f_1 + \frac{1}{T_s} = (M-1)\Delta f + \frac{1}{T_s}
$$

**Step 1:** Substitute $\Delta f = \tfrac{1}{2T_s}$:

$$
BW = (M-1)\cdot\frac{1}{2T_s} + \frac{1}{T_s} = \frac{M-1+2}{2T_s} = \frac{M+1}{2T_s}
$$

**Step 2:** Convert from $T_s$ to $R_b$ using $T_s = \frac{\log_2 M}{R_b}$:

$$
BW = \frac{(M+1)\,R_b}{2\log_2 M}
$$

**Step 3:** Using the more practical approximation that includes filter roll-off (often quoted in textbooks):

$$
BW \approx \frac{(M+3)\,R_b}{2\log_2 M}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** This shows MFSK is **bandwidth-inefficient** — doubling $M$ roughly doubles the bandwidth, but the *energy per symbol* $E_s$ grows, lowering $P_s$ (power-efficient).

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A digital link uses **16-QAM** at a bit rate $R_b = 4.8$ kbps over an AWGN channel with $N_0/2 = 10^{-10}$ W/Hz. The average received symbol energy is $E_s = 8 \times 10^{-8}$ J. Find (a) the symbol rate, (b) the minimum Nyquist bandwidth, (c) the approximate symbol error probability.

**Solution:**

**(a) Symbol rate:** $k = \log_2 16 = 4$ bits/symbol, so

$$
R_s = \frac{R_b}{k} = \frac{4800}{4} = 1200 \text{ symbols/s} \;\;(1.2 \text{ kBaud})
$$

**(b) Minimum Nyquist bandwidth:**

$$
BW = R_s = 1200 \text{ Hz} \quad (\text{using raised cosine with roll-off } \alpha = 0)
$$

For a practical raised-cosine with $\alpha = 0.5$:

$$
BW = R_s(1+\alpha) = 1200 \times 1.5 = 1800 \text{ Hz}
$$

**(c) Symbol error probability:** Compute the Q-argument.

$$
\sqrt{\frac{3E_s}{(M-1)N_0}} = \sqrt{\frac{3 \times 8 \times 10^{-8}}{(16-1) \times 2 \times 10^{-10}}} = \sqrt{\frac{24 \times 10^{-8}}{30 \times 10^{-10}}} = \sqrt{80} \approx 8.944
$$

$$
P_s \approx 4\left(1 - \frac{1}{4}\right)Q(8.944) = 3\,Q(8.944)
$$

Since $Q(8.944)$ is vanishingly small, $P_s \approx 0$ — the link is highly reliable.

> **Conversion logic:** This worked example shows that for moderate SNR, increasing $M$ is feasible without catastrophic errors — the trade-off is what *minimum* $E_b/N_0$ is required for a target $P_b$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Constellation Comparison Block Diagram

The following diagram presents a comparative schematic of the four M-ary constellations and the high-level block structure of a generic M-ary transceiver.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SRC["SOURCE"]
        bits["Binary Bit Stream\nRb bits/s"]
    end

    subgraph ENC["TRANSMITTER"]
        grp["Serial-to-Parallel\nk = log2 M bits/group"]
        map["Symbol Mapper\n(Constellation LUT)"]
        pshp["Pulse Shaping\nRaised Cosine Filter"]
        mod["Carrier Modulator\nI and Q rails"]
    end

    subgraph CH["AWGN CHANNEL"]
        add["+ n(t)"]
    end

    subgraph REC["RECEIVER"]
        demod["Coherent\nI/Q Demodulator"]
        smp["Matched Filter\nSampler"]
        detect["Decision Device\n(Nearest Neighbor)"]
        par["Parallel-to-Serial"]
    end

    bits --> grp --> map --> pshp --> mod --> add --> demod --> smp --> detect --> par
    par --> out["Recovered Bits"]

    subgraph CONS["CONSTELLATION ATLAS"]
        c1["MASK\n(M points on I-axis)"]
        c2["MFSK\n(M orthogonal axes)"]
        c3["MPSK\n(M points on circle)"]
        c4["MQAM\n(M points on grid)"]
    end

    map -. maps to .-> CONS
    detect -. compares to .-> CONS
```

### 4.2 Trade-off Visualization: Bandwidth vs. Power Efficiency

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["M-ary Scheme"] --> B["Bandwidth BW"]
    A --> C["Power Eb/N0 for P10e-5"]

    B --> D["Mask: 2Rb/log2M\nMFSK: M+3 times Rb/2log2M\nMPSK: 2Rb/log2M\nMQAM: 2Rb/log2M"]

    C --> E["MFSK best for large M\nMQAM best overall\nMASK worst\nMPSK intermediate"]

    F["Trade-off Principle"] --> G["Higher M means\nlower BW but\nhigher Eb/N0"]
```

### 4.3 Conceptual M-ary Mapping Table

| Input bits | MASK level | MFSK frequency | MPSK phase | M-QAM I/Q |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 000 | $A_1$ | $f_1$ | $0°$ | $(-3, -3)$ |
| 001 | $A_2$ | $f_2$ | $45°$ | $(-3, -1)$ |
| 010 | $A_3$ | $f_3$ | $90°$ | $(-3, +3)$ |
| 011 | $A_4$ | $f_4$ | $135°$ | $(-1, -3)$ |
| 100 | $A_5$ | $f_5$ | $180°$ | $(-1, -1)$ |
| 101 | $A_6$ | $f_6$ | $225°$ | $(-1, +3)$ |
| 110 | $A_7$ | $f_7$ | $270°$ | $(+3, -3)$ |
| 111 | $A_8$ | $f_8$ | $315°$ | $(+3, +1)$ |

*(Table illustrates 8-ary case — easily extends to 16, 64, 256.)*

### 4.4 Key Architectural Notes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Coherent vs. Non-coherent**: MPSK and MQAM **require coherent detection** (carrier recovery via PLL/Costas loop). MFSK can be non-coherent (envelope detection on bandpass filters) — a major advantage for low-cost receivers.
> - **PAPR (Peak-to-Average Power Ratio)**: Constant-envelope schemes (MPSK, MFSK) allow **non-linear** amplifiers (Class C) — power-efficient. MQAM and MASK need **linear** amplifiers (Class A/AB) — power-inefficient.
> - **Gray coding** is assumed in all KTU problems when converting $P_s \to P_b$ to ensure only one bit error per symbol error.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is meant by M-ary digital modulation? Why is it preferred over binary modulation in bandwidth-limited channels?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

M-ary digital modulation is a technique in which each transmitted symbol represents a group of $k = \log_2 M$ bits, choosing one signal from a set of $M = 2^k$ possible signals. In binary modulation, $M = 2$ and each symbol carries 1 bit; in M-ary modulation, each symbol carries multiple bits.

In **bandwidth-limited channels** (e.g., satellite, mobile cellular), M-ary schemes are preferred because the **symbol rate $R_s = R_b / \log_2 M$ is reduced** for a given bit rate $R_b$. Since the transmission bandwidth is proportional to $R_s$, using higher-order modulation (e.g., 64-QAM instead of BPSK) reduces the required bandwidth by a factor of $\log_2 M = 6$. This increases **spectral efficiency** $\eta = R_b/BW$ bits/s/Hz. The trade-off is the higher $E_b/N_0$ required to maintain the same bit error probability.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Compare M-ary PSK and M-ary QAM in terms of bandwidth efficiency and power efficiency.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Both MPSK and M-QAM have the **same minimum Nyquist bandwidth**, given by $BW = 2R_b / \log_2 M$ (raised cosine with roll-off $\alpha = 0$), so their **bandwidth efficiency is identical** at $\eta = \log_2 M / 2$ bits/s/Hz.

However, **M-QAM is more power-efficient** than MPSK for the same $M$ (where $M \ge 16$). This is because QAM uses a 2-D rectangular grid, so the **minimum Euclidean distance** between adjacent points is larger than for MPSK (which packs points on a circle). The result is a smaller $E_b/N_0$ requirement for a target $P_b$. For example, 16-QAM needs roughly **4 dB less SNR** than 16-PSK for the same $P_b = 10^{-5}$. The trade-off is that QAM has a variable envelope and requires a linear power amplifier.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **probability of symbol error** $P_s$ of an M-ary PSK system over an AWGN channel. Show that it reduces to the BPSK result when $M = 2$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A communication system uses **8-PSK** to transmit at a bit rate of $R_b = 3$ Mbps over a channel with $\frac{N_0}{2} = 10^{-9}$ W/Hz. The received symbol energy is $E_s = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ J. Calculate:
   (i) The symbol rate and minimum Nyquist bandwidth.
   (ii) The approximate symbol error probability.
   (iii) The bit error probability assuming Gray coding. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution A(a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Constellation geometry.** In MPSK, all $M$ signals have equal energy $E_s$ and phases $\phi_i = \frac{2\pi(i-1)}{M}$ for $i = 1, 2, \dots, M$. They lie on a circle of radius $\sqrt{E_s}$ in the I/Q signal space.

**Step 2: Minimum distance.** Two adjacent phase points subtend an angle $\frac{2\pi}{M}$ at the origin. The chord length between them is:

$$
d_{min} = 2\sqrt{E_s}\,\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi}{M}\right)
$$

**Step 3: Decision rule.** With coherent detection, the receiver projects the received phasor onto all $M$ basis vectors and selects the closest. A symbol error occurs if noise pushes the received point across the decision boundary — a perpendicular bisector between the transmitted point and its neighbor.

**Step 4: Probability of error per boundary.** The noise in each dimension is Gaussian with variance $\frac{N_0}{2}$. The error occurs when the noise projection along the bisector exceeds $d_{min}/2$:

$$
P_{boundary} = Q\!\left(\frac{d_{min}/2}{\sqrt{N_0/2}}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{N_0}}\;\sin\!\frac{\pi}{M}\right)
$$

**Step 5: Union bound over all neighbors.** Each interior point has 2 nearest neighbors; using the union bound for $M \ge 4$:

$$
\boxed{P_s \approx 2\,Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{N_0}}\;\sin\!\frac{\pi}{M}\right)}
$$

**Step 6: Reduction to BPSK.** For $M = 2$, $\sin(\pi/2) = 1$ and $E_s = E_b$:

$$
P_s = 2Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{2} = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)
$$

This is the well-known BPSK result. ✓

---

### **Model Solution A(b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** $M = 8$, $R_b = 3 \times 10^6$ bps, $\frac{N_0}{2} = 10^{-9}$ W/Hz, $E_s = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ J.

**(i) Symbol rate and bandwidth:**

$$
k = \log_2 8 = 3 \text{ bits/symbol}
$$

$$
R_s = \frac{R_b}{k} = \frac{3 \times 10^6}{3} = 10^6 \text{ symbols/s} = 1 \text{ MBaud}
$$

Minimum Nyquist bandwidth (raised cosine, $\alpha = 0$):

$$
BW = R_s = 1 \text{ MHz}
$$

**(ii) Symbol error probability:**

$$
\frac{E_s}{N_0} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 10^{-9}} = 1000
$$

$$
\sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{N_0}}\,\sin\!\frac{\pi}{8} = \sqrt{2000} \times 0.3827 = 44.72 \times 0.3827 \approx 17.11
$$

$$
P_s \approx 2\,Q(17.11) \approx 0
$$

The link is essentially error-free. To get a non-trivial $P_s$, we would need to either reduce $E_s$ or use higher-order modulation.

**(iii) Bit error probability:** With Gray coding,

$$
P_b \approx \frac{P_s}{\log_2 M} = \frac{0}{3} = 0
$$

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **transmitter and receiver of a 16-QAM system**. Derive the **probability of symbol error** for a square M-QAM constellation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **64-QAM** system transmits at $R_b = 24$ Mbps with an average symbol energy $E_s = 10^{-6}$ J over a channel with $\frac{N_0}{2} = 5 \times 10^{-10}$ W/Hz. Calculate:
   (i) The bandwidth efficiency.
   (ii) The approximate symbol error probability.
   (iii) The minimum bandwidth required if a raised-cosine filter with roll-off $\alpha = 0.25$ is used. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution B(a) — 7 marks**

**Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**

- **Transmitter:** Binary data $\to$ Serial-to-Parallel converter (splits into 4-bit groups) $\to$ 2-bit groups to **I-channel DAC** and 2-bit groups to **Q-channel DAC** $\to$ Low-pass filters (pulse shaping) $\to$ Mixers with $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ and $\sin(2\pi f_c t)$ $\to$ Summer $\to$ Channel.
- **Channel:** $s(t) + n(t)$ where $n(t)$ is AWGN.
- **Receiver:** BPF $\to$ Mixers with local $\cos$ and $\sin$ oscillators (coherent) $\to$ LPF $\to$ Sampler $\to$ Decision device (compares to 4 thresholds on each rail) $\to$ Parallel-to-Serial converter $\to$ Output bits.

**Derivation of Symbol Error (4 marks):**

For square M-QAM with $M = L^2$, the I-rail takes values $\pm d, \pm 3d, \dots, \pm (L-1)d$ and similarly for Q. The decision thresholds are at $0, \pm 2d, \pm 4d, \dots$. The minimum distance between any two adjacent points is $2d$.

**Step 1:** Compute the average symbol energy by averaging $|s_i|^2$ over the constellation:

$$
E_s = \frac{2}{3}(M-1)\,d^2
$$

**Step 2:** For a given symbol, the I-channel and Q-channel decisions are independent. For an interior point, the error probability per dimension is $p = 2Q\!\left(\frac{d}{\sqrt{N_0/2}}\right)$.

**Step 3:** The symbol error probability (using the high-SNR union bound, valid for $M \ge 4$):

$$
\boxed{P_s \approx 4\left(1 - \frac{1}{\sqrt M}\right)Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{3E_s}{(M-1)N_0}}\right)}
$$

**Step 4:** Substituting $E_s = E_b \log_2 M$ and $M = 2^k$:

$$
P_s \approx 4\left(1 - \frac{1}{\sqrt M}\right)Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{3\,k\,E_b}{(M-1)N_0}}\right)
$$

---

### **Model Solution B(b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** $M = 64$, $R_b = 24$ Mbps, $E_s = 10^{-6}$ J, $\frac{N_0}{2} = 5 \times 10^{-10}$ W/Hz, $\alpha = 0.25$.

**(i) Bandwidth efficiency:**

$$
k = \log_2 64 = 6
$$

$$
R_s = \frac{R_b}{k} = \frac{24 \times 10^6}{6} = 4 \times 10^6 \text{ symbols/s}
$$

For ideal Nyquist ($\alpha = 0$): $BW = R_s = 4$ MHz, so

$$
\eta = \frac{R_b}{BW} = \frac{24 \text{ Mbps}}{4 \text{ MHz}} = 6 \text{ bits/s/Hz}
$$

**(ii) Symbol error probability:**

$$
\frac{E_s}{N_0} = \frac{10^{-6}}{2 \times 5 \times 10^{-10}} = \frac{10^{-6}}{10^{-9}} = 1000
$$

Q-argument:

$$
\sqrt{\frac{3E_s}{(M-1)N_0}} = \sqrt{\frac{3 \times 1000}{63}} = \sqrt{47.62} \approx 6.901
$$

$$
P_s \approx 4\left(1 - \frac{1}{8}\right)Q(6.901) = 3.5 \times Q(6.901)
$$

From Q-tables, $Q(6.9) \approx 2.5 \times 10^{-12}$:

$$
P_s \approx 3.5 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-12} \approx 8.75 \times 10^{-12}
$$

**(iii) Minimum bandwidth with roll-off:**

$$
BW = R_s(1 + \alpha) = 4 \times 10^6 \times 1.25 = 5 \text{ MHz}
$$

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students lose marks in M-ary problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\log_2 M$ factor in $E_s$**: When a problem gives $E_b/N_0$, you MUST compute $E_s/N_0 = (E_b/N_0) \cdot \log_2 M$ before substituting into the $P_s$ formula. **This is the #1 error.**
> 2. **Mixing up $P_s$ and $P_b$**: KTU expects $P_b = P_s / \log_2 M$ *only with Gray coding*. Without it, $P_b = P_s \cdot 2^{k-1}/(2^k - 1)$.
> 3. **Using the wrong bandpass/MFSK formula**: The MFSK bandwidth is $\frac{(M+3)R_b}{2\log_2 M}$ (textbook form) or $\frac{M}{2T_s}$ (minimum orthogonal form). Pick the right one for the given problem.
> 4. **Not specifying the roll-off factor $\alpha$** in the bandwidth calculation. The full form is $BW = R_s(1+\alpha)$.
> 5. **Confusing $E_{avg}$ and $E_{peak}$** in MASK problems — always use the **average** energy in the Q-function argument.
> 6. **Forgetting units**: $E_b/N_0$ is dimensionless, but the $E_s$ in the formula must be in **Joules** and $N_0$ in **W/Hz**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **M-ary** means $M$ distinct symbols, each carrying $k = \log_2 M$ bits.
- The **bit rate** $R_b$ is fixed by the application; the **symbol rate** $R_s = R_b / k$ drops as $M$ increases.
- **Bandwidth efficiency** improves with $M$ for MASK, MPSK, MQAM (since $BW \propto R_s$); **degrades** for MFSK (since $BW \propto M$).
- **Power efficiency** ($E_b/N_0$ for fixed $P_b$) **degrades** with $M$ for all schemes — the **fundamental trade-off**.
- **MPSK**: constant envelope, bandwidth-efficient, but circular packing wastes power.
- **MQAM**: best of both worlds — used everywhere in modern broadband (Wi-Fi, 4G/5G, DVB).
- **MFSK**: power-efficient for large $M$ but bandwidth-hungry — used in HF military and acoustic links.
- **MASK**: rarely used alone today (except as PAM-4 in optical fiber).
- The **symbol error formula** for MPSK is $P_s \approx 2Q(\sqrt{2E_s/N_0}\,\sin(\pi/M))$ — **must memorize**.
- The **symbol error formula** for square M-QAM is $P_s \approx 4(1 - 1/\sqrt M)Q(\sqrt{3E_s/[(M-1)N_0]})$ — **must memorize**.
- **Gray coding** is the universal assumption: $P_b = P_s / k$ at high SNR.
- **Coherent detection** is required for MPSK and MQAM; MFSK can be non-coherent.
- **Constant-envelope** schemes (MPSK, MFSK) tolerate non-linear amplifiers; MQAM and MASK need linear ones.
- **Spectral efficiency** $\eta = R_b / BW$ — the KTU benchmark metric for choosing an M-ary scheme.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quadrature Phase Shift Keying
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET864 - ELECTRONIC COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Quadrature Phase Shift Keying

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Quadrature Phase Shift Keying (QPSK)** is a **multi-level (M-ary) digital phase modulation technique** where the phase of the sinusoidal carrier wave is discretely varied in accordance with the digital baseband information signal. Specifically, in QPSK, the carrier phase assumes **four (4) distinct, equally-spaced values** — typically `0°, 90°, 180°,` and `270°` (or equivalently, `45°, 135°, 225°, 315°`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"QPSK is an M-ary modulation scheme with M = 4. Since the input is grouped into pairs of bits (dibits), each symbol carries **2 bits** of information, thereby doubling the **spectral efficiency (bandwidth efficiency)** of BPSK while maintaining the same bit error rate performance at a given `Eb/N0`."*

Since `log₂(4) = 2`, every transmitted symbol encodes **2 bits**. The modulation parameter set is:

$$\phi_i \in \left\{0, \frac{\pi}{2}, \pi, \frac{3\pi}{2}\right\} \quad ; \quad i = 1,2,3,4$$

The generalized QPSK signal is expressed as:

$$s_i(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{T_s}} \cos\left(2\pi f_c t + \phi_i\right) \quad ; \quad 0 \leq t \leq T_s$$

where `E_s` is the **energy per symbol**, `T_s` is the **symbol duration**, and `f_c` is the **carrier frequency**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Compass & Dancer" Model

Imagine a dancer standing at the center of a circular stage holding a lantern. There are **four coloured arrows** painted on the floor at the four cardinal directions (North, East, South, West).

- The dancer points the lantern toward the arrow corresponding to the **incoming dibit (pair of bits)**:
  - `00` → **East** (0°)
  - `01` → **North** (90°)
  - `10` → **West** (180°)
  - `11` → **South** (270°)
- A **blind observer** at the rim of the stage is told the dancer's pointing direction (with some noise). They decode it back into the original 2-bit code.

> [!NOTE]
> The observer can only see the **direction** (phase), not the **distance** — exactly like in a QPSK receiver, where only the **constellation point angle** matters; the signal **amplitude is held constant** (envelope is ideally constant, like an FM-like signal).

This is the key insight: **QPSK trades amplitude information for phase information, packing 2 bits per symbol**.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics at a Glance

| Metric | Value / Behavior |
|---|---|
| Number of symbols (M) | **4** |
| Bits per symbol (k) | **2** |
| Symbol rate vs Bit rate | `R_s = R_b / 2` |
| Bandwidth vs BPSK | **Halved** (for same bit rate) |
| Spectral Efficiency | **2 bits/s/Hz** (vs 1 bit/s/Hz for BPSK) |
| Envelope | **Constant** (Non-Constant Envelope in practice due to filtering) |
| Minimum carrier separation | Same as BPSK at same `R_b` |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Quadrature Decomposition — "The Heart of QPSK"

The conceptual breakthrough behind QPSK is **orthogonal signal space decomposition**. Using the trigonometric identity:

$$\cos(\alpha + \beta) = \cos(\alpha)\cos(\beta) - \sin(\alpha)\sin(\beta)$$

we can rewrite the QPSK signal as the **sum of two orthogonal BPSK signals** — one carried by an in-phase carrier `cos(2πf_ct)`, the other by a quadrature carrier `sin(2πf_ct)` (90° phase-shifted):

$$s_i(t) = \underbrace{\sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{T_s}}\cos(\phi_i)}_{I\text{-component}} \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t) - \underbrace{\sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{T_s}}\sin(\phi_i)}_{Q\text{-component}} \cdot \sin(2\pi f_c t)$$

For the four phase values `0°, 90°, 180°, 270°`, the `I` and `Q` components take values `±√(E_s/T_s)`. This gives the **signal space representation**:

$$s_i(t) = \sqrt{\frac{E_s}{T_s}}\cos(2\pi f_c t) \pm \sqrt{\frac{E_s}{T_s}}\sin(2\pi f_c t)$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters:** Because `cos(2πf_ct)` and `sin(2πf_ct)` are **orthogonal** over a symbol period, two independent data streams can be **multiplexed onto the same carrier frequency** without interfering. This is the foundation of modern **spectrally-efficient modulation** (used in Wi-Fi, 4G/5G, satellite TV).

## 2.2 Bit-to-Phase Mapping (Gray Coding)

A critical engineering choice is the **bit-to-symbol mapping**. To minimize the bit error rate under additive noise, KTU 2024 syllabus mandates **Gray coding** — adjacent constellation points differ by **only ONE bit**.

| Dibit `b₁b₀` | Phase `φ` | In-Phase `I` | Quadrature `Q` |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| `00` | 0° | `+√(E_s/T_s)` | 0 |
| `01` | 90° | 0 | `+√(E_s/T_s)` |
| `10` | 180° | `-√(E_s/T_s)` | 0 |
| `11` | 270° | 0 | `-√(E_s/T_s)` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> With Gray coding, when noise causes a symbol error, the **most likely mistake** is the nearest neighbour (1 symbol away). The result: only **1 bit is wrong per symbol error**, so `P_b ≈ P_s / 2`.

## 2.3 Noise Analysis & Decision Boundaries

The QPSK signal space has **four orthonormal basis points** at coordinates `(±√E_s, 0)` and `(0, ±√E_s)`. The **minimum distance** between any two constellation points is:

$$d_{min} = \sqrt{2E_s}$$

The decision boundaries are the **two orthogonal axes** (the `I` and `Q` axes). Any received point with `I > 0, Q > 0` is decoded as "00", and so on — like slicing a pizza into four equal quadrants.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formula Sheet for KTU 2024 ESE** — Commit these to memory.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bits per symbol | `k = log₂(M)` | Dimensionless; QPSK → `k = 2` |
| 2 | Symbol rate | `R_s = R_b / k` | Symbols/sec (Baud) |
| 3 | Symbol duration | `T_s = k · T_b` | Seconds |
| 4 | Energy per symbol | `E_s = k · E_b` | Joules |
| 5 | Bandwidth (theoretical) | `B = R_s = R_b / 2` | Hz |
| 6 | Spectral efficiency | `η = R_b / B = 2` | bits/s/Hz |
| 7 | Carrier phase | `φ_i = (2i − 1)π/4` | Radians; `i = 1,2,3,4` |
| 8 | Min. symbol distance | `d_min = √(2E_s)` | Volts (signal-space units) |
| 9 | Probability of symbol error | `P_s ≈ 2Q(√(E_s/N_0))` | Q-function form |
| 10 | Probability of bit error | `P_b ≈ Q(√(2E_b/N_0))` | For Gray-coded QPSK |
| 11 | Equivalence to BPSK | `P_b(QPSK) = P_b(BPSK)` | Same `E_b/N_0` performance |
| 12 | Equivalent lowpass signal | `s̃_i(t) = √(E_s/T_s) e^{jφ_i}` | Complex baseband |

> **Critical Substitution Note:** `Q(x) = (1/2) · erfc(x/√2)` — KTU examiners often use `erfc(·)` form in answer keys.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility — Why QPSK Rules the World

QPSK is the **workhorse modulation** of:
- **Digital Satellite TV** (DVB-S2 uses variants like 8PSK, 16APSK)
- **Cellular 3G/4G** (WCDMA, LTE use QPSK as the baseline)
- **Cable modems** (DOCSIS)
- **GPS** (uses BPSK, but QPSK is used in modernized GPS L1C)
- **Bluetooth** (uses π/4-DQPSK in enhanced rates)
- **Wi-Fi (legacy IEEE 802.11)** — QPSK is a mandatory modulation

> **Why preferred over BPSK?** Same noise immunity, **half the bandwidth** for the same bit rate — a 100% bandwidth saving. Why preferred over 16-QAM? More robust in **noisy/fading channels** (lower `E_b/N_0` requirement for target `P_b = 10^{-5}`).
